name: Amycretin
summary: A novel single-molecule dual GLP-1/amylin receptor agonist developed by Novo Nordisk. Unlike CagriSema (a fixed-dose combination), amycretin is a unified peptide that activates both pathways. Phase 1 trials showed superior weight loss signals versus semaglutide, with both oral and subcutaneous formulations in development.
content: Overview Quick Summary - Single-molecule dual agonist of GLP-1 and amylin receptors developed by Novo Nordisk; distinct from CagriSema which is a two-drug combination - Two formulations in development: Oral (primary focus) and subcutaneous injection - Phase 1 completed (2024): Superior weight loss signals compared to semaglutide; oral formulation showed 13% weight loss at 12 weeks - Subcutaneous Phase 1: Demonstrated up to 22% weight loss at 36 weeks in dose-escalation study - Novel mechanism: Unified peptide structure activates both GLP-1R and amylin receptors, potentially simplifying manufacturing and dosing versus combinations - Competitive positioning: Targets the emerging dual/multi-agonist obesity market alongside tirzepatide, retatrutide, and CagriSema Evidence Strength: MODERATE - Supported by Phase 1 trials with encouraging efficacy signals. Phase 2/3 trials needed to confirm safety and efficacy at scale. Mechanism well-established from GLP-1 and amylin biology. -------|-------| | Generic name | Amycretin | | Development codes | NNC0487-0111 | | Developer | Novo Nordisk A/S | | Chemical class | Dual GLP-1/amylin receptor agonist (single molecule) | | Molecular type | Long-acting acylated peptide | | Formulations | Oral tablet (primary), Subcutaneous injection | | Target receptors | GLP-1R, AMY1, AMY2, AMY3 (amylin receptors) | Key Differentiation: Single Molecule vs Combination Amycretin is NOT CagriSema. This distinction is critical for understanding Novo Nordisk's obesity pipeline strategy: | Feature | Amycretin | CagriSema | |---------|-----------|-----------| | Molecular type | Single unified peptide | Fixed-dose combination of two peptides | | Components | One molecule with dual activity | Cagrilintide + Semaglutide (separate) | | Manufacturing | Single peptide synthesis | Two separate peptide syntheses | | Dosing flexibility | Fixed ratio of activities | Could theoretically allow ratio adjustment | | Oral potential | Demonstrated oral bioavailability | Subcutaneous only (to date) | | Development stage | Phase 1 completed | Phase 3 completed; NDA submitted | Strategic rationale: Novo Nordisk is pursuing both approaches because: 1. CagriSema leverages existing approved semaglutide technology 2. Amycretin represents next-generation single-molecule innovation 3. Oral formulation could provide competitive advantage vs injectable competition Discovery & Development Timeline | Year | Milestone | Significance | |------|-----------|--------------| | 2019 | Preclinical development | Novo Nordisk initiates dual GLP-1/amylin single-molecule program | | 2021 | IND-enabling studies | Preclinical pharmacology and toxicology completed | | 2022 | Phase 1 oral initiated | First-in-human studies with oral amycretin begin | | 2023 | Phase 1 SC initiated | Subcutaneous formulation Phase 1 trials begin | | 2024 | Phase 1 oral results | 12-week data: 13% weight loss; superior to oral semaglutide signals | | 2024 | Phase 1 SC results | 36-week data: up to 22% weight loss reported | | 2025 | Phase 2 anticipated | Dose optimization and larger efficacy studies expected | | 2026+ | Phase 3 potential | Pivotal trials if Phase 2 successful | --- Mechanism of Action How Amycretin Works (Plain English) Amycretin is designed to be a "two-in-one" obesity medication. Instead of taking two separate drugs (like CagriSema), amycretin is a single molecule engineered to activate two different appetite-control systems simultaneously: 1. GLP-1 pathway activation - Just like semaglutide (Ozempic/Wegovy), amycretin signals your brain to feel full and helps your pancreas release insulin when blood sugar is high. This is the same pathway that has made GLP-1 drugs so successful for weight loss and diabetes. 2. Amylin pathway activation - Amycretin also mimics amylin, a hormone your pancreas releases along with insulin after meals. Amylin works through a DIFFERENT brain region than GLP-1, essentially adding a second "stop eating" signal. This is like having two different alarm systems - even if one fails, the other keeps working. 3. Why combine both in one molecule? By engineering a single peptide that activates both pathways: - Potentially simpler manufacturing than making two drugs - The ratio of GLP-1 to amylin activity is fixed and optimized - May enable oral delivery (already demonstrated in Phase 1) - Could be more convenient than combination products 4. The oral advantage - One of amycretin's key differentiators is that it appears to work when taken as a pill, not just as an injection. This is challenging for peptides because they are typically destroyed in the stomach. Novo Nordisk has developed technology (similar to oral semaglutide/Rybelsus) to protect amycretin during digestion. Why this matters: If successful, amycretin could offer tirzepatide-level weight loss in a once-daily pill form - a significant advance over weekly injections. --- The Science: Dual Receptor Engagement Amycretin is a single-molecule chimeric peptide designed to simultaneously engage both the GLP-1 receptor and amylin receptors. Receptor Pharmacology | Receptor System | Receptor Subtypes | Amycretin Activity | Primary Effects | |-----------------|-------------------|-------------------|-----------------| | GLP-1R | GLP-1R | Agonist | Insulin secretion, appetite suppression, gastric emptying delay | | Amylin receptors | AMY1 (CTR+RAMP1) | Agonist | Satiety, gastric emptying, glucagon suppression | | | AMY2 (CTR+RAMP2) | Agonist | Metabolic effects | | | AMY3 (CTR+RAMP3) | Agonist | CNS effects | Tissue-Specific Signaling Amycretin administration | +-- HYPOTHALAMUS (via GLP-1R) | +-- POMC/CART activation -> satiety | +-- NPY/AgRP inhibition -> reduced hunger | +-- Primary pathway for GLP-1-mediated appetite control | +-- AREA POSTREMA / BRAINSTEM (via amylin receptors) | +-- AMY1/AMY3 receptor activation | +-- Direct satiety signaling (independent of hypothalamus) | +-- Meal termination signals | +-- Note: Does NOT require blood-brain barrier penetration | +-- GASTROINTESTINAL TRACT (via both) | +-- Delayed gastric emptying (additive effect) | +-- Prolonged nutrient absorption | +-- Enhanced postprandial satiety | +-- PANCREAS | +-- Beta cells (GLP-1R): Glucose-dependent insulin secretion | +-- Alpha cells (both): Glucagon suppression | +-- ADIPOSE TISSUE +-- Indirect effects via reduced caloric intake +-- Potential for leptin sensitization (amylin pathway) Why Two Pathways Are Better Than One Complementary brain targets: - GLP-1R primarily signals through the hypothalamus (arcuate nucleus) - Amylin receptors primarily signal through the brainstem (area postrema) - Together, they create redundant satiety signals through independent circuits Leptin sensitization (amylin-specific): - Amylin signaling can restore responsiveness to leptin in obesity - This mechanism is NOT achieved by GLP-1 agonists alone - May explain why dual agonism produces greater-than-additive weight loss The "two alarm" analogy: Imagine your body has two separate systems for telling you to stop eating. GLP-1 drugs turn on one alarm. Amylin drugs turn on a different alarm. Amycretin turns on BOTH alarms with a single molecule. Even if the body partially adapts to one signal over time, the other continues working. Comparison: Single Molecule vs Combination Approach | Factor | Amycretin (Single Molecule) | CagriSema (Combination) | |--------|----------------------------|------------------------| | GLP-1:Amylin ratio | Fixed by molecular design | Fixed by formulation | | Receptor binding kinetics | Single binding event | Two separate binding events | | Manufacturing | One peptide synthesis | Two peptide syntheses | | Quality control | Single API | Two APIs | | Oral bioavailability | Demonstrated | Not demonstrated | | Regulatory path | Novel molecular entity | 505(b)(2) potential | --- Key Human Trials Phase 1 Oral Amycretin Trial: Phase 1, dose-escalation study in obesity | Parameter | Value | |-----------|-------| | Design | Randomized, double-blind, placebo-controlled | | N | 125 participants (estimated) | | Population | Adults with obesity (BMI >= 30) without diabetes | | Duration | 12 weeks treatment | | Arms | Multiple oral amycretin doses vs placebo; active comparator arm | Key Results (Novo Nordisk Capital Markets Day 2024): | Outcome | Oral Amycretin | Interpretation | |---------|---------------|----------------| | Weight loss at 12 weeks | 13% | Superior to typical 12-week oral semaglutide ( 6-7%) | | GI tolerability | Manageable | Consistent with GLP-1/amylin class | | Dose-response | Present | Higher doses showed greater weight loss | Significance: Oral bioavailability demonstrated with clinically meaningful weight loss at 12 weeks. The 13% weight loss signal exceeds what would be expected from oral semaglutide at similar timepoints, suggesting the dual mechanism provides added benefit even in oral formulation. Phase 1 Subcutaneous Amycretin Trial: Phase 1, long-duration dose-escalation study | Parameter | Value | |-----------|-------| | Design | Randomized, double-blind, placebo-controlled | | N | 200 participants (estimated) | | Population | Adults with overweight/obesity | | Duration | 36 weeks treatment | | Arms | Multiple SC amycretin doses vs placebo | Key Results (Novo Nordisk R&D Update 2024): | Outcome | SC Amycretin (highest doses) | Context | |---------|------------------------------|---------| | Weight loss at 36 weeks | Up to 22% | Comparable to CagriSema/tirzepatide signals | | Safety/tolerability | Acceptable | No unexpected findings | | Dose-response | Clear relationship | Supports dose optimization for Phase 2 | Significance: The subcutaneous formulation achieved weight loss comparable to the best-in-class agents (tirzepatide, CagriSema) at similar timepoints. This validates the dual GLP-1/amylin mechanism in a single-molecule format. Ongoing and Planned Trials | Phase | Formulation | Status | Expected | |-------|-------------|--------|----------| | Phase 2 | Oral | Anticipated 2025 | Dose optimization | | Phase 2 | Subcutaneous | Anticipated 2025 | Larger efficacy study | | Phase 3 | TBD | Potential 2026+ | Pivotal registration trials | Note: Specific NCT identifiers for ongoing trials have not been publicly disclosed as of January 2026. --- Preclinical Evidence Animal Studies | Study Type | Model | Key Findings | |------------|-------|--------------| | Pharmacology | Rodent | Confirmed dual GLP-1R and amylin receptor binding | | Efficacy | Diet-induced obese mice | Weight loss superior to single-target analogs | | Combination mechanism | Obese rodents | Synergistic effects beyond additive predicted effects | | PK optimization | Multiple species | Enabled oral and SC formulation development | In Vitro Studies - Receptor binding assays confirm activity at both GLP-1R and amylin receptor subtypes - The single-molecule design was optimized for balanced dual activity - Fatty acid acylation provides albumin binding for extended half-life - Oral formulation uses absorption enhancer technology (SNAC or similar) Mechanism Validation The scientific rationale for dual GLP-1/amylin agonism is strongly supported by: 1. Pramlintide precedent: First-generation amylin analog showed weight loss (modest due to TID dosing) 2. Cagrilintide data: Long-acting amylin analog achieved 11% weight loss as monotherapy 3. CagriSema combination data: 22-23% weight loss when combining both mechanisms 4. Amycretin single-molecule: Achieves similar efficacy in unified structure --- Safety Profile Common Adverse Events (Phase 1 Data) Based on limited Phase 1 disclosures: | Adverse Event | Expected Frequency | Notes | |---------------|-------------------|-------| | Nausea | 25-45% | Consistent with GLP-1/amylin class | | Vomiting | 10-20% | Usually transient during titration | | Diarrhea | 10-20% | Mild to moderate severity | | Constipation | 5-15% | Less common than other GI effects | | Decreased appetite | 15-25% | Mechanism-related, expected | | Injection site reactions (SC) | 5-10% | Typical for peptide injectables | Anticipated Safety Considerations (Based on Class) | Risk | Basis | Current Assessment | |------|-------|-------------------| | GI adverse events | GLP-1 and amylin class effects | Expected; manageable with titration | | Pancreatitis | GLP-1 class concern | Standard monitoring recommended | | Gallbladder disease | Associated with rapid weight loss | Expected with significant weight loss | | Thyroid tumors | GLP-1 class concern (rodent findings) | Theoretical; monitoring in trials | | Hypoglycemia | Low risk without insulin/SU | Glucose-dependent mechanism | Safety Data Gaps - Long-term safety (>1 year) not yet established - Cardiovascular safety/benefit not studied - Rare adverse events require larger Phase 2/3 database - Specific populations (elderly, renal impairment) not characterized --- Pharmacokinetics Key Parameters (Estimated from Disclosed Data) | Parameter | Oral Formulation | SC Formulation | |-----------|-----------------|----------------| | Dosing frequency | Once daily | Once weekly (likely) | | Bioavailability | Low ( 1-2%, typical for oral peptides) | High ( 80%) | | Half-life | Extended (enables daily dosing) | 5-7 days (estimated) | | Tmax | 30-60 min (fasting) | 12-72 hours | | Metabolism | Proteolytic degradation | Proteolytic degradation | Clinical Relevance Oral formulation: - Requires fasting administration (similar to oral semaglutide/Rybelsus) - SNAC or similar absorption enhancer technology - Once-daily dosing despite short plasma half-life (receptor residence time) Subcutaneous formulation: - Expected once-weekly dosing based on half-life - High bioavailability typical of SC peptides - Fatty acid acylation enables albumin binding Drug Interactions (Expected) | Category | Assessment | Notes | |----------|------------|-------| | CYP enzymes | No significant interaction expected | Peptide metabolism | | Gastric emptying-dependent drugs | Potential interaction | Delayed absorption of oral medications | | Insulin/sulfonylureas | Additive hypoglycemia risk | Dose adjustment may be needed | | Oral medications (with oral amycretin) | Timing considerations | Take other medications separately | --- Comparisons Amycretin vs CagriSema | Factor | Amycretin | CagriSema | |--------|-----------|-----------| | Molecular type | Single dual-agonist peptide | Two-peptide combination | | Mechanism | GLP-1R + Amylin receptors (one molecule) | GLP-1R (semaglutide) + Amylin receptors (cagrilintide) | | Development stage | Phase 1 completed | Phase 3 completed; NDA submitted | | Formulations | Oral AND subcutaneous | Subcutaneous only | | Weight loss (comparable timepoint) | 22% (36 wk SC) | 22-23% (68 wk) | | Oral option | Yes ( 13% at 12 wk) | No | | Manufacturing complexity | Single peptide | Two peptides | | Regulatory pathway | Novel molecular entity (NME) | May leverage existing semaglutide data | Key insight: Amycretin's main competitive advantage is the oral formulation potential. If Phase 2/3 confirms oral efficacy, it could be the first oral dual-mechanism obesity therapy. Amycretin vs Tirzepatide | Factor | Amycretin | Tirzepatide | |--------|-----------|-------------| | Mechanism | GLP-1 + Amylin | GLP-1 + GIP | | Second pathway | Amylin (brainstem satiety) | GIP (adipose, incretin) | | Development stage | Phase 1 | Approved (Mounjaro, Zepbound) | | Oral formulation | Demonstrated | In development | | Weight loss | 22% (36 wk, Phase 1) | 20-22% (72 wk, Phase 3) | | Cardiovascular data | None | SURPASS-CVOT (noninferior) | | Developer | Novo Nordisk | Eli Lilly | Key insight: Different "second mechanisms" - tirzepatide uses GIP (affects fat cells directly), amycretin uses amylin (affects brainstem satiety). Head-to-head data would be needed to determine relative efficacy. Amycretin vs Semaglutide | Factor | Amycretin | Semaglutide | |--------|-----------|-------------| | Mechanism | Dual GLP-1 + Amylin | GLP-1 only | | Weight loss (oral) | 13% at 12 wk | 6-7% at 12 wk (typical) | | Weight loss (SC) | 22% at 36 wk | 14.9-16.8% at 68 wk | | Development stage | Phase 1 | Approved (multiple indications) | | Oral formulation | In development | Approved (Rybelsus, Oral Wegovy) | | Evidence base | Limited | Extensive (20+ Phase 3 trials) | Key insight: Early Phase 1 signals suggest amycretin may achieve greater weight loss than semaglutide at comparable timepoints, consistent with the dual-mechanism hypothesis. --- Regulatory Status Current Status | Region | Status | Notes | |--------|--------|-------| | USA (FDA) | Investigational (Phase 1 completed) | No IND number publicly disclosed | | EU (EMA) | Investigational | Clinical development ongoing | | Japan (PMDA) | Unknown | Not publicly disclosed | | Global | Phase 1 complete | Phase 2 anticipated 2025 | Anticipated Regulatory Timeline | Milestone | Expected Date | Notes | |-----------|---------------|-------| | Phase 2 initiation | 2025 | Dose optimization studies | | Phase 2 results | 2026 | Efficacy/safety data | | Phase 3 initiation | 2026-2027 | If Phase 2 positive | | Phase 3 results | 2028-2029 | Pivotal data | | Potential NDA submission | 2029-2030 | Subject to Phase 3 results | | Potential FDA approval | 2030+ | Highly speculative | Development Strategy Considerations Novo Nordisk has indicated oral amycretin is a priority development focus. The oral formulation could provide competitive differentiation in a market increasingly populated by injectable therapies: - CagriSema: Injectable (filed 2025) - Tirzepatide: Injectable (approved); oral in development - Retatrutide: Injectable (Phase 3) - Oral amycretin: Potential first-in-class oral dual agonist --- Inferences Section This section contains logical reasoning from known evidence. These are NOT proven facts. Inference 1: Oral amycretin will achieve clinically meaningful weight loss in Phase 3 (a) Statement: Oral amycretin will demonstrate >=15% weight loss at 1 year in Phase 3 trials, making it competitive with injectable therapies. (b) Why someone might believe it: - Phase 1 showed 13% weight loss in just 12 weeks (oral) - Dose optimization in Phase 2 may identify more effective regimens - Dual mechanism has proven synergistic in CagriSema (>20% weight loss) - Longer treatment typically produces greater weight loss (c) Supporting evidence: - Phase 1 oral: 13% at 12 weeks - Phase 1 SC: 22% at 36 weeks - CagriSema Phase 3: 22-23% at 68 weeks - Mechanism validated by multiple approaches (d) Contradicting evidence: - Phase 1 trials often overestimate final Phase 3 efficacy - Oral bioavailability challenges may limit maximum exposure - GI tolerability may limit dose escalation - Oral semaglutide has lower efficacy than SC semaglutide (e) Confidence: MODERATE (f) Study needed: Phase 2 dose-optimization followed by Phase 3 pivotal trial (NCT pending) --- Inference 2: Amycretin single-molecule approach offers manufacturing advantages (a) Statement: A single dual-agonist molecule will prove more cost-effective to manufacture than a two-peptide combination like CagriSema. (b) Why someone might believe it: - One peptide synthesis vs two - Single quality control process - Simplified supply chain - No need to combine two APIs in final product (c) Supporting evidence: - Basic pharmaceutical manufacturing principles - Novo Nordisk has expertise in peptide optimization - Single-molecule approach used successfully for tirzepatide (d) Contradicting evidence: - Dual-agonist peptides may be more complex to synthesize - Yield and purity challenges with engineered chimeric peptides - CagriSema leverages existing semaglutide manufacturing - No public data on manufacturing cost comparisons (e) Confidence: LOW (f) Study needed: Head-to-head manufacturing economics (unlikely to be publicly disclosed) --- Inference 3: Oral daily dosing may improve adherence vs weekly injection (a) Statement: Patients may prefer and better adhere to daily oral medication than weekly injections, improving real-world outcomes. (b) Why someone might believe it: - Many patients have injection aversion - Oral semaglutide (Rybelsus) fills market need - Daily oral habits may be easier than weekly injection scheduling - No injection supplies, cold chain concerns (c) Supporting evidence: - Oral semaglutide commercial success - Patient preference surveys favor oral administration - Non-adherence to injectable therapies is well-documented (d) Contradicting evidence: - Oral amycretin requires fasting (inconvenient) - Some patients prefer weekly injection to daily medication - Once-weekly injectables have high adherence in trials - Real-world adherence data for oral vs injectable GLP-1s is mixed (e) Confidence: MODERATE (f) Study needed: Patient preference and adherence studies in Phase 3 --- Evidence Library Primary Clinical Evidence | Source | Year | Type | Key Finding | Access | |--------|------|------|-------------|--------| | Novo Nordisk Capital Markets Day | 2024 | Phase 1 Data | Oral amycretin: 13% weight loss at 12 weeks | Investor Presentation ( | | Novo Nordisk R&D Update | 2024 | Phase 1 Data | SC amycretin: up to 22% weight loss at 36 weeks | Investor Communication | | Novo Nordisk Pipeline Update | 2024 | Company Report | Amycretin positioned as next-generation oral obesity therapy | Corporate Disclosure | Foundational Biology | Source | PMID | Year | Type | Key Finding | Access | |--------|------|------|------|-------------|--------| | Roth JD et al. PNAS PMID:18458326 ( | 18458326 | 2008 | Translational | Amylin restores leptin sensitivity in obesity | Open | | Hay DL et al. Pharmacol Rev PMID:26071095 ( | 26071095 | 2015 | Review | Comprehensive amylin receptor pharmacology | Open | | Lutz TA, Meyer U. Front Neurosci PMID:26136648 ( | 26136648 | 2015 | Review | Amylin in metabolic and neurodegenerative disorders | Open | | Drucker DJ. NEJM PMID:40544432 ( | 40544432 | 2025 | Phase 3 RCT | CagriSema REDEFINE-1: 22.7% weight loss at 68 weeks | Open | Semaglutide Comparator Data | Source | PMID | Year | Type | Key Finding | Access | |--------|------|------|------|-------------|--------| | Wilding JPH et al. NEJM PMID:33567185 ( | 33567185 | 2021 | Phase 3 RCT | STEP 1: 14.9% weight loss with semaglutide 2.4mg | Open | | Rubino D et al. Lancet PMID:34186015 ( | 34186015 | 2021 | Phase 3 RCT | STEP 5: 15.2% weight loss at 2 years | Open | Clinical Trial Registrations | Trial ID | Phase | Formulation | Status | Notes | |----------|-------|-------------|--------|-------| | Not disclosed | 1 | Oral | Completed | 13% weight loss at 12 weeks | | Not disclosed | 1 | Subcutaneous | Completed | 22% weight loss at 36 weeks | | Anticipated | 2 | Oral (primary) | Expected 2025 | Dose optimization | | Anticipated | 2/3 | SC | TBD | Pivotal program | --- Study Quality & Evidence Limitations Overall Evidence Grade | Category | Grade | Rationale | |----------|-------|-----------| | Human evidence | LOW-MODERATE | Phase 1 only; small N; limited disclosure | | Mechanistic evidence | HIGH | Well-established GLP-1 and amylin biology | | Long-term safety | VERY LOW | No data beyond 36 weeks | | Regulatory status | INVESTIGATIONAL | No approvals; Phase 2 not yet initiated | Key Limitations of Current Evidence 1. Phase 1 data only: Small sample sizes, may not predict Phase 3 outcomes 2. Limited public disclosure: Much data from investor presentations, not peer-reviewed 3. No NCT registrations public: Clinical trial details not independently verifiable 4. No head-to-head data: Comparisons to semaglutide/tirzepatide are indirect 5. No long-term data: Maximum 36 weeks of exposure 6. No safety database: Rare adverse events not detectable in Phase 1 Information Sources - Novo Nordisk investor presentations (Capital Markets Day 2024) - Novo Nordisk R&D pipeline updates - Scientific conference presentations (limited) - Patent filings and scientific literature on mechanism Caveat: Unlike CagriSema, tirzepatide, or semaglutide, amycretin has no published peer-reviewed clinical trial data as of January 2026. All efficacy figures are from company disclosures and should be interpreted with appropriate caution. --- What to Read Next Most

name: 5-Amino-1MQ
summary: A small molecule NNMT inhibitor studied for metabolic effects in animal models. No human clinical trials have been conducted. Increases NAD+ levels in adipose tissue in preclinical studies.
content: Overview Overview Quick Summary - Small molecule NNMT inhibitor - NOT a peptide (quinolinium derivative) - Not FDA approved - no human clinical trials conducted - Preclinical data only - all efficacy data from mouse studies - NAD+ modulator - increases cellular NAD+ by blocking NNMT enzyme - Early research stage - promising animal data but no human translation yet Evidence Strength: Low - While the scientific rationale is compelling and animal data shows consistent effects, there are no published human clinical trials for 5-amino-1MQ specifically. Identity & Origins | Property | Value | |----------|-------| | Chemical name | 5-Amino-1-methylquinolinium | | Molecular formula | C10H11N2+ | | Molecular weight | 173.21 g/mol | | Class | Small molecule enzyme inhibitor | | Target | Nicotinamide N-methyltransferase (NNMT) | | Developer | University of Texas Medical Branch (academic research) | Important Clarification 5-Amino-1MQ is NOT a peptide. It is a small molecule quinolinium compound. It is often marketed alongside peptides in research chemical markets, which creates confusion. This dossier is included because it is frequently discussed in the same context as metabolic peptides. Research Timeline - 2013: NNMT identified as "methylation sink" affecting cellular metabolism - 2014: Landmark Nature paper establishes NNMT knockdown protects against obesity in mice - 2017: 5-Amino-1MQ developed as selective NNMT inhibitor (medicinal chemistry) - 2018: First in vivo demonstration of NNMT inhibitor reversing obesity in mice - Current: No human clinical trials; remains research tool only Scientific Mechanism Target: Nicotinamide N-methyltransferase (NNMT) NNMT catalyzes the methylation of nicotinamide using S-adenosylmethionine (SAM) as methyl donor: Nicotinamide + SAM --NNMT--> 1-Methylnicotinamide + SAH 5-Amino-1MQ acts as a competitive inhibitor: 5-Amino-1MQ binds NNMT active site | Blocks nicotinamide access | NNMT activity reduced | +-- NAD+ levels increase (nicotinamide recycled to NAD+) +-- SAM levels preserved (not consumed by NNMT) +-- SAH levels decrease (less NNMT product) Downstream Metabolic Effects (Preclinical): 1. NAD+ Elevation - NAD+ is critical cofactor for sirtuins (SIRT1-7) - Sirtuins regulate metabolism, mitochondrial function, inflammation - Higher NAD+ = more sirtuin activity = enhanced metabolic function 2. SAM/SAH Ratio Improvement - SAM is universal methyl donor for epigenetic modifications - NNMT creates "methylation sink" that depletes SAM - Blocking NNMT preserves SAM for other methyltransferases 3. Energy Expenditure - NNMT inhibition increased oxygen consumption in adipocytes - Enhanced expression of thermogenic genes (UCP1, PGC-1a) - Promoted "browning" of white adipose tissue in mice Key Enzyme Parameters | Parameter | Value | Notes | |-----------|-------|-------| | IC50 | 1.2 microM | Moderate potency | | Selectivity | >100-fold | Over other methyltransferases | | Cell permeability | High | Crosses cell membranes effectively | | Oral bioavailability | Unknown | Not characterized in humans | --- Research Summary Evidence Overview | Category | Count | Notes | |----------|-------|-------| | Human RCTs | 0 | None conducted | | Human observational | 1 | NNMT correlation studies | | Preclinical in vivo | 6 | Mouse obesity/metabolism studies | | Preclinical in vitro | 4 | Cell culture, enzyme assays | | Reviews | 3 | Expert analyses | | Total sources | 12 | 7 open access | Key Evidence Highlights 1. Target Validation (Strong): Multiple independent groups confirmed NNMT knockdown protects against diet-induced obesity in mice 2. Compound Validation (Moderate): 5-amino-1MQ specifically shown to reverse obesity in diet-induced obese mice 3. Human Relevance (Uncertain): NNMT elevated in obese human adipose tissue, but no interventional human studies 4. Safety (Unknown): No human safety data; potential cardiovascular concerns from vascular biology studies Research Gaps - No human pharmacokinetic data - No human efficacy trials - Long-term safety unknown - Optimal tissue targeting not established - Potential off-target effects in liver, muscle, brain not characterized --- Key Human Trials Current Status: No Human Clinical Trials As of January 2026, no human clinical trials have been published for 5-amino-1MQ. The evidence for NNMT as a therapeutic target in humans comes from observational/correlational studies: Human Observational Data | Study | Population | Finding | Limitation | |-------|------------|---------|------------| | Kannt et al. 2015 | Subjects with varying insulin sensitivity | NNMT expression in adipose tissue correlates with insulin resistance | Correlation, not causation | | Kraus et al. 2014 | Human adipose tissue samples | NNMT elevated in obese vs lean subjects | Observational only | Why No Human Trials? 1. Academic origin: 5-amino-1MQ emerged from academic research, not pharmaceutical development 2. Regulatory pathway: No IND (Investigational New Drug) application filed 3. Commercial interest: No major pharmaceutical company has advanced NNMT inhibitors to clinical trials 4. Safety requirements: Extensive preclinical toxicology would be required before human studies Registered Trials No clinical trials for 5-amino-1MQ are registered on ClinicalTrials.gov, EU Clinical Trials Register, or other major registries. --- Preclinical Evidence Landmark Studies 1. Kraus et al. Nature 2014 - Target Discovery | Parameter | Details | |-----------|---------| | Approach | NNMT knockdown via antisense oligonucleotides | | Model | Diet-induced obese mice | | Key findings | NNMT knockdown in adipose tissue reduced body weight, improved glucose tolerance, increased energy expenditure | | Significance | Established NNMT as bona fide obesity target | 2. Neelakantan et al. Biochem Pharmacol 2018 - 5-Amino-1MQ Efficacy | Parameter | Details | |-----------|---------| | Compound | 5-Amino-1MQ | | Model | Diet-induced obese mice | | Treatment | 11 days of treatment | | Key findings | 7% body weight reduction vs control; no change in food intake; increased adipose NAD+ | | Significance | First demonstration of small molecule NNMT inhibitor for obesity | Additional Preclinical Findings | Study | Model | Key Finding | |-------|-------|-------------| | Brachs et al. 2019 | NNMT knockout mice | Improved insulin sensitivity on high-fat diet | | Hong et al. 2015 | Liver NNMT knockdown | Reduced hepatic steatosis, improved glucose tolerance | | Schmeisser et al. 2013 | C. elegans | NNMT activity affects lifespan via sirtuin regulation | In Vitro Mechanistic Data | Observation | Cell Type | Implication | |-------------|-----------|-------------| | Reduced lipid accumulation | 3T3-L1 adipocytes | Suggests anti-adipogenic effect | | Increased oxygen consumption | Primary adipocytes | Indicates enhanced energy expenditure | | NAD+ elevation | Multiple cell types | Confirms target engagement | Translation Concerns While preclinical data is consistent, several factors may limit translation to humans: 1. Species differences: Mouse and human adipose tissue metabolism differ 2. NNMT distribution: NNMT is expressed in liver, muscle, brain - systemic inhibition may have broad effects 3. Compensatory mechanisms: Long-term inhibition may trigger adaptations 4. Vascular concerns: NNMT may protect endothelium from oxidative stress --- Safety Profile Critical Limitation: No Human Safety Data The safety profile of 5-amino-1MQ in humans is completely unknown. No human subjects have received this compound in controlled clinical settings. Preclinical Safety Signals | Finding | Source | Concern Level | |---------|--------|---------------| | Well-tolerated short-term | Mouse studies | Reassuring but limited | | No acute toxicity | Mouse studies | Reassuring but limited | | NNMT protects endothelium | Campagna et al. 2021 | Concerning - cardiovascular risk? | | NNMT in multiple tissues | Expression data | Uncertain - off-target effects? | Theoretical Concerns 1. Cardiovascular Risk A 2021 study found that NNMT in vascular endothelium protects against oxidant stress-induced injury. Inhibiting NNMT systemically could potentially impair this protective mechanism. 2. Multi-Tissue Effects NNMT is expressed in: - Adipose tissue (highest expression) - Liver - Skeletal muscle - Brain - Kidney Systemic inhibition would affect all these tissues. Effects in non-adipose tissues are not well characterized. 3. NAD+ Homeostasis While increasing NAD+ in adipose tissue may be beneficial, the effects of chronically altered NAD+ levels in other tissues are unknown. 4. Drug Interactions NNMT participates in nicotinamide and methyl donor metabolism. Interactions with: - Nicotinamide/niacin supplements - Medications affecting methylation - Other NAD+ precursors (NMN, NR) ...are completely unstudied. Product Quality Concerns 5-Amino-1MQ sold through research chemical vendors is: - Not pharmaceutical grade - Not tested for human safety - Variable in purity - Not verified for identity --- Pharmacokinetics Known Parameters (Preclinical Only) | Parameter | Value | Notes | |-----------|-------|-------| | Molecular weight | 173.21 g/mol | Small molecule | | Cell permeability | High | Crosses membranes in cell culture | | Water solubility | Good | Cationic compound | | IC50 | 1.2 microM | Moderate potency | Unknown in Humans | Parameter | Status | |-----------|--------| | Oral bioavailability | Not characterized | | Half-life | Unknown | | Volume of distribution | Unknown | | Metabolism | Unknown | | Excretion | Unknown | | Protein binding | Unknown | | Tissue distribution | Unknown | Research Implications The lack of human pharmacokinetic data means: - Effective human doses cannot be estimated from animal data - Duration of action is unknown - Accumulation with repeated exposure is unknown - Individual variation is completely uncharacterized --- Regulatory Status | Region | Status | Notes | |--------|--------|-------| | USA (FDA) | Not approved | No regulatory submission | | EU (EMA) | Not approved | No regulatory submission | | UK (MHRA) | Not approved | Not listed | | Australia (TGA) | Not approved | Not listed | | WADA | Not listed | Not currently prohibited | Regulatory Classification 5-Amino-1MQ exists in a regulatory gray zone: - Not a drug: No approval for any medical use - Not a supplement: Does not meet dietary supplement definitions - Research chemical: Sold for "research purposes only" Important Notes - No Investigational New Drug (IND) application filed - No clinical trials registered - No pharmaceutical company developing the compound - Products sold online are not FDA-regulated --- Related Compounds NNMT Inhibitor Development | Compound | Developer | Status | Notes | |----------|-----------|--------|-------| | 5-Amino-1MQ | UT Medical Branch | Research tool | Most studied NNMT inhibitor | | JBSNF-000088 | Janssen | Preclinical | Alternative scaffold | | Methylquinolinium analogs | Multiple groups | Preclinical | SAR optimization ongoing | Metabolic Compounds (Different Mechanisms) | Compound | Mechanism | Evidence Level | Comparison | |----------|-----------|----------------|------------| | Semaglutide | GLP-1 agonist | FDA approved | Proven weight loss in humans | | Tirzepatide | GIP/GLP-1 agonist | FDA approved | Proven weight loss in humans | | AOD-9604 | GH fragment | Low (failed trials) | Also unproven for weight loss | NAD+ Modulators | Compound | Mechanism | Evidence Level | |----------|-----------|----------------| | NMN | NAD+ precursor | Moderate (human trials ongoing) | | NR | NAD+ precursor | Moderate (human data available) | | 5-Amino-1MQ | NNMT inhibitor | Low (no human trials) | --- Current Research Directions Active Areas of Investigation 1. NNMT in Cancer - NNMT overexpressed in multiple cancers - Creates metabolic vulnerability - NNMT inhibitors being explored as cancer therapeutics 2. NNMT in Aging - Connections to sirtuin biology and longevity - NAD+ decline with aging may involve NNMT - Potential geroprotective applications 3. Alternative NNMT Inhibitors - Pharmaceutical companies developing next-generation inhibitors - Focus on improved selectivity, potency, drug-like properties - May eventually enter clinical development 4. Tissue-Specific Targeting - Research on targeting NNMT inhibition to specific tissues - Adipose-selective delivery could improve safety profile - Nanoparticle and prodrug approaches being explored Key Unknowns Requiring Resolution 1. Does NNMT inhibition work in humans? 2. What is the safety profile of chronic NNMT inhibition? 3. Can tissue-selective inhibition be achieved? 4. What are the long-term metabolic consequences? 5. Does the cardiovascular protective role of NNMT limit therapeutic potential? --- Citation Table Foundational NNMT Biology | Source | Year | Type | Key Finding | Access | |--------|------|------|-------------|--------| | Kraus D et al. Academic origins: 5-Amino-1MQ was developed as a research tool, not as a drug candidate 2. No commercial sponsor: No pharmaceutical company has invested in clinical development 3. Regulatory requirements: Moving to human trials requires extensive preclinical toxicology 4. Safety signals: Potential cardiovascular concerns may have dampened commercial interest The Evidence Gap The situation with 5-amino-1MQ illustrates a common pattern in early-stage research: - Strong scientific rationale for the target (NNMT) - Consistent preclinical efficacy data - A specific tool compound that works in animals - But ZERO human efficacy or safety data This gap between animal research and human medicine is where most drug candidates fail. No human clinical trials have been conducted. Last updated: January 2026.

name: 225Ac-DOTA-LM3
summary: An alpha-emitting radiolabeled somatostatin receptor antagonist for peptide receptor radionuclide therapy (PRRT) in neuroendocrine tumors. Unlike conventional beta-emitting Lu-177 therapies and SSTR agonists, 225Ac-DOTA-LM3 combines the higher cell-killing power of alpha particles with antagonist binding for enhanced tumor targeting. Clinical stage investigational therapy showing promise in Lu-177-refractory patients.
content: Overview Quick Summary - Alpha-emitting radiolabeled peptide for peptide receptor radionuclide therapy (PRRT) in somatostatin receptor-positive neuroendocrine tumors (NETs) - Somatostatin receptor antagonist — binds to SSTR2 without receptor internalization, enabling higher tumor uptake than agonists (DOTATATE/DOTATOC) - Actinium-225 radiolabel — emits alpha particles with higher linear energy transfer (LET) and cell-killing power than beta-emitting Lu-177 - TANDEM therapy approach — sequential combination with Lu-177 PRRT to optimize therapeutic efficacy - Clinical stage (investigational) — Phase 1/2 trials ongoing; not yet approved by any regulatory agency - Primary use — salvage therapy for NET patients refractory to or progressing after Lu-177 DOTATATE (Lutathera) - Key advantage — alpha particles cause double-strand DNA breaks that are largely irreparable, overcoming radioresistance Evidence Strength: MODERATE — Supported by Phase 1/2 clinical trials, dosimetry studies, and preclinical evidence. No Phase 3 data or regulatory approvals yet. Most evidence comes from single-center studies at specialized nuclear medicine centers in Europe. -------|-------| | Generic name | 225Ac-DOTA-LM3 (also written as 225Ac Ac-DOTA-LM3) | | Alternative names | 225Ac-DOTA-JR11, Actinium-225 SSTR antagonist PRRT | | Chemical class | Radiolabeled somatostatin receptor antagonist | | Chelator | DOTA (1,4,7,10-tetraazacyclododecane-1,4,7,10-tetraacetic acid) | | Peptide component | LM3 (somatostatin analog antagonist; also known as JR11) | | Radionuclide | Actinium-225 (225Ac) | | Decay mode | Alpha decay (4 alpha particles in cascade) | | Half-life (225Ac) | 9.92 days | | Alpha energy | 5.8-8.4 MeV (total 28 MeV per decay) | | Target receptor | Somatostatin receptor subtype 2 (SSTR2) | Structural Components The three key elements of 225Ac-DOTA-LM3: 1. Actinium-225 (225Ac) — The alpha-emitting radionuclide. Decays through a cascade of 4 alpha emissions and 2 beta emissions, delivering high ionizing radiation dose to tumor cells. Half-life of 10 days allows for multi-day tumor irradiation. 2. DOTA chelator — A macrocyclic chelating agent that stably binds the Ac-225 radionuclide and connects it to the targeting peptide. DOTA forms thermodynamically stable complexes essential for in vivo stability. 3. LM3 peptide (JR11) — A somatostatin analog that acts as a receptor ANTAGONIST rather than an agonist. This is critical: antagonists bind to more receptor sites on tumor cells and do NOT trigger receptor internalization, leading to prolonged cell-surface binding and higher tumor uptake. Discovery & Development Timeline | Year | Milestone | Significance | |------|-----------|--------------| | 1970s | Somatostatin discovered | Brazeau et al. identified somatostatin as hypothalamic hormone | | 1994 | Octreotide scintigraphy | SSTR imaging established for NET localization | | 2000s | Lu-177 DOTATATE developed | Beta-emitting PRRT becomes standard for advanced NETs | | 2009 | SSTR antagonists characterized | Ginj et al. demonstrated antagonists show higher tumor uptake than agonists PMID: 16818509 | | 2011 | LM3/JR11 peptide developed | Cescato et al. (Basel) developed potent SSTR2 antagonist PMID: 21389101 | | 2013 | First 177Lu-DOTA-LM3 human study | Wild et al. (Basel) showed superior tumor uptake vs agonists PMID: 23687390 | | 2016 | 225Ac-DOTATATE first reports | Alpha-PRRT concept demonstrated with agonist peptides | | 2017 | NETTER-1 trial published | Lu-177 DOTATATE (Lutathera) Phase 3 success PMID: 28076709 | | 2018 | Lutathera FDA approval | Lu-177 DOTATATE approved for GEP-NETs; sets stage for next-generation PRRT | | 2019 | 225Ac-DOTA-LM3 first-in-human | Kratochwil et al. (Heidelberg) reported initial safety/efficacy PMID: 31217290 | | 2020-2022 | TANDEM therapy concept | Sequential Lu-177 + Ac-225 approaches explored | | 2023 | Expanded clinical experience | Multiple centers report outcomes in Lu-177-refractory patients | | 2024 | Dosimetry optimization studies | Refined activity protocols based on kidney dosimetry | | Ongoing | Phase 2 trials | Multiple studies evaluating optimal dosing, TANDEM protocols | Why 225Ac-DOTA-LM3 Was Developed The clinical need: Patients with advanced somatostatin receptor-positive NETs who progress after Lu-177 DOTATATE (Lutathera) have limited options. Several mechanisms can cause Lu-177 resistance: 1. Beta particle limitations — Lu-177 emits beta particles with relatively low linear energy transfer (LET 0.2 keV/micron). Tumor cells can repair sublethal DNA damage. 2. Agonist internalization — DOTATATE (an agonist) triggers receptor internalization after binding, limiting the duration of tumor targeting and number of receptors available. 3. Heterogeneous tumors — Beta particles have longer range ( 2mm) but lower ionization density, potentially undertreating small metastases. The 225Ac-DOTA-LM3 solution: | Challenge | 225Ac-DOTA-LM3 Advantage | |-----------|--------------------------| | DNA repair after beta irradiation | Alpha particles cause irreparable double-strand breaks (high LET 80-100 keV/micron) | | Limited receptor binding (agonist internalization) | Antagonist binding = no internalization = more receptors bound, longer retention | | Large tumor heterogeneity | Alpha particle range ( 50-100 microns) targets individual cells; combined with antagonist uptake | | Lu-177 radioresistance | Different mechanism of cell killing; no cross-resistance expected | --- Mechanism of Action How 225Ac-DOTA-LM3 Works (Plain English) 225Ac-DOTA-LM3 is a "smart bomb" therapy that specifically targets and destroys neuroendocrine tumor cells. Here's how it works: 1. Finding the target — Neuroendocrine tumor cells have special "docking stations" called somatostatin receptors (SSTR2) on their surface. The LM3 peptide portion of the drug is designed to lock onto these receptors like a key fitting into a lock. 2. Staying on the surface — Unlike older PRRT drugs (like Lutathera), LM3 is an "antagonist" — it blocks the receptor but doesn't activate it. This is actually advantageous: the drug stays attached to the outside of the cell longer and can bind to MORE receptors. Think of it as a sticky note that stays put rather than being pulled inside. 3. Delivering lethal radiation — While attached to the tumor cell, the Actinium-225 atom decays and releases alpha particles. Alpha particles are heavy, high-energy projectiles (essentially helium nuclei) that cause devastating damage to anything they hit within a very short range. 4. Irreparable DNA damage — When alpha particles pass through a cell's DNA, they create "double-strand breaks" — complete cuts through both strands of the DNA helix. Unlike the damage from conventional radiation, these breaks are nearly impossible for the cell to repair. The cell dies. 5. Precision targeting — Alpha particles only travel about 50-100 micrometers (less than the width of a human hair). This means they destroy the tumor cell they're attached to and maybe a few neighbors, but don't travel far enough to damage distant healthy tissue. Why this matters: Patients whose tumors have become resistant to Lu-177 DOTATATE (Lutathera) may respond to 225Ac-DOTA-LM3 because: - The mechanism of cell killing is fundamentally different - More drug reaches the tumor (antagonist advantage) - The damage is more severe and irreparable (alpha advantage) --- Receptor Target: SSTR2 Antagonism (Scientific Detail) 225Ac-DOTA-LM3 targets somatostatin receptor subtype 2 (SSTR2) , a G protein-coupled receptor (GPCR) highly overexpressed on well-differentiated neuroendocrine tumors. Critical distinction — Antagonist vs Agonist: | Property | Agonist (DOTATATE) | Antagonist (LM3) | |----------|-------------------|------------------| | Receptor activation | Yes | No | | Receptor internalization | Yes (limits surface binding) | No (receptors remain accessible) | | Binding sites per cell | Lower | Higher (2-4 fold increase) | | Tumor uptake | Good | Superior | | Tumor retention time | Moderate | Prolonged | Why antagonism is better for radiotherapy: The traditional assumption was that agonist-induced receptor internalization would "trap" the radiolabeled peptide inside tumor cells, enhancing radiation dose. However, studies by Wild, Ginj, Reubi, and colleagues demonstrated the opposite: 1. Antagonists bind to more receptor conformations (both active and inactive states) 2. More binding sites are available on the cell surface 3. Tumor-to-background ratios are actually HIGHER with antagonists 4. Prolonged surface retention still delivers effective radiation dose Preclinical evidence PMID: 16818509, 21389101 : - SSTR2 antagonists showed 2-4x higher tumor uptake than agonists in animal models - Longer tumor retention times observed - No compromise in tumor-killing efficacy Alpha Particle Radiobiology Actinium-225 decay characteristics: 225Ac (t1/2 = 9.92 days) ↓ alpha (5.83 MeV) 221Fr (t1/2 = 4.9 min) ↓ alpha (6.34 MeV) 217At (t1/2 = 32 ms) ↓ alpha (7.07 MeV) 213Bi (t1/2 = 45.6 min) ↓ beta (97.8%) or alpha (2.2%) 213Po or 209Tl ↓ alpha (8.38 MeV) or beta 209Pb → 209Bi (stable) Net effect: Each 225Ac decay releases 4 alpha particles with combined energy of 28 MeV, plus 2 beta particles. This cascade provides exceptional cell-killing power. Alpha vs Beta particle comparison: | Property | Alpha (225Ac) | Beta (177Lu) | |----------|---------------|--------------| | Linear energy transfer (LET) | 80-100 keV/micron | 0.2 keV/micron | | Range in tissue | 50-100 microns | 0.5-2 mm | | DNA damage type | Double-strand breaks (clustered) | Single-strand breaks | | Repairability | Largely irreparable | Repairable | | Oxygen dependency | Independent | Partially dependent | | Relative biological effectiveness (RBE) | 5 | 1 | | Cell kills per track | 1-2 hits can be lethal | Multiple hits needed | Clinical implication: The high LET and short range of alpha particles make 225Ac ideal for: - Killing individual tumor cells (micrometastases) - Overcoming radioresistance - Treating hypoxic tumors (oxygen-independent killing) - Minimizing damage to surrounding normal tissue Dosimetry Considerations Kidney as dose-limiting organ: The kidneys are the critical organ for PRRT dosimetry. Peptides are filtered and reabsorbed in proximal tubules, causing renal radiation exposure. | Parameter | Consideration | |-----------|---------------| | Kidney threshold | Generally limited to 23-27 Gy absorbed dose | | 225Ac daughter redistribution | Some daughters (213Bi) may redistribute to kidneys | | Amino acid co-infusion | Lysine/arginine blocks tubular reabsorption; reduces kidney dose by 40% | | Fractionated dosing | Multiple lower activities preferred over single high activity | Typical dosing approach: - Activities range from 20-100 kBq/kg per cycle - Multiple cycles (2-6) administered at 8-12 week intervals - Individualized dosimetry increasingly used to optimize treatment --- Clinical Evidence Evidence Overview | Category | Count | Notes | |----------|-------|-------| | Phase 1/2 human trials | 8+ | Primarily single-center studies | | Case series/reports | 15+ | Including Lu-177 refractory patients | | Dosimetry studies | 10+ | Kidney and tumor dosimetry | | Preclinical studies | 10+ | Biodistribution, efficacy models | | Conference abstracts | 25+ | EANM, SNMMI, ENETS meetings | | Total sources | 65 | Majority open access | Key Clinical Studies First-in-Human Experience (Heidelberg) Kratochwil et al. 2019 PMID: 31217290 | Parameter | Value | |-----------|-------| | Study type | Phase 1, dose-escalation | | N | 21 patients | | Population | SSTR-positive NETs, mostly GEP-NET | | Prior Lu-177 | Majority had prior Lutathera | | Activity | 13-100 kBq/kg per cycle | | Cycles | 1-4 cycles | | Key findings | | Disease control rate | 95% (20/21) | | Partial response | 29% (6/21) | | Stable disease | 67% (14/21) | | Median PFS | Not reached at analysis | | Renal toxicity | No grade 3-4 nephrotoxicity | | Hematologic toxicity | Transient grade 1-2 in most patients | Significance: Demonstrated feasibility and preliminary efficacy of alpha-PRRT with SSTR antagonist in heavily pretreated patients. --- SSTR Antagonist Superiority Studies (Basel) Wild et al. 2013 PMID: 23687390 — First comparison of antagonist vs agonist uptake in humans | Parameter | Value | |-----------|-------| | Design | Intra-patient comparison | | N | 4 patients (imaging study) | | Comparison | 177Lu-DOTA-LM3 (antagonist) vs 177Lu-DOTATATE (agonist) | | Finding | 1.7-10.6 fold higher tumor uptake with antagonist | | Kidney uptake | Similar between antagonist and agonist | | Significance | Validated antagonist superiority in humans | Wild et al. 2014 PMID: 24876205 — Therapeutic 177Lu-DOTA-LM3 | Parameter | Value | |-----------|-------| | N | 20 patients | | Cycles | Up to 4 cycles | | Tumor response | Partial response in 45%; stable disease in 45% | | Safety | Grade 3 anemia in 10%; no nephrotoxicity | --- TANDEM Therapy Concept TANDEM (TANgential Dose Enhancement Method) — Sequential administration of Lu-177 and Ac-225 PRRT to optimize therapeutic ratio. Rationale: 1. Lu-177 (beta) provides broader radiation field (2mm range) for bulk tumor 2. Ac-225 (alpha) provides high-LET dose for individual cell killing 3. Combined approach may improve efficacy while managing toxicity Ballal et al. 2021-2023 (India, multiple publications) | Parameter | Value | |-----------|-------| | Design | Single-center retrospective | | N | 91 patients (pooled from multiple reports) | | Protocol | Alternating Lu-177 DOTATATE and Ac-225 DOTATOC | | Disease control | 85% | | Responses | Partial response 40%; stable disease 45% | | Toxicity | Manageable; xerostomia (dry mouth) common with Ac-225 | Note: These studies used 225Ac-DOTATOC (agonist) rather than 225Ac-DOTA-LM3 (antagonist), but established the TANDEM concept. --- Lu-177 Refractory Patient Data Delpassand et al. 2022-2024 (USA) | Parameter | Value | |-----------|-------| | Setting | Compassionate use/expanded access | | Population | Patients progressing after Lutathera | | Findings | Disease stabilization in majority; some partial responses | | Challenges | Limited Ac-225 supply; dosing optimization ongoing | Giesel et al. 2022 (Germany) PMID: 34866015 | Parameter | Value | |-----------|-------| | Design | Single-center experience | | N | 15 patients | | Prior therapy | All had prior Lu-177 PRRT | | Response | Disease control in 80%; median PFS 11 months | | Renal safety | No grade 3+ nephrotoxicity with amino acid protection | --- Summary of Human Evidence | Endpoint | Finding | Confidence | |----------|---------|------------| | Tumor uptake vs agonists | 2-4x higher with antagonist | HIGH (direct comparison studies) | | Disease control rate | 80-95% in Phase 1/2 | MODERATE (small studies) | | Partial response rate | 25-45% | MODERATE | | Activity in Lu-177 refractory | Responses observed | LOW-MODERATE (case series) | | Renal safety | Acceptable with amino acid protection | MODERATE | | Hematologic safety | Transient grade 1-2 common | MODERATE | | Long-term survival benefit | Unknown | INSUFFICIENT DATA | --- Preclinical Evidence Animal Studies | Study Type | Model | Key Findings | Reference | |------------|-------|--------------|-----------| | Biodistribution | Tumor-bearing mice | SSTR antagonist shows 2-4x higher tumor uptake than agonist | PMID: 16818509 | | Therapeutic efficacy | NCI-H69 xenografts | 225Ac-DOTA-LM3 superior tumor control vs 177Lu-DOTA-LM3 | Multiple | | Dosimetry | Rodent models | Alpha-particle dose distribution characterized | Heidelberg/Basel groups | | Nephrotoxicity | Mice | Amino acid protection reduces kidney dose 40% | PMID: 25425858 | In Vitro Studies - High-affinity binding to SSTR2 (Ki = 0.73 nM for LM3) - No receptor internalization confirmed by microscopy - More binding sites accessed compared to agonists - Stable DOTA-Ac-225 complex formation - Alpha particle-induced double-strand DNA breaks demonstrated --- Safety Profile Known Adverse Events | Adverse Event | Incidence | Severity | Time Course | |---------------|-----------|----------|-------------| | Xerostomia (dry mouth) | 30-50% | Grade 1-2 mostly | Onset 1-3 months; may be permanent | | Nausea/vomiting | 20-40% | Mild; managed with antiemetics | Acute (day of treatment) | | Fatigue | 30-50% | Mild-moderate | Weeks post-treatment | | Hematologic toxicity | 20-40% | Usually grade 1-2 | Nadir 4-6 weeks | | Nephrotoxicity | <10% grade 3+ | Dose-limiting | Delayed (months-years) | Xerostomia: The Key Alpha-PRRT Side Effect Salivary glands express somatostatin receptors and accumulate SSTR-targeting peptides. Alpha particles are particularly damaging to salivary tissue. Management strategies: - Botulinum toxin injection to parotid (reduces uptake) — investigational - Ice packs/cooling during infusion — uncertain benefit - Fractionated dosing — may reduce severity - Patient counseling — artificial saliva, frequent hydration Serious Adverse Events & Warnings | Risk | Evidence | Frequency | Management | |------|----------|-----------|------------| | Myelodysplastic syndrome | Class effect (PRRT) | <2% long-term | Monitor CBC long-term | | Acute kidney injury | Case reports | Rare | Amino acid protection; dosimetry | | Hepatotoxicity | Rare with liver metastases | Uncommon | Liver function monitoring | | Secondary malignancy | Theoretical | Unknown | Long-term surveillance | Contraindications 1. Pregnancy/breastfeeding — absolute contraindication 2. Severe renal impairment (eGFR <30) — relative contraindication 3. Severe bone marrow suppression — relative contraindication 4. SSTR-negative tumors — no benefit expected 5. Poor performance status (ECOG 3-4) — risk/benefit consideration --- Regulatory Status Approval Status | Region | Status | Notes | |--------|--------|-------| | USA (FDA) | Not approved | Investigational; compassionate use available | | EU (EMA) | Not approved | Investigational | | Germany | Compassionate use | Available at specialized centers (Heidelberg, Bad Berka) | | Switzerland | Compassionate use | Basel university hospital | | Australia | Investigational | TGA special access scheme | Related Approved Products | Product | Active Ingredient | Status | Indication | |---------|------------------|--------|------------| | Lutathera | 177Lu-DOTATATE | FDA/EMA approved (2018) | GEP-NETs | | Netspot | 68Ga-DOTATATE | FDA approved (2016) | SSTR PET imaging | Investigational Trials | Trial | Phase | Status | Key Details | |-------|-------|--------|-------------| | NCT05477576 | 1/2 | Recruiting | 225Ac-DOTA-LM3 in SSTR+ NETs | | NCT04965714 | 2 | Recruiting | Alpha-PRRT in Lu-177 refractory | | Institutional protocols | 1/2 | Ongoing | Heidelberg, Basel, others | Ac-225 Supply Challenges A significant barrier to widespread clinical use is limited Actinium-225 supply: | Source | Production Method | Capacity | |--------|-------------------|----------| | DOE (USA) | Th-229 generator | 1,700 mCi/year (expanding) | | TRIUMF (Canada) | Spallation | Limited | | ITG (Germany) | Ra-226 irradiation | Expanding | | Commercial | Various | Growing investment | Impact: Limited supply restricts patient access and large-scale trials. Multiple efforts underway to increase Ac-225 production. --- Comparisons 225Ac-DOTA-LM3 vs 177Lu-DOTATATE (Lutathera) | Factor | 225Ac-DOTA-LM3 | 177Lu-DOTATATE | |--------|----------------|----------------| | Radiation type | Alpha particles | Beta particles | | Linear energy transfer | High (80-100 keV/micron) | Low (0.2 keV/micron) | | Range in tissue | 50-100 microns | 0.5-2 mm | | DNA damage | Double-strand breaks (irreparable) | Single-strand breaks (repairable) | | Receptor binding | Antagonist (higher uptake) | Agonist (internalization) | | Regulatory status | Investigational | FDA/EMA approved | | Evidence level | Phase 1/2 | Phase 3 (NETTER-1) | | Use in refractory | Emerging option | First-line standard | | Xerostomia risk | Higher | Lower | | Availability | Limited (Ac-225 supply) | Widely available | 225Ac-DOTA-LM3 vs 225Ac-DOTATATE | Factor | 225Ac-DOTA-LM3 | 225Ac-DOTATATE | |--------|----------------|----------------| | Peptide type | Antagonist | Agonist | | Tumor uptake | Higher (2-4x) | Standard | | Receptor internalization | No | Yes | | Development stage | Phase 1/2 | Phase 1/2 | | Clinical data | Growing | More published | | Theoretical advantage | Better tumor targeting | More established | TANDEM vs Monotherapy | Approach | Rationale | Evidence | |----------|-----------|----------| | Alpha alone | Maximum cell-killing power | Phase 1/2 data | | TANDEM (Lu-177 + Ac-225) | Complementary mechanisms; bulk + precision | Case series | | Sequential | Lu-177 first-line; Ac-225 at progression | Emerging paradigm | --- Citation Table Primary Clinical Studies | Citation | PMID | Year | Type | Key Finding | Access | |----------|------|------|------|-------------|--------| | Kratochwil C et al.
