name: BT5528
summary: A first-in-class Bicycle Toxin Conjugate (BTC) targeting EphA2-expressing tumors, developed by Bicycle Therapeutics. Combines a constrained bicyclic peptide targeting moiety with the cytotoxic payload MMAE. Phase 1/2 dose expansion ongoing with 67% ORR reported in EphA2-positive urothelial cancer patients. Represents a novel therapeutic modality bridging small molecules and antibody-drug conjugates.
content: Overview Quick Summary - First-in-class Bicycle Toxin Conjugate (BTC) targeting EphA2-expressing solid tumors - Developer: Bicycle Therapeutics (Cambridge, UK/USA) - Phase 1/2 dose expansion ongoing — evaluating monotherapy and combination with nivolumab - 67% ORR reported in EphA2-positive urothelial cancer patients (preliminary data) - Novel modality: Bicyclic peptide + MMAE (monomethyl auristatin E) cytotoxic payload - First-in-class EphA2 targeting agent with no approved EphA2-directed therapies on market - Combination studies with checkpoint inhibitor nivolumab actively enrolling Evidence Strength: Moderate — Phase 1/2 clinical data available with promising preliminary efficacy signals. No completed Phase 3 trials. First-in-class mechanism with limited long-term safety data. -------|-------| | Generic name | BT5528 | | Development code | BT5528 | | Chemical class | Bicycle Toxin Conjugate (BTC) | | Type | Bicyclic peptide-drug conjugate | | Developer | Bicycle Therapeutics | | Target | EphA2 (Ephrin type-A receptor 2) | | Payload | MMAE (monomethyl auristatin E) | | Structure | Constrained bicyclic peptide + cleavable linker + MMAE | What is a Bicycle Toxin Conjugate? Bicycle Toxin Conjugates represent a novel therapeutic modality that combines: 1. Bicyclic peptide — A chemically constrained peptide that forms two interlocking rings, creating a highly stable, compact structure ( 1.5-2 kDa) that binds targets with antibody-like affinity 2. Cleavable linker — Connects the targeting peptide to the cytotoxic payload; designed to release the payload inside tumor cells 3. Cytotoxic payload — In BT5528, this is MMAE, a potent microtubule-disrupting agent Why this matters: BTCs bridge the gap between small molecules and antibody-drug conjugates (ADCs): - Smaller than antibodies ( 7 kDa vs 150 kDa) — enables deeper tumor penetration - Faster clearance — potentially reduced systemic toxicity - Antibody-like specificity — selective targeting of tumor-expressed antigens - No Fc-mediated effects — avoids some ADC-associated toxicities Discovery & Development Timeline | Year | Milestone | Significance | |------|-----------|--------------| | 2009 | Bicycle Therapeutics founded | Cambridge, UK-based company | | 2009-2015 | Bicycle platform development | Constrained bicyclic peptide technology optimized | | 2016 | EphA2 target selection | EphA2 identified as high-value oncology target | | 2017-2018 | BT5528 preclinical development | Lead optimization, efficacy and safety studies | | 2019 | IND filing | Investigational New Drug application submitted to FDA | | 2019 | Phase 1/2 trial initiated | NCT04180371 — first-in-human study launched | | 2021 | Preliminary Phase 1 data | Initial safety and PK data presented | | 2022 | Dose expansion initiated | Recommended Phase 2 dose established | | 2023-2024 | Combination cohorts opened | Nivolumab combination studies initiated | | 2024-2025 | 67% ORR reported | Preliminary efficacy in EphA2+ urothelial cancer | | Current | Phase 1/2 dose expansion | Monotherapy and combination arms recruiting | Why EphA2 as a Target? EphA2 (Ephrin type-A receptor 2) is an attractive oncology target because: 1. Overexpressed in multiple solid tumors: - Urothelial/bladder cancer: 50-70% of tumors - Non-small cell lung cancer (NSCLC): 60-80% - Ovarian cancer: 70-90% - Pancreatic cancer: 70-80% - Gastric/esophageal cancer: 60-70% - Triple-negative breast cancer: 50-60% 2. Limited expression in normal tissues: - Low levels in normal epithelium - Provides therapeutic window for targeted therapy 3. Associated with aggressive disease: - High EphA2 expression correlates with poor prognosis - Associated with metastasis and treatment resistance 4. No approved EphA2-directed therapies: - BT5528 is first-in-class - Prior EphA2-targeting approaches (ADCs, CAR-T) had limited success --- Mechanism of Action How BT5528 Works (Plain English) BT5528 works like a "smart missile" that finds cancer cells and delivers a toxic payload directly to them: 1. Finding the target — The bicyclic peptide portion of BT5528 circulates through your bloodstream until it encounters EphA2, a protein that is abnormally abundant on the surface of many cancer cells. The peptide binds tightly and specifically to EphA2. 2. Getting inside — Once bound, the cancer cell swallows the BT5528 molecule through a process called receptor-mediated endocytosis. This is like the cell grabbing and engulfing the drug. 3. Releasing the payload — Inside the cell, the linker connecting the peptide to the toxin is cleaved by cellular enzymes (cathepsins), releasing the MMAE payload. 4. Killing the cancer cell — Free MMAE disrupts microtubules, which are essential structures cells need to divide. Without functional microtubules, the cancer cell cannot divide and dies. Why small size matters: Because BT5528 is much smaller than antibody-drug conjugates ( 7 kDa vs 150 kDa), it can penetrate solid tumors more effectively. Think of it like the difference between a motorcycle and a truck navigating through traffic — the smaller vehicle can reach places the larger one cannot. --- Scientific Mechanism (Technical Detail) 1. Target Binding BT5528 (bicyclic peptide-MMAE) circulates in bloodstream | v High-affinity binding to EphA2 receptor (Kd low nM) | v Receptor-mediated endocytosis | v Internalization into endosome/lysosome compartment 2. Payload Release Acidic lysosomal environment | v Cathepsin B cleavage of valine-citrulline linker | v Release of free MMAE into cytoplasm 3. Cytotoxic Mechanism Free MMAE binds tubulin at vinca alkaloid binding site | v Inhibition of tubulin polymerization | v Mitotic arrest (G2/M phase) | v Apoptosis (programmed cell death) EphA2 Biology | Property | Description | |----------|-------------| | Full name | Ephrin type-A receptor 2 | | Gene | EPHA2 | | Protein type | Receptor tyrosine kinase | | Size | 130 kDa | | Normal function | Cell-cell communication, embryonic development, tissue boundaries | | Cancer role | Promotes tumor growth, invasion, metastasis, angiogenesis | EphA2 in cancer: - Ligand-independent (oncogenic) signaling promotes tumor progression - Overexpression correlates with poor prognosis in multiple tumor types - Internalization upon ligand binding makes it suitable for drug delivery MMAE Payload | Property | Value | |----------|-------| | Name | Monomethyl auristatin E | | Class | Antimitotic agent (auristatin) | | Mechanism | Tubulin polymerization inhibitor | | Potency | Subnanomolar cytotoxicity | | Used in | Brentuximab vedotin (Adcetris), Enfortumab vedotin (Padcev), Polatuzumab vedotin (Polivy) | Why MMAE? MMAE is a proven, highly potent cytotoxic payload with established clinical utility in multiple approved ADCs. Its mechanism (microtubule disruption) is well understood and effective across many cancer types. Advantages of Bicycle Platform | Property | BT5528 (BTC) | Traditional ADC | Small Molecule | |----------|--------------|-----------------|----------------| | Size | 7 kDa | 150 kDa | <1 kDa | | Tumor penetration | High | Limited | High | | Target specificity | High | High | Variable | | Half-life | Hours | Days-weeks | Hours | | Systemic exposure | Lower | Higher | Higher | | Manufacturing | Chemical synthesis | Biological | Chemical synthesis | --- Key Human Trials Phase 1/2 Study (NCT04180371) The primary clinical trial evaluating BT5528 is an open-label, multicenter, Phase 1/2 study. | Parameter | Value | |-----------|-------| | NCT Number | NCT04180371 | | Phase | 1/2 | | Status | Active, recruiting (as of January 2026) | | Sponsor | Bicycle Therapeutics | | Design | Open-label, dose-escalation and expansion | | Start Date | December 2019 | | Estimated Completion | 2026 | Study Arms: 1. Monotherapy arm — BT5528 as single agent 2. Combination arm — BT5528 + nivolumab (anti-PD-1 checkpoint inhibitor) Tumor Types Studied: - Urothelial/bladder cancer - Non-small cell lung cancer (NSCLC) - Gastric/esophageal cancer - Pancreatic cancer - Ovarian cancer - Other EphA2-expressing solid tumors Preliminary Efficacy Data Urothelial Cancer Cohort (EphA2-positive): | Outcome | Result | |---------|--------| | Overall Response Rate (ORR) | 67% | | Complete Response (CR) | Data maturing | | Partial Response (PR) | Majority of responders | | Disease Control Rate | >80% | | Median Duration of Response | Data maturing | Important caveats: - Preliminary data from dose expansion cohort - Small patient numbers (exact N not publicly disclosed in detail) - EphA2-positive patient selection - Further data maturation needed Patient Selection EphA2 Expression Testing: - Immunohistochemistry (IHC) used to assess EphA2 expression - Patients selected for EphA2-positive tumors - Threshold for positivity defined in protocol Prior Therapy: - Most patients had received 1-3 prior lines of therapy - Many had prior checkpoint inhibitor exposure - Chemotherapy-refractory patients included Combination with Nivolumab Rationale: - EphA2 targeting may enhance immune infiltration - MMAE-induced immunogenic cell death may synergize with checkpoint inhibition - Checkpoint inhibitors are standard of care in many EphA2+ tumor types Status: - Combination cohorts actively enrolling - Preliminary safety data suggests combination is tolerable - Efficacy data expected 2025-2026 Clinical Trial Details | Trial Feature | Description | |---------------|-------------| | Primary endpoints | Safety, tolerability, recommended Phase 2 dose (Phase 1); ORR (Phase 2) | | Secondary endpoints | PFS, OS, PK parameters, immunogenicity | | Exploratory endpoints | Biomarkers, EphA2 expression correlation | | Key inclusion | Advanced/metastatic solid tumors, EphA2 expression | | Key exclusion | Active CNS metastases, prior severe immunotherapy toxicity | Ongoing and Planned Studies | Study | Phase | Population | Status | |-------|-------|------------|--------| | NCT04180371 | 1/2 | EphA2+ solid tumors | Active, recruiting | | Expansion cohorts | 2 | Urothelial, NSCLC, others | Enrolling | | Nivolumab combination | 1/2 | EphA2+ solid tumors | Enrolling | | Potential registrational | 2/3 | TBD based on Phase 2 | Planned | --- Safety Profile Overview BT5528 safety data is derived from the ongoing Phase 1/2 trial. The safety profile is generally consistent with MMAE-containing conjugates, with some differences attributable to the bicycle platform. Common Adverse Events | Adverse Event | Incidence (approx.) | Severity | Relation to Mechanism | |---------------|---------------------|----------|----------------------| | Fatigue | 30-40% | Mostly mild-moderate | General toxicity | | Nausea | 25-35% | Mild-moderate | MMAE payload | | Peripheral neuropathy | 20-30% | Mild-moderate | MMAE (microtubule disruption) | | Decreased appetite | 20-25% | Mild | General toxicity | | Alopecia | 15-20% | Mild | MMAE payload | | Neutropenia | 15-25% | Can be severe | MMAE myelosuppression | | Rash | 10-15% | Mild-moderate | Possible target-related | | Diarrhea | 10-15% | Mild-moderate | General toxicity | MMAE-Related Toxicities As BT5528 uses MMAE as its cytotoxic payload, certain toxicities are expected based on experience with other MMAE-conjugates (brentuximab vedotin, enfortumab vedotin): 1. Peripheral Neuropathy - Mechanism: MMAE disrupts microtubules essential for nerve function - Presentation: Numbness, tingling, pain in hands/feet - Management: Dose modification, supportive care - Usually reversible with treatment interruption 2. Neutropenia - Mechanism: MMAE affects rapidly dividing bone marrow cells - Management: Dose delays, G-CSF support if needed - Monitoring: Regular CBC required 3. Skin Reactions - May be partially target-related (EphA2 expression in skin) - Generally mild and manageable Comparison to ADC Toxicities | Toxicity | BT5528 | Traditional MMAE-ADC | |----------|--------|----------------------| | Peripheral neuropathy | Present | Present | | Neutropenia | Present | Present | | Ocular toxicity | Not prominent | Common with some ADCs | | Infusion reactions | Minimal | More common | | Fc-mediated toxicity | Absent | Present | Potential advantage: The smaller size and faster clearance of BTCs may result in lower systemic exposure to free MMAE, potentially reducing some toxicities. This hypothesis requires further clinical validation. Serious Adverse Events | SAE | Frequency | Management | |-----|-----------|------------| | Severe neutropenia | Uncommon | Dose delay, G-CSF | | Febrile neutropenia | Rare | Hospitalization, antibiotics | | Severe neuropathy | Uncommon | Dose modification/discontinuation | Immunogenicity - Anti-drug antibodies (ADAs) being monitored - Bicyclic peptides are generally low immunogenicity - No significant neutralizing antibody concerns reported to date Precautions and Monitoring | Parameter | Recommendation | |-----------|----------------| | CBC | Regular monitoring for neutropenia | | Neurologic exam | Baseline and periodic assessment | | Liver function | Periodic monitoring | | Renal function | Periodic monitoring | --- Preclinical Evidence In Vitro Studies | Study Type | Model | Key Findings | |------------|-------|--------------| | Binding affinity | Purified EphA2 | High-affinity binding (low nM Kd) | | Selectivity | Receptor panel | Selective for EphA2 vs other Eph receptors | | Internalization | EphA2+ cell lines | Rapid receptor-mediated internalization | | Cytotoxicity | Cancer cell lines | Potent killing of EphA2+ cells; minimal effect on EphA2- cells | | Bystander effect | Co-culture | Some bystander killing of neighboring cells | In Vivo Studies (Animal Models) | Model | Result | Significance | |-------|--------|--------------| | EphA2+ xenografts | Tumor regression at therapeutic doses | Proof-of-concept efficacy | | EphA2- xenografts | Minimal activity | Target-dependent activity | | Patient-derived xenografts | Activity in EphA2+ PDX models | Relevance to human tumors | | Pharmacokinetics | Rapid tissue distribution, faster clearance than ADCs | Supports tumor penetration hypothesis | | Biodistribution | Tumor accumulation, rapid clearance from normal tissues | Favorable safety profile predicted | Comparison to EphA2-Targeting ADCs Prior EphA2-targeting approaches using traditional antibody-drug conjugates showed limited clinical success due to: - Poor tumor penetration of large antibodies - High systemic exposure leading to toxicity - Insufficient therapeutic window BT5528 was designed to address these limitations through: - Smaller size enabling better tumor penetration - Faster clearance reducing systemic exposure - Chemical synthesis enabling consistent manufacturing --- Pharmacokinetics Known Parameters (Preliminary Clinical Data) | Parameter | Value | Clinical Relevance | |-----------|-------|-------------------| | Half-life | 2-4 hours (estimated) | Much shorter than ADCs ( 3-4 weeks) | | Volume of distribution | High | Suggests good tissue penetration | | Clearance | Rapid | Shorter systemic exposure than ADCs | | Metabolism | Peptide degradation + MMAE release | Standard peptide/MMAE metabolism | | Route | Intravenous | Weekly infusion | Dosing | Parameter | Value | |-----------|-------| | Route | Intravenous infusion | | Schedule | Weekly (QW) | | Recommended Phase 2 dose | Established in Phase 1 (specific dose under evaluation) | Pharmacokinetic Advantages of BTC Platform 1. Rapid tumor penetration — Small size enables fast extravasation and diffusion 2. Shorter half-life — May reduce prolonged systemic toxicity 3. No FcRn recycling — Unlike antibodies, not recycled; more predictable PK 4. Linear PK — Expected; simpler dosing considerations --- Regulatory Status Current Status by Region | Region | Status | Notes | |--------|--------|-------| | USA (FDA) | Investigational | Phase 1/2 ongoing | | EU (EMA) | Investigational | Not yet submitted | | Japan (PMDA) | Investigational | Not yet submitted | | China (NMPA) | Not submitted | Potential future territory | Regulatory Designations | Designation | Status | Significance | |-------------|--------|--------------| | Orphan Drug | Under consideration | Could apply for rare cancer subsets | | Fast Track | Not yet designated | Potential based on unmet need | | Breakthrough Therapy | Not yet designated | May pursue based on Phase 2 data | Path to Approval Based on current development: 1. Complete Phase 1/2 — Establish RP2D and preliminary efficacy 2. Registrational strategy — Likely accelerated approval pathway based on ORR 3. Indication selection — EphA2+ urothelial cancer appears most advanced 4. Companion diagnostic — EphA2 IHC test likely required --- Related Peptides Bicycle Therapeutics Pipeline | Compound | Target | Payload | Indication | Status | |----------|--------|---------|------------|--------| | BT5528 | EphA2 | MMAE | Solid tumors | Phase 1/2 | | BT8009 | Nectin-4 | MMAE | Solid tumors | Phase 1/2 | | BT7480 | Nectin-4 + CD137 | Immune agonist | Solid tumors | Phase 1/2 | | BT7455 | Nectin-4 + CD137 + EphA2 | Multi-target | Discovery | Preclinical | Competitive Landscape: EphA2-Targeting Agents | Agent | Type | Developer | Status | Notes | |-------|------|-----------|--------|-------| | BT5528 | BTC (peptide-drug conjugate) | Bicycle Therapeutics | Phase 1/2 | Most advanced EphA2 agent | | MEDI-547 | ADC | AstraZeneca/MedImmune | Discontinued | Poor tolerability | | DS-8895a | ADC | Daiichi Sankyo | Phase 1 | Limited data | | EphA2 CAR-T | Cell therapy | Academic | Phase 1 | Glioblastoma focus | Note: BT5528 is currently the only EphA2-targeting agent in active late-stage clinical development. Related Modalities: Peptide-Drug Conjugates | Compound | Target | Type | Developer | Status | |----------|--------|------|-----------|--------| | BT5528 | EphA2 | BTC | Bicycle | Phase 1/2 | | BT8009 | Nectin-4 | BTC | Bicycle | Phase 1/2 | | 177Lu-PSMA-617 | PSMA | Radiopharmaceutical | Novartis | Approved | | PEN-221 | SSTR2 | PDC | Tarveda | Discontinued | Comparison: BTCs vs ADCs vs Small Molecules | Feature | BT5528 (BTC) | ADC (e.g., Padcev) | Small Molecule TKI | |---------|--------------|-------------------|-------------------| | Size | 7 kDa | 150 kDa | <1 kDa | | Target | EphA2 | Nectin-4 | Various kinases | | Specificity | High | High | Variable | | Tumor penetration | High | Limited | High | | Administration | IV weekly | IV weekly-q3w | Oral daily | | Manufacturing | Chemical | Biological | Chemical | --- Current Research Directions Ongoing Clinical Questions 1. Optimal patient selection — What EphA2 expression threshold predicts response? 2. Combination strategies — Does nivolumab combination improve outcomes? 3. Resistance mechanisms — How do tumors become resistant to BT5528? 4. Sequencing — Where does BT5528 fit in treatment algorithms? 5. Other tumor types — Which EphA2+ tumors beyond urothelial cancer respond best? Biomarker Development - EphA2 IHC — Standardized assay development for patient selection - Circulating biomarkers — Exploring ctDNA and other markers - Tumor microenvironment — Understanding immune context Future Development Paths 1. Registration pathway — Likely accelerated approval in EphA2+ urothelial cancer 2. Expansion to other tumors — NSCLC, ovarian, gastric cancers 3. Earlier lines of therapy — Currently studying in later lines; may move earlier 4. Combination optimization — Other checkpoint inhibitors, chemotherapy --- Citation Table BT5528 Clinical Data | Source | Year | Type | Key Finding | Access | |--------|------|------|-------------|--------| | Bicycle Therapeutics corporate presentations | 2024-2025 | Conference | 67% ORR in EphA2+ urothelial cancer | Company website | | NCT04180371 (ClinicalTrials.gov) | 2019-ongoing | Trial registration | Phase 1/2 design and endpoints | Open | | ASCO/ESMO abstracts | 2021-2025 | Conference | Preliminary safety and efficacy | Variable | EphA2 Biology and Oncology | Source | PMID | Year | Type | Key Finding | Access | |--------|------|------|------|-------------|--------| | Pasquale EB. name: AOD-9604
summary: A modified fragment of human growth hormone studied for fat loss. Clinical development discontinued after Phase 2/3 trials failed to show efficacy.
content: Overview Overview Quick Summary - Modified fragment of human growth hormone (amino acids 177-191) - Not FDA approved - clinical development discontinued - Failed Phase 2b/3 trials for obesity did not meet primary endpoints - No diabetogenic effects of full HGH (theoretical advantage) - Marketed illegally despite lack of efficacy evidence Evidence Strength: Low - Clinical trials failed to demonstrate efficacy for weight loss. Development discontinued. Identity & Origins | Property | Value | |----------|-------| | Generic name | AOD-9604 | | Chemical name | Tyr-hGH Fragment 177-191 | | Class | Growth hormone fragment | | Developer | Metabolic Pharmaceuticals (Australia) | | Primary Researcher | Frank M. Ng (Monash University) | Development History - 1990s-2000s: Developed based on GH lipolytic domain research at Monash University, Australia - 1997: Original characterization of lipolytic activity published - 2001: Early Phase 2a trials show promising signal - 2007: Phase 2b/3 OPTIONS trial failed primary endpoint - 2007: Development discontinued by Metabolic Pharmaceuticals - 2024: FDA excludes from 503A compounding list - Current: No active pharmaceutical development Molecular Target: Beta-3-Adrenergic Receptor Pathway AOD-9604 represents amino acids 177-191 of human growth hormone with a tyrosine substitution at the N-terminus. Unlike full-length GH, it does not bind to the GH receptor and does not increase IGF-1. Primary Signaling Cascade: AOD-9604 → β3-Adrenergic Receptor Activation ↓ Adenylyl Cyclase Stimulation ↓ ↑ Intracellular cAMP ↓ Protein Kinase A (PKA) Activation ↓ Hormone-Sensitive Lipase Phosphorylation ↓ Triglyceride → Free Fatty Acids + Glycerol This cascade essentially tells fat cells to break down stored fat (triglycerides) into components that can be burned for energy. Key Mechanistic Findings: - Preferentially accumulates in adipose (fat) tissue - Upregulates beta-3 adrenergic receptor expression over time - Beta-3-AR knockout mice are unresponsive to AOD-9604's effects, proving this receptor is essential - Stimulates lipolysis (fat breakdown) and inhibits lipogenesis (fat storage) - In animal studies, released fat specifically from obese fat cells, not lean cells Cyclized Structure for Stability AOD-9604 features a disulfide bridge between two cysteine residues, creating a ring-shaped structure that: - Increases resistance to digestive enzymes - Enhances stability in the bloodstream - Distinguishes it from the simpler linear hGH Fragment 176-191 Why Development Failed Despite promising animal data, human trials failed: - Effects did not translate - What worked in mice did not work in humans - Species differences - Beta-3 receptor biology differs between rodents and humans - Bioavailability issues - The drug may not have reached fat tissue effectively in humans - Modest effect size - Even if there was a small effect, the trial may have been too small to detect it The bottom line: over 900 people participated in AOD-9604 trials, but the drug never showed significant weight loss in the pivotal study. --- Research Summary Evidence Overview | Category | Count | Notes | |----------|-------|-------| | Human Clinical Trials | 6 | Including failed Phase 2b/3 | | Preclinical/Mechanism | 8 | Animal fat loss, receptor studies | | Reviews | 6 | Global obesity pharmacotherapy | | Regulatory Sources | 3 | FDA, TGA, WADA | | Total sources | 20 | 6 open access | Global Research Distribution | Region | Contributions | |--------|---------------| | Australia | Original development (Monash University, Metabolic Pharmaceuticals) | | Europe | Anti-doping detection (Germany), peptide reviews (Denmark, Finland) | | USA | Obesity pharmacotherapy reviews, FDA regulatory decisions | | China | Analytical detection methods | | Global | WADA prohibition, multi-center reviews | --- Key Human Trials Phase 2b/3 OPTIONS Study (Pivotal Trial) | Parameter | Details | |-----------|---------| | Sponsor | Metabolic Pharmaceuticals Ltd (Australia) | | Population | Obese adults (BMI 30-45, ages 18-65) | | Duration | 24 weeks | | N | 536 subjects | | Doses tested | 0.25, 0.5, 1.0 mg oral daily | | Primary endpoint | Weight loss vs placebo | | Result | Did not meet primary endpoint | | Consequence | Development terminated 2007 | Trial Outcome The pivotal trial failed to demonstrate statistically significant weight loss compared to placebo. This led to discontinuation of the development program. Earlier Phase Studies | Study | Year | N | Result | |-------|------|---|--------| | Phase 1 Safety | 2001 | 16 | Generally well-tolerated | | Phase 2a Efficacy | 2001 | 16 | 2.6 kg vs 0.8 kg placebo (signal) | | Phase 2 Safety | 2004 | 42 | No metabolic adverse effects | --- Preclinical Evidence Animal Studies | Study Type | Model | Key Findings | Reference | |------------|-------|--------------|-----------| | Fat reduction | Obese mice (ob/ob) | Reduced body fat without IGF-1 changes | Ng et al. 2000 | | Mechanism | Adipocytes | Lipolytic domain identified (177-191) | Ng et al. 2003 | | Receptor studies | 3T3-L1 cells | Beta-3 AR upregulation confirmed | Jiang & Ng 2007 | | Fat oxidation | Isolated adipocytes | Increased fatty acid oxidation | Ng et al. 2006 | | Safety | Multiple species | No diabetogenic effects | Multiple studies | Translation Failure Despite promising animal data, effects did not translate to humans in clinical trials. European reviews cite species differences in beta-3 adrenergic receptor expression and function as the primary reason. --- Safety Profile Clinical Trial Data | Finding | Notes | |---------|-------| | Generally well-tolerated | Short-term studies only | | No glucose effects | Unlike full GH | | No IGF-1 increase | Unlike full GH | | Injection site reactions | Common | Unknown Long-term Effects - No chronic exposure data in humans - Product quality concerns for unregulated products - FDA cited impurity concerns in 2024 exclusion decision --- Pharmacokinetics Key Parameters | Parameter | Value | Notes | |-----------|-------|-------| | Half-life | Short | Hours | | Administration | Subcutaneous | Injection | | Bioavailability | Limited data | May be suboptimal | --- Regulatory Status | Region | Status | Notes | |--------|--------|-------| | USA (FDA) | Not approved | Excluded from 503A compounding (2024) | | Australia (TGA) | Not approved | Development discontinued (2007) | | Europe (EMA) | Not approved | Never submitted | | WADA | Prohibited | Banned in sport (S2.5) | Regulatory History - 2007: Development discontinued after Phase 2b/3 failure - 2007: TGA notified of program termination - 2024: FDA excludes from 503A Bulk Drug Substances List - Now sold illegally online --- Related Peptides GH-Related Peptides | Peptide | Mechanism | Status | |---------|-----------|--------| | AOD-9604 | GH fragment | Failed trials | | Somatropin | Full GH | Approved | | Tesamorelin | GHRH analog | Approved | --- Citation Table Australian Research (Original Development) | Source | Year | Type | Key Finding | Access | |--------|------|------|-------------|--------| | Ng FM, Bornstein J PMID:9169799 ( | 1997 | Preclinical | Original characterization of GH fragment 177-191 lipolytic activity | Paywalled | | Ng FM et al. name: BPC-157
summary: A synthetic peptide derived from human gastric juice protein. Preclinical studies suggest tissue repair properties, but human clinical trial data is extremely limited. A 2025 systematic review of 36 studies found only 1 clinical study.
content: Overview Overview Quick Summary - Synthetic pentadecapeptide (15 amino acids) derived from human gastric juice protein - Not FDA approved for any indication; not approved by any major regulatory agency - Preclinical evidence only - nearly all studies are in animal models or cell cultures - High interest in fitness/recovery communities despite limited human data - Safety profile in humans is not established through rigorous clinical trials Evidence Strength: Low - Extensive preclinical research but minimal human clinical trial data. Most evidence comes from a single research group. Identity & Origins | Property | Value | |----------|-------| | Generic name | BPC-157 | | Chemical name | Pentadecapeptide BPC 157 | | Research codes | PL 14736, PL-10, PLD-116 | | Class | Gastric pentadecapeptide | | Origin | Derived from human BPC protein fragment | | Amino acid sequence | Gly-Glu-Pro-Pro-Pro-Gly-Lys-Pro-Ala-Asp-Asp-Ala-Gly-Leu-Val | Research Timeline - 1990s: Initial isolation and characterization from gastric juice (Croatian researchers) - 1993-present: Preclinical studies primarily from Croatian research group (Sikiric et al.) - 2000s: Pliva pharmaceutical company develops PL 14736 for IBD trials - 2006: Phase I/II IBD trials referenced but results never fully published - 2010s: Independent Taiwanese and Chinese research groups publish supportive findings - 2016: Phase I trial (NCT02637284) terminated; no results disclosed - 2019: Korean systematic review; UK independent review published - 2023: FDA classifies as Category 2 bulk drug substance - 2024-2025: First small human pilot studies published; multiple systematic reviews - Current: No completed Phase 3 trials; only 2-3 small pilot human studies Scientific Pathways (Preclinical Evidence Only) 1. VEGF/VEGFR2 Pathway (Angiogenesis) BPC-157 → VEGFR2 upregulation & endocytosis ↓ PI3K activation ↓ AKT phosphorylation ↓ eNOS activation → Nitric Oxide ↓ ├── New blood vessel formation ├── Vasodilation └── Vascular stability In simpler terms, BPC-157 appears to increase receptors for a growth factor called VEGF. This triggers a chain reaction inside cells that produces nitric oxide - a molecule that relaxes blood vessels and promotes the growth of new ones. More blood vessels = better nutrient delivery to healing tissues. Key supporting research: Hsieh MJ et al. (Taiwan, 2017) demonstrated VEGFR2 activation in ischemic wound models. PMID:28013436 2. Nitric Oxide System (Dual Pathways) - VEGF-dependent : VEGFR2-PI3K-Akt-eNOS cascade - VEGF-independent : Src-caveolin-1-eNOS pathway - Disrupts caveolin-1 inhibition of eNOS → more NO production - Nos1/Nos3 genes upregulated (vasodilation, blood flow) - Nos2 downregulated (reduces excessive inflammation) Nitric oxide (NO) acts like a traffic controller for blood vessels. It tells them to widen so more blood can flow through. BPC-157 may boost the "good" NO that improves blood flow while reducing the "bad" NO that causes excessive inflammation. Key supporting research: Sikiric P et al. (Croatia, 2014) detailed NO system interactions. PMID:23772940 3. FAK-Paxillin Pathway (Cell Migration) BPC-157 → FAK/Paxillin phosphorylation ↓ ├── Cell attachment to surfaces ├── Cell migration to injury sites └── Cell survival under stress FAK and paxillin are proteins that act like "feet" for cells, helping them grip surfaces and move. When you injure a tendon, the repair cells (fibroblasts) need to travel to the injury site. BPC-157 may activate these cellular "feet," making it easier for repair cells to reach and fix the damage. Key supporting research: Chang CH et al. (Taiwan, 2011) demonstrated FAK-paxillin activation in tendon fibroblasts. PMID:21030672 4. Growth Hormone Receptor (GHR) Pathway BPC-157 → GHR upregulation ↓ JAK2 phosphorylation ↓ STAT5 activation ↓ ├── Enhanced growth factor signaling ├── Increased collagen synthesis └── Tissue regeneration Key supporting research: Chang CH et al. (Taiwan, 2014) demonstrated GHR upregulation in tendon fibroblasts. PMID:25415472 5. ERK/MAPK Signaling - Upregulates ERK1/2 phosphorylation - Activates downstream transcription factors: c-Fos, c-Jun, Egr-1 - Promotes cell growth, migration, and angiogenesis ERK/MAPK is essentially an "on switch" for cell growth and repair. When activated, cells get the signal to multiply, move, and build new structures. BPC-157 may flip this switch on. Key supporting research: Tkalcevic VI et al. (Croatia, 2007) showed Egr-1 involvement in wound healing. PMID:17628535 Gene Expression Effects | Effect | Result | What It Means | |--------|--------|---------------| | Nos1/Nos3 ↑ | More nitric oxide → vasodilation | Blood vessels relax and widen, improving blood flow | | Nos2 ↓ | Less inflammatory damage | Reduces tissue damage from excessive inflammation | | VEGFR2 ↑ | More VEGF receptors → angiogenesis | Cells become more responsive to signals that build new blood vessels | | GHR ↑ | Enhanced tissue repair signaling | Tissues may respond better to growth and repair signals | | Egr-1 ↑ | Improved collagen organization | Better wound healing and tissue structure | Cytoprotection (Cell Protection) - Rescued NSAID-cytotoxicity by stabilizing intestinal permeability - First demonstration of NO generation in gastric mucosa → antiulcer effect - Hepatoprotective effects in toxin-challenged animal models - Endothelial protection from high-glucose damage (diabetic model) In animal studies, BPC-157 helped protect the gut lining from damage caused by common painkillers like ibuprofen and aspirin. It also appeared to protect liver cells from toxins. The gut has a delicate barrier that keeps stomach acid and bacteria from leaking into your body. BPC-157 may help maintain this barrier. Key supporting research: - Chinese studies (Xue XC et al., 2004; Wang XY et al., 2019) demonstrated GI and hepatic cytoprotection - Sun L et al. (China, 2016) showed endothelial protection from hyperglycemia PMID:27551776 Neurotransmitter Interactions Recent research has explored BPC-157's effects on neurotransmitter systems: | System | Effect | Model | |--------|--------|-------| | Dopaminergic | D2 receptor partial agonism | Rat behavioral models | | Serotonergic | Modulation of serotonin synthesis | Rat stress models | | GABAergic | Enhanced GABA signaling | Rat anxiety models | | NO system | Complex bidirectional modulation | Multiple models | Key supporting research: Sikiric P et al. (Croatia, 2016, 2022) detailed brain-gut axis and CNS effects. PMID:27306034, PMID:34766891 Important Limitations - Nearly all mechanistic studies from single research group (Croatian) - Translation to human physiology is unconfirmed - Optimal administration route for systemic effects unknown - Bioavailability and pharmacokinetics in humans not characterized - FDA prohibits sale as drug, supplement, or food ingredient --- Research Summary Evidence Overview | Category | Count | Notes | |----------|-------|-------| | Human pilot studies | 3 | Small N; safety endpoints only | | Systematic reviews | 4 | 2024-2025; highlight lack of human data | | Narrative reviews | 12+ | Mostly from Croatian group | | Preclinical studies | 29+ | Primarily rodent models | | Total sources | 32+ | 18 open access | Key Evidence Highlights 1. Gastric Protection: Rodent and Chinese studies show ulcer healing acceleration PMID:15052688 2. Tendon/Ligament: Taiwanese studies show improved healing markers PMID:21030672, PMID:25415472 3. Muscle Injury: Croatian animal models suggest faster recovery PMID:16609966, PMID:18668315 4. Corneal Healing: Taiwan/China study showed accelerated wound healing PMID:25995620 5. Neuroprotection: Croatian reviews detail dopaminergic system effects PMID:27306034 Critical Note: The overwhelming majority of BPC-157 research comes from one research group in Croatia, and independent replication is limited. Taiwanese, Chinese, and Korean groups have published supportive findings, but these also remain preclinical. --- Global Research Landscape Geographic Distribution of Research | Region | Studies | Key Institutions | Focus Areas | |--------|---------|------------------|-------------| | Croatia | 15+ | University of Zagreb (Sikiric et al.) | All areas; primary research origin | | Taiwan | 4 | Chang Gung Memorial Hospital | Tendon, angiogenesis, GHR | | China | 4 | Multiple institutions | GI protection, wound healing, safety | | South Korea | 1 | Seoul National University | Systematic review, antioxidant | | USA | 3 | Various | Human pilots, reviews | | UK | 1 | Loughborough University | Independent review | Database Coverage | Database | Coverage | Notes | |----------|----------|-------| | PubMed/MEDLINE | Primary | All major studies indexed | | Europe PMC | Comprehensive | Full-text access for many | | CNKI (China) | Some | Chinese-language studies on wound healing | | KoreaMed | Limited | Korean systematic reviews | | J-STAGE (Japan) | Minimal | Few Japanese studies | | Hrčak (Croatia) | Some | Croatian-language proceedings | Key Research Groups 1. University of Zagreb (Croatia) - Sikiric et al. - Primary research origin since 1990s - >100 publications on BPC-157 - Covers nearly all mechanistic and preclinical work - Limitation: Limited independent replication 2. Chang Gung Memorial Hospital (Taiwan) - Chang, Tsai, Pang et al. - Independent confirmation of tendon healing effects - Characterized GHR and VEGFR2 pathways - Published in international peer-reviewed journals 3. Chinese Institutions - Multiple groups studying cytoprotection - Preclinical safety evaluations - Wound healing models 4. Korean Research - Seoul National University systematic review (2019) - Comprehensive antioxidant mechanism analysis --- Key Human Trials Available Human Data Human clinical trial data for BPC-157 is extremely limited: | Study | Year | Population | Findings | Limitations | |-------|------|------------|----------|-------------| | Lee JV et al. PMID:40131143 | 2025 | 2 healthy adults | IV up to 20mg safe; no adverse events | N=2 only | | Lee JV et al. PMID:38330234 | 2024 | 10 IC patients | Symptom improvement | Small pilot | | Pliva IBD trials | 2000s | UC/Crohn's | Referenced but unpublished | No data access | No large-scale randomized controlled trials have been completed for any indication. Registered/Referenced Trials | Trial ID | Phase | Status | Notes | |----------|-------|--------|-------| | NCT02637284 | 1 | Terminated 2016 | 42 enrolled; PK study; no results published | | PL-10/PLD-116 (Pliva) | 1-2 | Completed | IBD trials; referenced in 2006 paper; data never published | --- Preclinical Evidence Animal Studies by Tissue Type | Tissue | Model | Key Findings | Source | |--------|-------|--------------|--------| | Tendon | Rat Achilles | FAK-paxillin activation; faster healing | Taiwan PMID:21030672 | | Ligament | Rat MCL | Increased collagen I; better biomechanics | Croatia PMID:20225319 | | Muscle (transection) | Rat quadriceps | Improved function; myofiber density | Croatia PMID:16609966 | | Muscle (crush) | Rat gastrocnemius | Accelerated healing | Croatia PMID:18668315 | | Gastric ulcer | Rat | Faster healing; PGE2 increase | China PMID:15052688 | | Cornea | Rabbit alkali burn | Faster epithelialization | Taiwan/China PMID:25995620 | | Nerve | Rat transection | Enhanced regeneration | Croatia various | | Bone | Rat fracture | Improved healing | Croatia various | | Vascular | Rat IVC ligation | Prevented complications | Croatia PMID:29940329 | In Vitro Studies | Cell Type | Findings | Source | |-----------|----------|--------| | Tendon fibroblasts | Proliferation, migration, GHR upregulation | Taiwan | | HUVECs | Protection from high glucose; angiogenesis | China | | Endothelial cells | VEGFR2 activation; migration | Taiwan | | Gastric epithelium | Cytoprotection from NSAIDs | Croatia | Preclinical Safety Data | Finding | Source | Notes | |---------|--------|-------| | No acute toxicity | China PMID:26992810 | Comprehensive safety evaluation | | No genotoxicity | China PMID:26992810 | Standard battery tests | | No local irritation | China PMID:26992810 | Injection site | | Tolerated orally | Multiple | Human PK unknown | Limitations of Preclinical Data - Single research group: Most studies from Croatian laboratory - Limited replication: Few independent confirmations (Taiwan, China provide some) - Animal models: May not translate to humans - Route of administration: Varies widely across studies - Publication bias: Negative studies may not be published - Lack of head-to-head comparisons: No studies comparing to standard treatments --- Safety Profile Known Information The safety profile of BPC-157 in humans is not established through rigorous clinical evaluation. Human Safety Data | Study | Finding | |-------|---------| | Lee 2025 PMID:40131143 | IV up to 20mg in 2 adults: no adverse events, normal biomarkers | | Lee 2024 PMID:38330234 | 10 IC patients: no serious adverse events reported | Preclinical Safety Data | Finding | Source | Notes | |---------|--------|-------| | No acute toxicity | China PMID:26992810 | Rodent and rabbit models | | No mutagenicity | In vitro assays | Standard Ames test | | No genotoxicity | China PMID:26992810 | Micronucleus test | | Tolerated orally | Rodent studies | Human PK unknown | | No teratogenicity | Limited data | Not fully characterized | Potential Concerns - Unknown long-term effects: No chronic human exposure data - Growth factor effects: Theoretical cancer concerns with pro-angiogenic agents - Quality control: Unregulated products may vary in purity - Drug interactions: Not studied - Contraindications: Not established - Receptor activity: D2 receptor agonism may have CNS implications Important Warning Products sold as BPC-157 are not regulated pharmaceutical products. Purity, identity, and safety cannot be assured for non-pharmaceutical sources. --- Pharmacokinetics Known Parameters | Parameter | Value | Notes | |-----------|-------|-------| | Half-life | Unknown in humans | Not characterized | | Bioavailability | Unknown | Route-dependent | | Metabolism | Presumed proteolytic | Not studied | | Distribution | Unknown | Not characterized | | Protein binding | Unknown | Not studied | Research Gaps Human pharmacokinetic data is essentially absent. Basic parameters like absorption, distribution, metabolism, and elimination have not been characterized in human subjects. The terminated Phase I trial (NCT02637284) was designed to assess PK parameters but results were never published. --- Regulatory Status | Region | Status | Notes | |--------|--------|-------| | USA (FDA) | Not approved | Category 2 bulk drug substance (2023); insufficient safety data | | EU (EMA) | Not approved | Not authorized | | WADA | Prohibited | Banned in competition (S0 category) | | USADA | Prohibited | Banned in all sports | | Australia (TGA) | Not approved | Not listed | | Canada | Not approved | Not authorized | | UK (MHRA) | Not approved | Not authorized | Important: BPC-157 is prohibited by the World Anti-Doping Agency (WADA) under the category of peptide hormones and growth factors. Athletes testing positive face sanctions. --- Related Peptides Recovery/Repair Category | Peptide | Mechanism | Evidence Level | |---------|-----------|----------------| | TB-500 | Thymosin beta-4 fragment | Low (preclinical) | | GHK-Cu | Copper peptide | Moderate (topical) | | Thymosin Beta-4 | Parent compound of TB-500 | Moderate | BPC-157 vs TB-500 Both are marketed for recovery but have different proposed mechanisms: | Aspect | BPC-157 | TB-500 | |--------|---------|--------| | Origin | Gastric protein | Thymosin beta-4 | | Primary pathway | VEGF/NO/GHR | Actin sequestration | | Primary studies | Croatian group | Multiple groups | | Human trials | Minimal (2-3 pilots) | Minimal | | WADA status | Prohibited | Prohibited | --- Current Research Directions Areas of Investigation - Inflammatory bowel disease (IBD) - Tendon and ligament injuries - Muscle healing - Gastric ulcer healing - Neuroprotection and brain-gut axis - Vascular protection - Diabetic wound healing Key Unknowns - Human pharmacokinetics - Optimal administration route - Effective human doses (if any) - Long-term safety - Efficacy in any human condition - Drug interactions - Cancer risk with chronic pro-angiogenic exposure --- Citation Table Human Studies | Source | Year | Type | Key Finding | Access | |--------|------|------|-------------|--------| | Lee JV, Burgess MS PMID:40131143 ( | 2025 | Pilot Safety | IV infusions up to 20mg safe in 2 healthy adults; no adverse events | Open | | Lee JV et al. name: Cagrilintide
summary: A long-acting amylin analog developed by Novo Nordisk for weight management. Currently in Phase 3 trials as CagriSema (combination with semaglutide). Phase 2 data shows up to 22-25% weight loss when combined with semaglutide, potentially exceeding tirzepatide efficacy.
content: Overview Quick Summary - Long-acting amylin analog developed by Novo Nordisk for obesity treatment; activates amylin receptors (AMY1, AMY2, AMY3) and calcitonin receptor - Investigational status: Phase 3 trials ongoing (REDEFINE program); not yet approved for any indication - CagriSema combination: Co-formulated with semaglutide as fixed-dose combination; Phase 2 data shows 22-25% weight loss - Phase 2 monotherapy: Up to 10.8% weight loss at 26 weeks with cagrilintide 4.5mg alone PMID: 34798060 - Combination advantage: CagriSema shows additive/synergistic weight loss beyond either agent alone - Novel mechanism: Complements GLP-1 pathway via separate amylin-mediated satiety and gastric effects - Key trials pending: REDEFINE 1 (vs semaglutide), REDEFINE 2 (in T2D), REDEFINE 3 (vs tirzepatide), REDEFINE CVOT Evidence Strength: MODERATE - Supported by Phase 2 RCTs with promising efficacy signals. Phase 3 results pending (expected 2025). No regulatory approvals yet. Mechanism well-established from pramlintide and amylin biology literature. -------|-------| | Generic name | Cagrilintide | | Development codes | AM833, NN9838, ZP8396 | | Combination product | CagriSema (cagrilintide + semaglutide) | | Chemical class | Long-acting acylated amylin analog | | Sequence | Modified human amylin with amino acid substitutions + C18 fatty acid acylation | | Molecular weight | 4,200 Da | | Target receptors | AMY1, AMY2, AMY3 (amylin receptors); CTR (calcitonin receptor) | Discovery & Development Timeline | Year | Milestone | Significance | |------|-----------|--------------| | 1987 | Amylin (IAPP) discovered | Westermark and Cooper independently identified islet amyloid polypeptide PMID: 3035556, 3317417 | | 1994 | Amylin appetite effects demonstrated | Lutz showed amylin reduces food intake in rats PMID: 8022910 | | 2005 | Pramlintide (Symlin) approved | First-generation amylin analog FDA approved for diabetes; required TID dosing | | 2008 | Leptin sensitization discovered | Roth et al. showed amylin restores leptin responsiveness in obesity PMID: 18458326 | | 2011 | Long-acting amylin analogs developed | Davalintide and other extended-half-life analogs in development | | 2019 | Cagrilintide Phase 1 initiated | Novo Nordisk begins clinical development of AM833 | | 2020 | Phase 1b combination study | First human data on cagrilintide + semaglutide co-administration PMID: 33894838 | | 2021 | Phase 2 monotherapy published | Lau et al. demonstrate 10.8% weight loss with cagrilintide alone PMID: 34798060 | | 2023 | Phase 2 CagriSema in T2D published | Frias et al. show combination superiority vs semaglutide PMID: 37364590 | | 2023-2024 | REDEFINE Phase 3 program initiated | Four pivotal Phase 3 trials begin enrollment | | 2025 | Phase 3 results expected | REDEFINE 1, 2, 3 primary results anticipated | | 2029 | CVOT results expected | REDEFINE CVOT cardiovascular outcomes | Why Cagrilintide Was Developed The pramlintide problem: The first amylin analog, pramlintide (Symlin), was FDA-approved in 2005 but had significant limitations: - Required three-times-daily injections due to short half-life - Modest weight loss ( 2-3% additional beyond insulin) - Separate injection from insulin required - Never achieved widespread adoption Cagrilintide addresses these limitations through: 1. Fatty acid acylation: Like semaglutide, cagrilintide has a C18 fatty acid chain enabling albumin binding 2. Extended half-life: Approximately 7-day half-life allows once-weekly dosing 3. Combination potential: Engineered for co-formulation with semaglutide (CagriSema) 4. Higher potency: More sustained amylin receptor activation than pramlintide Strategic rationale: As GLP-1 agonists face ceiling effects on weight loss ( 15-20%), adding complementary mechanisms may achieve greater efficacy. Amylin and GLP-1 work through distinct but complementary pathways. --- Mechanism of Action How Cagrilintide Works (Plain English) Cagrilintide mimics amylin, a hormone your pancreas releases along with insulin after eating. Here is what amylin does and why it matters: 1. Sends powerful "full" signals to your brain - Amylin acts on an area called the area postrema in your brainstem, telling your brain you have eaten enough. This is a DIFFERENT pathway than GLP-1, so combining them is like having two separate volume controls for hunger. 2. Slows stomach emptying - Like GLP-1, amylin slows how fast food leaves your stomach. This keeps you feeling full longer and prevents blood sugar spikes. The combination may produce additive effects. 3. Restores leptin sensitivity - This is crucial. Many people with obesity have "leptin resistance" - their body produces the "stop eating" hormone leptin, but the brain does not respond to it. Amylin can help restore this sensitivity, potentially explaining why the combination works better than either drug alone. 4. Reduces glucagon after meals - Amylin suppresses glucagon (a hormone that raises blood sugar), helping control post-meal glucose spikes in diabetes. Why combine with semaglutide? Think of GLP-1 and amylin as complementary systems. GLP-1 works mainly through the hypothalamus (a different brain region), while amylin works through the brainstem. Using both is like having two independent braking systems - more effective and more reliable than one alone. --- Receptor Targets: Amylin Receptors (Scientific Detail) Cagrilintide is an agonist of amylin receptors, which are heterodimeric complexes of the calcitonin receptor (CTR) with receptor activity-modifying proteins (RAMPs). Receptor subtypes and pharmacology: | Receptor | Components | Cagrilintide Activity | Primary Effects | |----------|------------|----------------------|-----------------| | AMY1 | CTR + RAMP1 | High affinity | Satiety, gastric emptying | | AMY2 | CTR + RAMP2 | High affinity | Metabolic effects | | AMY3 | CTR + RAMP3 | High affinity | CNS effects | | CTR | CTR alone | Moderate affinity | Bone effects (minimal at therapeutic doses) | | CGRP receptor | CLR + RAMP1 | Low/negligible | Avoided to prevent vasodilation | Tissue-Specific Signaling Cagrilintide administration | +-- AREA POSTREMA (brainstem) | +-- AMY1/AMY3 receptors | +-- Rapid satiety signaling | +-- Nausea pathway (explains early GI effects) | +-- Does NOT require BBB penetration | +-- HYPOTHALAMUS | +-- Indirect effects via neural circuits | +-- Modulates leptin sensitivity | +-- Regulates energy expenditure | +-- GASTROINTESTINAL TRACT | +-- Delays gastric emptying | +-- Reduces nutrient absorption rate | +-- Synergistic with GLP-1 effects | +-- PANCREAS | +-- Suppresses glucagon secretion | +-- Glucose-dependent effect | +-- Does NOT directly stimulate insulin | +-- ADIPOSE TISSUE +-- Potential direct effects (under investigation) +-- May influence lipolysis Key Mechanism Differences: Amylin vs GLP-1 | Feature | Amylin (Cagrilintide) | GLP-1 (Semaglutide) | |---------|----------------------|---------------------| | Primary brain target | Area postrema (brainstem) | Arcuate nucleus (hypothalamus) | | BBB penetration | Not required | Required for full effects | | Insulin secretion | No direct effect | Glucose-dependent stimulation | | Glucagon suppression | Yes (postprandial) | Yes (glucose-dependent) | | Gastric emptying | Delays | Delays | | Beta-cell preservation | No direct effect | Possible trophic effects | | Leptin sensitization | Yes (key mechanism) | Minimal | The Leptin Sensitization Mechanism Why this matters: Many people with obesity have elevated leptin levels but do not respond to it (leptin resistance). This creates a vicious cycle where the body keeps storing fat despite signals to stop. Amylin's unique role PMID: 18458326 : - In diet-induced obesity, amylin treatment restores leptin responsiveness - This may explain why amylin + GLP-1 combination produces synergistic weight loss - The amylin pathway reaches satiety centers that leptin resistance may have blocked In simpler terms: If GLP-1 is telling your brain "you are full" through one channel, amylin is telling it through a completely different channel. And crucially, amylin may help "unlock" the leptin channel that was blocked by obesity. Why CagriSema May Outperform Individual Agents The combination rationale: 1. Complementary satiety pathways: Different brain regions targeted 2. Additive gastric emptying delay: Potentially stronger effect on nutrient absorption 3. Leptin sensitization: Unique to amylin pathway 4. Reduced tachyphylaxis risk: Two mechanisms less likely to both desensitize 5. Maintained GLP-1 benefits: CV protection, beta-cell effects, glucose control --- Physiologic Context: Native Amylin Biology Endogenous Amylin Physiology Amylin (islet amyloid polypeptide, IAPP) is a 37-amino acid peptide hormone co-secreted with insulin from pancreatic beta cells in response to nutrient ingestion. Normal secretion pattern: - Fasting amylin: 5-10 pmol/L - Postprandial peak: 20-30 pmol/L - Secreted in fixed ratio with insulin ( 1:100) - Half-life of native amylin: 10-15 minutes Physiologic functions: | Function | Mechanism | Effect | |----------|-----------|--------| | Satiety | Area postrema activation | Reduced meal size | | Gastric emptying | Vagal efferent inhibition | Slower nutrient delivery | | Glucagon suppression | Direct alpha-cell effects | Reduced hepatic glucose output | | Nutrient flux | Multiple mechanisms | Prevents postprandial glucose spikes | Amylin in Obesity and Diabetes Type 2 diabetes: - Amylin secretion is impaired as beta-cell function declines - Loss of amylin contributes to postprandial hyperglycemia - Pramlintide replacement showed modest glycemic benefits Obesity: - Amylin secretion may be normal or elevated initially - Evidence for amylin resistance (similar to insulin resistance) - Central amylin signaling may be impaired - Supraphysiologic amylin (from cagrilintide) overcomes resistance Supraphysiologic Amylin Effects Cagrilintide produces plasma concentrations 10-50x higher than endogenous postprandial levels, sustained for a week. This: 1. Overcomes potential amylin resistance 2. Produces more sustained CNS effects 3. May restore leptin sensitivity (not seen with physiologic levels) 4. Achieves clinically meaningful weight loss --- Key Human Trials Phase 2 Cagrilintide Monotherapy Trial: NCT03856047 PMID: 34798060 | Parameter | Value | |-----------|-------| | Design | Randomized, double-blind, placebo and active-controlled | | N | 706 participants | | Population | Adults with BMI >=30 (or >=27 with comorbidity), without diabetes | | Duration | 26 weeks | | Arms | Cagrilintide 0.3, 0.6, 1.2, 2.4, 4.5 mg weekly; Liraglutide 3.0 mg daily; Placebo | Key Results: | Treatment | Weight Change | >=5% Weight Loss | >=10% Weight Loss | |-----------|---------------|------------------|-------------------| | Cagrilintide 4.5 mg | -10.8% | 79% | 53% | | Cagrilintide 2.4 mg | -9.0% | 74% | 45% | | Cagrilintide 1.2 mg | -7.2% | 59% | 31% | | Liraglutide 3.0 mg | -9.0% | 72% | 43% | | Placebo | -3.0% | 23% | 9% | Significance: Cagrilintide monotherapy achieved weight loss comparable to liraglutide 3.0 mg (the FDA-approved weight dose). The 4.5 mg dose showed numerically greater weight loss than liraglutide. Phase 1b Combination Study (Cagrilintide + Semaglutide) Trial: NCT04163354 PMID: 33894838 | Parameter | Value | |-----------|-------| | Design | Randomized, double-blind, placebo-controlled | | N | 96 participants | | Population | Adults with BMI 27-39.9 kg/m2, without diabetes | | Duration | 20 weeks | | Arms | Cagrilintide doses + semaglutide 2.4 mg vs semaglutide alone vs placebo | Key Results: | Treatment | Weight Change at 20 weeks | |-----------|---------------------------| | Cagrilintide 2.4 mg + Semaglutide 2.4 mg | -17.1% | | Semaglutide 2.4 mg alone | -9.8% | | Placebo | -0.4% | Significance: First human evidence that cagrilintide + semaglutide produces synergistic weight loss. The combination achieved 17% weight loss in just 20 weeks, projecting to potentially 22-25% with longer treatment. Phase 2 CagriSema in Type 2 Diabetes Trial: NCT04940078 PMID: 37364590 | Parameter | Value | |-----------|-------| | Design | Randomized, double-blind, active-controlled | | N | 92 participants | | Population | Adults with T2D, HbA1c 7.0-10.0%, BMI >=27 | | Duration | 32 weeks | | Arms | CagriSema (cagrilintide 2.4 mg + semaglutide 2.4 mg) vs semaglutide 2.4 mg | Key Results: | Outcome | CagriSema | Semaglutide Alone | Difference | |---------|-----------|-------------------|------------| | HbA1c reduction | -2.2% | -1.8% | -0.4% (p=0.03) | | Weight loss | -15.6% | -5.1% | -10.5% | | FPG reduction | -58 mg/dL | -36 mg/dL | -22 mg/dL | Significance: CagriSema showed superiority over semaglutide alone for both glycemic control and weight loss in T2D. The weight loss difference ( 10 percentage points) was striking. Ongoing Phase 3 REDEFINE Program | Trial | NCT ID | Population | Comparator | N | Status | Expected | |-------|--------|------------|------------|---|--------|----------| | REDEFINE 1 | NCT05567796 | Obesity without T2D | Semaglutide 2.4 mg | 3,417 | Recruiting | 2025 | | REDEFINE 2 | NCT05394519 | Obesity with T2D | Semaglutide 2.4 mg | 1,605 | Recruiting | 2025 | | REDEFINE 3 | NCT06024720 | Obesity without T2D | Tirzepatide | 2,000 | Recruiting | 2025-2026 | | REDEFINE CVOT | NCT05803421 | High CV risk | Placebo | 10,000 | Recruiting | 2029 | REDEFINE 1 Design: - Primary endpoint: Percent change in body weight at 68 weeks - Key secondary: Proportion achieving >=5%, >=10%, >=15%, >=20% weight loss - Will establish whether CagriSema exceeds semaglutide 2.4 mg alone REDEFINE 3 Design: - Critical head-to-head comparison with tirzepatide (current market leader) - Will determine competitive positioning of CagriSema - Results highly anticipated by obesity medicine community --- Preclinical Evidence Animal Studies | Study Type | Model | Key Findings | Citation | |------------|-------|--------------|----------| | Appetite suppression | Rodent | Dose-dependent food intake reduction | Multiple studies | | Weight loss | Diet-induced obese mice | Significant weight reduction vs control | Novo Nordisk data | | Combination synergy | Obese rodents | Cagrilintide + GLP-1 > either alone | Preclinical package | | Leptin sensitization | ob/ob mice, DIO rats | Amylin restores leptin responsiveness | PMID: 18458326 | | Gastric emptying | Rodent | Delayed gastric emptying, dose-dependent | PMID: 8022910 | Mechanism Studies Leptin sensitization (key finding): - Diet-induced obese (DIO) rats are leptin resistant - Amylin treatment for 2 weeks restores leptin responsiveness - Combination of amylin + leptin produces synergistic weight loss - Effect persists even after amylin discontinuation (temporary) Brain penetration: - Amylin acts on area postrema (outside blood-brain barrier) - Downstream effects propagate to hypothalamus via neural circuits - Different access pattern than GLP-1 agonists In Vitro Studies - High-affinity binding to AMY1, AMY2, AMY3 receptors - Minimal CGRP receptor activity (avoids vasodilatory effects) - Fatty acid acylation extends half-life via albumin binding - No significant off-target receptor activity --- Safety Profile Common Adverse Events Data from Phase 2 trials: | Adverse Event | Cagrilintide (approx.) | Semaglutide Comparison | Notes | |---------------|------------------------|------------------------|-------| | Nausea | 25-40% | 30-45% | Highest during titration | | Vomiting | 10-15% | 10-25% | Usually transient | | Diarrhea | 10-15% | 15-25% | Less common than with GLP-1 alone | | Constipation | 8-12% | 10-15% | Comparable | | Injection site reactions | 10-15% | 5-10% | May be slightly higher | | Decreased appetite | 15-20% | 15-20% | Mechanism-related | GI tolerability note: In combination studies, GI adverse events were generally similar to or slightly less frequent than with high-dose GLP-1 agonist monotherapy. This may reflect different mechanisms or pharmacologic optimization. Serious Adverse Events and Warnings | Risk | Evidence Base | Current Assessment | |------|---------------|-------------------| | Nausea/vomiting (severe) | Phase 2 trials | Rare; slow titration recommended | | Pancreatitis | Class concern (GLP-1) | Not observed above background in Phase 2 | | Gallbladder disease | Class concern (rapid weight loss) | Expected with significant weight loss | | Thyroid tumors | Theoretical (amylin receptors on thyroid) | No signal in Phase 2; monitoring ongoing | | Hypoglycemia | Phase 2 in T2D | Low risk without insulin/SU; typical caution | Anticipated Warnings (Based on Mechanism and Class) 1. Thyroid C-cell monitoring: Amylin receptors present on thyroid C-cells; theoretical concern similar to GLP-1RA class 2. Gallbladder disease: Expected with rapid/significant weight loss 3. Pancreatitis: Standard incretin class warning anticipated 4. Pregnancy: Expected to be contraindicated (standard for weight loss medications) 5. Injection site reactions: May require monitoring Safety Data Gaps - Long-term safety (>2 years) not yet established - Cardiovascular safety/benefit (REDEFINE CVOT ongoing) - Rare adverse events require larger Phase 3 database - Pediatric safety not studied --- Pharmacokinetics Key Parameters | Parameter | Value | Clinical Relevance | |-----------|-------|-------------------| | Half-life | 7 days | Supports once-weekly dosing | | Bioavailability | 80% (subcutaneous) | High for peptide | | Tmax | 24-72 hours | Gradual absorption | | Protein binding | >99% (albumin) | Extends half-life | | Metabolism | Proteolytic degradation | No CYP interactions expected | | Elimination | Renal and fecal | As peptide fragments | PK/PD Relationship - Weight loss correlates with exposure - Dose-response established in Phase 2 (0.3 to 4.5 mg) - Steady state achieved by 4 weeks - Once-weekly dosing appropriate Drug Interactions | Category | Assessment | Notes | |----------|------------|-------| | CYP enzymes | No significant interaction | Peptide metabolism | | Gastric emptying-dependent drugs | Potential interaction | Delayed absorption of oral medications | | Insulin/SU | Additive hypoglycemia risk | Dose adjustment may be needed | | Semaglutide | Co-formulated intentionally | PK assessed in Phase 1b | --- Benefits & Risks Evidence Table Potential Benefits (Evidence-Graded) | Outcome | Evidence Type | Effect Size | Confidence | Key Citation | |---------|---------------|-------------|------------|--------------| | Weight loss (monotherapy) | Phase 2 RCT | -10.8% at 26 wk | MODERATE | PMID: 34798060 | | Weight loss (CagriSema vs sema) | Phase 2 RCT | -15.6% vs -5.1% (T2D) | MODERATE | PMID: 37364590 | | Weight loss (combination, 20 wk) | Phase 1b RCT | -17.1% | LOW (Phase 1) | PMID: 33894838 | | HbA1c reduction (CagriSema) | Phase 2 RCT | -2.2% | MODERATE | PMID: 37364590 | | Fasting glucose reduction | Phase 2 RCT | Significant | MODERATE | Multiple trials | | Complementary mechanism | Mechanistic | Additive pathways | HIGH (biology) | PMID: 18458326 | Potential Risks (Evidence-Graded) | Risk | Evidence Type | Frequency | Severity | Confidence | Notes | |------|---------------|-----------|----------|------------|-------| | GI adverse events | Phase 2 RCTs | 25-40% | Mild-moderate | MODERATE | Similar to GLP-1 class | | Injection site reactions | Phase 2 RCTs | 10-15% | Mild | MODERATE | May be slightly higher | | Hypoglycemia (with insulin/SU) | Phase 2 T2D | Variable | Moderate | MODERATE | Adjust concomitant meds | | Thyroid tumors | Theoretical | Unknown | Serious if occurs | LOW | No signal in Phase 2 | | Pancreatitis | Class concern | Rare | Serious | LOW | Standard monitoring | Key Unknowns 1. Phase 3 efficacy confirmation - Will Phase 2 results replicate at scale? 2. Head-to-head vs tirzepatide - REDEFINE 3 is critical for positioning 3. Cardiovascular outcomes - REDEFINE CVOT results not until 2029 4. Long-term safety - Limited data beyond 1 year 5. Body composition - Lean mass preservation unknown 6. Durability - Weight maintenance after stopping unknown 7. Optimal patient selection - Who benefits most from dual mechanism? --- Inferences Section This section contains logical reasoning from known evidence. These are NOT proven facts. Inference 1: CagriSema will demonstrate >20% weight loss in Phase 3 (a) Statement: The CagriSema combination will demonstrate mean weight loss exceeding 20% in Phase 3 trials, potentially matching or exceeding tirzepatide. (b) Why someone might believe it: - Phase 1b showed 17.1% at only 20 weeks (projected higher with longer treatment) - Phase 2 in T2D showed 15.6% (typically less than non-diabetic populations) - Complementary mechanisms (amylin + GLP-1) should produce additive effects - Both components at maximum dose (2.4 mg each) (c) Supporting evidence: - Phase 1b: -17.1% at 20 weeks PMID: 33894838 - Phase 2 T2D: -15.6% at 32 weeks PMID: 37364590 - Monotherapy: -10.8% at 26 weeks PMID: 34798060 - Mechanistic rationale: distinct satiety pathways (d) Contradicting evidence: - Phase 2 trials are small and can overestimate effects - GI tolerability may limit dose escalation in larger populations - Tirzepatide achieved 22% without needing combination - Regulatory/commercial success requires head-to-head superiority (e) Confidence: MODERATE (f) Study needed: REDEFINE 1 and REDEFINE 3 results (expected 2025) --- Inference 2: Amylin adds cardiovascular benefit beyond GLP-1 (a) Statement: The amylin component of CagriSema will provide additional cardiovascular benefit beyond what semaglutide alone offers. (b) Why someone might believe it: - More weight loss generally correlates with CV benefit - Amylin affects glucagon and postprandial glucose (CV risk factors) - Different mechanism may affect different CV pathways (c) Supporting evidence: - Weight loss strongly associated with CV risk reduction - Greater weight loss with combination likely translates to greater CV benefit - SELECT showed 20% MACE reduction with semaglutide for obesity (d) Contradicting evidence: - No direct CV outcome data for cagrilintide or amylin analogs - Pramlintide did not demonstrate CV benefit in its indication - CV benefit may be GLP-1-specific mechanism, not just weight loss - REDEFINE CVOT not designed to show superiority vs semaglutide (e) Confidence: LOW (f) Study needed: REDEFINE CVOT (NCT05803421) results expected 2029. Note: This trial compares CagriSema to placebo, not semaglutide, so cannot directly test amylin's incremental CV benefit. --- Inference 3: Cagrilintide preserves more lean mass than GLP-1 alone (a) Statement: The amylin pathway may lead to preferential fat loss with better lean mass preservation compared to GLP-1 agonist monotherapy. (b) Why someone might believe it: - Amylin works through different satiety pathways - Leptin sensitization affects energy expenditure regulation - Different metabolic signaling may affect body composition (c) Supporting evidence: - Limited; theoretical based on mechanism - Some preclinical suggestions of differential metabolic effects - Leptin pathway affects both intake and expenditure (d) Contradicting evidence: - No body composition data from Phase 2 CagriSema trials - Tirzepatide showed similar 75/25 fat/lean loss ratio - Rapid weight loss typically affects lean mass regardless of mechanism - No clinical evidence supports this inference (e) Confidence: LOW (f) Study needed: DEXA or MRI body composition substudies within REDEFINE program --- Comparisons Cagrilintide/CagriSema vs Semaglutide | Factor | CagriSema | Semaglutide 2.4 mg | |--------|-----------|-------------------| | Mechanism | Dual: Amylin + GLP-1 receptors | GLP-1 receptor only | | Weight loss (Phase 2, T2D) | -15.6% | -5.1% (same trial) | | Weight loss (projected Phase 3) | 22-25% (expected) | 14.9-20.7% (proven) | | HbA1c reduction | -2.2% | -1.8% (head-to-head) | | Cardiovascular outcomes | Unknown (CVOT ongoing) | Proven 20% MACE reduction | | Regulatory status | Investigational | FDA approved (multiple indications) | | Administration | Once weekly (combination pen) | Once weekly | | GI tolerability | Similar to GLP-1 | Well-characterized | | Cost (anticipated) | Unknown (likely premium) | $1,000-1,500/month | Summary: CagriSema appears to offer meaningfully greater weight loss than semaglutide alone based on Phase 2 data. However, semaglutide has extensive Phase 3 data, proven CV outcomes, multiple FDA approvals, and years of post-marketing experience. CagriSema's advantage will be confirmed or refuted by REDEFINE 1 and 2 results. name: CagriSema
summary: A fixed-dose combination of cagrilintide (amylin analog) and semaglutide (GLP-1 agonist) for chronic weight management. Phase 3 REDEFINE trials demonstrate 20-23% weight loss at 68 weeks. NDA submitted to FDA December 2025. First GLP-1/amylin combination therapy.
content: Overview Overview Quick Summary - Fixed-dose combination of cagrilintide 2.4mg (amylin analog) + semaglutide 2.4mg (GLP-1 agonist) - NDA submitted to FDA December 2025; review expected 2026 - REDEFINE-1 trial: 22.7% weight loss at 68 weeks (treatment policy estimand) - 60% of participants achieved ≥20% weight loss; 40.4% achieved ≥25% weight loss - Dual mechanism targets both GLP-1 and amylin pathways for synergistic appetite suppression - First-in-class GLP-1/amylin combination therapy for obesity Evidence Strength: High — Large Phase 3 RCTs (4,600+ patients), NEJM publication, NDA filed, consistent results across populations. Identity & Origins | Property | Value | |----------|-------| | Generic name | Cagrilintide-semaglutide | | Brand name | CagriSema (proposed) | | Research code | NNC0174-0833 | | Class | GLP-1/Amylin dual analog combination | | Manufacturer | Novo Nordisk | Discovery Timeline - 2019: Cagrilintide monotherapy Phase 2 trials initiated - 2021: REDEFINE program (combination) Phase 3 trials initiated - 2024: REDEFINE-1 and REDEFINE-2 topline results announced - 2025 (June): Full results presented at ADA 2025; published in NEJM - 2025 (December): NDA submitted to FDA for obesity indication - 2026: FDA review expected; REDEFINE-3 CV outcomes trial ongoing The Science: Why Two Pathways Matter CagriSema harnesses two distinct but complementary hormonal systems: The Amylin System (Cagrilintide) What is amylin? A 37-amino acid peptide co-secreted with insulin from pancreatic β-cells. Natural functions: - Promotes satiety signaling in the hindbrain - Slows gastric emptying - Suppresses postprandial glucagon secretion - Reduces food intake through area postrema and nucleus tractus solitarius Why it was overlooked: Unlike GLP-1, amylin deficiency in obesity was not well-characterized. Pramlintide (amylin analog) was approved for diabetes but never achieved widespread use due to multiple daily injections. The GLP-1 System (Semaglutide) What is GLP-1? A 30-amino acid incretin hormone released from intestinal L-cells. Natural functions: - Glucose-dependent insulin secretion - Appetite suppression via hypothalamic POMC/CART neurons - Delayed gastric emptying - Reduced glucagon secretion Combined Signaling CagriSema administration ↓ ├── BRAIN (Hypothalamus) - via semaglutide │ ├── POMC/CART activation → satiety ↑ │ └── NPY/AgRP inhibition → hunger ↓ │ ├── BRAIN (Hindbrain/Area Postrema) - via cagrilintide │ ├── Direct satiety signaling │ └── Meal termination signals │ ├── BRAIN (Septum/Reward Centers) - via both │ └── Reduced hedonic eating │ ├── STOMACH - via both (additive) │ └── Delayed gastric emptying → prolonged fullness │ ├── PANCREAS (α-cells) - via both │ └── ↓ Glucagon → better glycemic control │ └── PANCREAS (β-cells) - via semaglutide └── ↑ Glucose-dependent insulin secretion Why Combination Exceeds Monotherapy | Effect | Semaglutide Alone | Cagrilintide Alone | Combination | Interpretation | |--------|-------------------|-------------------|-------------|----------------| | Weight loss (68wk) | -14.9% | -11.5% | -22.7% | Synergistic (>additive) | | ≥20% weight loss | 35% | 20% | 60% | Enhanced responder rate | | ≥25% weight loss | 20% | 10% | 40.4% | Surgical-level in many | | HbA1c (T2D) | -1.5% | -0.8% | -2.2% | Complementary glycemic control | Key insight: The combination produces weight loss exceeding the sum of individual components. This pharmacological synergy results from complementary actions on distinct but interconnected neural circuits controlling appetite. --- Research Summary Evidence Overview | Category | Count | Notes | |----------|-------|-------| | Phase 3 RCTs | 4+ | REDEFINE-1, REDEFINE-2, REDEFINE-3 (ongoing), REDEFINE-11 | | Total Phase 3 patients | 4,600+ | REDEFINE-1 and REDEFINE-2 combined | | Human studies (all phases) | 10+ | Phase 1-3 trials | | Regulatory submissions | 1 | FDA NDA December 2025 | | Peer-reviewed publications | 3+ | Including NEJM 2025 | | Regions | 12+ countries | US, Europe, Latin America, Asia-Pacific | Key Evidence Highlights 1. REDEFINE-1: 22.7% weight loss at 68 weeks in obesity without diabetes; 60% achieved ≥20% weight loss 2. REDEFINE-2: 13.7% weight loss in obesity with T2D; 73.5% achieved HbA1c ≤6.5% 3. Synergy demonstrated: Combination exceeded semaglutide alone (-14.9%) and cagrilintide alone (-11.5%) 4. Cardiometabolic benefits: Improved BP, waist circumference, lipids, glycemic parameters 5. Prediabetes reversal: 88% of participants with prediabetes returned to normoglycemia --- Key Human Trials REDEFINE-1 (Obesity without Diabetes) | Trial | NCT ID | Population | Duration | N | |-------|--------|------------|----------|---| | REDEFINE-1 | NCT05567796 ( | BMI ≥30 or ≥27 with comorbidity | 68 weeks | 3,417 | Design: Phase 3a, double-blind, randomized, placebo-controlled and active-controlled trial. Participants randomized 21:3:3:7 to CagriSema, semaglutide, cagrilintide, or placebo. Key Results PMID: 40544432 : - Primary endpoint: -20.4% weight loss with CagriSema vs -3.0% placebo (ETD -17.3%; P<0.001) - Treatment policy estimand: -22.7% weight loss (accounting for adherence) - ≥20% weight loss: 60% with CagriSema - ≥25% weight loss: 40.4% with CagriSema - ≥30% weight loss: 23% with CagriSema Active comparator results: | Group | Weight Loss (68wk) | |-------|-------------------| | CagriSema | -22.7% | | Semaglutide 2.4mg | -14.9% | | Cagrilintide 2.4mg | -11.5% | | Placebo | -3.0% | REDEFINE-2 (Obesity with Type 2 Diabetes) | Trial | NCT ID | Population | Duration | N | |-------|--------|------------|----------|---| | REDEFINE-2 | NCT05394519 ( | BMI ≥27, HbA1c 7-10%, T2D | 68 weeks | 1,200 | Design: Phase 3a, double-blind, randomized, placebo-controlled trial across 12 countries. Participants randomized 3:1 to CagriSema or placebo. Key Results PMID: 40544432 : - Weight loss: -13.7% with CagriSema vs -3.4% placebo (ETD -10.4%; P<0.001) - HbA1c ≤6.5%: 73.5% with CagriSema vs 15.9% placebo - HbA1c reduction: -2.2% with CagriSema REDEFINE-3 (Cardiovascular Outcomes) | Trial | NCT ID | Population | Status | |-------|--------|------------|--------| | REDEFINE-3 | NCT pending | Obesity with established CV disease | Ongoing (event-driven) | Design: Event-driven cardiovascular outcomes trial evaluating MACE (CV death, nonfatal MI, nonfatal stroke). REDEFINE-11 (Extended Duration) | Trial | NCT ID | Population | Duration | |-------|--------|------------|----------| | REDEFINE-11 | NCT pending | BMI ≥30 | Extended (>68 weeks) | Design: Phase 3 trial with longer treatment duration to assess weight loss maintenance and continued efficacy. --- Safety Profile Common Adverse Events Based on REDEFINE-1 and REDEFINE-2 data: | Event | CagriSema | Placebo | Notes | |-------|-----------|---------|-------| | Any GI event | 79.6% | 39.9% | Most common category | | Nausea | 45-50% | 15% | Usually transient | | Vomiting | 20-25% | 5% | Decreases over time | | Diarrhea | 25-30% | 10% | Mild-moderate severity | | Constipation | 15-20% | 5% | Less common than other GI | Severity and Duration - GI adverse events were mostly mild to moderate in severity - Events were transient , typically occurring during dose titration - Discontinuation due to AEs: 5-7% (similar to GLP-1 monotherapy) Serious Adverse Events - Pancreatitis: Rare; monitor for symptoms - Gallbladder disease: Increased risk with rapid weight loss (class effect) - Hypoglycemia: Low risk as monotherapy; increased with insulin/sulfonylureas Precautions (Expected Based on Class) - Personal/family history of medullary thyroid carcinoma (MTC) - Multiple endocrine neoplasia syndrome type 2 (MEN2) - History of pancreatitis --- Pharmacokinetics Key Parameters | Parameter | Cagrilintide | Semaglutide | |-----------|--------------|-------------| | Half-life | 7 days | 7 days | | Administration | Once weekly SC | Once weekly SC | | Metabolism | Proteolytic degradation | Proteolytic degradation | | Bioavailability | High (SC) | High (SC) | Clinical Relevance The matched pharmacokinetic profiles of both components enable once-weekly co-administration as a fixed-dose combination. Both peptides use fatty acid acylation for albumin binding, extending half-life and enabling weekly dosing. --- Regulatory Status | Region | Agency | Status | Date | |--------|--------|--------|------| | USA | FDA | NDA Submitted | December 2025 | | EU | EMA | Expected submission | 2026 | | Japan | PMDA | Anticipated | TBD | FDA Review Details: - NDA submitted December 2025 for chronic weight management - Indication: Adults with obesity (BMI ≥30) or overweight (BMI ≥27) with weight-related comorbidity - Review expected 2026 - If approved, would be first GLP-1/amylin combination therapy --- Related Peptides CagriSema vs Tirzepatide | Outcome | CagriSema (REDEFINE-1) | Tirzepatide (SURMOUNT-1) | |---------|------------------------|-------------------------| | Weight loss (trial max) | -22.7% | -22.5% | | ≥20% weight loss | 60% | 57% | | ≥25% weight loss | 40.4% | 36% | | Mechanism | GLP-1 + Amylin | GLP-1 + GIP | | Dosing | Weekly SC | Weekly SC | Key difference: Different dual mechanisms — CagriSema uses amylin pathway (satiety/hindbrain), tirzepatide uses GIP pathway (adipose tissue/insulin). No head-to-head data available.
