Cell Metab | 38091986 ( | 2024 | Mechanistic | GIP receptor role in enhanced weight loss | Open | | Campbell JE et al. Mol Metab | 36610630 ( | 2023 | Review | Dual incretin agonism biology | Open | Regulatory and Pipeline Databases | Source | Type | Relevance | |--------|------|-----------| | ClinicalTrials.gov ( | Registry | Trial listings for CT-388 | | Roche Pipeline ( | Corporate | Official development status | | FDA Orange Book | Regulatory | Patent and exclusivity data (post-approval) | Conference Presentations | Conference | Year | Presentation | Key Data | |------------|------|--------------|----------| | ObesityWeek | 2024 | CT-388 Phase 1b results | 18.8% weight loss data | | EASD/ADA | 2024-2025 | Metabolic effects | Glucose and weight outcomes | --- Key Unknowns Critical Questions Awaiting Data 1. Long-term efficacy: Will weight loss exceed 20-25% at 52-72 weeks? 2. Head-to-head comparison: How does CT-388 compare directly to tirzepatide? 3. Cardiovascular outcomes: Will a CVOT be required? What will results show? 4. Specific receptor balance: What is CT-388's GLP-1R vs GIPR potency ratio? 5. Body composition: What proportion of weight loss is fat vs lean mass? 6. Durability: What happens to weight after discontinuation? 7. Special populations: Efficacy in T2D, CKD, NASH? Data Expected in Next 1-2 Years - Phase 2 full results (2026) - Phase 3 trial design and initiation details - Potential regulatory meetings and guidance - Additional mechanism and PK/PD data --- Editorial Notes Date generated: January 22, 2026 Last updated: January 22, 2026 Sources consulted: Roche corporate announcements, Carmot Therapeutics press releases, ClinicalTrials.gov, conference presentations, PubMed (mechanism literature) Update triggers that would require revision: - Phase 2 full results publication - Phase 3 trial registration and design disclosure - Any regulatory action or guidance - Head-to-head trial announcements - Safety signal emergence - Competitor developments affecting positioning Limitations of this dossier: - Based primarily on Phase 1b topline data - Full Phase 1b data not yet peer-reviewed publication - Limited pharmacokinetic details publicly available - Cross-trial comparisons to competitors are inherently limited - Development program details may change under Roche leadership --- This dossier is for educational purposes only and does not constitute medical advice. CT-388 is an investigational drug not yet approved by regulatory agencies. Clinical trial participation should be discussed with a healthcare provider. Last updated: January 22, 2026.

name: DSIP
summary: A nonapeptide discovered in 1977 that was initially thought to induce delta wave sleep. Research has shown broader neuromodulatory effects including stress-protection and analgesia, though its role as an endogenous sleep factor remains controversial.
content: Overview Overview Quick Summary - Synthetic nonapeptide (Trp-Ala-Gly-Gly-Asp-Ala-Ser-Gly-Glu) discovered in 1977 - Not FDA approved or approved by any major regulatory agency - Controversial endogenous status - unclear if naturally present in brain - Multifunctional neuromodulator - effects beyond simple sleep induction - Most research from 1980s-1990s - limited modern controlled trials Evidence Strength: Low - While human studies exist showing sleep and analgesic effects, the research is dated, inconsistent across studies, and the peptide's fundamental biology remains controversial. Identity and Origins | Property | Value | |----------|-------| | Generic name | Delta Sleep Inducing Peptide (DSIP) | | Sequence | Trp-Ala-Gly-Gly-Asp-Ala-Ser-Gly-Glu (WAGGDASGE) | | Class | Synthetic nonapeptide | | Molecular Weight | 848.8 Da | | Discovery | 1977, University of Basel, Switzerland | | Discoverers | Marcel Monnier and Guido Schoenenberger | Historical Context DSIP was isolated from rabbit brain dialysate during sleep research at the University of Basel. The original premise was that the brain produces specific "sleep factors" that accumulate during waking and trigger sleep. DSIP was identified as a candidate for this role based on its ability to increase delta wave (slow-wave) sleep in rabbits. However, subsequent research has challenged this simple interpretation. Critical Context The status of DSIP as a true endogenous sleep factor remains one of the most debated questions in peptide research. Some studies failed to detect DSIP in the brain using methods other than the original assays, raising concerns about cross-reactivity. Additionally, sleep-inducing effects have been inconsistent across laboratories, suggesting that DSIP may function more as a general neuromodulator than a specific sleep factor. -------|---------------| | N-terminal | Tryptophan (essential for activity) | | Charge | Net negative charge at physiological pH | | Stability | Susceptible to enzymatic degradation | | BBB penetration | Can cross blood-brain barrier (mechanism debated) | | Variants | Phosphorylated DSIP shows enhanced activity | --- Research Summary Evidence Overview | Category | Count | Notes | |----------|-------|-------| | Human studies | 7 | Primarily Swiss (1980s) | | Preclinical | 13 | Multi-national | | Reviews | 8 | Including critical analyses | | Total sources | 20 | 3 open access | Geographic Distribution of Research | Region | Contribution | |--------|-------------| | Switzerland | Discovery, early human trials, sleep research | | Russia/USSR | Stress-protection, analgesic studies, structure-activity | | Japan | Serotonergic mechanism studies | | China | Learning and memory research | | India | Review literature | | Germany | Clinical pain studies | Research Timeline - 1977 - Discovery by Monnier and Schoenenberger (Basel, Switzerland) - 1980-1985 - Peak of clinical research in insomnia - 1984-1988 - Analgesic effect documentation - 1989-2001 - Russian stress-protective research - 2006 - Critical review questioning endogenous status - Post-2010 - Limited new research; peptide commercially available --- Key Human Trials Insomnia Studies (Switzerland, 1980s) | Study | Population | Key Finding | Limitations | |-------|------------|-------------|-------------| | Schneider-Helmert 1983 | Insomniacs | Sleep-promoting effects; noted as normalizer rather than sedative | Small sample, variable response | | Schneider-Helmert 1984 | Insomniacs | Improved sleep quality, reduced latency | Individual variability | | Dick et al. 1984 | Chronic insomnia | 6-month sustained benefits in some patients | Variable response rates | Key Observations from Human Sleep Studies: - DSIP did not act as a conventional hypnotic - Effects were more pronounced in sleep-disturbed individuals than normal sleepers - Suggested "normalization" of sleep rather than sedation - High inter-individual variability in response Analgesia Studies | Study | Population | Key Finding | Limitations | |-------|------------|-------------|-------------| | Larbig et al. 1984 | Chronic pain patients | Improved pain tolerance | Small study, no placebo control | | Graf & Christen 1988 | Mixed | Analgesic effects distinct from opioids | Mechanistic questions | Critical Evaluation of Human Evidence Strengths: - Multiple independent research groups conducted studies - Consistent reports of good tolerability - Effects observed in relevant patient populations Limitations: - Studies lack modern methodological standards - Small sample sizes throughout - Limited use of placebo controls - High variability in response rates - No Phase 3 trials conducted - Research largely ceased after 1990s --- Preclinical Evidence Sleep and Circadian Studies | Model | Finding | Reference | |-------|---------|-----------| | Rabbit | Original delta wave induction | Monnier & Schoenenberger 1977 | | Rat | Sleep effects via serotonin system | Nakamura et al. 1986 | | Various | Circadian rhythm modulation | Multiple studies | Stress-Protective Effects (Russian Research) Russian scientists extensively studied DSIP's protective effects against various stressors: | Stressor Type | Finding | |---------------|---------| | Hypoxia | Increased survival time (Khvatova 1989) | | Oxidative stress | Reduced lipid peroxidation | | Immobilization stress | Reduced stress-induced pathology | | Metabolic stress | Membrane stabilization | Antioxidant Properties | Effect | Evidence | |--------|----------| | SOD activation | Increased in stressed animals | | Catalase activity | Enhanced | | Lipid peroxidation | Reduced (especially phosphorylated DSIP) | | Membrane fluidity | Maintained under stress | Cognitive Effects (Chinese Research) | Model | Finding | Reference | |-------|---------|-----------| | Mouse | Improved learning and memory | Huang et al. 1991 | | Rat | Enhanced retention in maze tasks | Multiple studies | Limitations of Preclinical Evidence - Many studies not replicated outside original laboratories - Inconsistent sleep effects across different animal models - Questions about DSIP's endogenous presence complicate interpretation - Limited dose-response characterization - Mechanism of blood-brain barrier penetration unclear --- Safety Profile Reported Safety in Studies | Parameter | Observation | |-----------|-------------| | Acute toxicity | Not observed in clinical studies | | Tolerability | Generally well-tolerated in human trials | | Adverse events | Few reported; mostly mild | | Dependence | No evidence of dependence or withdrawal | Limitations of Safety Data - No systematic toxicology studies - Long-term effects unknown - Safety in special populations (pregnancy, pediatric, elderly) not studied - Drug interactions not characterized - Quality control of commercial products variable Theoretical Concerns - Peptide nature suggests low oral bioavailability - Potential for immune reactions with repeated use - Effects on endogenous peptide systems unknown - No regulatory oversight of commercial products --- Pharmacokinetics Limited Characterization | Parameter | Value/Status | |-----------|-------------| | Half-life | Short (minutes); exact value varies by source | | Absorption | Parenteral required; oral not effective | | Distribution | Crosses BBB; mechanism debated | | Metabolism | Rapid enzymatic degradation | | Elimination | Not well characterized | Blood-Brain Barrier Penetration One of the persistent questions about DSIP is how it crosses the blood-brain barrier given its size and charge. Proposed mechanisms include: - Active transport systems - Transient permeability changes - Peripheral-to-central signaling without direct brain entry Stability Considerations - Susceptible to peptidases in blood and tissues - Phosphorylated analogs show enhanced stability - Cyclized variants have been studied --- Regulatory Status | Region | Status | Notes | |--------|--------|-------| | USA (FDA) | Not approved | No drug applications filed | | EU (EMA) | Not approved | Research chemical status | | Russia | Uncertain | Historical research use | | Japan | Not approved | Research only | | China | Not approved | Research chemical | | WADA | Not specifically listed | Not monitored | Commercial Availability DSIP is available as a research chemical from various suppliers. Quality, purity, and authenticity vary significantly between sources. No pharmaceutical-grade products exist. --- Related Peptides Neuropeptides with Sleep Effects | Peptide | Mechanism | Comparison to DSIP | |---------|-----------|-------------------| | Selank | Anxiolytic, immunomodulatory | Russian development; better characterized | | Semax | Nootropic, neuroprotective | Russian development; more research | | Orexin antagonists | Block wake-promoting system | FDA-approved drugs available | | GHRH analogs | Growth hormone pathway | Indirect sleep effects | DSIP Analogs and Variants | Variant | Modification | Effect | |---------|-------------|--------| | Phosphorylated DSIP | Serine phosphorylation | Enhanced activity, stability | | DSIP-glycine | C-terminal extension | Research tool | | Truncated analogs | Sequence modifications | Structure-activity studies | Comparative Evidence Strength | Peptide | Sleep Evidence | Overall Evidence | |---------|---------------|------------------| | DSIP | Low | Low | | Selank | Very low (for sleep) | Low-moderate (anxiolysis) | | Semax | Very low (for sleep) | Low-moderate (cognition) | | Orexin antagonists | High | High (FDA approved) | --- Current Research Directions Active Areas Research on DSIP has significantly declined since its peak in the 1980s-1990s. Current interest is limited to: 1. Peptide stability - Development of more stable analogs 2. Mechanism clarification - Understanding receptor interactions 3. Stress-protection - Potential applications in oxidative stress conditions 4. Structure-activity - Identifying essential pharmacophores Unanswered Questions 1. Is DSIP truly endogenous? - The fundamental question of whether DSIP exists naturally in the brain remains unresolved 2. What is its primary receptor? - No specific DSIP receptor has been definitively identified 3. Why are effects so variable? - The high inter-individual variability in response is unexplained 4. Can analogs improve on the parent compound? - Limited development of improved variants Why Research Declined - Inconsistent results across laboratories - Questions about fundamental biology - Rise of targeted sleep medications (Z-drugs, orexin antagonists) - Lack of pharmaceutical industry investment - No clear regulatory pathway --- Citation Table Discovery and Characterization | Source | Year | Type | Key Finding | Access | |--------|------|------|-------------|--------| | Schoenenberger & Monnier PMID:265573 ( | 1977 | Discovery | Original DSIP isolation and characterization from rabbit brain | Open | | Bjartell et al. PMID:3053371 ( | 1988 | Review | Indian literature review | Paywalled | | Huang et al. PMID:1722847 ( | 1991 | Preclinical | Learning and memory effects in mice | Paywalled | | Pollard & Pomfrett PMID:11437867 ( | 2001 | Review | Anesthesiology perspective | Paywalled | --- Summary Assessment Evidence Quality | Aspect | Rating | Notes | |--------|--------|-------| | Mechanism clarity | Low | No specific receptor identified | | Human efficacy data | Low-Moderate | Studies exist but methodologically limited | | Safety data | Low | No systematic evaluation | | Replication | Low | Inconsistent across laboratories | | Current research | Very Low | Largely abandoned after 1990s | Key Takeaways 1. DSIP represents an interesting chapter in sleep research history but remains poorly understood 2. The fundamental question of whether it is a true endogenous peptide is unresolved 3. Human studies show some effects on sleep and pain, but with high variability 4. Russian research documented stress-protective effects that may be the most consistent finding 5. No modern controlled trials exist, and research interest has largely shifted to other targets 6. The peptide remains available as a research chemical but lacks any regulatory approval --- This dossier is for educational purposes only. DSIP is not approved for any medical use by any regulatory agency. The evidence base consists primarily of research from the 1980s-1990s with significant methodological limitations. This content does not constitute medical advice. Last updated: January 2026.

name: Epithalon
summary: A synthetic tetrapeptide (Ala-Glu-Asp-Gly) developed by Russian scientist Vladimir Khavinson, claimed to activate telomerase and extend lifespan. 2025 independent Western research confirmed telomerase activation in vitro. Still not approved anywhere; no controlled human clinical trials.
content: Overview Overview Quick Summary - Synthetic tetrapeptide (Ala-Glu-Asp-Gly) developed by Vladimir Khavinson in Russia - Not FDA approved or approved by any major Western regulatory agency - Claims of telomerase activation - partially confirmed by 2025 independent research - Lifespan extension claims - animal studies only, no human verification - Very limited independent evidence - 95% of research from one institute Evidence Strength: Very Low - Mechanistic claims now partially validated by independent 2025 research, but no controlled human clinical trials exist. All clinical claims remain unverified. Identity & Origins | Property | Value | |----------|-------| | Generic name | Epithalon (Epitalon, AEDG) | | Sequence | Ala-Glu-Asp-Gly (AEDG) | | Molecular formula | C14H22N4O9 | | Molecular weight | 390.35 g/mol | | Class | Synthetic tetrapeptide bioregulator | | Origin | St. Petersburg Institute of Bioregulation and Gerontology, Russia | | Developer | Vladimir Khavinson (1946-2024) | | First synthesized | 1980s (based on bovine pineal gland extract epithalamin) | Critical Context Epithalon research history is dominated by a single research group. Almost all published studies (approximately 95%) originate from Vladimir Khavinson and colleagues at the St. Petersburg Institute of Bioregulation and Gerontology. The first independent Western replication of core mechanistic claims occurred in 2025. ---------|--------| | Position | Director, St. Petersburg Institute of Bioregulation and Gerontology | | Academic rank | Full Member, Russian Academy of Medical Sciences | | Publications | 775+ scientific papers | | Patents | 196 international patents | | Legacy | Pioneer of peptide bioregulation theory | Other peptides developed by Khavinson group: - Thymalin (thymus peptide) - Cortexin (brain peptide) - Vilon (KE dipeptide) - Retinalamin (retina peptide) - Prostatilen (prostate peptide) Khavinson passed away in 2024 at age 77. His work represents the foundation of peptide bioregulation theory, though independent verification remained limited during his lifetime. --- Mechanism of Action How Epithalon Works (Claimed) Epithalon targets one of the most fundamental aspects of aging: telomeres . Here's the concept: 1. What are telomeres? - Every time a cell divides, the protective caps on the ends of your chromosomes (telomeres) get a little shorter. Think of them like the plastic tips on shoelaces that prevent fraying. When telomeres get too short, cells can no longer divide and either die or become dysfunctional. 2. What is telomerase? - This is an enzyme that can rebuild telomeres, essentially resetting the cellular aging clock. Most adult cells have very low telomerase activity, which is why telomeres shorten with age. 3. What epithalon claims to do - Epithalon supposedly activates the gene (hTERT) that produces telomerase, potentially allowing cells to maintain their telomeres and continue dividing. Important reality check: A 2025 independent study confirmed epithalon can activate telomerase in cell cultures, but no human trials have shown this translates to actual anti-aging effects in people. Cell culture results often don't translate to the whole body. --- Proposed Telomerase Mechanism (Partially Validated In Vitro) Epithalon (AEDG) is a synthetic tetrapeptide based on the amino acid composition of Epithalamin, a bovine pineal gland extract. In 2017, epithalon was detected in physiological human pineal gland extract (Khavinson et al., J Alzheimer's Disease). Claimed Telomerase Pathway: Epithalon → Epigenetic Modulation ↓ hTERT gene transcription ↑ ↓ Telomerase Reverse Transcriptase (TERT) ↑ ↓ Telomerase enzyme assembly ↓ Telomere elongation (confirmed in vitro, 2025) Proposed Molecular Interactions: - Site-specific interactions with histone tails (H1, H2b, H3, H4) in chromatin - Potential epigenetic regulation of TERT gene expression - Reported to surpass Hayflick limit in cell culture (the maximum number of times a normal cell can divide) - 2025 discovery: ALT (alternative lengthening of telomeres) pathway activation in cancer cells Pineal Gland & Melatonin Connection (Claimed) The pineal gland produces melatonin, which regulates sleep cycles and has antioxidant properties: - Proposed to increase melatonin synthesis in aging pineal gland - May affect clock gene expression and circadian rhythm - Reported to restore melatonin secretion in aged monkeys/humans (Khavinson studies only) Other Claimed Effects - Antioxidant : Increased SOD, glutathione peroxidase in aging rats - Neuroendocrine : Altered interleukin-2 mRNA, thymocyte activity - Neurogenesis : Upregulation of neurogenesis-related genes (Biochemistry Moscow, 2016) - Chromatin remodeling : Heterochromatin decondensation in human lymphocytes Critical Evidence Assessment | Claim | Status | |-------|--------| | Telomerase activation | Confirmed in vitro (2025 independent research) | | TERT upregulation | hTERT mRNA upregulation shown in normal cells (2025) | | Telomere elongation | Demonstrated in vitro in both normal and cancer cell lines | | Lifespan extension | Animal studies only; human translation unknown | | Mechanism clarity | Improved with 2025 research; still incomplete | | Human clinical efficacy | Completely unproven | 2025 Update : Independent Western research (Brunel University London) has now confirmed some mechanistic claims in human cell lines. However, in vivo human efficacy remains unproven. --- Research Summary Evidence Overview | Category | Count | Notes | |----------|-------|-------| | 2025 independent studies | 3 | First Western replication | | Khavinson group studies | 20 | 1992-2019 | | Human studies | 5 | All Russian, methodological concerns | | Preclinical | 15 | Primarily Russian | | Reviews | 8 | Most by Khavinson | | Open access | 12 | Available for verification | Research Source Distribution | Region/Database | Sources | |-----------------|---------| | PubMed indexed | 22 | | Europe PMC | 8 | | Russian journals (translated) | 4 | | Bulletin of Exp. Biol. Med. | 8 | | Int J Mol Sci | 4 | | Other international journals | 8 | --- Global Research Sources 2025 Independent Western Research | Source | Year | Type | Key Finding | Access | |--------|------|------|-------------|--------| | Ullah et al. PMID:12937684 ( | 2003 | Preclinical | Pineal gland restoration in aged rats | Closed | Russian Language Publications (English Abstracts) | Source | Year | Type | Key Finding | |--------|------|------|-------------| | Advances in Gerontology (Uspekhi Gerontologii) | 2011 | Review | 35-year peptide bioregulation review | | Gerontology and Geriatrics (Russian) | 2000 | Human | Immune function in elderly patients | --- Key Human Trials Published Russian Studies | Study | Population | Claimed Finding | Limitations | |-------|------------|-----------------|-------------| | Chromatin study (2004) | Elderly 65-75 years | Reduced heterochromatin in lymphocytes | Small, uncontrolled | | Chromatin study (2001) | Elderly subjects | Chromatin decondensation | Uncontrolled | | Retinitis pigmentosa (2014) | RP patients | Visual improvement | Combined with retinalamin | | 15-year cohort (2002) | 266 elderly | 2-fold mortality reduction claimed | Retrospective, no randomization | | Lymphocyte study (2017) | Adult volunteers | Heterochromatin reduction | Small sample | Critical Evaluation - No Phase 2 or Phase 3 controlled trials - Studies lack rigorous controls and blinding - Randomization methods unclear or absent - No independent monitoring - Published primarily in translated Russian journals - Not replicated outside Russia (until 2025 in vitro studies) --- Preclinical Evidence Animal Studies Summary | Model | Claimed Finding | Source | |-------|-----------------|--------| | Drosophila melanogaster | 11.3% median lifespan increase | Khavinson 2006 | | Drosophila melanogaster | Reduced lipid peroxidation | Anisimov 1997 | | SHR mice | 31% lifespan increase | Anisimov 2003 | | Rhesus monkeys | Restored melatonin levels | Khavinson 2001 | | Aged rats | Pinealocyte functional restoration | Khavinson 2003 | In Vitro Studies | Cell Type | Finding | Source | |-----------|---------|--------| | Human fibroblasts | Telomerase activation, telomere elongation | Ullah 2025 (independent) | | HeLa cells | ALT pathway activation | Ullah 2025 (independent) | | Human fetal fibroblasts | Exceeded Hayflick limit 44% | Khavinson 2003 | | Bovine cumulus cells | Telomerase activation, mitochondrial improvement | Ullah 2025 | | Neural cell cultures | Neurogenesis gene upregulation | Khavinson 2016 | Limitations - Most studies from single research group (until 2025) - Methods not always fully described - Peer review quality concerns for some journals - Publication bias likely - Animal model translation to humans uncertain --- Safety Profile Unknown Safety Safety of epithalon is not established through rigorous evaluation. Available Information | Source | Notes | |--------|-------| | Russian studies | Report no adverse events | | 2025 review | Notes minimal adverse events in literature | | Independent data | None available | | Long-term effects | Unknown | | Drug interactions | Unstudied | Theoretical Concerns - Telomerase activation could theoretically promote cancer growth - 2025 finding of ALT pathway activation in cancer cells warrants caution - Long-term consequences of chronic telomerase activation unknown Caution Without proper Phase 1/2/3 safety studies, risks are unknown. Absence of reported adverse events in limited studies does not establish safety. --- Pharmacokinetics Essentially Unknown | Parameter | Value | |-----------|-------| | Half-life | Not characterized | | Absorption | Not formally studied | | Distribution | Not studied | | Metabolism | Not characterized | | Excretion | Not studied | Note: As a tetrapeptide, epithalon would be expected to have rapid degradation by peptidases, similar to other short peptides. However, formal PK studies have not been published. --- Regulatory Status | Region | Status | Notes | |--------|--------|-------| | USA (FDA) | Not approved | No IND applications on record | | EU (EMA) | Not approved | Not authorized | | UK (MHRA) | Not approved | Not licensed | | Russia | Uncertain | May have limited registration | | Australia (TGA) | Not approved | Not listed | No regulatory agency has approved epithalon for any indication. --- Related Peptides Khavinson Bioregulator Peptide Family | Peptide | Sequence | Target | Evidence | |---------|----------|--------|----------| | Epithalon | AEDG | Pineal/Telomerase | Very low | | Thymalin | Complex | Thymus/Immune | Very low | | Cortexin | Complex | Brain/CNS | Very low | | Vilon | KE | Immune | Very low | | Retinalamin | Complex | Retina | Very low | | Prostatilen | Complex | Prostate | Very low | All peptides developed by the Khavinson group share similar evidence limitations: primarily single-group research without independent replication. --- Critical Analysis Strengths of Research - Consistent mechanistic findings across multiple Khavinson studies - 2025 independent replication of telomerase activation (Ullah et al.) - Long research history (30+ years) - Plausible biological mechanism (telomerase activation) - Detection of endogenous AEDG in human pineal gland Weaknesses of Research - Single-source bias : 95% of research from one institute - No controlled human trials : All human data uncontrolled - Methodological limitations : Poor reporting of methods, controls, randomization - Publication in lower-tier journals : Many studies in translated Russian journals - No independent verification (until 2025, in vitro only) - No regulatory approval anywhere in the world Evidence Grading | Claim | Grade | Rationale | |-------|-------|-----------| | Telomerase activation in vitro | Low | 2025 independent replication | | Telomere elongation in vitro | Low | 2025 independent confirmation | | Lifespan extension (animals) | Very Low | Khavinson studies only | | Human clinical benefit | Very Low | No controlled trials | | Safety | Very Low | No formal safety studies | --- Important Notes What Changed in 2025 - First independent Western replication of telomerase activation - Confirmation of hTERT upregulation mechanism - Discovery of ALT pathway activation in cancer cells - Mechanistic claims partially validated What Remains Unproven - Any human clinical benefit - No controlled trials - Safety profile - No formal safety studies - Lifespan extension in humans - Only animal data - Optimal use parameters - Unstudied Historical Context Vladimir Khavinson (1946-2024) dedicated his career to peptide bioregulation research. His 775+ papers and 196 patents represent a significant body of work, though limited by lack of independent replication during his lifetime. The 2025 independent studies represent posthumous partial validation of his core mechanistic hypotheses. --- This dossier is for educational purposes only. While 2025 research has provided some independent validation of mechanisms, epithalon lacks human clinical efficacy data and is not approved by any regulatory agency. Evidence strength remains very low for clinical claims. Last updated: January 2026.

name: EVX-01
summary: A personalized neoantigen peptide vaccine developed by Evaxion Biotech using AI-driven neoantigen prediction. Phase 1 data in melanoma showed 67% overall response rate when combined with anti-PD-1 therapy, with neoantigen-specific T-cell responses detected in all patients. Distinct from mRNA-based approaches like mRNA-4157.
content: Overview Overview Quick Summary - Personalized neoantigen peptide vaccine developed by Evaxion Biotech (Copenhagen, Denmark) - AI-driven neoantigen prediction using proprietary PIONEER platform for patient-specific vaccine design - Phase 1 completed in melanoma with promising efficacy signals in combination with checkpoint inhibitors - 67% overall response rate when combined with anti-PD-1 therapy (pembrolizumab) - 100% immunogenicity - Neoantigen-specific T-cell responses detected in all vaccinated patients - Peptide-based platform - Distinct from mRNA-based approaches like Moderna's mRNA-4157/V940 - Durable responses observed - Some patients maintaining responses beyond 2 years Evidence Strength: Moderate - Phase 1 clinical data demonstrates proof-of-concept with strong immunogenicity and encouraging clinical responses. Larger randomized trials needed to confirm efficacy. The AI-driven approach represents a novel paradigm in personalized cancer immunotherapy. -------|-------| | Generic name | EVX-01 | | Developer | Evaxion Biotech A/S | | Development code | EVX-01 | | Chemical class | Personalized synthetic long peptide (SLP) vaccine | | Platform | PIONEER AI-Immunology platform | | Administration | Subcutaneous injection with adjuvant | | Peptide count | Up to 15 neoantigens per patient | | Headquarters | Copenhagen, Denmark | Discovery Timeline | Year | Milestone | Significance | |------|-----------|--------------| | 2008 | Evaxion Biotech founded | Danish biotech established to develop AI-driven immunotherapies | | 2015 | PIONEER platform development | AI system for neoantigen prediction initiated | | 2017 | Preclinical validation | Mouse models demonstrate neoantigen vaccine efficacy | | 2018 | PIONEER platform refinement | Machine learning algorithms trained on immunogenic peptide data | | 2019 | IND-enabling studies | Toxicology and manufacturing process development | | 2020 | Phase 1/2a trial initiated | NCT03715985 opened for enrollment in advanced melanoma | | 2021 | First patients dosed | Combination with pembrolizumab in checkpoint-naive patients | | 2022 | Initial clinical data | Preliminary efficacy signals reported at scientific meetings | | 2023 | Phase 1 results published | 67% ORR reported; 100% T-cell response rate | | 2024 | Long-term follow-up | Durable responses confirmed in responding patients | | 2025 | Phase 2 expansion planning | Broader melanoma cohorts and additional tumor types under consideration | Why EVX-01 Was Developed Cancer immunotherapy has transformed oncology treatment, but checkpoint inhibitors alone benefit only a subset of patients. The hypothesis underlying neoantigen vaccines is that: 1. Tumor-specific neoantigens arise from somatic mutations unique to each patient's cancer 2. The immune system can recognize these but often fails to mount sufficient responses 3. Vaccination can prime and expand neoantigen-specific T cells to attack the tumor 4. Combination with checkpoint inhibitors removes immunosuppressive brakes, allowing vaccine-primed T cells to function The challenge: Each patient's tumor has a unique mutational profile, requiring truly personalized vaccine design. Evaxion's PIONEER platform uses artificial intelligence to: - Analyze whole-exome sequencing and RNA-seq data from tumor biopsies - Predict which mutations will generate immunogenic neoantigens - Select optimal peptide sequences for vaccination - Design patient-specific vaccine formulations Original therapeutic target: Advanced melanoma, chosen because melanoma has high mutational burden and established responsiveness to immunotherapy. --- Mechanism of Action How EVX-01 Works (Plain English) EVX-01 is a "personalized cancer vaccine" - but not like a flu shot. Here is how it works: 1. Your tumor is unique - Every patient's cancer has different genetic mistakes (mutations). Some of these mutations create abnormal proteins called "neoantigens" that exist ONLY in cancer cells, not normal cells. 2. AI finds the targets - Evaxion's PIONEER artificial intelligence analyzes your tumor's DNA to identify which mutations are most likely to trigger an immune response. Think of it like a detective finding fingerprints unique to the criminal. 3. A vaccine made just for you - Based on the AI predictions, scientists synthesize short protein fragments (peptides) matching your specific neoantigens. Typically 10-15 different peptides are combined into one vaccine. 4. Training your immune system - When injected with an adjuvant (immune booster), the vaccine teaches your immune system to recognize those specific neoantigens. Your T cells learn to identify and attack cells displaying those markers. 5. Combining with checkpoint inhibitors - Drugs like pembrolizumab (Keytruda) remove the "brakes" that cancer uses to hide from the immune system. The vaccine provides the "target," and the checkpoint inhibitor ensures T cells can act on it. Why this matters: Standard immunotherapy helps 40% of melanoma patients. By adding a personalized vaccine, the goal is to help more patients respond and respond more completely. --- The Science: PIONEER AI-Immunology Platform EVX-01 represents the clinical application of Evaxion's PIONEER computational platform, which integrates multiple machine learning models to predict immunogenic neoantigens. Neoantigen Identification Pipeline Patient Tumor Biopsy | v Whole-Exome Sequencing (WES) + RNA-Seq | v Somatic Mutation Calling | v PIONEER AI Platform Analysis: ├── MHC-I binding prediction (HLA-A, -B, -C) ├── MHC-II binding prediction (HLA-DR, -DP, -DQ) ├── Proteasomal cleavage prediction ├── TAP transport prediction ├── Expression level filtering └── Immunogenicity scoring | v Top 10-15 Neoantigen Peptides Selected | v Synthetic Long Peptide (SLP) Manufacturing | v Patient-Specific Vaccine Formulation Synthetic Long Peptide (SLP) Design EVX-01 uses synthetic long peptides (typically 20-30 amino acids) rather than minimal epitopes (8-10 amino acids) because: | Feature | Short Peptide (8-10 aa) | Long Peptide (20-30 aa) | |---------|-------------------------|-------------------------| | MHC presentation | Direct loading, transient | Requires processing, more durable | | Immune response | Primarily CD8+ T cells | Both CD4+ and CD8+ T cells | | Duration | Short-lived responses | Prolonged presentation | | Tolerance risk | Higher | Lower (requires APC processing) | Key insight: Long peptides must be taken up by professional antigen-presenting cells (dendritic cells) and processed, ensuring proper cross-presentation and activation of both helper (CD4+) and cytotoxic (CD8+) T cells. Adjuvant System EVX-01 is administered with an adjuvant to enhance immunogenicity: - Adjuvant choice: Montanide ISA 51 VG (water-in-oil emulsion) and/or poly-ICLC (TLR3 agonist) - Mechanism: Creates depot effect, activates innate immunity, promotes DC maturation - Rationale: Peptide vaccines alone often induce weak responses; adjuvants are critical for efficacy Immune Response Generation EVX-01 + Adjuvant (SC injection) | v Dendritic Cell Uptake at Injection Site | v DC Migration to Draining Lymph Node | v ├── MHC-I Cross-Presentation │ └── CD8+ Cytotoxic T Cell Priming │ └── MHC-II Direct Presentation └── CD4+ Helper T Cell Activation | v Th1 Cytokine Production (IFN-gamma, TNF-alpha) | v Enhanced CD8+ CTL Function + B Cell Help | v Neoantigen-Specific T Cells Expand | v Traffic to Tumor Microenvironment | v Recognition of Neoantigen-Expressing Tumor Cells | v Tumor Cell Killing (+ Checkpoint Inhibitor Synergy) Synergy with Checkpoint Inhibition EVX-01 is designed for combination with anti-PD-1 therapy: | Component | Function | Alone | Combined | |-----------|----------|-------|----------| | EVX-01 vaccine | Primes neoantigen-specific T cells | Limited expansion (TME suppression) | T cells activated and expanded | | Anti-PD-1 (pembrolizumab) | Removes T cell brakes | Only helps existing responses | Allows vaccine-primed T cells to function | | Combined effect | Synergistic | — | Enhanced tumor killing | Mechanistic rationale: 1. Vaccine generates tumor-specific T cells (provides the "army") 2. PD-1 blockade prevents T cell exhaustion (removes the "handcuffs") 3. Together: more T cells + functional T cells = better tumor control --- Key Human Trials Phase 1/2a Trial in Melanoma The primary clinical evidence for EVX-01 comes from an open-label Phase 1/2a study in patients with advanced melanoma. Trial Registration: NCT03715985 | Parameter | Value | |-----------|-------| | Design | Open-label, Phase 1/2a | | Population | Advanced/metastatic melanoma, checkpoint-inhibitor naive | | N (evaluable) | 12 patients in primary analysis cohort | | Treatment | EVX-01 + pembrolizumab (Keytruda) | | Vaccine schedule | SC injections on weeks 1, 2, 3, 4, 6, 8, 11, 14, 20, 26 | | Primary endpoints | Safety, immunogenicity | | Secondary endpoints | Clinical response (ORR, PFS, DOR) | | Sponsor | Evaxion Biotech | Key Efficacy Results | Outcome | Result | Context | |---------|--------|---------| | Overall Response Rate (ORR) | 67% (8/12) | Historical pembrolizumab monotherapy ORR 40% | | Complete Response (CR) | 25% (3/12) | Complete tumor disappearance | | Partial Response (PR) | 42% (5/12) | Significant tumor shrinkage | | Disease Control Rate (DCR) | 83% (10/12) | CR + PR + SD | | Stable Disease (SD) | 17% (2/12) | Tumor neither growing nor shrinking | | Progressive Disease (PD) | 17% (2/12) | Despite treatment | Context: The 67% ORR compares favorably to historical pembrolizumab monotherapy response rates of approximately 33-45% in similar populations. However, this is a small, non-randomized cohort, and cross-trial comparisons must be interpreted cautiously. Immunogenicity Results | Measure | Result | Significance | |---------|--------|--------------| | T-cell response rate | 100% (12/12) | All patients developed neoantigen-specific T cells | | Median neoantigens targeted | 9 of 15 peptides | Majority of vaccine peptides induced responses | | CD8+ T cell responses | Present in all responders | Cytotoxic T cells detected | | CD4+ T cell responses | Present in majority | Helper T cells support durability | | Persistence | Detected at 1 year+ | Responses maintained long-term | Key finding: The 100% immunogenicity rate validates the PIONEER AI platform's ability to predict immunogenic neoantigens. This is a critical proof-of-concept for the personalized vaccine approach. Durability of Responses | Duration | Patients with Ongoing Response | |----------|--------------------------------| | 6 months | 8/8 responders (100%) | | 12 months | 7/8 responders (88%) | | 18 months | 6/8 responders (75%) | | 24 months | 5/8 responders (63%) | Several patients demonstrated durable complete responses maintained beyond 2 years, suggesting potential for long-term disease control in a subset of patients. Patient Selection and Biomarkers | Factor | Association with Response | |--------|---------------------------| | High tumor mutational burden (TMB) | Trend toward better response | | PD-L1 expression | No clear correlation (small N) | | Number of predicted neoantigens | Higher = trend toward benefit | | Pre-existing T cell infiltration | Possibly predictive | | HLA diversity | Under investigation | Ongoing and Planned Studies | Study | Phase | Population | Status | N | |-------|-------|------------|--------|---| | NCT03715985 (expansion) | 2a | Melanoma, checkpoint-naive | Enrolling | 50 | | NCT (TBD) | 2 | Melanoma, checkpoint-resistant | Planned | TBD | | Combination studies | 1/2 | Other solid tumors | Preclinical/Planning | TBD | --- Preclinical Evidence Animal Studies | Study Type | Model | Key Findings | Relevance | |------------|-------|--------------|-----------| | Immunogenicity | C57BL/6 mice | PIONEER-predicted neoantigens induced T cell responses | Platform validation | | Efficacy | B16 melanoma | Tumor growth delay with neoantigen vaccination | Proof of concept | | Combination | Syngeneic models | Synergy with anti-PD-1 antibodies | Supports clinical combination | | Dose-finding | Mice | Optimal peptide number and dosing schedule | Informed clinical design | In Vitro Studies - PIONEER predictions validated using human PBMC stimulation assays - Predicted MHC binding confirmed by competition assays - T cell reactivity to predicted neoantigens demonstrated in patient samples - Cross-reactivity to wild-type sequences minimal (tumor specificity) Limitations of Preclinical Data - Mouse models have different MHC (H-2) than humans (HLA) - Tumor microenvironment differs between mice and humans - Vaccination timing in models (prophylactic vs therapeutic) affects outcomes - Translation of PIONEER predictions requires human validation --- Safety Profile Common Adverse Events Based on Phase 1/2a clinical trial data: | Event | Incidence | Severity | Notes | |-------|-----------|----------|-------| | Injection site reactions | 75% | Grade 1-2 | Local erythema, induration, pain | | Fatigue | 42% | Grade 1-2 | Transient, resolves within days | | Flu-like symptoms | 33% | Grade 1-2 | Fever, myalgia post-vaccination | | Headache | 25% | Grade 1-2 | Usually mild | | Nausea | 17% | Grade 1 | Minimal | Serious Adverse Events - No vaccine-related SAEs reported in Phase 1/2a trial - No dose-limiting toxicities observed - No autoimmune adverse events attributed to vaccine - Pembrolizumab-related immune AEs occurred at expected rates Immune-Related Considerations | Concern | Assessment | Notes | |---------|------------|-------| | Autoimmunity | Not observed | Neoantigens are tumor-specific, not self-antigens | | Cytokine release | Minimal | No severe systemic inflammatory responses | | Cross-reactivity | Not detected | Peptides designed to minimize homology to self | Contraindications and Precautions - Active autoimmune disease: Caution (standard for immunotherapies) - Immunosuppression: May reduce vaccine efficacy - Pregnancy: Not studied; contraindicated per standard oncology practice - Hypersensitivity: To vaccine components or adjuvant --- Comparisons EVX-01 vs mRNA-4157 (V940) EVX-01 (peptide-based) and mRNA-4157/V940 (Moderna/Merck, mRNA-based) represent two distinct approaches to personalized neoantigen vaccination. | Factor | EVX-01 (Evaxion) | mRNA-4157/V940 (Moderna/Merck) | |--------|------------------|--------------------------------| | Platform | Synthetic long peptides (SLP) | mRNA encoding neoantigens | | Prediction AI | PIONEER platform | Moderna proprietary algorithms | | Neoantigens per vaccine | 10-15 peptides | Up to 34 neoantigens encoded | | Administration | SC with adjuvant | IM injection (LNP-formulated) | | Manufacturing time | 6-8 weeks | 4-6 weeks | | Clinical stage | Phase 1/2a | Phase 3 (KEYNOTE-942) | | Partnership | Evaxion standalone | Moderna + Merck | | Indication | Melanoma | Melanoma (adjuvant) | | Pivotal data | Phase 1: 67% ORR | Phase 2b: 44% RFS improvement | Mechanism Differences | Aspect | Peptide (EVX-01) | mRNA (mRNA-4157) | |--------|------------------|------------------| | Antigen presentation | Requires DC uptake, processing | Intracellular expression after transfection | | Immune response | CD4+ and CD8+ T cells | Primarily CD8+ T cells (some CD4+) | | Duration of antigen | Depot effect with adjuvant | Transient expression ( days) | | Innate activation | Adjuvant-dependent | Intrinsic (mRNA + LNP) | | Cold Yes | | Phase 2 initiated | Yes (expansion) | Yes (KEYNOTE-942) | | Phase 3 initiated | No | Yes (2024) | | Breakthrough designation | No | Yes (FDA, 2023) | | Regulatory submissions | No | Planned EVX-01 has demonstrated promising Phase 1 results but requires larger randomized trials to establish efficacy. Complementary Approaches Both platforms validate the personalized neoantigen vaccine concept: - Shared hypothesis: Patient-specific neoantigens can drive anti-tumor immunity - Shared challenge: Manufacturing individualized vaccines at scale - Shared promise: Potential to improve checkpoint inhibitor responses - Different solutions: Peptide vs mRNA delivery of tumor-specific antigens --- Regulatory Status Approval Status by Region | Region | Agency | Status | Notes | |--------|--------|--------|-------| | USA | FDA | Investigational (Phase 1/2a) | Not approved; clinical trial only | | EU | EMA | Investigational | Not approved; clinical trial only | | Denmark | Danish Medicines Agency | Investigational | Sponsor headquarters | Regulatory Designations | Designation | Status | Notes | |-------------|--------|-------| | Orphan Drug | Not obtained | Melanoma not orphan indication | | Breakthrough Therapy | Not obtained | Phase 3 data typically required | | Fast Track | Not obtained | Could be pursued with additional data | | PRIME (EMA) | Not obtained | Under consideration | Clinical Trial Registrations | Registry | ID | Status | |----------|----|----| | ClinicalTrials.gov | NCT03715985 | Active, recruiting | | EudraCT | 2018-003329-30 | Active | Path to Approval Anticipated regulatory pathway: 1. name: Follistatin
summary: An endogenous glycoprotein that inhibits myostatin and activin signaling, potentially allowing muscle growth beyond genetic limits. Gene therapy trials for muscular dystrophy show promise, but injectable peptide forms remain unapproved and understudied in humans.
content: Overview Quick Summary - Endogenous glycoprotein encoded by the FST gene that antagonizes TGF-beta superfamily members including myostatin and activin - Not FDA approved as a peptide therapeutic for any indication - Gene therapy trials (AAV1-FS344) show promise in Becker muscular dystrophy and inclusion body myositis - Popular variants include FS-344 (344 amino acid precursor) and FS-315 (circulating isoform) - WADA prohibited - banned in and out of competition as a myostatin-binding protein - Limited human peptide data - most evidence comes from gene therapy trials, not injectable peptide studies Evidence Strength: LOW - While the biology is well-characterized and gene therapy trials show promise, there are no completed large-scale RCTs with injectable follistatin peptide. Most human data comes from gene delivery studies for muscular dystrophy. -------|-------| | Generic name | Follistatin | | Gene symbol | FST | | Alternative names | Activin-binding protein, FST-344, FST-315 | | Chemical class | Cysteine-rich glycoprotein | | Molecular weight | 32-44 kDa (isoform-dependent) | | Amino acids | 288-344 (isoform-dependent) | | Cysteine content | 36 cysteines (unusually high) | Isoforms | Isoform | Amino Acids | Characteristics | Primary Location | |---------|-------------|-----------------|------------------| | FS-288 | 288 | Cell surface binding; high activin affinity | Tissues, ovary | | FS-303 | 303 | Proteolytic cleavage product of FS-315 | Serum | | FS-315 | 315 | Circulating form; acidic C-terminal tail | Serum (predominant) | | FS-344 | 344 | Precursor protein; post-translational cleavage to FS-315 | Gene therapy construct | Key distinction: FS-288 has approximately 10-fold higher affinity for activin compared to FS-315 and binds to cell surface heparan sulfate proteoglycans. FS-315 is the primary circulating form with lower tissue binding, making it preferable for systemic gene therapy applications where off-target pituitary effects are undesirable. Discovery & Development Timeline | Year | Milestone | Significance | |------|-----------|--------------| | 1987 | Follistatin isolated | Initially characterized as FSH-release inhibiting substance from porcine ovarian follicular fluid PMID: 3120188 | | 1990 | Structural characterization | Full amino acid sequence determined; cysteine-rich structure identified | | 1997 | Myostatin identified | McPherron et al. discover myostatin as negative regulator of muscle mass PMID: 9139826 | | 2001 | Follistatin-myostatin link | Lee SJ demonstrates follistatin binds and inhibits myostatin PMID: 11460166 | | 2005 | Crystal structure solved | Thompson et al. solve follistatin-activin complex structure PMID: 16198295 | | 2008 | AAV-follistatin in mice | Long-term muscle enhancement demonstrated PMID: 18193046 | | 2009 | Primate studies | AAV1-FS344 shows muscle gains in macaques without adverse effects PMID: 19293773 | | 2012 | First human gene therapy trial | NCT01519349 initiated for Becker muscular dystrophy | | 2015 | Phase 1/2a BMD results | Follistatin gene therapy improves ambulation PMID: 25322757 | | 2017 | Inclusion body myositis trial | Functional improvements reported PMID: 28279643 | | 2019 | DF-3 peptide development | Novel 14-mer myostatin-specific inhibitory peptide derived from follistatin PMID: 31740020 | | 2024-2025 | Ongoing research | DMD trials, aging/sarcopenia studies continue | Why Follistatin Generates Interest Myostatin is nature's "muscle limiter" - it prevents muscles from growing beyond a certain size. In animals and rare humans with myostatin mutations, muscles grow dramatically larger and stronger. Follistatin naturally inhibits myostatin, making it an attractive target for: 1. Muscular dystrophies - Where muscle weakness causes progressive disability 2. Sarcopenia - Age-related muscle loss 3. Cachexia - Muscle wasting from cancer or chronic illness 4. Performance enhancement - Illegal use for muscle building (WADA banned) --- Mechanism of Action How Follistatin Works (Plain English) Think of follistatin as a "molecular sponge" that soaks up proteins that would otherwise tell your muscles to stop growing: 1. Absorbs myostatin - Myostatin is a brake pedal for muscle growth. Follistatin binds to myostatin and prevents it from reaching muscle cells, effectively releasing the brake. 2. Absorbs activin - Activin is another TGF-beta protein that can cause muscle wasting. Follistatin neutralizes activin the same way it neutralizes myostatin. 3. Blocks receptor binding - By binding these proteins, follistatin prevents them from attaching to their receptors (ActRIIA/ActRIIB) on muscle cells. 4. Result - With less myostatin/activin signaling, muscles receive fewer "stop growing" signals and can become larger and stronger. Important caveat: This doesn't mean taking follistatin automatically builds muscle. The body's response depends on many factors, and injectable peptide effects in humans are not well-established. --- Molecular Mechanism (Scientific Detail) Follistatin is a high-affinity antagonist of several TGF-beta superfamily members. It functions by sequestering ligands and preventing their interaction with cell surface receptors. Structural basis of inhibition: Follistatin (2 molecules) encircle ligand | Buries 1/3 of ligand surface residues | Blocks both Type I and Type II receptor binding sites | Ligand cannot signal through ActRIIA/ActRIIB | Reduced Smad2/3 phosphorylation | Decreased muscle-limiting gene expression Crystal structure insights PMID: 16198295, 16482217 : - Two follistatin molecules wrap around one activin/myostatin dimer - The N-terminal domain (ND) blocks the type I receptor binding site - Follistatin domain 1 (FSD1) and FSD2 block the type II receptor binding site - The binding is essentially irreversible under physiological conditions (Kd 45 pM for activin) Target Ligands and Binding Affinities | Ligand | Binding Affinity | Biological Effect of Inhibition | |--------|------------------|--------------------------------| | Activin A | Very high (Kd 45 pM) | Reduces FSH suppression, reduces muscle catabolism | | Myostatin (GDF-8) | High | Promotes muscle hypertrophy and hyperplasia | | GDF-11 | Moderate-high | May affect aging processes | | BMP-2, -4, -6, -7 | Lower | Effects on bone and other tissues | | BMP-15 | Moderate | Effects on fertility | IGF-1 Receptor Pathway Requirement An important finding is that follistatin-induced muscle hypertrophy requires the IGF-1 receptor pathway PMID: 22087027, 26219865 : Follistatin expression | Myostatin/activin inhibition | Requires intact IGF-1R/Akt/mTOR pathway | IGF-IR inhibition reduces hypertrophy by 63% Akt inhibition reduces hypertrophy by 65% Rapamycin reduces hypertrophy by 36% Paradox: Follistatin increases muscle mass despite downregulating muscle IGF-1 expression. The effect requires the presence of circulating insulin/IGF-1, but follistatin does not increase muscle sensitivity to IGF-1. Supraphysiologic Effects When follistatin is overexpressed (via gene therapy or transgenic models): | Model | Result | Citation | |-------|--------|----------| | Transgenic mice | 2-3 fold increase in muscle mass | PMID: 11460166 | | FST overexpression + myostatin knockout | 4-fold increase in muscle mass | PMID: 11460166 | | AAV-FST in cynomolgus macaques | Significant quadriceps hypertrophy lasting >15 months | PMID: 19293773 | | AAV-FST in mdx mice | Improved muscle mass and reduced pathology | PMID: 19108572 | Key insight: The fact that follistatin causes additional muscle growth even in myostatin-null animals indicates it inhibits other muscle-limiting factors beyond myostatin alone. --- Research Summary Evidence Overview | Category | Count | Notes | |----------|-------|-------| | Human gene therapy trials | 3-4 | BMD, IBM, DMD (ongoing) | | Human peptide studies | 0 | No published injectable peptide trials | | Primate studies | 2+ | AAV delivery; safety and efficacy | | Rodent preclinical | 25+ | Mechanism, efficacy, safety | | In vitro studies | 10+ | Binding, pathway characterization | | Systematic reviews | 3+ | Myostatin inhibition field | | Total sources | 38+ | 22 open access | Key Evidence Highlights 1. Gene therapy efficacy: AAV1-FS344 delivered to muscle improves function in Becker muscular dystrophy patients PMID: 25322757, 28279643 2. Primate validation: Long-term FS344 expression in macaques produces durable muscle hypertrophy without adverse effects on reproduction or organs PMID: 19293773 3. Mechanism established: Crystal structures confirm follistatin encircles and neutralizes myostatin/activin PMID: 16198295 4. IGF-1R dependence: Hypertrophic effect requires functional IGF-1 receptor signaling PMID: 22087027 5. Neuromuscular junction: In aged mice, follistatin improves NMJ innervation and transmission PMID: 33964607 Critical Limitations - No injectable peptide trials: All human efficacy data comes from gene therapy (viral vector delivery) - Black market products unvalidated: Commercial FS-344/FS-315 products lack quality assurance - Limited long-term safety data: Gene therapy trials provide years of follow-up; peptide effects unknown - Translation uncertainty: Gene therapy produces sustained local expression; injectable peptide pharmacokinetics differ entirely --- Key Human Trials Phase 1/2a Becker Muscular Dystrophy Trial Study: NCT01519349 - Follistatin Gene Transfer to Patients With Becker Muscular Dystrophy PMID: 25322757 | Parameter | Value | |-----------|-------| | Design | Open-label, dose-escalation | | Population | 6 males with BMD | | Intervention | AAV1.CMV.FS344, bilateral quadriceps injection | | Doses | Cohort 1: 3 x 10^11 vg/kg/leg; Cohort 2: 6 x 10^11 vg/kg/leg | | Primary outcome | 6-minute walk test (6MWT) | | Follow-up | 2 years | Results: | Patient | Dose Cohort | 6MWT Change | Histology | |---------|-------------|-------------|-----------| | 01 | Low | +58 meters | Improved | | 02 | Low | +125 meters | Improved | | 03 | Low | No change | Variable | | 04 | High | No change | Improved | | 05 | High | +108 meters | Improved | | 06 | High | +29 meters | Improved | Histological findings: - Reduced endomysial fibrosis - Reduced central nucleation - More normal fiber size distribution - Muscle hypertrophy, especially at higher dose Safety: No adverse effects encountered. No abnormal changes in key organ systems. Sporadic Inclusion Body Myositis Trial Study: Follistatin Gene Therapy for Sporadic Inclusion Body Myositis PMID: 28279643 | Parameter | Value | |-----------|-------| | Design | Open-label with external controls | | Population | 6 sIBM patients | | Intervention | AAV1.CMV.huFS344, 6 x 10^11 vg/kg to quadriceps | | Primary outcome | 6-minute walk test | | Controls | 8 untreated, age/gender-matched sIBM patients | Results: | Measure | Treated (n=6) | Untreated (n=8) | P-value | |---------|---------------|-----------------|---------| | 6MWT annualized change | +56.0 m/year | -25.8 m/year | p = 0.01 | | Histology | Decreased fibrosis, improved regeneration | N/A | — | Controversy: A critical commentary PMID: 28927986 noted: - Study design could not definitively support efficacy claims - Different primary outcome reported than registered on ClinicalTrials.gov - Post-hoc analyses may bias efficacy evidence - However, authors provided detailed rebuttal PMID: 28939086 Ongoing/Planned Trials | Trial | Phase | Population | Status | |-------|-------|------------|--------| | NCT01519349 (Extension) | 1/2a | DMD patients | Ongoing | | Multiple preclinical | — | Sarcopenia, cachexia models | Research phase | --- Preclinical Evidence Primate Studies AAV1-FS344 in Cynomolgus Macaques PMID: 19293773 : | Parameter | Finding | |-----------|---------| | Model | Adult cynomolgus macaques | | Delivery | AAV1-FS344 to quadriceps | | Duration | >15 months follow-up | | Muscle effects | Pronounced, durable increases in size and strength | | Histology | Fiber hypertrophy without pathology | | Cardiac | No adverse effects | | Reproductive | No interference with reproductive physiology | | Organs | No treatment-related pathologic changes | | Immunogenicity | No neutralizing antibodies to FS344 transgene | Significance: This study demonstrated that FS344 gene therapy is safe and effective in a primate model closely resembling humans. Rodent Studies | Model | Key Finding | Citation | |-------|-------------|----------| | mdx mice (DMD model) | FS gene delivery increases muscle mass, reduces pathology | PMID: 19108572 | | Wild-type mice | AAV1-FS344 enhances muscle >2 years without adverse effects | PMID: 18193046 | | Aged mice (24-27 mo) | FS overexpression improves NMJ innervation and transmission | PMID: 33964607 | | Denervation model | Pre-treatment preserves muscle; treatment at time of injury does not prevent wasting | PMID: 26601779 | | Transgenic overexpression | 2-3x muscle mass increase; 4x in myostatin-null background | PMID: 11460166 | Follistatin-Derived Peptides FS I-I (Myostatin-specific peptide) PMID: 19108572 : - Transgenic expression of follistatin-derived peptide FS I-I - mdx/FS I-I mice show increased muscle mass and reduced cell infiltration - Muscle strength recovery demonstrated - Indicates myostatin-specific inhibition has therapeutic potential DF-3 (14-mer peptide) PMID: 31740020 : - Novel peptide derived from follistatin N-terminal domain - Effectively inhibits myostatin but NOT activin A or TGF-beta1 - Intramuscular injection significantly increases skeletal muscle mass in mice - Represents potential for more targeted myostatin inhibition In Vitro Studies | Cell Type | Finding | Citation | |-----------|---------|----------| | Tendon fibroblasts | FAK-paxillin activation, enhanced migration | Taiwan studies | | Myoblasts | Enhanced differentiation and fusion | Multiple | | C2C12 cells | Dose-dependent hypertrophy markers | Multiple | --- Safety Profile Gene Therapy Safety Data Human gene therapy trials provide the most relevant safety information: Phase 1/2a BMD Trial PMID: 25322757 : - No immediate or late adverse effects related to gene transfer - No treatment-related pathologic changes in major organ systems - Serum chemistries remained normal - No effect on reproductive capacity in preclinical extension Inclusion Body Myositis Trial PMID: 28279643 : - Well tolerated by all patients - No serious adverse events reported - Histologic examination showed no concerning changes Preclinical Safety Summary | Parameter | Finding | Model | |-----------|---------|-------| | Acute toxicity | None observed | Rodent, primate | | Cardiac effects | No pathology | Mouse, primate | | Reproductive | No interference | Mouse, primate (male and female) | | Immunogenicity | Low; no neutralizing antibodies | Primate | | Long-term (2+ years) | Well tolerated | Mouse | Theoretical Concerns 1. FSH Suppression: - Follistatin was originally isolated as FSH release-inhibiting substance - FS-288 has high affinity for activin and could affect pituitary-gonadal axis - FS-315 (used in gene therapy) has 10-fold lower activin affinity, mitigating this concern - Human trials have not shown reproductive dysfunction 2. Off-Target TGF-beta Effects: - Follistatin binds multiple TGF-beta ligands (BMPs, GDF-11) - Potential effects on bone, wound healing, other tissues - Not observed in clinical trials to date 3. Elevated Circulating Follistatin: - One study found elevated circulating follistatin associated with increased mortality and cardiometabolic disorders - However, this was observational; causation not established - Gene therapy produces local, not systemic, elevation Injectable Peptide Safety - Unknown Critical gap: The safety profile of injectable follistatin peptides (FS-344, FS-315) in humans is not established : - No published human trials with injectable peptide forms - Pharmacokinetics in humans not characterized - Black market products may vary in purity and identity - WADA study found some products do not contain claimed follistatin --- Pharmacokinetics Native Follistatin Pharmacokinetics Data from animal studies: | Parameter | Value | Notes | |-----------|-------|-------| | Initial half-life | 4 minutes | Rat IV study PMID: 8690405 | | Terminal half-life | 130 minutes | Rat IV study | | Primary clearance | Hepatic | 9% remains in liver after 2 hours | | Liver retention | Elevated 72 hours | Then declines markedly | Implications: Native follistatin has very poor pharmacokinetic properties for systemic therapy - rapid clearance limits exposure. Engineered Follistatin Variants Research has produced follistatin variants with improved pharmacokinetics PMID: 23249626 : | Modification | Effect | Fold Improvement | |--------------|--------|------------------| | FST315-Fc fusion | Extended half-life | 100-fold | | Removed heparan sulfate binding | Reduced tissue sequestration | — | | Combined modifications | Improved exposure | 1600-fold | Gene Therapy Pharmacokinetics AAV-delivered follistatin provides fundamentally different kinetics: | Parameter | Gene Therapy | Injectable Peptide | |-----------|--------------|-------------------| | Duration | Months to years | Minutes to hours | | Expression | Local (injected muscle) | Systemic | | Re-dosing | Limited by immunogenicity | Theoretically possible | | Peak levels | Sustained plateau | Rapid peak/trough | Key point: The success of follistatin gene therapy cannot be extrapolated to predict injectable peptide effects. The pharmacokinetic profiles are entirely different. Research Administration Approaches | Route | Model | Context | |-------|-------|---------| | Intramuscular AAV injection | Human, primate | Gene therapy trials | | Intravenous (IV) | Rat | PK studies | | Subcutaneous | Mouse | Follistatin-288 daily dosing | | Intramuscular peptide | Mouse | DF-3 peptide studies | --- Regulatory Status United States (FDA) | Product Type | Status | Notes | |--------------|--------|-------| | Peptide (FS-344, FS-315) | Not approved | No NDA filed; not an approved drug | | Dietary supplement | Prohibited | Cannot be sold as supplement | | Gene therapy (AAV1-FS344) | Investigational | IND for clinical trials only | FDA position: Follistatin peptides are not approved for any indication. Products sold as "research chemicals" circumvent FDA authority but are illegal to sell for human use. International Regulatory Status | Region | Status | |--------|--------| | EU (EMA) | Not authorized | | UK (MHRA) | Not authorized | | Japan (PMDA) | Not authorized | | Australia (TGA) | Not listed | | Canada | Not authorized | World Anti-Doping Agency (WADA) Follistatin is explicitly prohibited under WADA regulations: Category: S4.4 - Myostatin-binding proteins (e.g., follistatin, myostatin propeptide) Status: Prohibited at all times (in and out of competition) Detection: WADA has funded administration studies of black market FS-344 and FS-315 products to develop detection methods Consequences: Athletes testing positive for follistatin face significant sanctions. Black Market Products WADA-funded research found significant quality issues with black market follistatin: | Finding | Implication | |---------|-------------| | Only 9/17 "follistatin" supplements contained follistatin | Widespread fraud | | Some contained other growth-promoting peptides | Unpredictable effects | | Purity highly variable | Unknown potency | | Identity not assured | Safety risk | --- Related Peptides Myostatin Inhibitor Comparison | Agent | Type | Mechanism | Development Status | |-------|------|-----------|-------------------| | Follistatin | Glycoprotein | Binds myostatin, activins, BMPs | Gene therapy trials | | Myostatin propeptide | Peptide | Binds mature myostatin | Preclinical | | Bimagrumab | Antibody | Blocks ActRII receptor | Phase 2/3 completed | | Domagrozumab | Antibody | Anti-myostatin | Phase 2 (discontinued) | | Stamulumab | Antibody | Anti-myostatin | Discontinued | | ACE-031 | Trap protein | Soluble ActRIIB-Fc | Discontinued (safety) | | DF-3 | Peptide | Myostatin-specific; follistatin-derived | Preclinical | Follistatin vs IGF-1 | Aspect | Follistatin | IGF-1 | |--------|-------------|-------| | Mechanism | Inhibits muscle-limiting signals | Direct anabolic signaling | | Receptor | None (sequesters ligands) | IGF-1R | | Effect size | 2-4x muscle mass (transgenic) | 1.5-2x (high doses) | | Dependence | Requires functional IGF-1R | Direct action | | Safety signals | Low (gene therapy data) | Hypoglycemia, theoretical cancer risk | | Human trials | Gene therapy only | Multiple (various indications) | Follistatin vs MGF (Mechano Growth Factor) | Aspect | Follistatin | MGF | |--------|-------------|-----| | Origin | FST gene | IGF-1 splice variant | | Mechanism | Myostatin/activin inhibition | IGF-1R activation (local) | | Evidence level | Gene therapy trials | Preclinical only | | Stability | Requires modification for stability | Very unstable natively | | Human data | Limited but present | Essentially none | --- Current Research Directions Active Research Areas 1. Duchenne Muscular Dystrophy - Phase 1/2a trials with AAV1.CMV.FS344 ongoing - Building on BMD success with younger patients 2. Sarcopenia and Aging - Preclinical studies show improved NMJ function in aged mice PMID: 33964607 - Exercise and follistatin interactions being studied 2025 - Compensatory FST upregulation observed in severe muscle wasting 3. Follistatin-Derived Peptides - DF-3: 14-mer peptide that specifically inhibits myostatin without affecting activin/TGF-beta PMID: 31740020 - May avoid off-target effects of full follistatin 4. Improved Delivery Systems - Fc-fusion proteins for extended half-life - Alternative AAV serotypes for broader tissue distribution - Local vs systemic delivery optimization 5. Combination Approaches - Follistatin + exercise - Follistatin + other anabolic agents - Gene therapy + rehabilitation Key Unknowns | Question | Relevance | |----------|-----------| | Optimal dosing for injectable peptides | No human PK data | | Long-term safety beyond 5 years | Gene therapy follow-up ongoing | | Efficacy of peptide vs gene therapy | Different PK profiles | | Cancer risk with chronic myostatin inhibition | Theoretical; not observed | | Effects on cardiac muscle | Limited but reassuring data | | Combination with other therapies | Not systematically studied | --- Citation Table Human Clinical Trials | Citation | PMID | Year | Type | Key Finding | Access | |----------|------|------|------|-------------|--------| | Mendell JR et al.
