name: FOXO4-DRI
summary: A D-retro-inverso peptide designed to disrupt the FOXO4-p53 interaction, selectively eliminating senescent cells (senolytic). Preclinical studies in aged mice demonstrated restored fitness, fur density, and renal function. No human clinical trials conducted. More selective than BCL-2 inhibitors but remains unproven in humans.
content: Overview Overview Quick Summary - D-retro-inverso peptide designed to disrupt the FOXO4-p53 protein interaction - Senolytic mechanism - selectively induces apoptosis in senescent cells while sparing normal cells - Not FDA approved - no human clinical trials conducted - Preclinical only - all efficacy data from aged mouse studies - Protease-resistant design - D-amino acid configuration provides stability against enzymatic degradation - More selective than BCL-2 inhibitors - potentially improved safety profile over compounds like ABT-737/navitoclax Evidence Strength: Low - While the scientific rationale is compelling and animal data shows consistent effects on senescent cell clearance and functional rejuvenation, there are no published human clinical trials for FOXO4-DRI. Identity & Origins | Property | Value | |----------|-------| | Generic name | FOXO4-DRI (FOXO4-D-Retro-Inverso) | | Sequence | D-retro-inverso version of FOXO4 residues 459-503 | | Peptide length | 45 amino acids (D-configuration) | | Molecular weight | Approximately 5.5 kDa | | Class | D-retro-inverso therapeutic peptide / Senolytic | | Target | FOXO4-p53 protein-protein interaction | | Origin | Erasmus University Medical Center, Rotterdam, Netherlands | | Developer | Peter de Keizer and colleagues | | First published | 2017 (Cell) | Critical Context FOXO4-DRI represents a novel approach to senolytic therapy - targeting the specific mechanism by which senescent cells resist apoptosis rather than broadly inhibiting anti-apoptotic proteins. The D-retro-inverso design confers exceptional protease resistance, potentially enabling therapeutic peptide stability in vivo. However, the compound remains entirely in preclinical development with no human data. ---|-----------|--------------| | 2011 | Senescent cell burden linked to aging pathology | Baker et al. demonstrate removing p16-positive cells extends healthspan in progeroid mice | | 2015 | First senolytic drugs identified | Dasatinib + quercetin shown to kill senescent cells | | 2016 | BCL-2 family inhibitors tested as senolytics | Navitoclax (ABT-263) shows senolytic activity but with platelet toxicity | | 2017 | FOXO4-DRI published | De Keizer et al. Cell paper demonstrates selective senolysis via FOXO4-p53 disruption | | 2017 | In vivo efficacy demonstrated | Aged mice show restored fitness, fur density, and renal function | | 2020 | Additional mechanism studies | Further characterization of FOXO4-p53 binding interface | | 2023 | Follow-up studies published | Replication and extension of original findings | | 2025 | Structural insights published | Nature Communications paper on FOXO4-p53 complex structure | | Current | No human trials | Remains in preclinical stage only | --- Mechanism of Action How FOXO4-DRI Works (Plain English) To understand FOXO4-DRI, you first need to understand why senescent cells are problematic and why they resist dying: 1. What are senescent cells? - When cells become damaged or reach their replication limit, they can enter a "zombie-like" state called senescence. They stop dividing but don't die. Instead, they secrete inflammatory factors (called SASP - senescence-associated secretory phenotype) that damage surrounding tissue and contribute to aging. 2. Why don't senescent cells die naturally? - Normal damaged cells would undergo apoptosis (programmed cell death). But senescent cells have hijacked survival mechanisms. Specifically, a protein called FOXO4 binds to p53 (the "guardian of the genome" that normally triggers cell death) and sequesters it in the nucleus, preventing p53 from initiating apoptosis. 3. How FOXO4-DRI works - FOXO4-DRI is designed to interfere with this survival trick. It's a modified version of part of the FOXO4 protein that competes with natural FOXO4 for binding to p53. By blocking the FOXO4-p53 interaction, FOXO4-DRI releases p53 to do its job - triggering apoptosis specifically in senescent cells. 4. Why does this only kill senescent cells? - Normal, healthy cells don't rely on the FOXO4-p53 interaction for survival. Only senescent cells have become dependent on sequestering p53 via FOXO4. So when FOXO4-DRI disrupts this interaction, only the senescent cells are vulnerable. The D-retro-inverso advantage: Normal peptides are rapidly degraded by enzymes (proteases) in the body. FOXO4-DRI uses D-amino acids (mirror images of normal L-amino acids) arranged in reverse order. This makes it "invisible" to proteases while maintaining the same binding surface to interact with p53. --- Scientific Mechanism Target: FOXO4-p53 Protein-Protein Interaction In senescent cells, FOXO4 forms a complex with p53 in the nucleus: Senescent Cell Survival Mechanism: DNA damage/stress → p53 activation ↓ FOXO4 binds p53 → Nuclear sequestration ↓ p53 prevented from mitochondrial translocation ↓ Apoptosis blocked → Cell survives despite damage FOXO4-DRI Mechanism: FOXO4-DRI enters cell (cell-penetrating peptide) ↓ Competes with endogenous FOXO4 for p53 binding ↓ FOXO4-DRI binds p53 (decoy effect) ↓ Native FOXO4-p53 complex disrupted ↓ p53 released from nuclear sequestration ↓ p53 translocates to mitochondria ↓ Intrinsic apoptosis pathway activated → Senescent cell death D-Retro-Inverso Design The peptide engineering strategy provides key advantages: | Design Feature | Benefit | |----------------|---------| | D-amino acids | Resistant to proteolytic degradation | | Retro sequence | Reversed N-to-C direction | | Inverso configuration | Side chain orientation preserved | | Net effect | Same binding surface, exceptional stability | Structural considerations: - Maintains functional mimicry of native FOXO4 binding domain - Cell-penetrating properties enable cytoplasmic/nuclear delivery - Predicted to have extended half-life compared to L-peptides Selectivity for Senescent Cells | Cell State | FOXO4-p53 Dependence | FOXO4-DRI Effect | |------------|----------------------|------------------| | Normal proliferating | Low | Minimal effect | | Quiescent | Low | Minimal effect | | Senescent | High | Apoptosis induced | | Cancer (some) | Variable | Under investigation | This selectivity profile distinguishes FOXO4-DRI from BCL-2 family inhibitors (like navitoclax/ABT-737), which affect all cells expressing BCL-2 proteins, leading to on-target toxicity particularly in platelets. --- Preclinical Evidence Landmark Study: De Keizer et al. Cell 2017 Publication: Baar MP, Brandt RMC, Putavet DA, et al. "Targeted Apoptosis of Senescent Cells Restores Tissue Homeostasis in Response to Chemotoxicity and Aging." Cell. 2017;169(1):132-147.e16. PMID: 28340339 ( | Parameter | Details | |-----------|---------| | Models | Fast-aging XpdTTD/TTD mice; naturally aged wild-type mice | | Treatment | FOXO4-DRI peptide, three times per week | | Duration | 3-4 weeks | | Key endpoints | Physical function, fur density, organ pathology, senescence markers | Key Results in Aged Mice: | Outcome | Finding | Significance | |---------|---------|--------------| | Running wheel activity | Significantly increased | Improved physical fitness | | Fur density | Restored | Visible rejuvenation phenotype | | Renal function | Improved | Reduced age-related decline | | Senescent cell burden | Reduced (p16, p21 markers) | Target engagement confirmed | | SASP factors | Decreased | Reduced inflammatory secretome | | Survival | No adverse mortality | Short-term tolerability | Mechanism Validation Studies | Observation | Method | Implication | |-------------|--------|-------------| | FOXO4-p53 co-localization disrupted | Immunofluorescence | Target engagement confirmed | | p53 mitochondrial translocation | Subcellular fractionation | Mechanistic pathway validated | | Caspase activation | Western blot | Apoptosis pathway activated | | SA-beta-gal+ cell reduction | Histochemistry | Senescent cell clearance | | Normal cell sparing | Multiple assays | Selectivity demonstrated | In Vitro Studies | Cell Type | Senescence Induction | FOXO4-DRI Effect | |-----------|----------------------|------------------| | IMR90 fibroblasts | Irradiation | Selective killing of senescent cells | | WI38 fibroblasts | Replicative | Apoptosis in senescent only | | BJ fibroblasts | Oncogene-induced | Effective clearance | | Primary hepatocytes | Multiple methods | Similar selectivity | Comparison to Other Senolytics | Senolytic | Mechanism | Selectivity | Key Limitation | |-----------|-----------|-------------|----------------| | FOXO4-DRI | FOXO4-p53 disruption | High | No human data; complex synthesis | | Navitoclax (ABT-737/263) | BCL-2/BCL-xL inhibition | Moderate | Severe thrombocytopenia | | Dasatinib + Quercetin | Multi-target kinase/flavonoid | Moderate | Drug interactions; limited potency | | Fisetin | Flavonoid (mechanism unclear) | Low-moderate | Poor bioavailability; weak effect | FOXO4-DRI advantages: More selective mechanism, potentially better safety profile than BCL-2 inhibitors FOXO4-DRI disadvantages: Complex peptide synthesis, stability/delivery challenges, no human data --- Safety Profile Critical Limitation: No Human Safety Data The safety profile of FOXO4-DRI in humans is completely unknown. No human subjects have received this compound in controlled clinical settings. Preclinical Safety Observations | Finding | Source | Assessment | |---------|--------|------------| | Well-tolerated in mice | De Keizer 2017 | Short-term reassuring | | No acute toxicity | Original publication | Limited duration | | No impact on normal cells | In vitro studies | Mechanistically expected | | Normal tissue architecture preserved | Histopathology | No overt tissue damage | | No mortality signal | Animal studies | Short-term only | Theoretical Concerns 1. Long-term Effects Unknown - Chronic dosing effects not characterized - Impact on tissue stem cell pools unclear - Potential for immunogenicity with repeated dosing 2. Off-Target p53 Effects - p53 is tumor suppressor - disruption could theoretically promote cancer - However, FOXO4-DRI does not inhibit p53; it releases p53 from sequestration - Net effect may actually enhance tumor suppression 3. Immune System Impact - Some immune cells are senescent or semi-senescent - Impact on immune function not fully characterized - Could affect immunosurveillance 4. Tissue-Specific Effects - Senescent cells may have beneficial functions in some contexts - Wound healing roles of senescent cells - Tissue-specific dosing requirements unknown 5. Immunogenicity - Peptide therapeutics can generate antibodies - D-retro-inverso design may reduce but not eliminate immunogenicity - Repeated dosing effects unknown Product Quality Concerns FOXO4-DRI available through research suppliers: - Not pharmaceutical grade - Complex synthesis prone to errors - Purity variable - Identity verification limited - Stability/storage requirements critical --- Pharmacokinetics Known Parameters (Preclinical Only) | Parameter | Value | Notes | |-----------|-------|-------| | Molecular weight | 5.5 kDa | 45-mer D-peptide | | Cell penetration | Demonstrated | Enters cells and nucleus | | Protease resistance | High | D-amino acids avoid degradation | | Route in studies | Intraperitoneal injection | Systemic delivery in mice | Unknown in Humans | Parameter | Status | |-----------|--------| | Bioavailability | Not characterized | | Half-life | Unknown (expected longer than L-peptides) | | Volume of distribution | Unknown | | Metabolism | Unknown (likely minimal due to D-configuration) | | Excretion | Unknown (likely renal for peptide) | | Protein binding | Unknown | | Tissue distribution | Partially characterized in mice | Theoretical Pharmacokinetic Advantages The D-retro-inverso design should confer: - Extended circulating half-life (no protease degradation) - Potential for less frequent dosing - Possible oral bioavailability (speculative) However, these advantages remain unvalidated in controlled pharmacokinetic studies. --- Regulatory Status | Region | Status | Notes | |--------|--------|-------| | USA (FDA) | Not approved | No IND application on record | | EU (EMA) | Not approved | Not authorized | | UK (MHRA) | Not approved | Not licensed | | Australia (TGA) | Not approved | Not listed | | Japan (PMDA) | Not approved | Not authorized | No regulatory agency has approved FOXO4-DRI for any indication. Clinical Development Status - No Investigational New Drug (IND) application filed - No clinical trials registered on ClinicalTrials.gov - No European clinical trial registry entries - Remains research tool/preclinical compound --- Open Questions Key Unknowns Requiring Resolution 1. Human efficacy - Does FOXO4-DRI clear senescent cells in humans? 2. Human pharmacokinetics - What are absorption, distribution, metabolism, excretion parameters? 3. Optimal dosing - What dose and frequency achieve senolytic effect without adverse events? 4. Long-term safety - What are consequences of chronic senescent cell depletion? 5. Immunogenicity - Will antibodies develop with repeated dosing? 6. Delivery optimization - Can oral or alternative delivery routes be developed? 7. Manufacturing scale-up - Can D-retro-inverso peptide be manufactured at scale economically? Research Gaps | Gap | Current Status | Impact | |-----|----------------|--------| | Human PK data | None | Cannot predict human dosing | | Human safety data | None | Unknown risk profile | | Chronic toxicology | Limited | Long-term effects unknown | | Drug interactions | Unstudied | Cannot advise on combinations | | Biomarkers of response | Limited | Hard to confirm senolysis | | Optimal patient population | Unknown | Who would benefit most? | Competing Approaches The senolytic field is advancing rapidly with multiple approaches: - Small molecule senolytics entering clinical trials (UBX0101, though failed in Phase 2) - Senolytic CAR-T approaches - Prodrug senolytics activated by senescent cell enzymes - Second-generation BCL-2 inhibitors with improved selectivity FOXO4-DRI's niche may be superseded if small molecules prove effective and safer. --- Citation Table Primary Research | Citation | PMID | Year | Type | Key Finding | Access | |----------|------|------|------|-------------|--------| | Baar MP et al. name: GHK-Cu
summary: A naturally occurring copper-binding tripeptide with wound healing and skin regeneration properties. Extensively studied globally with moderate-to-high evidence for cosmetic applications.
content: Overview Overview Quick Summary - Naturally occurring tripeptide (Gly-His-Lys) that binds copper - Endogenous to human plasma - levels decline with age - Primarily topical use in cosmetic and wound healing applications - Moderate-to-high evidence for skin regeneration and wound healing - Found in many skincare products as active ingredient - Extensively researched globally - USA, China, South Korea, Japan, Europe Evidence Strength: Moderate-High - Strong evidence base from multiple RCTs, systematic reviews, and meta-analysis for topical wound healing and cosmetic applications. Identity & Origins | Property | Value | |----------|-------| | Generic name | GHK-Cu (Copper tripeptide-1) | | Sequence | Glycyl-L-Histidyl-L-Lysine | | Complex | With copper (Cu2+) ion | | Class | Copper peptide | | Discovery | Loren Pickart (1970s) | Natural Occurrence GHK-Cu is found naturally in human plasma, urine, and saliva. Plasma levels decline significantly with age (200 ng/mL at age 20 to 80 ng/mL at age 60). Gene Expression Modulation GHK-Cu is capable of up- and downregulating at least 4,000 human genes , essentially resetting DNA expression toward a healthier state. This is a remarkably broad effect for such a small molecule. Key Gene Categories Affected: | Effect | Genes/Pathways | What This Means | |--------|----------------|-----------------| | Upregulated | Collagen (Types I, III), decorin, glycosaminoglycans, TGF-B pathway | Increases production of structural proteins that give skin firmness and elasticity | | Downregulated | 70% of genes overexpressed in metastatic colon cancer (at 1uM) | May help normalize cells that are behaving abnormally | | Anti-inflammatory | Reduced IL-6, TNF-a; modulated NF-kB signaling | Calms down chronic inflammation that accelerates aging | | Stem cell | Upregulates p63 and integrins in epidermal stem cells | May help skin maintain its ability to regenerate | Wound Healing Cascade When tissue is injured, GHK-Cu is naturally released from damaged collagen by a protein called SPARC. It then acts as a chemical signal that attracts repair cells to the wound: Cellular Recruitment: Tissue Injury -> SPARC protein releases GHK from collagen | GHK-Cu acts as chemoattractant | |-- Mast cells recruited (immune response) |-- Macrophages recruited (clean up debris) |-- Fibroblasts migrate (produce new collagen) |-- Endothelial cells migrate (form new blood vessels) | Tissue repair proteins released Collagen Production: - Increases collagen production up to 70% in laboratory studies - Enhances Type I collagen (provides structural support) and Type III collagen (provides flexibility during repair) - Copper serves as essential cofactor for lysyl oxidase and lysyl hydroxylase , enzymes that crosslink collagen fibers together like rebar reinforcing concrete Essential Role of Copper The copper ion (Cu2+) is required for biological activity. GHK without copper doesn't show the same healing effects: - Lysyl oxidase - Crosslinks collagen and elastin fibers together for strength - Superoxide dismutase (SOD) - Neutralizes damaging free radicals - Cytochrome c oxidase - Powers cellular energy production - Only GHK-Cu, not GHK alone, shows wound healing effects Antioxidant Properties GHK-Cu acts as a powerful antioxidant, protecting cells from oxidative stress: - Blocks formation of reactive oxygen/carbonyl species (the harmful molecules that damage DNA and proteins) - Detoxifies toxic lipid peroxidation products (acrolein), byproducts of fat breakdown that cause cellular damage - Protects skin cells (keratinocytes) from lethal UVB radiation damage - Blocks liver damage from toxic chemical radicals Natural Source & Decline with Age GHK-Cu is released from collagen after tissue injury via SPARC protein . - Plasma levels: approximately 200 ng/mL at age 20, dropping to approximately 80 ng/mL at age 60 (a 60% decline) - This age-related decline may explain why older skin heals more slowly and is more prone to damage Pharmacokinetic Limitations - Half-life : 30 minutes in plasma - Skin absorption : Poor due to high hydrophilicity - Clearance : 95% excreted rapidly after dermal injection --- Research Summary Evidence Overview | Category | Count | Notes | |----------|-------|-------| | Human clinical/RCT | 10 | Wound healing, cosmetic, comparative | | Reviews | 9 | Including systematic reviews and meta-analysis | | Preclinical | 16 | Mechanism, delivery, biomaterials | | Total sources | 35 | 18 open access | Global Research Distribution | Region | Focus Areas | Key Contributions | |--------|-------------|-------------------| | USA | Foundational research, gene expression, clinical dermatology | Discovery (Pickart), gene modulation studies, cosmetic RCTs, meta-analysis | | China | Wound healing biomaterials, tissue engineering, drug delivery | Hydrogels, nanofibers, 3D scaffolds, diabetic wound applications | | South Korea | K-beauty applications, delivery systems, post-procedure recovery | Clinical anti-aging studies, formulation science, laser recovery | | Japan | Mechanism studies, hair growth, pigmentation | Wnt/B-catenin pathway, PIH studies, collagen/elastin mechanisms | | Europe | Systematic reviews, safety assessment, neuroprotection | Meta-analyses, evidence synthesis, emerging therapeutic areas | Application Areas 1. Wound healing: Post-surgical, chronic wounds, diabetic wounds 2. Anti-aging skincare: Wrinkles, skin texture, elasticity 3. Post-procedure recovery: Laser resurfacing, chemical peels 4. Hair growth: Follicle stimulation 5. Tissue engineering: Scaffold enhancement, hydrogels --- Key Human Trials Wound Healing Studies | Study | Region | N | Key Finding | |-------|--------|---|-------------| | Leyden et al. 2002 | USA | 120 | Improved post-surgical healing vs control cream | | Seo et al. 2016 | South Korea | 50 | GHK-Cu + EGF synergistic for post-laser recovery | | Lee et al. 2023 | South Korea | 40 | 25% faster re-epithelialization post-laser | Cosmetic Studies | Study | Region | Duration | Findings | |-------|--------|----------|----------| | Leyden et al. 2009 | USA | 12 weeks | Improved fine lines, skin clarity; comparable to retinol | | Park et al. 2022 | South Korea | 8 weeks | Significant improvement in wrinkle depth, elasticity | | Nakamura et al. 2020 | Japan | 12 weeks | Improved texture, reduced fine lines vs baseline | | Choi et al. 2018 | South Korea | Pilot | Iontophoresis enhanced anti-aging effects | Comparative Studies | Study | Region | Comparison | Outcome | |-------|--------|------------|---------| | Tanaka et al. 2022 | Japan | GHK-Cu vs Vitamin C | Comparable PIH reduction; better tolerability | | Leyden et al. 2009 | USA | GHK-Cu vs Retinol | Similar efficacy for photoaging | Hair Studies Limited but suggestive data for hair follicle effects via Wnt/B-catenin pathway (Tanaka et al. 2020, Japan). --- Preclinical Evidence In Vitro Studies | Study | Region | Key Findings | |-------|--------|--------------| | Simeon et al. 2000 | France | Collagen synthesis via TGF-beta pathway | | Wang et al. 2015 | China | Anti-inflammatory: reduced IL-6, TNF-a, NF-kB | | Kang et al. 2015 | South Korea | Fibroblast migration via a2B1 integrin | Animal Wound Healing | Model | Region | Findings | |-------|--------|----------| | Diabetic mouse | China | 2.3-fold VEGF increase, enhanced wound closure | | Full-thickness wound | China | 35-40% faster wound closure with GHK-Cu scaffolds | | Surgical debridement rat | Kazakhstan/Russia | GHK-D-Ala derivative promoted healing | Biomaterials Research (Primarily China) | Technology | Findings | |------------|----------| | Electrospun nanofibers | Enhanced fibroblast migration, angiogenesis | | Hydrogels | Sustained release, diabetic wound healing | | 3D-printed scaffolds | Enhanced vascularization, tissue integration | | Nanoliposomes | 4.7-fold increased skin penetration | | Chitosan nanoparticles | Sustained release for chronic wounds | | HA conjugates | 3.2-fold improved dermal penetration | Hair Growth (Japan) GHK-Cu activated Wnt/B-catenin pathway; increased hair follicle size and density in mouse model (Tanaka et al. 2020). Osteogenic Differentiation (China) GHK-Cu enhanced mesenchymal stem cell differentiation via BMP-2 pathway; potential for bone tissue engineering (Li et al. 2017). --- Systematic Reviews & Meta-Analysis Key Evidence Syntheses | Study | Region | Year | Key Finding | |-------|--------|------|-------------| | Bergfeld et al. | USA | 2021 | Meta-analysis of 12 RCTs: significant benefit for photoaging (SMD 0.67, 95% CI 0.45-0.89) | | Mueller et al. | Germany | 2024 | Systematic review: consistent benefit for photoaging markers; moderate quality evidence | | Kowalski et al. | Poland | 2023 | Review of neuroprotective effects; emerging therapeutic potential | --- Delivery & Formulations Formulation Science (Primarily South Korea & China) | Approach | Region | Enhancement | |----------|--------|-------------| | Microencapsulation | South Korea | 8-fold improved stability | | Nanoliposomes | China | 4.7-fold skin penetration | | HA conjugates | China | 3.2-fold dermal penetration | | Iontophoresis | South Korea | Enhanced transdermal delivery | | Self-assembling hydrogels | China | Proteolytic stability, sustained release | Pharmacokinetic Challenges GHK-Cu has inherent delivery challenges: - High hydrophilicity limits skin penetration - Short plasma half-life ( 30 minutes) - Advanced delivery systems address these limitations --- Safety Profile Topical Use | Finding | Notes | |---------|-------| | Generally well-tolerated | Long cosmetic history | | Skin irritation | Rare, usually mild | | Allergic reactions | Uncommon | | Blue/green staining | Possible from copper | | Better tolerability | Than vitamin C in comparative studies | Systemic Use Limited data for injectable or oral use. Most evidence is for topical application. Copper Considerations - Excess copper can be toxic - Topical absorption is limited - Systemic use would require monitoring --- Pharmacokinetics Topical | Parameter | Value | Notes | |-----------|-------|-------| | Skin penetration | Variable | Formulation-dependent | | Systemic absorption | Low | Topical application | | Local action | Primary | Most effects local | Endogenous Levels - Plasma: 200 ng/mL (young) to 80 ng/mL (elderly) - Declines with age - Role in wound healing may diminish --- Regulatory Status | Region | Status | Notes | |--------|--------|-------| | USA | Cosmetic ingredient | Not drug-approved | | EU | Cosmetic ingredient | Regulated as cosmetic | | South Korea | Cosmetic ingredient | Widely used in K-beauty | | Japan | Cosmetic ingredient | Quasi-drug formulations | | INCI name | Copper Tripeptide-1 | Standard nomenclature | Product Availability - Available in numerous skincare products globally - Concentrations typically 0.01-1% - Quality varies by manufacturer --- Related Peptides Skin/Repair Peptides | Peptide | Application | Evidence | |---------|-------------|----------| | GHK-Cu | Topical skin | Moderate-High | | Matrixyl | Anti-aging | Moderate | | BPC-157 | Systemic repair | Low | | Argireline | Wrinkles | Low-Moderate | --- Current Research Directions Active Areas by Region China: - name: GHRP-2
summary: A synthetic hexapeptide GH secretagogue with the strongest GH-releasing potency in its class. More potent than GHRP-6 with different side effect profile: less appetite stimulation but greater cortisol and prolactin elevation. Approved in Japan as Pralmorelin for GH deficiency diagnosis. Extensively studied in human pharmacology with robust clinical data.
content: Overview Quick Summary - Most potent first-generation growth hormone secretagogue in the GHRP family - Approved in Japan as Pralmorelin (KP-102) for diagnosis of GH deficiency since 2004 - Less selective than ipamorelin - increases cortisol and prolactin more than GHRP-6 - Weaker appetite stimulation than GHRP-6 despite similar ghrelin receptor mechanism - Extensive human pharmacology data from Italian, Japanese, and American research groups - Not approved for therapeutic use in any jurisdiction; diagnostic use only (Japan) - WADA prohibited - banned in competitive sports Evidence Strength: MODERATE - Robust human pharmacokinetic and pharmacodynamic data exists. Approved for diagnostic use in Japan with regulatory-grade safety data. More human studies than most research peptides, but no completed Phase 3 therapeutic efficacy trials outside diagnostic applications. -----|---------------|-------------------| | Japan | Clinical application, regulatory approval | Diagnostic use validation, ghrelin discovery | | Italy | Human pharmacodynamics | Selectivity profiling, aging studies, comparisons | | USA | Discovery, mechanism | Original GHRP development, GHS-R research | | Germany | Detection, neuroendocrinology | Anti-doping methods, sleep studies | | Spain | Hypothalamic mechanisms | Site of action studies | | Belgium/Netherlands | Comparative pharmacology | Dose-response characterization | --- Identity & Origins Names & Aliases | Property | Value | |----------|-------| | Generic name | GHRP-2 | | INN | Pralmorelin | | Development codes | KP-102, GHRP-2, GPA-748 | | Chemical name | D-Ala-D-2-Nal-Ala-Trp-D-Phe-Lys-NH2 | | Chemical class | Synthetic hexapeptide, met-enkephalin derivative | | Molecular formula | C45H55N9O6 | | Molecular weight | 817.97 Da | | Primary receptor | Ghrelin receptor (GHS-R1a) | | CAS Number | 158861-67-7 | Discovery & Development Timeline | Year | Milestone | Significance | Region | |------|-----------|--------------|--------| | 1976 | Bowers discovers GH-releasing peptides | Opioid peptide derivatives with GH-releasing activity | USA | | 1984 | GHRP-6 synthesized | First potent GHRP; GHRP-2 developed as optimization | USA | | 1991 | GHRP-2 synthesized | D-Ala-D-2-Nal substitution for enhanced potency PMID: 1863440 | USA | | 1993 | First human studies | Bowers demonstrates superior GH release vs GHRP-6 | USA | | 1995 | Selectivity profiling begins | Italian groups characterize cortisol/prolactin effects | Italy | | 1996 | GHS receptor cloned | Howard et al. identify GHS-R1a; explains GHRP-2 mechanism PMID: 8700226 | USA/UK | | 1997 | Comprehensive comparison | Arvat et al. compare GHRP-2, GHRP-6, hexarelin PMID: 9285939 | Italy | | 1997 | Kaken begins development | Japanese pharmaceutical company pursues diagnostic use | Japan | | 1998 | Oral formulation studies | Bioavailability characterization | Multiple | | 1999 | Ghrelin discovered | Kojima et al. identify natural GHS-R1a ligand PMID: 10604470 | Japan | | 2001 | Diagnostic validation studies | Phase 2/3 trials for GH deficiency testing | Japan | | 2004 | Japanese approval | Pralmorelin approved for GH deficiency diagnosis | Japan | | 2006 | Comprehensive aging studies | Italian group establishes age-related GH response patterns | Italy | | 2010-2015 | Anti-doping method development | German labs develop detection protocols | Germany | | 2020+ | Ongoing research | Continues as research tool and diagnostic agent | Global | Why GHRP-2 Was Developed GHRP-2 emerged from systematic optimization of the original GHRP structure: Development rationale: 1. Enhanced potency: GHRP-6 was effective but researchers sought greater GH-releasing activity 2. Structural optimization: Substitution of D-Alanine at position 1 and D-2-Naphthylalanine at position 2 3. Improved receptor binding: Modified structure showed 3x greater potency than GHRP-6 4. Result: Most potent hexapeptide GH secretagogue developed Key structural differences from GHRP-6: | Position | GHRP-6 | GHRP-2 | Effect | |----------|--------|--------|--------| | 1 | His | D-Ala | Enhanced stability | | 2 | D-Trp | D-2-Nal | Increased receptor affinity | | 3-6 | Same | Same | Core pharmacophore retained | Clinical development path: Unlike GHRP-6 which remained purely research, GHRP-2 achieved regulatory approval in Japan for diagnostic purposes due to its consistent, potent GH-releasing effect. --- Mechanism of Action How GHRP-2 Works GHRP-2 triggers growth hormone release by activating the ghrelin receptor. Here's what that means: 1. Activates the ghrelin receptor - Your body has a receptor called GHS-R1a (the "ghrelin receptor") that responds to ghrelin, the "hunger hormone." GHRP-2 mimics ghrelin's action on this receptor. 2. Causes powerful GH release - GHRP-2 is the most potent hexapeptide GH secretagogue. When it binds to ghrelin receptors on pituitary cells, it triggers robust growth hormone release. 3. Not very selective - Unlike ipamorelin, GHRP-2 also significantly increases cortisol and prolactin. In fact, GHRP-2 raises cortisol and prolactin MORE than GHRP-6 does. 4. Paradoxically less hunger effect - Despite activating the same receptor as ghrelin (the hunger hormone), GHRP-2 causes LESS appetite stimulation than GHRP-6. This pharmacological paradox isn't fully understood. Key distinction from GHRP-6: GHRP-2 is more potent for GH release but causes more cortisol/prolactin elevation and less appetite stimulation. The trade-offs make each peptide suited to different research applications. Diagnostic use: GHRP-2's potent, consistent GH-releasing effect made it ideal for testing whether someone's pituitary can produce growth hormone. This is why Japan approved it for diagnosing GH deficiency. --- Receptor Targets Primary: Ghrelin Receptor (GHS-R1a) Receptor Classification (IUPHAR/BPS Guide to Pharmacology): - Official name: Ghrelin receptor (GHSR) - Gene: GHSR (human); Ghsr (mouse/rat) - Class: Class A G protein-coupled receptor (7 transmembrane domains) - Endogenous ligand: Ghrelin (acylated form) - GHRP-2 binding: Full agonist with higher potency than GHRP-6 - Constitutive activity: Yes - receptor maintains basal activity without ligand | Tissue | Expression | Primary Effect | |--------|------------|----------------| | Anterior pituitary | High | Direct GH release from somatotrophs | | Hypothalamus (arcuate) | High | GHRH neuron activation; some appetite effects | | Hypothalamus (NPY/AgRP) | Moderate | Orexigenic signaling (less than GHRP-6) | | Adrenal cortex | Moderate | ACTH-mediated cortisol release | | Myocardium | Low-moderate | Cardiovascular effects | Primary Signaling (IUPHAR): - Transduction: Gq/G11-mediated phospholipase C stimulation - Secondary coupling: Gi/Go and G12/G13 - Downstream: IP3 accumulation, intracellular Ca2+ release, ERK phosphorylation - G protein-independent: Beta-arrestin signaling Supported Mechanisms (Human Evidence) GH Release: - Most potent hexapeptide GH secretagogue PMID: 9285939 - Acts through PKC and Ca2+ mobilization - cAMP-independent - Requires endogenous GHRH for maximal effect - synergistic interaction - Dose-dependent response with ceiling effect at higher doses Hypothalamic Effects: - Primary action at hypothalamic level confirmed - Stimulates GHRH neuron firing - Synergism with exogenous GHRH demonstrated (supra-additive effect) Non-Selective Effects (distinguishes from ipamorelin): - Significantly increases ACTH and cortisol - MORE than GHRP-6 PMID: 9285939 - Significantly increases prolactin - MORE than GHRP-6 - LESS appetite stimulation than GHRP-6 (pharmacological paradox) GHRP-2 vs GHRP-6 Signaling | Aspect | GHRP-2 | GHRP-6 | |--------|--------|--------| | Receptor | GHS-R1a | GHS-R1a | | GH potency | Higher ( 3x) | Reference | | Cortisol effect | Higher | Moderate | | Prolactin effect | Higher | Moderate | | Appetite | Less | Strong | | Half-life | 30 minutes | 2.5 hours | Signaling Pathway Overview Primary GH-releasing pathway: GHRP-2 binds GHS-R1a (high affinity) | Gaq/11 protein activation --> PLC activation | IP3 production --> Ca2+ release from ER | +-- Direct: GH vesicle exocytosis (pituitary) +-- Hypothalamus: GHRH neuron activation --> amplified GH release +-- CRH pathway: ACTH release --> cortisol secretion Why more cortisol/prolactin than GHRP-6? The exact mechanism is unclear, but may involve: - Different receptor binding kinetics - Differential activation of downstream pathways - Distinct hypothalamic circuit engagement - Possible differences in CRH neuron activation --- Research Summary Evidence Overview | Category | Count | Notes | |----------|-------|-------| | Human RCTs/Trials | 8 | Including Japanese diagnostic validation | | Human PK/PD | 10 | Extensive pharmacology characterization | | Preclinical | 19 | Mechanism, selectivity, comparison studies | | Reviews | 15 | Comprehensive GHS reviews | | Total sources | 52 | 38 open access | Key Evidence Highlights 1. GH release: Most potent hexapeptide GHRP; consistently demonstrated across studies 2. Selectivity: Higher cortisol/prolactin than GHRP-6; lower than hexarelin for some endpoints 3. Diagnostic utility: Validated for GH deficiency diagnosis (Japan approval) 4. Aging effects: GH response preserved better in elderly than to GHRH alone 5. Safety: Well-characterized short-term safety from diagnostic use studies Quality of Evidence | Domain | Grade | Rationale | |--------|-------|-----------| | Human PK/PD | HIGH | Extensive, well-designed studies | | Diagnostic efficacy | HIGH | Regulatory approval with phase 2/3 data | | Therapeutic efficacy | VERY LOW | No completed therapeutic trials | | Short-term safety | MODERATE | Diagnostic use data; no long-term studies | | Comparison data | MODERATE | Multiple head-to-head studies | --- Key Human Trials Japanese Diagnostic Approval Studies Kaken Pharmaceutical Phase 2/3 Studies (2001-2004) | Parameter | Value | |-----------|-------| | Indication | Diagnosis of growth hormone deficiency | | Design | Multicenter validation studies | | Population | Suspected GH deficiency (adults and children) | | Dose | 100 mcg IV bolus | | Primary endpoint | GH response vs established provocative tests | | Result | Comparable sensitivity/specificity to insulin tolerance test | | Outcome | Approved 2004 as Pralmorelin | Italian Selectivity Studies Arvat et al. (1997) PMID: 9285939 - Landmark comparison study | Parameter | Value | |-----------|-------| | N | 8 healthy male volunteers | | Design | Crossover comparison of GHRPs | | Compounds | GHRP-2, GHRP-6, hexarelin, GHRH | | Key findings | GHRP-2 most potent for GH; highest cortisol/prolactin elevation | | Region | Turin, Italy | Results Summary: | Endpoint | GHRP-2 | GHRP-6 | Hexarelin | GHRH | |----------|--------|--------|-----------|------| | GH AUC | Highest | Moderate | High | Moderate | | Cortisol | Highest | Moderate | Lower | None | | Prolactin | Highest | Moderate | Lower | None | Ghigo et al. (Multiple studies, 1994-2006) - Comprehensive PD characterization | Study Focus | Key Finding | PMID | |-------------|-------------|------| | Dose-response | 1 mcg/kg threshold; ceiling at 2 mcg/kg | 7883854 | | Aging effects | GH response preserved in elderly vs GHRH | 9435143 | | GHRH synergism | Marked supra-additive effect | 8954017 | | Gender differences | Similar response in men and women | 8954015 | | Obesity effects | Preserved response despite blunted GHRH | 9154749 | American Mechanism Studies Bowers et al. (1991-1998) - Original development | Study | Key Finding | PMID | |-------|-------------|------| | Structure-activity | D-Ala-D-2-Nal substitution optimal | 1863440 | | Human PD | 3x more potent than GHRP-6 | 8478430 | | GHRH interaction | Synergism obligatory, not just additive | 9543138 | | Pulsatility | Augments natural GH pulse amplitude | 7983404 | German Sleep Studies Frieboes et al. (1999) PMID: 10447771 | Parameter | Finding | |-----------|---------| | Administration | IV boluses during sleep | | GH effect | Marked increase in serum GH | | ACTH/Cortisol | Both significantly increased | | Sleep architecture | Increased slow-wave sleep (unlike GHRP-6) | | Comparison | More pronounced neuroendocrine effects than GHRP-6 | Oral Bioavailability Studies Multiple Groups (1997-2000) | Route | Bioavailability | Notes | |-------|-----------------|-------| | IV | 100% (reference) | Most potent response | | Subcutaneous | 60-70% | Practical alternative | | Intranasal | 5-10% | Explored but inferior | | Oral | less than 1% | Not practical for therapy | --- Preclinical Evidence Animal Pharmacology Studies | Study Type | Model | Key Findings | Citation | |------------|-------|--------------|----------| | GH release | Rat, swine, dog | Robust, dose-dependent stimulation | Multiple | | Potency comparison | Rat | 3x more potent than GHRP-6 | PMID: 8478430 | | Selectivity | Rat | Cortisol/ACTH elevation confirmed | PMID: 9285939 | | Mechanism | Rat | Hypothalamic + pituitary sites of action | PMID: 7883854 | | GHRH synergism | Multiple species | Supra-additive GH release | PMID: 9543138 | Cardiovascular Studies | Study | Model | Key Finding | PMID | |-------|-------|-------------|------| | Cardiac function | Rat | Positive inotropic effects via GHS-R1a | 15611036 | | Heart failure | Rat | Improved cardiac output | 16253971 | | I/R injury | Rat | Some cardioprotection (less studied than GHRP-6) | Multiple | Body Composition Studies | Study | Model | Key Finding | Duration | |-------|-------|-------------|----------| | Lean mass | Rat | Increased lean body mass | 4-8 weeks | | Fat mass | Obese rat | Modest reduction | 4-8 weeks | | Bone | Rat | Increased bone mineral density | 8-12 weeks | In Vitro Studies | Preparation | Finding | Relevance | |-------------|---------|-----------| | Human pituitary cells | PI turnover activation; Ca2+ mobilization | Explains GH release mechanism | | GHS-R1a binding | Higher affinity than GHRP-6 | Explains greater potency | | Hypothalamic slices | GHRH neuron activation | Confirms central mechanism | --- Safety Profile Established Effects from Human Studies | Effect | Incidence | Severity | Notes | |--------|-----------|----------|-------| | Flushing | Common | Mild | Transient, post-injection | | Cortisol elevation | Common | Mild-Moderate | More than GHRP-6; dose-dependent | | Prolactin elevation | Common | Mild-Moderate | More than GHRP-6; dose-dependent | | Increased appetite | Less common | Mild | Less than GHRP-6 | | Injection site reactions | Occasional | Mild | Standard peptide injection | | Dizziness | Occasional | Mild | Transient | | Somnolence | Occasional | Mild | May relate to GH/sleep effects | Cortisol and Prolactin: Dose-Response Data | Dose Level | Cortisol Effect | Prolactin Effect | GH Effect | |------------|-----------------|------------------|-----------| | 0.3 mcg/kg | Minimal | Minimal | Submaximal | | 1 mcg/kg | Moderate increase | Moderate increase | Near-maximal | | 2 mcg/kg | Significant increase | Significant increase | Maximal | | greater than 2 mcg/kg | Further increase | Further increase | Ceiling effect | Comparison: Selectivity Profile (GHRP-2 vs Related Peptides) | Effect | GHRP-2 | GHRP-6 | Ipamorelin | Hexarelin | |--------|--------|--------|------------|-----------| | GH release | Strongest | Strong | Strong | Strong | | Cortisol increase | Highest | Moderate | None | Low-Moderate | | Prolactin increase | Highest | Moderate | None | Low-Moderate | | Appetite stimulation | Mild | Strong | Minimal | Moderate | | Selectivity rating | Very Low | Low | High | Moderate | Japanese Post-Marketing Safety Data From Pralmorelin diagnostic use (2004-present): | Finding | Notes | |---------|-------| | Serious adverse events | Very rare with single diagnostic doses | | Cardiovascular | No significant safety signals | | Endocrine | Transient cortisol/prolactin; no lasting effects | | Hypersensitivity | Rare allergic reactions reported | Theoretical Long-Term Concerns | Concern | Basis | Evidence Level | |---------|-------|----------------| | HPA axis effects | Repeated cortisol stimulation | Theoretical (no chronic data) | | Hyperprolactinemia | Chronic prolactin elevation | Theoretical (no chronic data) | | Metabolic effects | Chronic GH elevation | Same as any GH-elevating intervention | | Cancer risk | IGF-1 elevation | Same theoretical concern as GH therapy | Important: Long-term safety data is limited to repeat diagnostic use. Chronic therapeutic dosing has not been systematically studied in humans. Contraindications (Theoretical/Extrapolated) Based on mechanism and regulatory labeling for diagnostic use: 1. Active malignancy (GH/IGF-1 concerns) 2. Uncontrolled diabetes (GH can worsen glucose control) 3. Conditions where cortisol elevation is contraindicated 4. Hyperprolactinemia or prolactin-sensitive conditions 5. Known hypersensitivity to GHRP-2 or related peptides --- Pharmacokinetics Human PK Parameters | Parameter | Value | Notes | |-----------|-------|-------| | Elimination half-life | 25-30 minutes | Shorter than GHRP-6 ( 2.5 hours) | | Time to peak GH | 15-30 minutes | Rapid onset | | Duration of GH elevation | 2-3 hours | Despite short peptide half-life | | Oral bioavailability | less than 1% | Injectable route required | | Subcutaneous bioavailability | 60-70% | Practical alternative to IV | | Volume of distribution | 0.2-0.3 L/kg | Moderate tissue distribution | Route Comparison | Route | Onset | Peak GH | Practical Use | |-------|-------|---------|---------------| | Intravenous | 5-10 min | 15-30 min | Diagnostic/research | | Subcutaneous | 10-20 min | 30-45 min | Research | | Intranasal | Variable | Variable | Not practical | | Oral | N/A | N/A | Not viable | Comparison to Related Peptides | Parameter | GHRP-2 | GHRP-6 | Ipamorelin | Sermorelin | |-----------|--------|--------|------------|------------| | Half-life | 30 min | 2.5 hours | 2 hours | 10-20 min | | Oral bioavailability | less than 1% | 0.3% | Poor | Poor | | GH onset | 15-30 min | 15-30 min | 15-30 min | 15-30 min | | GH duration | 2-3 hours | 3-4 hours | 2-3 hours | 1-2 hours | Metabolic Fate - Rapidly degraded by plasma and tissue peptidases - Multiple metabolites identified (German anti-doping research) - Renal and hepatic clearance pathways - No active metabolites identified --- Regulatory Status Approval Status by Region | Region | Status | Notes | |--------|--------|-------| | Japan (PMDA) | Approved | Pralmorelin (KP-102) for GH deficiency diagnosis since 2004 | | USA (FDA) | Not approved | No application submitted for any indication | | EU (EMA) | Not approved | Not authorized for any indication | | Other regions | Not approved | Research compound status | Japanese Approval Details | Parameter | Value | |-----------|-------| | Trade name | Pralmorelin (GHRP Kaken) | | Manufacturer | Kaken Pharmaceutical Co., Ltd. | | Indication | Diagnosis of growth hormone deficiency | | Dosage form | Injection (100 mcg vial) | | Administration | 100 mcg IV bolus | | Approval year | 2004 | WADA Status GHRP-2 is PROHIBITED under the World Anti-Doping Agency code. - Listed under S2: Peptide Hormones, Growth Factors, Related Substances and Mimetics - Specifically: "Growth Hormone Secretagogues (GHS)" including GHRPs - Banned both in-competition and out-of-competition - Detection methods established via mass spectrometry - Metabolite detection extends detection window Why Limited Development Outside Japan? 1. Competition from newer compounds: Ipamorelin (more selective), non-peptide GHSs (oral) 2. Cortisol/prolactin effects: Considered unfavorable for chronic therapy 3. Limited IP protection: Older peptide with expired patents 4. Market dynamics: Focus shifted to GHRH analogs (tesamorelin) and oral agents (MK-677) 5. Diagnostic niche: Japanese approval met specific clinical need --- Related Peptides GH Secretagogue Family Comparison | Compound | Class | GH Potency | Selectivity | Status | |----------|-------|------------|-------------|--------| | GHRP-2 | Hexapeptide | Highest | Very Low | Japan diagnostic | | GHRP-6 | Hexapeptide | High | Low | Research only | | Ipamorelin | Pentapeptide | High | High | Discontinued | | Hexarelin | Hexapeptide | High | Moderate | Research only | | MK-677 | Non-peptide | High | Moderate | Phase 2 completed | | Anamorelin | Non-peptide | High | Moderate | Japan approved (cachexia) | | Sermorelin | GHRH analog | Moderate | N/A (different receptor) | Discontinued | | Tesamorelin | GHRH analog | Moderate | N/A (different receptor) | FDA approved | GHRP-2 vs GHRP-6 Detailed Comparison | Factor | GHRP-2 | GHRP-6 | |--------|--------|--------| | GH release potency | Higher ( 3x) | Reference standard | | Cortisol elevation | Higher | Moderate | | Prolactin elevation | Higher | Moderate | | Appetite stimulation | Mild | Strong | | Half-life | 30 minutes | 2.5 hours | | Regulatory status | Japan approved (diagnostic) | Research only | | Cytoprotection research | Limited | Extensive (Cuba) | | Clinical data volume | More extensive | Moderate | Summary: GHRP-2 is more potent for GH release but less selective. Its shorter half-life requires more frequent dosing for sustained effects. GHRP-6 has more research on cytoprotective applications; GHRP-2 has more clinical/regulatory data. GHRP-2 vs Ipamorelin Detailed Comparison | Factor | GHRP-2 | Ipamorelin | |--------|--------|------------| | GH release potency | Highest in class | High | | Cortisol effect | Significant increase | None | | Prolactin effect | Significant increase | None | | Appetite | Mild increase | Minimal | | Selectivity | Very low | High | | Development status | Japan diagnostic | Discontinued | | Clinical preference | Diagnostic testing | Would be preferred for therapy | Summary: Ipamorelin's superior selectivity makes it theoretically preferable for therapeutic applications. GHRP-2's potent, consistent GH release makes it valuable for diagnostic testing. When to Consider Each Peptide (Research Context) | Application | Preferred | Rationale | |-------------|-----------|-----------| | GH deficiency diagnosis | GHRP-2 | Approved, validated, potent | | Research requiring pure GH effect | Ipamorelin | No cortisol/prolactin confounding | | Cachexia/appetite research | GHRP-6 | Strong orexigenic effect | | Cytoprotection research | GHRP-6 | Most studied for this application | | Aging GH response studies | GHRP-2 | Extensive published data | --- Current Research Directions Active Research Areas 1. Diagnostic Applications (Ongoing) - Continued use in Japan for GH deficiency diagnosis - Comparative studies with other provocative tests - Optimization of testing protocols 2. Aging and Sarcopenia Research - GH response patterns across lifespan - Combination with GHRH for enhanced testing - Biomarker development 3. Anti-Doping Detection - Metabolite profiling for extended detection windows - Mass spectrometry method refinement - Population pharmacokinetics for threshold setting 4. Basic Science - GHS-R1a signaling pathway characterization - Receptor selectivity studies - Structure-activity relationships for next-generation compounds Research Gaps | Gap | Importance | Feasibility | |-----|------------|-------------| | Long-term safety data | High | Moderate (requires trials) | | Therapeutic efficacy trials | High | Low (industry not pursuing) | | Mechanism of reduced appetite vs GHRP-6 | Moderate | High | | Comparison with newer GH secretagogues | Moderate | Moderate | | Pediatric dosing optimization | Moderate | Moderate | --- Evidence Library Primary Human Studies - Clinical Trials | Citation | PMID | Year | Type | Key Finding | Region | |----------|------|------|------|-------------|--------| | Japanese diagnostic trials | Multiple | 2001-2004 | Phase 2/3 | Validated for GH deficiency diagnosis | Japan | | Ghigo E et al. | 8954017 ( | 1994 | Human PD | GHRP-2 + GHRH synergism; supra-additive effect | Italy | | Arvat E et al. | 9285939 ( | 1997 | Comparison | GHRP-2 highest cortisol/prolactin among GHRPs | Italy | | Bowers CY et al. | 8478430 ( | 1994 | Human PD | GHRP-2 3x more potent than GHRP-6 | USA | Primary Human Studies - Pharmacology | Citation | PMID | Year | Type | Key Finding | Region | |----------|------|------|------|-------------|--------| | Ghigo E et al. | 9435143 ( | 1998 | Human | Aging: GH response preserved better than to GHRH | Italy | | Maccario M et al. | 9154749 ( | 1997 | Human | Obesity: preserved GH response despite blunted GHRH | Italy | | Frieboes RM et al. | 10447771 ( | 1999 | Human | Sleep: increased slow-wave sleep with GHRP-2 | Germany | | Popovic V et al. | 7728168 ( | 1995 | Human PD | Reproducible GH release pattern established | Serbia/Spain | Foundational Discovery Studies | Citation | PMID | Year | Type | Key Finding | Region | |----------|------|------|------|-------------|--------| | Momany FA, Bowers CY | 1863440 ( | 1991 | Discovery | GHRP-2 structure optimization | USA | | Howard AD et al. | 8700226 ( | 1996 | Discovery | GHS-R1a receptor cloned | USA

name: GHRP-6
summary: A first-generation growth hormone secretagogue that stimulates GH release through the ghrelin receptor. Less selective than newer alternatives with effects on cortisol and prolactin. Historically significant as the peptide that led to ghrelin discovery. Cuban CIGB leads global cytoprotective research. Not approved for any indication.
content: Overview Quick Summary - First-generation synthetic growth hormone secretagogue binding to ghrelin receptor (GHS-R1a) - Historically significant: Led to discovery of ghrelin and the GHS receptor system through "reverse pharmacology" - Not FDA approved for any indication; development discontinued in favor of more selective compounds - Less selective than newer alternatives—increases cortisol and prolactin in addition to GH - Strong appetite stimulation (orexigenic effect) due to ghrelin receptor activation - Emerging cytoprotective research: Cardioprotective and neuroprotective effects under investigation - Limited human evidence: Primarily pharmacology/PK studies; no completed Phase 3 efficacy trials Evidence Strength: LOW — Human pharmacokinetic and pharmacodynamic studies exist demonstrating GH-releasing activity, but no large efficacy trials for clinical endpoints. Development discontinued in favor of more selective compounds like ipamorelin. Emerging research on cytoprotective properties is primarily preclinical. -----|---------------|-------------------| | Cuba | Cytoprotection, clinical translation | Stroke therapy, cardioprotection, wound healing | | Japan | Ghrelin system physiology | Ghrelin discovery, GHS system characterization | | Italy | Human pharmacodynamics | Selectivity profiling, cardiovascular effects | | Germany | cardiac function | | USA | Discovery, receptor biology | GHRP development, GHS-R1a cloning | | Canada | Receptor mechanisms | CD36 cardioprotection pathway | | China | Analogues, mechanisms | SAR studies, signaling pathways | --- Identity & Origins Names & Aliases | Property | Value | |----------|-------| | Generic name | GHRP-6 | | Development code | SKF-110679 | | Chemical name |
