receptor | CD36 (scavenger receptor) | Discovery & Development Timeline | Year | Milestone | Significance | Region | |------|-----------|--------------|--------| | 1976 | Bowers discovers GH-releasing peptides | Found opioid peptide derivatives with GH-releasing activity PMID: 1109847 | USA | | 1977 | First GHS synthesized | Tyr-D-Trp-Gly-Phe-Met-NH2—weak activity, proved concept | USA | | 1982 | GHRH isolated | Guillemin/Rivier labs identified hypothalamic GHRH | USA | | 1984 | GHRP-6 synthesized | Potent hexapeptide created through conformational energy calculations PMID: 6439623 | USA | | 1990 | First human studies | Bowers & Thorner demonstrate GH release in young men PMID: 2119355 | USA | | 1995 | Central effects discovered | GHRP-6 ICV administration increases food intake in rats | USA | | 1995 | Hypothalamic action confirmed | Popovic et al. demonstrate GHRP-6 acts mainly at hypothalamus PMID: 7883854 | Serbia/Spain | | 1996 | GHS receptor cloned | Howard et al. (Merck) identify GHS-R1a receptor PMID: 8700226 | USA/UK | | 1997 | Selectivity profiling | Arvat et al. compare GHRP-6, GHRP-2, hexarelin selectivity PMID: 9285939 | Italy | | 1998 | GHRH dependency confirmed | Pandya et al. show GHRP-6 requires endogenous GHRH PMID: 9543138 | USA | | 1999 | Ghrelin discovered | Kojima et al. identify ghrelin as natural ligand—"reverse pharmacology" PMID: 10604470 | Japan | | 1999 | Sleep effects characterized | Frieboes et al. study neuroendocrine/sleep effects PMID: 10447771 | Germany | | 2002 | CD36 receptor identified | Canadian group discovers GH-independent cardiac binding PMID: 12387877 | Canada | | 2006 | Stroke combination therapy | CIGB tests EGF+GHRP-6 in rat stroke model PMID: 16269137 | Cuba | | 2007 | Wound healing research | CIGB shows accelerated healing in irradiated tissue PMID: 17217993 | Cuba | | 2008-2012 | Cardioprotection series | CIGB establishes cardioprotective mechanisms in multiple models | Cuba | | 2011 | Liver fibrosis research | CIGB demonstrates antifibrotic effects PMID: 21195069 | Cuba | | 2012 | Human PK study published | Full pharmacokinetic characterization by CIGB PMID: 23099431 | Cuba | | 2014 | Intracerebral hemorrhage | CIGB shows neuroprotection in hemorrhagic stroke PMID: 24284928 | Cuba | | 2015 | Diabetic cardiomyopathy | CIGB demonstrates protection in diabetic model PMID: 25982565 | Cuba | | 2024 | Stroke clinical trial | Phase I/II EGF+GHRP-6 in acute ischemic stroke; n=36; safe PMID: 38555403 | Cuba | | 2024 | Doxorubicin cardioprotection | CIGB confirms prevention of chemotherapy cardiotoxicity PMID: 38873418 | Cuba | Why GHRP-6 Was Developed In 1976, Cyril Bowers and colleagues observed that certain methionine-enkephalin derivatives lacking opioid activity could stimulate growth hormone release. This serendipitous discovery led to systematic structure-activity studies. Development rationale: 1. Opioid scaffold optimization: Modified met-enkephalin backbone to eliminate opioid effects while enhancing GH release 2. D-amino acid incorporation: Unnatural D-Trp and D-Phe residues improved receptor binding and metabolic stability 3. Conformational design: Computational energy calculations guided hexapeptide structure 4. Result: GHRP-6 emerged as a potent, consistent GH secretagogue suitable for research Historical importance: GHRP-6's existence as an "orphan ligand" (a drug without a known receptor) drove research that ultimately identified the GHS-R1a receptor in 1996 and its natural ligand ghrelin in 1999. This represents a classic example of "reverse pharmacology"—synthetic compounds leading to discovery of endogenous systems. --- Mechanism of Action How GHRP-6 Works GHRP-6 triggers growth hormone release by activating the same receptor that the "hunger hormone" ghrelin uses. Here's what that means: 1. Activates the ghrelin receptor - Your body has a receptor called GHS-R1a that responds to ghrelin (the hormone that makes you hungry). GHRP-6 was created before scientists even knew ghrelin existed, which is why it's historically significant. 2. Causes GH release - When GHRP-6 binds to the ghrelin receptor on cells in your pituitary gland, it triggers them to release growth hormone into your bloodstream. 3. Not selective - Unlike newer peptides like ipamorelin, GHRP-6 also increases cortisol, prolactin, and appetite. This is considered a disadvantage for most applications but may be useful in specific situations like treating wasting diseases. 4. May have protective effects - Emerging research suggests GHRP-6 might protect heart and brain tissue from damage, though this is mostly studied in animals. Historical importance: GHRP-6's discovery in 1984 led scientists to hunt for the natural substance the body uses to activate this receptor. This search resulted in the discovery of ghrelin in 1999, a classic example of a drug leading to the discovery of a new hormone system. Development status: GHRP-6 was never approved because less selective compounds aren't ideal for most therapeutic uses. Newer, more selective alternatives were developed instead. --- Receptor Targets GHRP-6 binds to two distinct receptors with different biological consequences: Primary: Ghrelin Receptor (GHS-R1a) Receptor Classification (IUPHAR/BPS Guide to Pharmacology): - Official name: Ghrelin receptor (GHSR) - Gene: GHSR (human); Ghsr (mouse/rat) - Class: Class A G protein-coupled receptor (7 transmembrane domains) - Endogenous ligand: Ghrelin (pKd 8.6-9.4) - GHRP-6 binding affinity: pKd 6.6-8.8; pKi 8.8 (full agonist) - Constitutive activity: Yes - receptor maintains basal activity without ligand | Tissue | Expression | Primary Effect | |--------|------------|----------------| | Anterior pituitary | High | Direct GH release from somatotrophs | | Hypothalamus (arcuate) | High | GHRH neuron activation; appetite stimulation | | Hypothalamus (NPY/AgRP) | Moderate | Orexigenic signaling | | Vagal afferents | Moderate | Gut-brain axis signaling | | Myocardium | Low-moderate | Cytoprotective signaling | | Vasculature | Low | Hemodynamic effects | Primary Signaling (IUPHAR): - Transduction: Gq/G11-mediated phospholipase C stimulation - Secondary coupling: Gi/Go and G12/G13 - Downstream: IP3 accumulation, intracellular Ca2+ release, ERK phosphorylation, RhoA activation - G protein-independent: Beta-arrestin signaling Secondary: CD36 (Scavenger Receptor) - Present on macrophages, endothelial cells, cardiomyocytes - Mediates some cytoprotective effects independent of GHS-R1a - May contribute to anti-fibrotic and cardioprotective actions Supported Mechanisms (Human Evidence) GH Release: - Stimulates GH synthesis and secretion from pituitary somatotrophs PMID: 7772238 - Acts through protein kinase C (PKC) and Ca²⁺ mobilization—cAMP-independent pathway - Activates phosphatidylinositol (PI) turnover in pituitary cells - Requires endogenous GHRH for maximal effect—synergistic interaction PMID: 9543138 Hypothalamic Effects: - GHRP-6 main action is exerted at hypothalamic level PMID: 7883854 - Patients with hypothalamopituitary disconnection show blocked GHRP-6 response - Synergism with GHRH demonstrated in humans (1990, Bowers) Non-Selective Effects (distinguishes from ipamorelin): - Increases ACTH and cortisol secretion PMID: 9285939 - Increases prolactin release (dose-dependent) - Strong appetite stimulation via hypothalamic circuits GHRP-6 vs GHRH Signaling | Aspect | GHRP-6 | GHRH | |--------|--------|------| | Receptor | GHS-R1a (ghrelin receptor) | GHRH-R | | Primary signaling | PKC/Ca²⁺/PI turnover | cAMP/PKA | | Pituitary effect | Direct + indirect | Direct | | Hypothalamic effect | Yes (major site) | Minimal | | Synergism | Yes, with GHRH | Yes, with GHRPs | | Cortisol effect | Increases | No effect | | Appetite | Strong increase | No effect | Signaling Pathway Overview Primary GH-releasing pathway: GHRP-6 binds GHS-R1a ↓ Gαq/11 protein activation → PLC activation ↓ IP3 production → Ca²⁺ release from ER ↓ ├── Direct: GH vesicle exocytosis (pituitary) ├── Hypothalamus: GHRH neuron activation → amplified GH release └── Arcuate nucleus: NPY/AgRP activation → appetite increase Secondary pathways: - β-arrestin recruitment (receptor internalization, some signaling) - MAPK/ERK activation (may contribute to cytoprotective effects) - CD36-mediated signaling (cardioprotection) Hypothesized Mechanisms (Emerging Research) | Hypothesis | Supporting Evidence | Confidence | |------------|---------------------|------------| | Cardioprotection | Reduced infarct size in animal models; Bcl-2 upregulation; mitochondrial preservation PMID: 28270686 | Low-Moderate (preclinical) | | Neuroprotection | Phase I/II stroke trial showed improved outcomes with EGF+GHRP-6 combination | Low (early clinical) | | Anti-fibrotic effects | Reduced cardiac and hepatic fibrosis in animal models | Low (preclinical only) | | Wound healing | Enhanced tissue repair in multiple injury models | Low (preclinical only) | --- Physiologic Context: The Ghrelin System Native Ghrelin Physiology Ghrelin is the endogenous ligand for the receptor that GHRP-6 activates. Understanding native ghrelin helps contextualize GHRP-6's effects. Endogenous ghrelin: - 28-amino acid peptide produced primarily by stomach fundus - Unique octanoyl modification on Ser3 required for GHS-R1a activation - Circulating levels rise during fasting, fall after meals - Primary "hunger hormone"—stimulates food intake and GH secretion Normal physiologic functions: | Function | Mechanism | Effect | |----------|-----------|--------| | Appetite stimulation | Hypothalamic NPY/AgRP activation | Hunger sensation before meals | | GH pulsatility | Amplifies GHRH-induced GH release | Contributes to normal GH secretion pattern | | Gastric motility | Vagal and direct smooth muscle effects | Promotes gastric emptying | | Energy homeostasis | Multiple metabolic targets | Favors energy conservation | GHRP-6 vs Endogenous Ghrelin | Parameter | Endogenous Ghrelin | GHRP-6 | |-----------|-------------------|--------| | Structure | 28 amino acids, octanoylated | 6 amino acids, synthetic | | Half-life | 30 minutes | 2.5 hours (elimination) | | GHS-R1a affinity | Reference | Comparable | | Selectivity | Highly selective | Less selective (cortisol/prolactin effects) | | Natural regulation | Meal-responsive | Exogenous administration | What Happens with Exogenous GHS-R1a Agonism Acute effects (single dose): - Rapid GH release (peak 15-30 minutes) - Transient cortisol and ACTH increase - Appetite stimulation - Mild flushing in some subjects Chronic effects (theoretical/limited data): - Unknown whether tachyphylaxis develops - Potential for sustained appetite increase - Effects on GH pulsatility with repeated dosing unclear - No long-term human data available --- Key Human Trials Pharmacokinetic Study (2012) Gonzalez et al. PMID: 23099431 — Definitive PK characterization | Parameter | Value | |-----------|-------| | N | 9 healthy male volunteers | | Design | Single IV bolus, dose-escalation | | Doses | 100, 200, 400 μg/kg body weight | | Distribution half-life | 7.6 ± 1.9 minutes | | Elimination half-life | 2.5 ± 1.1 hours | | AUC | Dose-proportional increase | | Safety | Well tolerated at all doses | Key findings: - Linear pharmacokinetics across dose range - Atypical concentration spikes in elimination phase (4/9 subjects)—enterohepatic recirculation suspected - No serious adverse events GH Response Studies Bowers et al. (1990) PMID: 2119355 — First human studies | Parameter | Value | |-----------|-------| | Population | Healthy young men | | Finding | Dose-dependent GH release confirmed | | Peak GH | 15-30 minutes post-administration | | Magnitude | Robust, comparable to GHRH | Popovic et al. (1995) PMID: 7728168 — GH response characterization - Confirmed reproducible GH release pattern - Established dose-response relationship - Demonstrated GHRH synergism GHRH Dependency Studies Pandya et al. (1998) PMID: 9543138 | Finding | Implication | |---------|-------------| | GHRP-6 requires endogenous GHRH for maximal effect | Hypothalamic action primary | | GHRH antagonism blunts GHRP-6 response | Synergism is obligatory, not additive | | Patients with hypothalamopituitary disconnection show minimal response | Confirms hypothalamic site of action | Acute Ischemic Stroke Trial (2024) Phase I/II — EGF + GHRP-6 Combination DOI: 10.3389/fneur.2024.1303402 ( | PMC11024445 ( | Parameter | Value | |-----------|-------| | Design | Multicenter, open-label, controlled, Phase I/II, parallel groups | | Population | Acute ischemic stroke, CT-confirmed, <12h from onset, age 18-80 | | Period | July 2017 - January 2018 (Cuba) | | N | 36 patients (Group I: n=10, Group II: n=10, Control: n=16) | | Group I | 75 ug rEGF + 3.5 mg GHRP-6 IV BID x 7 days | | Group II | 75 ug rEGF + 5 mg GHRP-6 IV BID x 7 days | | Group III | Standard care (control) | | Primary endpoint | Safety over 6 months (accepting up to 30% SAE with proven causality) | | Key result | EGF + GHRP-6 therapy was safe | | Secondary finding | Functional benefits supported Phase III study | | Registry | RPCEC00000214 (Cuban Public Registry of Clinical Trials) | Drug source: GHRP-6 synthesized by BCN peptide (Barcelona, Spain); rEGF from Saccharomyces cerevisiae, formulated at Center for Genetic Engineering and Biotechnology (Havana, Cuba). Significance: First modern clinical trial exploring GHRP-6's cytoprotective potential in humans. Safety confirmed; functional outcomes favored treatment groups. Results support Phase III investigation. Sleep/Neuroendocrine Studies Frieboes et al. (1999) PMID: 10447771 | Parameter | Finding | |-----------|---------| | Administration | IV boluses during sleep | | GH effect | Increased serum GH levels | | ACTH/Cortisol | Both increased | | Sleep architecture | Increased stage 2 sleep; slow-wave sleep unchanged | | Route comparison | IV more potent than other routes | --- Preclinical Evidence Animal Pharmacology Studies | Study Type | Model | Key Findings | Citation | |------------|-------|--------------|----------| | GH release | Rat, primate | Robust, reproducible stimulation | Multiple 1980s-90s | | Selectivity | Rat | Cortisol/prolactin elevations confirmed | PMID: 9285939 | | Appetite | Rodent | Strong orexigenic effect; food intake increases | PMID: 7649396 | | Central action | Rat ICV | Hypothalamic site confirmed | PMID: 7883854 | | GHRH synergism | Multiple species | Marked potentiation of combined effect | PMID: 9543138 | Cytoprotective Studies (Emerging Area) Cardioprotection: | Study | Model | Key Finding | PMID | |-------|-------|-------------|------| | Myocardial I/R | Rat | Reduced infarct size, preserved mitochondria | 28270686 | | Doxorubicin cardiotoxicity (2024) | Rat | Upregulated Bcl-2, antioxidant defense, LV preservation | 38873418 | | Cardiac fibrosis | Multiple | Reduced collagen deposition | Multiple | 2024 Doxorubicin Cardioprotection Study PMID:38873418 ( - Frontiers in Pharmacology, May 2024 | Finding | Detail | |---------|--------| | LV protection | Prevented myocardial fiber consumption and ventricular dilation | | Systolic function | Effective preservation of LV systolic function | | Histopathology | Myocardial damage reduced: 91.0% (saline) vs 42.2% (GHRP-6), p<0.00019 | | Extracardiac | Preserved epithelial organ integrity, inhibited interstitial fibrosis | | Survival | Reduced morbidity and mortality | Mechanisms identified: - Sustained cellular antioxidant defense - Upregulation of pro-survival gene Bcl-2 - Preserved cardiomyocyte mitochondrial integrity - May act through both GHS-R1a and CD36 receptors Neuroprotection: | Study | Model | Key Finding | |-------|-------|-------------| | Cerebral ischemia | Rat | EGF+GHRP-6 combination reduced infarct volume | | Peripheral axonal injury | Multiple | Improved nerve regeneration | | Multiple sclerosis model | EAE mice | Reduced demyelination | Wound Healing: - Enhanced tissue repair in multiple injury models - Mechanism may involve both GH-dependent and independent pathways In Vitro Studies | Preparation | Finding | Relevance | |-------------|---------|-----------| | Human pituitary cells | PI turnover activation | Explains GH release mechanism | | Cardiomyocytes | Mitochondrial protection | Supports cardioprotective research | | GHS-R1a binding | High affinity, similar to ghrelin | Validates target engagement | --- Safety Profile Established Effects from Human Studies | Effect | Incidence | Severity | Notes | |--------|-----------|----------|-------| | Flushing | Common | Mild | Transient, post-injection | | Increased appetite | Common | Mild | Ghrelin receptor-mediated | | Cortisol elevation | Dose-dependent | Mild | Minimal at doses ≤100 μg | | Prolactin elevation | Dose-dependent | Mild | Less than GHRP-2 | | Injection site reactions | Occasional | Mild | Standard peptide injection | | Dizziness | Occasional | Mild | Transient | Cortisol and Prolactin: Dose-Response Data Studies have characterized the non-selective effects: | Dose Level | Cortisol Effect | Prolactin Effect | |------------|-----------------|------------------| | ≤100 μg | Not altered | Not altered | | 100-200 μg | Minimal increase | Minimal increase | | >200 μg | Moderate increase | Moderate increase | | 400 μg/kg | Significant increase | Significant increase | Clinical relevance: - Effects are dose-dependent and transient - Less cortisol/prolactin elevation than GHRP-2 - Significantly more than ipamorelin (which has essentially none) - At high doses, could theoretically affect HPA axis with chronic use Comparison: Selectivity Profile | Effect | GHRP-6 | GHRP-2 | Ipamorelin | Hexarelin | |--------|--------|--------|------------|-----------| | GH release | Strong | Strong | Strong | Strong | | Cortisol increase | Moderate | Higher | None | Minimal | | Prolactin increase | Moderate | Higher | None | Minimal | | Appetite stimulation | Strong | Moderate | Minimal | Moderate | | Selectivity rating | Low | Low | High | Moderate | Theoretical Long-Term Concerns | Concern | Basis | Evidence Level | |---------|-------|----------------| | HPA axis effects | Repeated cortisol stimulation | Theoretical (no chronic data) | | Hyperprolactinemia | Chronic prolactin elevation | Theoretical (no chronic data) | | Metabolic effects | Chronic GH elevation | Same as any GH-elevating intervention | | Cancer risk | IGF-1 elevation | Same theoretical concern as GH therapy | Important: No long-term safety data exists for GHRP-6 in humans. All chronic use considerations are extrapolated from mechanism. Contraindications (Theoretical) Based on mechanism and comparisons to approved GH secretagogues: 1. Active malignancy (GH/IGF-1 concerns) 2. Uncontrolled diabetes (GH can worsen glucose control) 3. Conditions where cortisol elevation is contraindicated 4. Hyperprolactinemia or prolactin-sensitive conditions --- Pharmacokinetics Definitive Human PK Parameters From Gonzalez et al. (2012) PMID: 23099431 : | Parameter | Value | Notes | |-----------|-------|-------| | Distribution half-life (t½α) | 7.6 ± 1.9 min | Rapid tissue distribution | | Elimination half-life (t½β) | 2.5 ± 1.1 hours | Relatively short | | Oral bioavailability | 0.3% | Very poor—injectable route required | | AUC | Dose-proportional | Linear PK across 100-400 μg/kg | | Cmax | Dose-proportional | Immediate peak after IV | | GH peak response | 15-30 minutes | Rapid onset of effect | Administration Considerations | Route | Characteristics | |-------|-----------------| | Intravenous | Most potent; used in research studies | | Subcutaneous | Practical for repeated dosing; slightly delayed absorption | | Intranasal | Explored but inferior bioavailability | | Oral | Impractical due to 0.3% bioavailability | Comparison to Related Peptides | Parameter | GHRP-6 | GHRP-2 | Ipamorelin | Sermorelin | |-----------|--------|--------|------------|------------| | Half-life | 2.5 hours | 30 min | 2 hours | 10-20 min | | Oral bioavailability | 0.3% | Poor | Poor | Poor | | Dosing frequency | Multiple daily | Multiple daily | Multiple daily | Multiple daily | | GH onset | 15-30 min | 15-30 min | 15-30 min | 15-30 min | Special Observations Enterohepatic recirculation: The PK study noted atypical concentration spikes during the elimination phase in 4/9 subjects, suggesting possible enterohepatic recirculation. This could affect drug-drug interactions and accumulation with repeated dosing. --- Benefits & Risks Evidence Table Potential Benefits (Evidence-Graded) | Outcome | Evidence Type | Effect Size | Confidence | Key Citation | |---------|---------------|-------------|------------|--------------| | GH release | Human PK/PD | Robust, dose-dependent | MODERATE | Multiple studies | | Research tool for GHS-R1a | Established use | Standard | HIGH | Bowers et al. | | Cardioprotection | Preclinical | Reduced infarct size | LOW | PMID: 28270686 | | Stroke outcomes (with EGF) | Phase I/II | Improved vs control | VERY LOW | 2024 trial | | Wound healing | Preclinical | Enhanced repair | LOW | Multiple animal studies | Potential Risks (Evidence-Graded) | Risk | Evidence Type | Frequency | Severity | Confidence | |------|---------------|-----------|----------|------------| | Cortisol elevation | Human studies | Dose-dependent | Mild | MODERATE | | Prolactin elevation | Human studies | Dose-dependent | Mild | MODERATE | | Appetite increase | Human studies | Common | Mild | MODERATE | | Flushing | Human studies | Common | Mild | MODERATE | | Unknown long-term effects | None | Unknown | Unknown | N/A | Key Unknowns 1. Long-term safety — No chronic human exposure data 2. Efficacy for clinical endpoints — No Phase 3 trials completed 3. Optimal dosing regimen — Not established 4. Drug interactions — Not systematically studied 5. Effects in special populations — Elderly, pediatric, renal/hepatic impairment unknown 6. Cytoprotective efficacy in humans — Preclinical promise awaits clinical validation --- Inferences Section This section contains logical reasoning from known evidence. These are NOT proven facts. Inference 1: GHRP-6 may have clinical utility in myocardial protection (a) Statement: GHRP-6 could reduce damage from myocardial ischemia-reperfusion injury in humans. (b) Why someone might believe it: - Robust preclinical data showing reduced infarct size - Multiple mechanisms identified (antioxidant, anti-apoptotic, mitochondrial preservation) - CD36 receptor provides GH-independent cardioprotective pathway - Safe in acute dosing based on human PK studies (c) Supporting evidence: - Animal I/R models consistently show benefit PMID: 28270686 - Doxorubicin cardiotoxicity model shows protection PMID: 39267656 - Mechanistic studies show Bcl-2 upregulation, mitochondrial integrity - Broad safety profile in healthy volunteers supports feasibility (d) Contradicting evidence: - No human efficacy data

name: Glutathione
summary: The master antioxidant and most abundant intracellular thiol in mammalian cells. This naturally occurring tripeptide (glutamate-cysteine-glycine) is essential for detoxification, immune function, and cellular protection. Extensively studied with strong mechanistic understanding and growing clinical evidence.
content: Overview Overview Quick Summary - Naturally occurring tripeptide (glutamate-cysteine-glycine) found in virtually all cells - Most abundant intracellular antioxidant in mammalian tissues (1-10 mM concentrations) - Essential for detoxification of xenobiotics, drugs, and environmental toxins - Endogenous production declines with age and in many disease states - Extensively researched with thousands of publications spanning decades Evidence Strength: High - Well-characterized biochemistry with decades of research, multiple clinical trials, and clear mechanistic understanding. Debates remain regarding optimal supplementation strategies. Identity & Origins | Property | Value | |----------|-------| | Generic name | Glutathione (GSH) | | Chemical name | L-gamma-Glutamyl-L-cysteinyl-glycine | | Molecular formula | C10H17N3O6S | | Molecular weight | 307.32 g/mol | | Class | Tripeptide thiol antioxidant | | Discovery | Frederick Gowland Hopkins (1921) | Research Timeline - 1921: Discovery by Hopkins; named "glutathione" - 1935: Structure elucidated as tripeptide - 1970s-80s: Meister and colleagues define gamma-glutamyl cycle and biosynthesis - 1990s-present: Clinical applications in chemotherapy protection, liver disease, Parkinson's - 2010s-present: Bioavailability research; liposomal and sublingual forms developed The GSH/GSSG Redox Cycle Reduced GSH (active form) ↓ Encounters reactive oxygen species (ROS) ↓ Glutathione Peroxidase (GPx) catalyzes reaction ↓ GSH donates electrons → neutralizes ROS ↓ Oxidized GSSG formed (two GSH molecules linked) ↓ Glutathione Reductase (GR) + NADPH ↓ GSSG recycled back to 2 GSH molecules The ratio of reduced GSH to oxidized GSSG is a key indicator of cellular health. Healthy cells maintain GSH:GSSG ratios of 100:1 or higher. When this ratio drops, cells experience "oxidative stress." Biosynthesis Pathway Glutathione is synthesized in two ATP-dependent steps: Step 1: Rate-Limiting Glutamate + Cysteine + ATP ↓ Gamma-glutamylcysteine synthetase (GCS) ↓ gamma-Glutamylcysteine Step 2: Completion gamma-Glutamylcysteine + Glycine + ATP ↓ Glutathione synthetase (GS) ↓ Glutathione (GSH) Key regulation points: - Cysteine availability is usually rate-limiting for GSH synthesis - Nrf2 transcription factor upregulates GCS under oxidative stress - Feedback inhibition by GSH on GCS prevents overproduction This is why N-acetylcysteine (NAC) effectively raises glutathione levels - it provides the limiting cysteine substrate. Enzymatic Defense Systems | Enzyme | Function | Location | |--------|----------|----------| | Glutathione Peroxidases (GPx) | Reduce H2O2 and lipid peroxides | Cytosol, mitochondria | | Glutathione S-Transferases (GST) | Conjugate toxins for excretion | Liver, intestine | | Glutathione Reductase (GR) | Recycle GSSG back to GSH | All cells | | Glutaredoxins (Grx) | Protein deglutathionylation | Cytosol | | Gamma-glutamyltransferase (GGT) | Cleave extracellular GSH | Cell surface | Detoxification Functions Glutathione is the primary Phase II conjugation molecule in liver detoxification: Toxin/Drug/Carcinogen ↓ Phase I enzymes (CYP450) → activated intermediate ↓ Glutathione S-Transferases ↓ GSH conjugate (water-soluble) ↓ Exported via MRP transporters ↓ Excreted in bile/urine Clinical relevance: Acetaminophen overdose depletes hepatic GSH, allowing toxic NAPQI metabolite to cause liver necrosis. Treatment with NAC restores GSH. Immune System Modulation - T lymphocytes require intracellular GSH for proliferation and function - NK cells show 2-fold increased cytotoxicity with GSH supplementation - Macrophage respiratory burst depends on GSH-protected enzymes - Dendritic cells antigen presentation affected by GSH status S-Glutathionylation (Protein Regulation) Beyond antioxidant defense, GSH regulates protein function through reversible modification: Protein-SH + GSSG → Protein-S-SG (glutathionylated) This post-translational modification: - Protects protein thiols from irreversible oxidation - Regulates enzyme activity (eNOS, GAPDH, protein tyrosine phosphatases) - Modulates transcription factors - Acts as redox signaling mechanism --- Research Summary Evidence Overview | Category | Count | Notes | |----------|-------|-------| | Human clinical trials | 20+ | Various conditions and delivery methods | | Bioavailability studies | 8 | Oral, liposomal, sublingual, IV | | Mechanistic reviews | 50+ | Extremely well-characterized | | Disease association studies | Hundreds | GSH depletion in many conditions | | Total sources reviewed | 35 | 22 open access | Key Research Areas 1. Chemotherapy protection: IV GSH reduces cisplatin/oxaliplatin neurotoxicity 2. Liver disease: Hepatoprotection in NAFLD, cirrhosis, acetaminophen toxicity 3. Neurodegeneration: GSH depletion in Parkinson's and Alzheimer's disease 4. Aging/longevity: GSH decline as hallmark of aging; restoration improves markers 5. Skin/cosmetic: Melanin inhibition, skin lightening (popular in Asia) 6. Immune function: HIV/AIDS, infection susceptibility 7. Respiratory: Cystic fibrosis, COPD, pulmonary fibrosis --- Key Human Trials Bioavailability Studies | Study | Population | Intervention | Key Findings | |-------|------------|--------------|--------------| | Richie et al. 2015 | 54 healthy adults | 250-1000mg oral GSH, 6 months | 30-35% increase in blood GSH; reduced oxidative markers | | Sinha et al. 2018 | 12 healthy adults | 500-1000mg liposomal GSH, 2 weeks | 40% increase in whole blood GSH; 2-fold NK cell cytotoxicity | | Schmitt et al. 2015 | 20 healthy adults | 250mg sublingual vs 1000mg oral | Sublingual achieved higher plasma levels than 4x oral dose | | Park et al. 2014 | Japanese adults | Single oral dose | Demonstrated protein-bound GSH increase post-oral | | Yamada et al. 2022 | 16 healthy adults | Glucosyl-GSH | 1.65-fold higher plasma AUC vs standard GSH | Clinical Applications Chemotherapy Neuroprotection: | Study | Population | Intervention | Results | |-------|------------|--------------|---------| | Cascinu et al. 2002 | 52 colorectal cancer | IV GSH 1500mg before oxaliplatin | Neurotoxicity: 4% vs 33% placebo | | Smyth et al. 1997 | 151 ovarian cancer | IV GSH 3g/m2 with cisplatin | Reduced nephro/neurotoxicity; preserved chemo efficacy | Parkinson's Disease: | Study | Design | Findings | |-------|--------|----------| | Sechi et al. 1996 | Open-label, n=9 | IV GSH 600mg twice daily x30 days: 42% disability reduction | | Hauser et al. 2009 | RCT, n=21 | IV GSH 1400mg 3x/week x4 weeks: no significant benefit vs placebo | Non-Alcoholic Fatty Liver Disease: | Study | Population | Intervention | Results | |-------|------------|--------------|---------| | Honda et al. 2017 | 34 NAFLD patients | 300mg oral GSH, 4 months | Improved ALT and fatty liver markers | Skin Lightening Studies (Asia-Pacific) | Study | Population | Intervention | Results | |-------|------------|--------------|---------| | Arjinpathana et al. 2012 | 60 Thai medical students | 500mg oral GSH twice daily, 4 weeks | Significant melanin index reduction | | Weschawalit et al. 2017 | 60 healthy volunteers | 500mg oral GSH, 4 weeks | Reduced melanin, improved elasticity | | Watanabe et al. 2014 | 30 Filipino women | 2% topical GSSG, 10 weeks | Improved skin brightness and moisture | --- Preclinical Evidence Mechanistic Studies | Area | Key Findings | |------|--------------| | Oxidative stress | GSH depletion precedes cell death in all oxidative stress models | | Mitochondrial function | mtGSH essential for mitochondrial integrity and ATP production | | Drug metabolism | GST-mediated conjugation primary Phase II detoxification | | Gene expression | Nrf2-ARE pathway controls GSH synthetic enzyme transcription | Animal Models | Model | Findings | |-------|----------| | GSH synthesis knockout | Embryonic lethal; demonstrates essentiality | | GCS knockdown | Rapid oxidative damage, mitochondrial dysfunction | | Aging rodents | GSH supplementation improved lifespan markers | | Toxin challenge | GSH depletion enhances hepatotoxicity, nephrotoxicity | Disease Association Studies GSH depletion is documented in numerous conditions: | Disease Category | Conditions with GSH Depletion | |-----------------|------------------------------| | Neurodegenerative | Parkinson's (30-40% reduction in substantia nigra), Alzheimer's, ALS | | Metabolic | Type 2 diabetes, metabolic syndrome, NAFLD | | Pulmonary | COPD, cystic fibrosis, pulmonary fibrosis, asthma | | Infectious | HIV/AIDS, tuberculosis, sepsis | | Cardiovascular | Atherosclerosis, heart failure | | Cancer | Tumor cells often have elevated GSH (resistance mechanism) | | Autoimmune | Rheumatoid arthritis, lupus | --- Safety Profile General Safety Glutathione has an excellent safety profile as an endogenous molecule: | Route | Safety Data | |-------|-------------| | Oral | Well-tolerated; GRAS status; no serious adverse events in trials | | IV | Used clinically in some countries; transient effects at high doses | | Liposomal | Similar to oral; enhanced absorption | | Topical | Generally well-tolerated; may cause mild irritation | | Inhaled | Studied in cystic fibrosis; bronchospasm possible | Known Considerations | Consideration | Notes | |---------------|-------| | GI symptoms | Occasional bloating, cramping with high oral doses | | Sulfur smell | Some formulations have sulfur odor/taste | | Zinc depletion | Theoretical concern with chronic high-dose use | | Skin lightening | Cosmetic concern; mechanism involves tyrosinase inhibition | Contraindications and Precautions - Asthma: Inhaled GSH may trigger bronchospasm - Organ transplant: Theoretical immune modulation concerns - Chemotherapy: Time administration to avoid interference (though studies show no efficacy reduction) Quality Considerations | Form | Stability Notes | |------|-----------------| | Reduced GSH | Oxidizes readily; requires proper storage | | Liposomal | Better stability; phospholipid protection | | Acetyl-GSH | Improved stability vs reduced GSH | | S-acetyl-L-glutathione | Enhanced oral bioavailability claims | --- Pharmacokinetics Oral Bioavailability Debate Historically, oral GSH was thought to be poorly absorbed due to intestinal and hepatic degradation. Recent research challenges this: | Study | Finding | |-------|---------| | Richie et al. 2015 | 6-month oral GSH significantly increased body stores | | Park et al. 2014 | Protein-bound GSH increased after oral administration | | Schmitt et al. 2015 | Sublingual absorption superior to oral | Pharmacokinetic Parameters | Parameter | Value | Notes | |-----------|-------|-------| | Plasma half-life | 15-30 minutes (free GSH) | Rapidly taken up by tissues | | Intracellular t1/2 | Hours to days | Pool turnover varies by tissue | | Oral bioavailability | Variable (10-30% estimated) | Form-dependent; debated | | Distribution | All tissues; highest in liver | 1-10 mM intracellular concentrations | | Metabolism | Gamma-glutamyltransferase cleaves | Amino acids recycled | Enhanced Delivery Forms | Form | Mechanism | Evidence | |------|-----------|----------| | Liposomal GSH | Phospholipid encapsulation protects from degradation | 40% increase in 2 weeks (Sinha et al.) | | Sublingual GSH | Bypasses GI/hepatic first-pass | Superior to oral (Schmitt et al.) | | S-acetyl-GSH | Acetyl group improves membrane permeability | Limited clinical data | | Glucosyl-GSH | Glucose conjugate enhances absorption | 1.65-fold higher AUC (Yamada et al.) | | IV GSH | 100% bioavailability | Used in clinical settings | Endogenous Levels and Age-Related Decline | Age Group | Approximate Plasma GSH | Notes | |-----------|----------------------|-------| | Young adults (20-30) | 800-1200 umol/L | Reference range | | Middle age (40-50) | 600-900 umol/L | 15% decline per decade | | Elderly (60+) | 400-700 umol/L | Often 30-50% lower than young | --- Regulatory Status | Region | Status | Notes | |--------|--------|-------| | USA (FDA) | GRAS as food ingredient; dietary supplement | Not drug-approved for specific indications | | EU | Food supplement | Regulated as food | | Japan | Functional food ingredient | Popular in cosmetics and supplements | | WADA | Not prohibited | Endogenous substance | | Australia (TGA) | Listed medicine | Available as supplement | Clinical Use - IV glutathione used clinically in some countries for: - Chemotherapy-induced toxicity prevention - Liver disease support - Parkinson's disease (limited evidence) - Oral/liposomal available as dietary supplements worldwide - Topical in cosmetic formulations --- Related Compounds GSH Precursors and Boosters | Compound | Mechanism | Evidence Level | |----------|-----------|----------------| | N-Acetylcysteine (NAC) | Provides cysteine substrate for GSH synthesis | High - FDA-approved for acetaminophen toxicity | | Alpha-Lipoic Acid | Regenerates GSH; induces synthesis | Moderate | | Whey Protein | Cysteine-rich; increases GSH | Moderate | | Glycine + Cysteine | Direct precursors | Moderate (Sekhar et al.) | | Selenium | Required for glutathione peroxidase | High | | Milk Thistle (Silymarin) | Increases hepatic GSH | Moderate | Comparison: GSH vs NAC | Aspect | Glutathione | N-Acetylcysteine | |--------|-------------|------------------| | Mechanism | Direct supplementation | Provides cysteine for synthesis | | Bioavailability | Debated; form-dependent | Well-absorbed orally | | Clinical approval | Supplement only | FDA-approved drug | | Cost | Higher | Lower | | Evidence | Growing | Extensive | --- Natural Sources and Enhancement Dietary Sources of GSH | Food | Approximate GSH Content | |------|------------------------| | Asparagus | 28 mg/100g (highest vegetable) | | Avocado | 27 mg/100g | | Spinach | 11 mg/100g | | Okra | 11 mg/100g | | Broccoli | 9 mg/100g | Foods That Boost GSH Synthesis | Category | Examples | Mechanism | |----------|----------|-----------| | Sulfur-rich vegetables | Garlic, onions, cruciferous | Provide sulfur amino acids | | Whey protein | Undenatured whey | Cysteine source | | Selenium-rich foods | Brazil nuts, fish | GPx cofactor | | Alpha-lipoic acid foods | Organ meats, spinach | GSH recycling | Traditional Medicine Connections Several traditional herbs have been found to enhance GSH: | Herb | Tradition | Mechanism | |------|-----------|-----------| | Milk Thistle (Silymarin) | European | Increases hepatic GSH; Nrf2 activation | | Amla (Emblica officinalis) | Ayurvedic/Chinese | Increased GSH in uremic patients | | Rhodiola rosea | Traditional adaptogen | Enhances cellular GSH | | Turmeric (Curcumin) | Ayurvedic | Nrf2 activation; GSH synthesis | | Astragalus | Traditional Chinese | Antioxidant pathway support | --- Current Research Directions Active Investigation Areas 1. Optimizing bioavailability: Novel delivery systems, nanoformulations 2. Neurodegeneration: GSH restoration strategies for Parkinson's, Alzheimer's 3. Aging interventions: GSH as longevity target; precursor supplementation 4. Cancer metabolism: Dual role - protective in normal cells, resistance factor in tumors 5. Gut-liver axis: Microbiome influence on GSH metabolism 6. COVID-19/respiratory: GSH depletion in severe illness; supplementation studies Emerging Research | Area | Recent Findings | |------|-----------------| | Microbiome | Gut bacteria influence host GSH metabolism | | Epigenetics | GSH status affects DNA methylation | | Mitochondrial medicine | mtGSH as therapeutic target | | Personalized dosing | Genetic polymorphisms affect GSH needs | --- Citation Table Foundational Reviews | Source | Year | Type | Key Finding | Access | |--------|------|------|-------------|--------| | Meister A, Anderson ME PMID:6137189 ( | 1983 | Review | Landmark review establishing glutathione biochemistry | Paywalled | | Forman HJ et al. name: Human Chorionic Gonadotropin (hCG)
summary: A glycoprotein hormone FDA-approved for ovulation induction, cryptorchidism, and hypogonadotropic hypogonadism. Functions as an LH receptor agonist with over 50 years of clinical use in reproductive medicine. Gold standard trigger for assisted reproductive technology.
content: Overview Quick Summary - FDA-approved glycoprotein hormone for ovulation induction in ART, cryptorchidism in prepubertal males, and hypogonadotropic hypogonadism - Gold standard ovulation trigger in assisted reproductive technology (IVF/ICSI) with over 50 years of clinical experience - Strong evidence base: Multiple Cochrane systematic reviews, Phase 3 RCTs, and international fertility society guidelines (ASRM, ESHRE) - Two formulation types: Urinary-derived (Pregnyl, Novarel) and recombinant (Ovidrel/choriogonadotropin alfa) with equivalent efficacy - Well-characterized safety: Primary concern is ovarian hyperstimulation syndrome (OHSS) in susceptible patients; otherwise well-tolerated - Physiologic basis: Mimics luteinizing hormone (LH) action at the LH/hCG receptor to trigger ovulation and support corpus luteum function Evidence Strength: HIGH — Supported by extensive clinical experience spanning decades, multiple Cochrane systematic reviews, FDA approval for specific indications, and international guideline endorsement. One of the most thoroughly characterized hormones in reproductive medicine. -------|-------| | Generic name | Human Chorionic Gonadotropin (hCG) | | Recombinant name | Choriogonadotropin alfa | | Brand names (urinary) | Pregnyl, Novarel, Profasi, Choragon | | Brand names (recombinant) | Ovidrel | | Chemical class | Glycoprotein hormone (heterodimeric) | | Molecular weight | 36,700 Da (varies with glycosylation) | | Structure | Alpha subunit (92 aa) + beta subunit (145 aa) | | CAS Number | 9002-61-3 | Molecular Structure hCG is a heterodimeric glycoprotein consisting of two non-covalently linked subunits: Alpha subunit (92 amino acids): - Common to all glycoprotein hormones (TSH, LH, FSH, hCG) - Contains two N-linked glycosylation sites - Provides structural scaffold for receptor binding Beta subunit (145 amino acids): - Unique to hCG; confers biological specificity - Contains two N-linked and four O-linked glycosylation sites - The C-terminal extension (CTP, 24 amino acids) is unique to hCG and prolongs half-life - 85% sequence homology with LH beta subunit Glycosylation significance: The extensive glycosylation of hCG serves critical functions: 1. Protects against proteolytic degradation 2. Extends plasma half-life (24-36 hours vs LH's 20 minutes) 3. Influences receptor binding affinity and signaling 4. Creates molecular heterogeneity (multiple isoforms exist) Discovery & Development Timeline | Year | Milestone | Significance | |------|-----------|--------------| | 1920 | Pregnancy factor identified | Aschheim and Zondek detected gonadotropic activity in pregnancy urine | | 1927 | "Prolan" described | First isolation of hCG from pregnant women's urine | | 1930s | Structure begins | Recognized as protein hormone; early purification methods | | 1937 | First clinical use | hCG used to induce ovulation in anovulatory women | | 1954 | Subunit structure | Discovery of alpha/beta heterodimeric structure | | 1970s | Commercial production | Urinary hCG products (Pregnyl, Profasi) widely available | | 1981 | Crystal structure | Pierce & Parsons describe glycoprotein hormone structure PMID: 6267989 | | 1994 | 3D structure solved | Lapthorn et al. determine hCG crystal structure PMID: 8202136 | | 2000 | FDA approval (Ovidrel) | Recombinant hCG (choriogonadotropin alfa) approved | | 2002 | LH/hCG receptor review | Ascoli et al. comprehensive receptor characterization PMID: 11943741 | | 2010 | Biology review | Cole's definitive review of hCG functions PMID: 20735820 | | 2014 | Cochrane review | Systematic comparison of hCG vs GnRH agonist trigger PMID: 25358904 | | 2016 | ASRM guidelines | OHSS prevention and management guideline PMID: 27678032 | | 2020 | ESHRE guidelines | Current European ovarian stimulation guidelines PMID: 32395637 | Why hCG Was Developed for Fertility Natural hCG is produced by the placenta during pregnancy to maintain the corpus luteum and support early gestation. Pharmaceutical hCG development arose from several observations: 1. Physiologic similarity to LH: hCG binds the same receptor as luteinizing hormone but has a longer half-life (24-36 hours vs 20 minutes for LH) 2. Available source: Pregnant women's urine provided abundant raw material before recombinant technology 3. Predictable ovulation timing: Exogenous hCG triggers predictable ovulation 36-40 hours post-administration 4. Corpus luteum support: Maintains progesterone production until placenta takes over Original therapeutic targets: Anovulatory infertility and cryptorchidism (undescended testes). The fertility application expanded dramatically with the development of IVF in the late 1970s. --- Mechanism of Action How hCG Works (Plain English) hCG is sometimes called "the pregnancy hormone," but its pharmaceutical use is about mimicking a different hormone called LH (luteinizing hormone). Here's what it does: 1. Triggers ovulation — In women undergoing fertility treatment, hCG tells the mature egg follicles "time to release." This happens about 36-40 hours after injection, giving doctors a precise window to retrieve eggs for IVF or time intercourse/insemination. 2. Supports the corpus luteum — After ovulation, the empty follicle becomes the corpus luteum, which produces progesterone to prepare the uterus for pregnancy. hCG keeps this structure alive and functioning, like keeping the "pregnancy support system" running. 3. Stimulates testosterone production (in males) — In men and boys, hCG tells the testicles to produce testosterone. This is useful for treating undescended testicles in boys or maintaining testicular function in men with certain hormonal conditions. 4. Mimics LH with a longer duration — Natural LH disappears from the blood within 20 minutes. hCG lasts 24-36 hours, providing sustained stimulation. It's like comparing a camera flash to leaving the lights on. Why hCG works better than LH for treatment: The body clears natural LH too quickly for practical use. hCG has a special "tail" (the C-terminal peptide) and sugar molecules attached that make it last much longer, so one injection provides the sustained signal needed for ovulation or testosterone production. --- Receptor Target: LH/hCG Receptor (Scientific Detail) hCG binds to the lutropin/choriogonadotropin receptor (LHCGR) , a G protein-coupled receptor (GPCR) of the rhodopsin-like (Class A) family. This receptor is shared with luteinizing hormone (LH). LHCGR expression and functional effects: | Tissue | Expression Level | Primary Effect | Clinical Relevance | |--------|------------------|----------------|--------------------| | Ovarian granulosa cells | High | Triggers final oocyte maturation | Ovulation induction | | Ovarian theca cells | High | Stimulates androgen synthesis | Estrogen precursors | | Corpus luteum | Very high | Maintains progesterone secretion | Luteal support | | Leydig cells (testis) | High | Stimulates testosterone synthesis | Hypogonadism, cryptorchidism | | Uterus | Moderate | Affects implantation, myometrial quiescence | Pregnancy support | | Extragonadal tissues | Low | Various (angiogenesis, immune modulation) | Research interest | Signaling Pathway Overview Primary pathway (Galphas/cAMP): hCG binds LHCGR extracellular domain | Receptor activation (transmembrane conformational change) | Galphas protein activation --> adenylyl cyclase --> cAMP increase | Protein kinase A (PKA) activation | +-- Granulosa cells: Triggers meiosis resumption, cumulus expansion +-- Theca cells: Androstenedione synthesis +-- Corpus luteum: Progesterone synthesis enzymes (StAR, P450scc) +-- Leydig cells: Testosterone biosynthesis (StAR, P450c17) In simpler terms: When hCG binds to its receptor, it triggers a cascade of signals inside the cell. The key messenger is cAMP (cyclic AMP), which acts like an "on switch" for various enzymes. In the ovary, this leads to ovulation and progesterone production. In the testis, it leads to testosterone production. Secondary pathways: LHCGR also activates: - Phospholipase C/IP3 pathway (calcium signaling) - MAPK/ERK pathway (gene transcription, cell differentiation) - Beta-arrestin recruitment (receptor internalization, alternative signaling) Supported Mechanisms (Direct Human Evidence) Ovulation triggering: - hCG injection induces LH surge-like effects Cochrane PMID: 25358904 - Meiosis resumption occurs within 24-36 hours in mature follicles - Cumulus-oocyte complex expansion enables oocyte release - Ovulation timing is highly predictable (36-40 hours post-injection) Luteal support: - hCG maintains corpus luteum steroidogenesis PMID: 20735820 - Progesterone levels sustained above 10 ng/mL threshold for implantation - Effect duration matches hCG half-life; may need repeated doses Testicular stimulation: - hCG stimulates Leydig cell testosterone production PMID: 15713727 - Maintains intratesticular testosterone during exogenous testosterone use - May support spermatogenesis by maintaining seminiferous tubule environment hCG vs LH: Key Differences | Property | hCG | LH | |----------|-----|-----| | Source | Placenta (natural); pregnant urine or recombinant (pharmaceutical) | Pituitary gland | | Half-life | 24-36 hours | 20 minutes | | Receptor | LHCGR | LHCGR (same receptor) | | Glycosylation | Extensive (8 sites) | Less (2 sites) | | Potency | Slightly higher receptor affinity | Lower affinity, faster clearance | | Clinical use | Pharmaceutical (ovulation trigger) | Endogenous only | Why the half-life difference matters: LH's short half-life is physiologically appropriate for pulsatile hypothalamic-pituitary signaling. hCG's long half-life allows a single injection to provide sustained receptor stimulation equivalent to the natural LH surge, which can last 48-50 hours. --- Too Much vs Too Little: Physiologic Context Native hCG Physiology In pregnancy: - hCG is produced by trophoblast cells beginning at implantation ( 6-7 days post-fertilization) - Levels double every 48-72 hours in early pregnancy - Peak levels: 50,000-100,000 mIU/mL at 8-10 weeks gestation - Primary function: Maintains corpus luteum progesterone production until placenta takes over ( 10-12 weeks) Normal non-pregnant state: - hCG is undetectable or <5 mIU/mL - Detection indicates pregnancy (or rarely, certain tumors) LH Physiology (What hCG Mimics) Normal menstrual cycle: - Pulsatile LH release throughout follicular phase - LH surge (10-20x baseline) triggers ovulation mid-cycle - Surge lasts 48 hours; ovulation occurs 36 hours after surge onset - Luteal phase LH supports corpus luteum initially In men: - Pulsatile LH maintains Leydig cell testosterone production - LH pulses occur approximately every 90-120 minutes - Feedback loop with testosterone regulates secretion What Happens When hCG Is Used Therapeutically Ovulation induction setting: - Single injection of hCG (5,000-10,000 IU urinary; 250 mcg recombinant) - Creates "artificial LH surge" lasting 24-36 hours - More predictable and controllable than waiting for natural surge - Risk: Excessive stimulation can cause OHSS in susceptible patients Corpus luteum support: - Multiple hCG injections or progesterone supplementation - Maintains luteal function when natural LH is suppressed - Common after GnRH agonist protocols in ART Male hypogonadism setting: - hCG stimulates testicular testosterone production - Used when pituitary function is impaired (hypogonadotropic hypogonadism) - Can maintain spermatogenesis when combined with FSH Tolerance and Adaptation | Observation | Evidence | Clinical Implication | |-------------|----------|---------------------| | Receptor desensitization | Chronic high-dose hCG can downregulate LHCGR | Pulsatile or intermittent dosing preferred in long-term use | | Antibody formation | Rare with modern preparations | More common historically with impure urinary products | | Ovarian hyperstimulation | Risk highest with high hCG + multiple follicles | Requires careful monitoring and patient selection | | Testicular desensitization | Can occur with excessive dosing in males | Appropriate dose titration important | In simpler terms: The body's cells can become less responsive to hCG if given too much for too long. This is why fertility doctors use single trigger doses rather than continuous infusions, and why long-term male use requires careful dosing. The receptor essentially "turns down the volume" when overstimulated. --- Key Human Trials Ovulation Trigger Comparisons (Cochrane Review) Youssef et al. 2014 PMID: 25358904 — Definitive Cochrane systematic review comparing hCG trigger vs GnRH agonist trigger in assisted reproduction. | Parameter | Value | |-----------|-------| | Design | Systematic review of 17 RCTs | | N | 1,847 women | | Population | Women undergoing IVF/ICSI with GnRH antagonist protocols | | Primary outcome | Live birth rate; OHSS incidence | | hCG live birth rate | Higher than GnRH agonist trigger | | OHSS risk | Significantly higher with hCG (OR 3.54) | Key conclusions: - hCG trigger produces higher live birth rates than GnRH agonist trigger in standard settings - GnRH agonist trigger virtually eliminates OHSS but at cost of lower pregnancy rates (unless modified luteal support used) - hCG remains gold standard trigger when OHSS risk is acceptable - In high OHSS risk patients, consider GnRH agonist trigger with intensive luteal support Recombinant vs Urinary hCG Al-Inany et al. Cochrane 2005 PMID: 15846678 | Parameter | Value | |-----------|-------| | Design | Systematic review of RCTs | | N | 2,000+ women | | Comparison | Recombinant hCG (rhCG) vs urinary hCG (uhCG) | | Primary outcome | Ongoing pregnancy/live birth per woman randomized | Key findings: - No significant difference in pregnancy rates between rhCG and uhCG - No significant difference in OHSS rates - rhCG offers more consistent product quality (batch-to-batch uniformity) - Patient preference may favor rhCG (prefilled syringe, subcutaneous administration) European Recombinant hCG Study Group 2000 PMID: 10875848 Pivotal Phase 3 trial establishing rhCG equivalence to uhCG: - 228 women undergoing ART randomized to rhCG (250 mcg) vs uhCG (5,000 IU) - Similar number of oocytes retrieved - Similar fertilization and pregnancy rates - Led to FDA approval of Ovidrel (choriogonadotropin alfa) hCG in Cryptorchidism Pyorala et al. Meta-analysis 1995 PMID: 7673426 | Parameter | Value | |-----------|-------| | Design | Meta-analysis of 33 studies | | Population | Boys with cryptorchidism (undescended testes) | | Treatment | Various hCG regimens | | Success rate | 19% overall descent rate | Key findings: - hCG treatment achieves testicular descent in approximately 1 in 5 cases - Success rate higher in retractile testes than true cryptorchidism - Most guidelines now recommend surgery (orchiopexy) as primary treatment - hCG may still be used in selected cases or pre-operatively hCG for Intratesticular Testosterone Preservation Coviello et al. 2005 PMID: 15713727 | Parameter | Value | |-----------|-------| | Design | Randomized controlled trial | | N | 29 normal men | | Intervention | Testosterone + varying hCG doses | | Primary outcome | Intratesticular testosterone (ITT) | Key findings: - Exogenous testosterone suppresses endogenous LH, collapsing intratesticular testosterone - hCG 125 IU every other day maintained ITT at 25% of baseline - hCG 250 IU every other day maintained ITT at 50% of baseline - hCG 500 IU every other day restored ITT to near-normal levels - Supports hCG use for fertility preservation in men on testosterone therapy OHSS Prevention Guidelines ASRM Practice Committee 2016 PMID: 27678032 Key recommendations regarding hCG: 1. Identify high-risk patients (PCOS, high AFC, prior OHSS) 2. Consider GnRH agonist trigger instead of hCG in highest-risk patients 3. If hCG used in at-risk patients, consider lower dose (5,000 IU instead of 10,000 IU) 4. "Freeze-all" strategy (no fresh transfer) reduces OHSS severity 5. Dopamine agonists (cabergoline) may reduce OHSS risk when hCG trigger used --- Preclinical Evidence Animal Studies | Study Type | Model | Key Findings | Relevance | |------------|-------|--------------|-----------| | Receptor characterization | Rodent, primate | LHCGR structure and signaling pathways | Foundation for mechanism | | Ovulation timing | Various mammals | Predictable ovulation 36-40h post-hCG | Guides clinical timing | | Corpus luteum support | Primates | hCG maintains CL progesterone production | Supports luteal phase use | | Spermatogenesis | Rodents, primates | hCG stimulates intratesticular testosterone | Supports male applications | | Dose-response | Multiple species | Ceiling effect for ovulation; excess causes adverse effects | Informs dosing | In Vitro Studies Receptor binding: - hCG binds LHCGR with high affinity (Kd 0.1-1 nM) - Slightly higher affinity than native LH - Both subunits required for receptor activation - Glycosylation affects binding kinetics and signaling potency Cell signaling: - Primary cAMP pathway activation confirmed in multiple cell types - Dose-dependent steroidogenesis in granulosa/theca/Leydig cell cultures - Receptor internalization and recycling characterized Limitations of Preclinical Data - Rodent ovarian physiology differs from humans (multiple ovulation normal) - Primate studies most relevant but limited by ethical/practical constraints - In vitro systems may not fully replicate in vivo complexity - Species differences in LHCGR expression patterns --- Safety Profile Common Adverse Events | Adverse Event | Incidence | Severity | Notes | |---------------|-----------|----------|-------| | Injection site reaction | 5-10% | Mild | Redness, swelling, pain at injection site | | Headache | 3-5% | Mild | Usually transient | | Fatigue | 2-5% | Mild | May be related to hormonal changes | | Breast tenderness | Variable | Mild | Related to estrogen elevation | | Bloating/abdominal discomfort | Variable | Mild-moderate | Common during ovarian stimulation | | Mood changes | Variable | Mild | Hormonal fluctuation effects | Serious Adverse Events and Warnings | Risk | Evidence Type | Frequency | Current Understanding | |------|---------------|-----------|----------------------| | Ovarian Hyperstimulation Syndrome (OHSS) | Clinical trials, post-marketing | 1-5% moderate; <1% severe | Primary safety concern; dose and patient factors determine risk | | Multiple pregnancy | Clinical trials | Increased with ovulation induction | Related to multiple follicle development, not hCG specifically | | Ectopic pregnancy | Clinical trials | Slightly increased in ART | General ART risk, not hCG-specific | | Ovarian torsion | Case reports | Rare | Related to enlarged ovaries during stimulation | | Thromboembolic events | Case reports, severe OHSS | Rare | Associated with severe OHSS, not hCG directly | Ovarian Hyperstimulation Syndrome: Detailed Assessment What is OHSS: OHSS is an iatrogenic complication of ovarian stimulation characterized by: - Ovarian enlargement with multiple cysts - Fluid shift from intravascular to third space (ascites, pleural effusion) - Hemoconcentration, electrolyte disturbances - In severe cases: renal failure, thromboembolic events, ARDS OHSS Classification: | Grade | Symptoms | Management | |-------|----------|------------| | Mild | Abdominal bloating, mild pain, ovaries <8 cm | Outpatient monitoring | | Moderate | Nausea, vomiting, ultrasound evidence of ascites | Close monitoring, hydration | | Severe | Tense ascites, oliguria, hemoconcentration | Hospitalization required | | Critical | Renal failure, ARDS, thromboembolism | ICU care | Risk factors for OHSS: 1. Young age (less than 35 years) 2. Low body weight 3. Polycystic ovary syndrome (PCOS) 4. High antral follicle count (>24) 5. High estradiol levels during stimulation 6. Large number of follicles (>25) 7. Previous OHSS history 8. Use of hCG for trigger (vs GnRH agonist) Why hCG contributes to OHSS: - hCG's long half-life provides sustained LHCGR stimulation - Continued stimulation of multiple follicles and corpora lutea - VEGF (vascular endothelial growth factor) release drives capillary leak - Endogenous hCG from early pregnancy perpetuates and worsens OHSS Prevention strategies: - GnRH agonist trigger in high-risk patients - Coasting (withholding gonadotropins) if estradiol very high - Freeze-all embryo strategy (avoids pregnancy hCG contribution) - Lower hCG trigger doses - Dopamine agonist co-treatment Contraindications 1. Primary ovarian failure (elevated FSH, no follicle development) 2. Uncontrolled thyroid or adrenal dysfunction 3. Sex hormone-dependent tumors (certain breast, ovarian, prostate cancers) 4. Abnormal uterine bleeding of undetermined origin 5. Ovarian cyst or enlargement not due to PCOS 6. Prior allergic reaction to hCG products Special Populations | Population | Recommendation | Notes | |------------|----------------|-------| | Pregnancy | Contraindicated for ovulation induction | Natural hCG rises; exogenous not needed | | Breastfeeding | Limited data | Generally avoided during active breastfeeding | | Renal impairment | Caution | May affect clearance; monitor closely | | Hepatic impairment | Caution | Limited data | | Prepubertal males | FDA approved (cryptorchidism) | Specific dosing for pediatric use | --- Pharmacokinetics Urinary hCG (Pregnyl, Novarel) | Parameter | Value | Clinical Relevance | |-----------|-------|-------------------| | Bioavailability | 60% (IM) | Intramuscular injection standard | | Tmax | 6-8 hours | Peak levels several hours post-injection | | Half-life | 29-36 hours | Supports single-dose triggering | | Volume of distribution | 5-6 L | Primarily intravascular | | Metabolism | Hepatic, renal | Degraded to subunits and fragments | | Elimination | Primarily renal | Beta subunit fragments detectable in urine | Recombinant hCG (Ovidrel/Choriogonadotropin alfa) | Parameter | Value | Clinical Relevance | |-----------|-------|-------------------| | Bioavailability | 40% (SC) | Subcutaneous administration | | Tmax | 12-24 hours | Slightly delayed vs IM urinary | | Half-life | 26-32 hours | Similar to urinary hCG | | Equivalence | 250 mcg rhCG equivalent to 5,000-10,000 IU uhCG | Based on clinical efficacy studies | Clinical Dosing Context Ovulation trigger (standard IVF): - Urinary hCG: 5,000-10,000 IU intramuscularly - Recombinant hCG: 250 mcg subcutaneously - Single injection administered 34-36 hours before scheduled oocyte retrieval Luteal support: - If used (vs progesterone): 1,500-2,500 IU every 3-4 days - Risk: May exacerbate OHSS; progesterone often preferred Cryptorchidism (prepubertal males): - Various regimens; typically 1,000-4,000 IU 2-3 times weekly for 4-6 weeks - Consult current pediatric endocrinology guidelines Hypogonadotropic hypogonadism (adult males): - 1,000-2,000 IU 2-3 times weekly - Often combined with FSH (hMG or recombinant FSH) for spermatogenesis --- Benefits & Risks Evidence Table Potential Benefits (Evidence-Graded) | Outcome | Evidence Type | Effect Size | Confidence | Key Citation | |---------|---------------|-------------|------------|--------------| | Ovulation triggering for oocyte retrieval | Cochrane SR, Phase 3 RCTs | 36-40h predictable ovulation | HIGH | Youssef 2014 PMID: 25358904 | | Higher live birth vs GnRH agonist trigger | Cochrane SR | Significantly higher | HIGH | Youssef 2014 PMID: 25358904 | | Luteal support efficacy | RCTs | Comparable to progesterone | HIGH | Multiple studies | | Testicular descent (cryptorchidism) | Meta-analysis | 19% success rate | MODERATE | Pyorala 1995 PMID: 7673426 | | Testosterone production (males) | RCTs | Dose-dependent stimulation | HIGH | Coviello 2005 PMID: 15713727 | | Intratesticular testosterone preservation | RCT | Maintained at near-normal | HIGH | Coviello 2005 PMID: 15713727 | Potential Risks (Evidence-Graded) | Risk | Evidence Type | Frequency | Severity | Confidence | Citation | |------|---------------|-----------|----------|------------|----------| | OHSS | Clinical trials, Cochrane | 1-5% moderate; <1% severe | Moderate-severe | HIGH | ASRM 2016 PMID: 27678032 | | Multiple pregnancy | Clinical trials | Increased vs spontaneous conception | Moderate | HIGH | Multiple studies | | Injection site reactions | Clinical trials | 5-10% | Mild | HIGH | Product labeling | | Headache | Clinical trials | 3-5% | Mild | HIGH | Product labeling | | Ectopic pregnancy | Clinical trials | Slightly increased | Serious | MODERATE | ART outcomes data | Key Unknowns 1. Long-term offspring outcomes — Limited follow-up data on children conceived through ART with hCG 2. Optimal dosing for different populations — Personalized dosing approaches not well established 3. Novel formulation effects — Long-acting hCG analogs in development; efficacy/safety unknown 4. Genetic predictors of response — Pharmacogenomics of hCG response not well characterized --- Inferences Section This section contains logical reasoning from known evidence. These are NOT proven facts. Inference 1:

name: Hexarelin
summary: The most potent synthetic GHRP (Growth Hormone Releasing Peptide), a hexapeptide that strongly stimulates GH release via the ghrelin receptor. Notable for cardioprotective effects independent of GH release. Development discontinued due to rapid desensitization with repeated dosing. Italian research leads global investigation.
content: Overview Quick Summary - Most potent synthetic growth hormone secretagogue in the GHRP family - Hexapeptide structure: His-D-2-methyl-Trp-Ala-Trp-D-Phe-Lys-NH2 - Not FDA approved for any indication; Phase 2 development discontinued - Unique cardioprotective effects independent of GH release—acts directly on cardiac tissue - Rapid desensitization: GH response significantly diminishes within days of repeated dosing - Moderate selectivity: Some cortisol and prolactin elevation, but less than GHRP-6/GHRP-2 - Italian research leadership: University of Turin (Ghigo, Arvat) produced most human clinical data Evidence Strength: MODERATE — Extensive human pharmacokinetic and pharmacodynamic studies exist demonstrating potent GH release and cardioprotective effects. However, clinical development was discontinued due to desensitization, and no efficacy trials for clinical endpoints were completed. -------|-------| | Generic name | Hexarelin | | INN name | Examorelin | | Development code | MF-6003, EP-23905 | | Chemical name | His-D-2-methyl-Trp-Ala-Trp-D-Phe-Lys-NH2 | | Chemical class | Synthetic hexapeptide, GHRP family | | Molecular weight | 887.0 Da | | Primary receptor | Ghrelin receptor (GHS-R1a) | | Secondary receptor | CD36 (cardiac scavenger receptor) | Discovery & Development Timeline | Year | Milestone | Significance | Region | |------|-----------|--------------|--------| | 1984 | GHRP-6 synthesized | Foundation for GHRP class | USA | | 1993 | Hexarelin synthesized | Mediolanum Pharmaceuticals develops most potent GHRP PMID: 8190006 | Italy | | 1994 | First human studies | Ghigo et al. demonstrate superior potency vs GHRP-6 PMID: 7806864 | Italy | | 1995 | IV/SC/Intranasal comparison | SC route established as practical alternative | Italy | | 1996 | GHS-R1a receptor cloned | Howard et al. identify target receptor | USA | | 1997 | Phase 2 trials initiated | GH deficiency and aging indications explored | Italy/Europe | | 1997 | Selectivity profiling | Arvat et al. compare GHRP family cortisol/prolactin effects | Italy | | 1998 | Desensitization characterized | Rapid tachyphylaxis limits clinical utility PMID: 9636063 | Italy | | 1999 | Ghrelin discovered | Kojima et al. identify endogenous GHS-R1a ligand | Japan | | 1999 | Cardioprotective effects | Direct cardiac actions independent of GH identified PMID: 10516699 | Italy | | 2000 | Phase 2 completed | Efficacy demonstrated but desensitization problematic | Europe | | 2001 | Ischemia-reperfusion studies | Cardiac protection in animal models confirmed | Italy/Sweden | | 2003 | Development discontinued | Desensitization prevents chronic therapeutic use | International | | 2007-2015 | Cardioprotection research | Ongoing investigation of GH-independent cardiac effects | Multiple | | Current | Research compound | No active pharmaceutical development; cardiac research continues | Academic | Why Hexarelin is the "Most Potent" GHRP Hexarelin earned this designation through systematic comparative studies: 1. Structure optimization: Addition of 2-methyl group to D-Trp enhanced receptor binding 2. GH release magnitude: Produces 2-3x higher GH peaks than GHRP-6 at equivalent doses 3. Potency confirmation: Multiple head-to-head human studies confirmed superiority 4. Italian leadership: Mediolanum Pharmaceuticals and University of Turin collaborated on development The potency paradox: Despite being the most potent GHRP, hexarelin's rapid desensitization made it unsuitable for chronic therapy—the very situation where GH secretagogues would be most useful. --- Mechanism of Action How Hexarelin Works Hexarelin triggers growth hormone release by activating the ghrelin receptor, but it also has unique direct effects on the heart. Here's what makes it different: 1. Activates the ghrelin receptor - Like other GHRPs, hexarelin binds to GHS-R1a receptors on pituitary cells and triggers GH release. It does this more powerfully than any other synthetic GHRP. 2. Causes potent GH release - A single dose produces very high GH peaks, typically 2-3 times higher than GHRP-6. This was exciting for researchers initially. 3. Desensitizes rapidly - The major limitation: after just a few days of repeated dosing, the GH response drops dramatically. By day 4-7, responses are often 50-80% lower than the initial dose. This is called tachyphylaxis. 4. Has direct heart effects - Uniquely among GHRPs, hexarelin appears to protect heart tissue through mechanisms that don't require GH release at all. This acts through CD36 receptors on cardiac cells. Why development stopped: The rapid desensitization meant hexarelin couldn't maintain GH elevation with chronic use, making it impractical for treating GH deficiency or aging. However, the cardiac protective effects remain an area of research interest. Comparison to family members: Hexarelin is more potent but desensitizes faster than ipamorelin, GHRP-6, or GHRP-2. This appears to be related to its higher receptor affinity causing more rapid receptor downregulation. --- Receptor Targets Primary: Ghrelin Receptor (GHS-R1a) Receptor Classification (IUPHAR/BPS Guide to Pharmacology): - Official name: Ghrelin receptor (GHSR) - Gene: GHSR (human) - Class: Class A G protein-coupled receptor (7 transmembrane domains) - Endogenous ligand: Ghrelin (pKd 8.6-9.4) - Hexarelin binding affinity: High affinity agonist; pEC50 approximately 8.5 - Constitutive activity: Yes - receptor maintains basal activity without ligand | Tissue | Expression | Primary Effect | |--------|------------|----------------| | Anterior pituitary | High | Direct GH release from somatotrophs | | Hypothalamus | High | GHRH neuron activation; amplifies GH response | | Myocardium | Low-moderate | Direct cardioprotective signaling | | Vasculature | Low | Hemodynamic effects | Primary Signaling (IUPHAR): - Transduction: Gq/G11-mediated phospholipase C stimulation - Downstream: IP3 accumulation, intracellular Ca2+ release - Additional: ERK phosphorylation, beta-arrestin signaling Secondary: CD36 (Cardiac Scavenger Receptor) This is hexarelin's unique contribution to GHRP research: - CD36 is expressed on cardiomyocytes - Hexarelin binding activates protective signaling independent of GHS-R1a - Mechanism involves PPAR-gamma activation and anti-apoptotic pathways - May explain cardioprotection observed even when GH response is desensitized Desensitization Mechanism The rapid tachyphylaxis that limited hexarelin's development: Day 1: Full GH response (100%) ↓ Days 2-3: GHS-R1a receptor internalization begins ↓ Days 4-7: 50-80% reduction in GH response ↓ Mechanism: Receptor downregulation + reduced receptor recycling ↓ Result: Chronic dosing ineffective for GH elevation Why hexarelin desensitizes faster than other GHRPs: - Higher receptor affinity = more rapid receptor occupancy - Stronger receptor activation = more pronounced internalization - Possible unique receptor conformation effects Unique Selectivity Profile | Effect | Hexarelin | GHRP-6 | GHRP-2 | Ipamorelin | |--------|-----------|--------|--------|------------| | GH release | Strongest | Strong | Strong | Strong | | ACTH/Cortisol | Minimal-Moderate | Moderate | Higher | None | | Prolactin | Minimal | Moderate | Higher | None | | Appetite | Moderate | Strong | Moderate | Minimal | | Cardioprotection | Strongest evidence | Some | Limited | Less studied | | Desensitization | Rapid | Moderate | Moderate | Slower | --- Research Summary Evidence Overview | Category | Count | Notes | |----------|-------|-------| | Human RCTs/trials | 12 | Phase 1/2; PK/PD studies | | Human PK/PD | 6 | Pharmacology characterization | | Preclinical | 15 | Mechanism, cardiac effects | | Reviews | 9 | Comprehensive GHS reviews | | Total sources | 42 | 28 open access | Geographic Distribution of Research | Region | Studies | Key Institutions | |--------|---------|-----------------| | Italy | 18 | University of Turin (Ghigo, Arvat), Mediolanum Pharmaceuticals | | USA | 8 | Tulane University (Bowers), NIH, University of Virginia | | Germany | 5 | Max Planck Institute, German Sport University Cologne | | Sweden | 4 | Sahlgrenska University Hospital (Bengtsson, Isgaard) | | Japan | 3 | Kurume University (Kojima, Kangawa) | | Canada | 2 | Montreal Heart Institute (CD36 research) | | Other Europe | 5 | France, Belgium, Netherlands, UK, Spain | Key Evidence Highlights 1. Potency: Confirmed as most potent synthetic GH secretagogue in human studies 2. Desensitization: GH response decreases 50-80% within one week of daily dosing 3. Cardioprotection: Direct cardiac effects independent of GH documented 4. Selectivity: Less cortisol/prolactin elevation than GHRP-6/GHRP-2, but more than ipamorelin 5. Development outcome: Discontinued due to desensitization preventing chronic use --- Key Human Trials Phase 1/2 Studies Summary | Study | N | Phase | Key Finding | PMID | |-------|---|-------|-------------|------| | Ghigo E et al. 1994 | 24 | 1 | 2x more potent than GHRP-6 for GH release | 7806864 | | Arvat E et al. 1994 | 36 | 1 | SC route comparable to IV | 7809041 | | Rahim A et al. 1998 | 18 | 1/2 | Desensitization within 4-7 days | 9636063 | | Frieboes RM et al. 1999 | 7 | 1 | Sleep effects; increased slow-wave sleep | 10447775 | | Broglio F et al. 2002 | 6 | 1 | Acute cardiac effects; improved LVEF | 11916712 | Potency Comparison Study (1994) Ghigo E et al. PMID: 7806864 ( | Parameter | Value | |-----------|-------| | N | 24 healthy volunteers | | Design | Randomized, crossover comparison | | Comparator | GHRP-6, GHRH | | Finding | Hexarelin GH response approximately 2x GHRP-6 | | Significance | Established hexarelin as most potent synthetic GHRP | Key finding: At equivalent doses, hexarelin produced significantly higher GH peaks than GHRP-6, establishing its position as the most potent GHRP available. Despite initial potent GH release, the response attenuated rapidly, and IGF-1 (the downstream marker of GH action) did not show sustained elevation with chronic dosing. PMID: 7809041 ( | Route | GH Response | Practicality | Notes | |-------|-------------|--------------|-------| | Intravenous | Reference (100%) | Low | Research setting | | Subcutaneous | 85-95% | High | Preferred clinical route | | Intranasal | 60-80%, variable | Moderate | Less reliable absorption | Comparison to Related Peptides | Parameter | Hexarelin | GHRP-6 | Ipamorelin | Sermorelin | |-----------|-----------|--------|------------|------------| | Half-life | 60-70 min | 2.5 hours | 2 hours | 10-20 min | | GH peak onset | 15-30 min | 15-30 min | 30-60 min | 15-30 min | | Desensitization | Days | Weeks | Weeks-months | Minimal | | Dosing frequency | Multiple daily | Multiple daily | Multiple daily | Multiple daily | --- Regulatory Status Approval Status by Region | Region | Status | Notes | |--------|--------|-------| | USA (FDA) | Not approved | Development discontinued | | EU (EMA) | Not approved | Phase 2 completed but not advanced | | Japan (PMDA) | Not approved | No application | | Other regions | Not approved | No known approvals | WADA Status Hexarelin is PROHIBITED under the World Anti-Doping Agency code. - Listed under S2: Peptide Hormones, Growth Factors, Related Substances and Mimetics - Specifically: "Growth Hormone Secretagogues (GHS)" - Banned both in-competition and out-of-competition - Detection methods available via mass spectrometry - Specific urinary metabolite detection developed (German researchers) Anti-Doping Detection Thomas A et al. developed LC-MS/MS methods for hexarelin detection in urine, enabling enforcement of WADA prohibition. Why Development Was Discontinued 1. Rapid desensitization: GH response decreased 50-80% within one week 2. No sustained IGF-1 elevation: Primary endpoint for GH deficiency indications 3. Competing compounds: Ipamorelin (less desensitization), tesamorelin (GHRH analog) advanced 4. Limited chronic utility: Desensitization prevented long-term therapeutic use 5. Cardiac potential unrealized: GH-independent effects not pursued clinically --- Related Peptides GHRP Family Comparison | Peptide | Structure | Potency | Selectivity | Desensitization | Status | |---------|-----------|---------|-------------|-----------------|--------| | Hexarelin | Hexapeptide | Highest | Moderate | Rapid | Discontinued | | GHRP-6 | Hexapeptide | High | Low | Moderate | Research | | GHRP-2 | Hexapeptide | High | Very low | Moderate | Research | | Ipamorelin | Pentapeptide | High | High | Slow | Discontinued | Hexarelin vs Ipamorelin (Key Comparison) | Factor | Hexarelin | Ipamorelin | |--------|-----------|------------| | GH release potency | Higher | Lower | | Cortisol effect | Minimal-Low | None | | Prolactin effect | Minimal | None | | Desensitization | Rapid (days) | Slow (weeks-months) | | Selectivity | Moderate | High | | Cardioprotection | Well-documented | Less studied | | Development outcome | Discontinued (desensitization) | Discontinued (efficacy) | Clinical implication: Despite being less potent, ipamorelin may be more useful for sustained GH elevation due to slower desensitization and better selectivity. Hexarelin vs GHRP-6 | Factor | Hexarelin | GHRP-6 | |--------|-----------|--------| | GH release | More potent | Potent | | Desensitization | Faster | Slower | | Cortisol effect | Less | More | | Appetite stimulation | Moderate | Strong | | Cardioprotection | Best documented | Some evidence | | Research history | Moderate | Extensive | Why Hexarelin's Cardioprotection is Unique Among all GHRPs, hexarelin has the most extensive cardiac research: 1. Largest body of evidence for direct cardiac effects 2. GH-independent mechanism best characterized 3. CD36 receptor interaction most thoroughly studied 4. Clinical cardiac effects documented in human studies 5. Italian research leadership produced systematic investigation This makes hexarelin interesting for potential cardiac applications even though it failed as a GH secretagogue. --- Current Research Directions Active Areas of Investigation Cardioprotection: - Mechanism of CD36-mediated protection - Potential for acute cardiac applications (where desensitization irrelevant) - Comparison with other cardioprotective peptides Receptor Biology: - GHS-R1a desensitization mechanisms - Why hexarelin desensitizes faster than other GHRPs - CD36 signaling pathway characterization Structural Modifications: - Can hexarelin derivatives retain potency without rapid desensitization? - Structure-activity relationships for cardiac vs. GH effects Potential Future Applications | Application | Rationale | Feasibility | |-------------|-----------|-------------| | Acute cardioprotection | Single-dose cardiac surgery | Moderate (desensitization irrelevant) | | Diagnostic use | GH reserve testing | Low (GHRH preferred) | | Chronic GH elevation | Desensitization prevents | Not feasible | | Cachexia treatment | Short-term use possible | Low (other options exist) | Why Hexarelin Remains of Research Interest Despite failed development, hexarelin contributes to understanding: 1. GHS receptor desensitization - Model compound for receptor downregulation 2. GH-independent GHRP effects - Separating cardiac from hormonal actions 3. CD36 signaling - Novel cardioprotective pathway 4.
