name: HMG
summary: A urinary-derived glycoprotein hormone preparation containing both FSH and LH in approximately 1:1 ratio, FDA-approved for female infertility (ovulation induction, ART) and male hypogonadotropic hypogonadism. First used clinically in 1961 by Bruno Lunenfeld, HMG enabled the birth of the first American IVF baby in 1981 and remains a cornerstone of fertility medicine with over 60 years of clinical experience. Highly purified preparations (HP-hMG) demonstrate comparable or slightly superior live birth rates compared to recombinant FSH in IVF.
content: Overview Quick Summary - FDA-approved urinary gonadotropin preparation containing both FSH and LH for ovulation induction, assisted reproductive technology (ART), and male hypogonadotropic hypogonadism - Historical significance: First successful clinical use in 1961 by Bruno Lunenfeld; instrumental in the first American IVF baby (Elizabeth Carr, 1981) - Strong evidence base: Multiple Cochrane systematic reviews, Phase 3 RCTs, international fertility guidelines (ASRM, ESHRE), and over 60 years of clinical experience - Current formulations: Highly purified hMG (HP-hMG) preparations like Menopur; earlier products Pergonal and Repronex have been discontinued - Comparable to recombinant FSH: Meta-analyses show HP-hMG achieves similar or slightly better live birth rates in IVF, particularly in conventional IVF vs ICSI - Key safety concern: Ovarian hyperstimulation syndrome (OHSS) in 1-5% of cycles; multiple pregnancy risk with ovulation induction Evidence Strength: HIGH — Supported by decades of clinical use, multiple Cochrane systematic reviews, FDA approval for specific indications, and endorsement by international fertility societies. One of the foundational treatments in reproductive endocrinology. -------|-------| | Generic name | Human Menopausal Gonadotropin (hMG) / Menotropins | | Highly purified form | HP-hMG (Highly Purified Human Menopausal Gonadotropin) | | Brand names (current) | Menopur (Ferring) | | Brand names (discontinued) | Pergonal (Serono), Repronex (Ferring) | | Chemical class | Glycoprotein hormone mixture (urinary-derived) | | Active components | Follicle-Stimulating Hormone (FSH) + Luteinizing Hormone (LH) activity | | FSH:LH ratio | Approximately 1:1 (75 IU FSH : 75 IU LH per ampoule) | | Source | Urine of postmenopausal women | | CAS Number | 9002-68-0 | Molecular Composition HMG is a mixture of glycoprotein hormones extracted and purified from the urine of postmenopausal women: FSH (Follicle-Stimulating Hormone): - Heterodimer: Alpha subunit (92 aa) + Beta subunit (111 aa) - Molecular weight: 30,000 Da - Primary function: Stimulates follicular development in ovaries; supports spermatogenesis in testes LH (Luteinizing Hormone): - Heterodimer: Alpha subunit (92 aa) + Beta subunit (121 aa) - Molecular weight: 28,000 Da - Primary function: Triggers ovulation; stimulates steroidogenesis Important note on LH activity: In HP-hMG preparations, the LH bioactivity is partially derived from supplementary hCG added during manufacturing. This is because purification processes cause preferential loss of native LH activity, which is compensated with pharmacologically significant quantities of hCG that binds the same receptor. Discovery & Development Timeline | Year | Milestone | Significance | |------|-----------|--------------| | 1949 | Donini extracts gonadotropins | Piero Donini at Serono develops method to extract gonadotropins from postmenopausal urine | | 1950 | First hMG preparations | Initial hMG registered in Italy; impure with unstandardized FSH/LH ratios | | 1957 | Lunenfeld-Donini collaboration | Bruno Lunenfeld contacts Donini; Vatican partnership secured for urine collection from nuns | | 1960 | First human studies | Lunenfeld reports expected hormonal changes in women receiving hMG | | 1961 | First successful pregnancy | First ovulation induction pregnancy achieved with Pergonal (Lunenfeld) PMID: 29699102 | | 1962 | Clinical trials published | Lunenfeld publishes successful ovulation induction with hMG + hCG combination | | 1969 | FDA approval (Pergonal) | First US approval for menotropins for infertility | | 1978 | First IVF baby (Louise Brown) | Natural cycle IVF; demonstrated fertility medicine feasibility | | 1981 | First American IVF baby | Elizabeth Carr born using Pergonal-stimulated IVF (Howard & Georgianna Jones) | | 1990s | HP-hMG development | Highly purified preparations with <5% urinary proteins developed | | 2004 | FDA approval (Menopur) | HP-hMG (Menopur) approved for ART NDA 21-663 | | 2008 | Pivotal comparative trial | Andersen et al. HP-hMG vs rFSH trial (PMID: 16873892) | | 2011 | Cochrane review update | van Wely et al. comprehensive comparison PMID: 21328277 | | 2014 | Pergonal discontinued | Manufacturing ceased; Menopur becomes primary product | | 2019 | Cost-effectiveness studies | MEGASET-HR trial demonstrates HP-hMG economic advantages | Why HMG Was Developed Natural FSH and LH are pituitary hormones essential for reproduction: - In women: FSH recruits and develops ovarian follicles; LH triggers ovulation and supports the corpus luteum - In men: FSH supports Sertoli cells and spermatogenesis; LH stimulates Leydig cells to produce testosterone The clinical need: Women with hypothalamic-pituitary dysfunction (hypogonadotropic hypogonadism) cannot produce adequate FSH and LH, preventing ovulation. Men with this condition have impaired testosterone production and spermatogenesis. Why postmenopausal urine: After menopause, the ovaries no longer respond to FSH and LH. The pituitary continues secreting these hormones, which accumulate in urine and can be extracted. This provided an abundant source before recombinant technology. --- Mechanism of Action How HMG Works (Plain English) HMG provides both of the "master hormones" needed for reproduction. Think of it as giving the body the signals it needs to: 1. Grow eggs (in women) — The FSH component tells the ovaries to develop multiple follicles, each containing an egg. Without FSH, follicles don't mature properly. 2. Prepare for ovulation — The LH component helps follicles mature fully and prepares them for release. LH also stimulates estrogen production, which prepares the uterus. 3. Support egg quality — Having both FSH and LH (rather than FSH alone) may improve oocyte quality and the cells surrounding the egg, potentially leading to better embryos. 4. Restore testosterone production (in men) — The LH activity stimulates the testicles to produce testosterone, and both hormones together support sperm production. Why both hormones matter: The ovary needs a "dialogue" between FSH and LH. FSH alone can develop follicles, but the addition of LH activity appears to improve steroidogenesis (hormone production) and may enhance oocyte quality in some patient populations. --- Receptor Targets: FSH Receptor & LH/hCG Receptor HMG acts through two distinct G protein-coupled receptors (GPCRs): FSH Receptor (FSHR): | Tissue | Expression | Primary Effect | Clinical Relevance | |--------|------------|----------------|--------------------| | Ovarian granulosa cells | Very high | Follicular development, aromatase activation | Multiple follicle growth in IVF | | Sertoli cells (testis) | High | Support spermatogenesis, inhibin production | Male fertility restoration | LH/hCG Receptor (LHCGR): | Tissue | Expression | Primary Effect | Clinical Relevance | |--------|------------|----------------|--------------------| | Ovarian theca cells | High | Androgen synthesis (substrate for estrogen) | Estrogen production | | Granulosa cells (late) | Acquired | Final maturation, luteinization | Ovulation preparation | | Corpus luteum | Very high | Progesterone synthesis | Luteal support | | Leydig cells (testis) | High | Testosterone synthesis | Male hypogonadism treatment | Signaling Pathway Overview FSH Receptor Activation: FSH binds FSHR (granulosa/Sertoli cells) | Galphas protein activation --> adenylyl cyclase --> cAMP increase | Protein kinase A (PKA) activation | +-- Granulosa: Aromatase expression (estrogen synthesis) +-- Granulosa: Inhibin B production +-- Follicle growth and development +-- Sertoli: Support spermatogenesis LH Receptor Activation: LH/hCG binds LHCGR (theca cells, mature granulosa, Leydig cells) | Galphas protein activation --> cAMP increase | +-- Theca cells: Androstenedione synthesis +-- Granulosa: Final maturation, luteinization +-- Corpus luteum: Progesterone production +-- Leydig cells: Testosterone synthesis The "Two-Cell, Two-Gonadotropin" Model Ovarian steroidogenesis requires coordinated FSH and LH action: 1. LH acts on theca cells → Produces androgens (androstenedione) 2. Androgens diffuse to granulosa cells 3. FSH acts on granulosa cells → Activates aromatase 4. Aromatase converts androgens to estrogens This explains why HMG (with both FSH and LH) may provide more physiologic stimulation than FSH alone in some patients. Supported Mechanisms (Human Evidence) Follicular development: - Multiple follicle growth demonstrated in IVF Cochrane PMID: 21328277 - Dose-dependent oocyte yield established in clinical trials - Optimal starting doses: 150-225 IU/day in most protocols Steroidogenesis: - Estradiol production correlates with follicle development - LH activity supports androgen substrate for estrogen synthesis - HP-hMG may produce different steroid profiles than rFSH alone Male spermatogenesis: - 75-85% success rate in inducing spermatogenesis in hypogonadotropic males PMID: 3928676 - Combined hCG + HMG therapy more effective than hCG alone initially --- Research Summary Evidence Overview HMG has one of the longest and most robust clinical track records of any fertility medication, spanning over 60 years. | Evidence Category | Assessment | Key Points | |-------------------|------------|------------| | Clinical experience | VERY HIGH | 60+ years of use; millions of cycles worldwide | | Randomized trials | HIGH | Multiple Phase 3 RCTs comparing to rFSH | | Systematic reviews | HIGH | Cochrane reviews with 9000+ patients | | Guidelines endorsement | HIGH | ASRM, ESHRE, international fertility societies | | Real-world outcomes | HIGH | National registry data from multiple countries | Key Meta-Analysis Findings: HMG vs Recombinant FSH Cochrane Review 2011 PMID: 21328277 : - 42 RCTs with 9,606 women - HMG/HP-hMG associated with significantly higher live birth rates than rFSH - Odds ratio 0.84 (95% CI 0.72-0.99) favoring HMG - 4% absolute increase in live birth rate with HMG Al-Inany et al. 2008/2009 PMID: 18056719; PMID: 19340668 : - HP-hMG associated with 3-4% higher live birth rate vs rFSH in long agonist protocols - Benefit most pronounced in IVF (not ICSI) cycles - Statistical significance reached in pooled analysis Coomarasamy et al. 2008 PMID: 18056719 : - 7 RCTs, 2,159 women - Relative risk for live birth: 1.18 (95% CI 1.02-1.38) favoring HMG - Fewer gonadotropin ampoules required with HMG IVF Outcomes with HP-hMG (Menopur) Andersen et al. 2006 PMID: 16873892 : - 731 patients across 37 centers in Europe and Israel - HP-hMG vs rFSH in GnRH agonist long protocol - HP-hMG: 11.3% top-quality embryos vs rFSH: 9.0% - Ongoing pregnancy rate higher in HP-hMG IVF subgroup (31% vs 20%) MEGASET-HR Trial PMID: 29345163 : - 620 predicted high-responder women - HP-hMG: 35.5% ongoing pregnancy rate vs rFSH: 30.7% - Lower OHSS incidence with HP-hMG - Lower total treatment cost per live birth --- Key Human Trials Landmark Trials in HMG Development Lunenfeld et al. 1962 — First Successful Ovulation Induction | Parameter | Value | |-----------|-------| | Design | Case series | | Location | Israel | | N | Multiple patients with hypogonadotropic anovulation | | Intervention | Pergonal (hMG) + hCG | | Key outcome | First pregnancies achieved in women with hypothalamic amenorrhea | This foundational work established that exogenous gonadotropins could restore fertility in anovulatory women, launching the era of controlled ovarian stimulation. --- Major Randomized Controlled Trials Andersen et al. 2006 PMID: 16873892 — HP-hMG vs rFSH in IVF | Parameter | Value | |-----------|-------| | Design | Multicenter, open-label, assessor-blind RCT | | N | 731 women (HP-hMG: 363; rFSH: 368) | | Centers | 37 fertility clinics in Europe and Israel | | Population | First or second IVF/ICSI cycle | | Protocol | Long GnRH agonist down-regulation | | Primary endpoint | Ongoing pregnancy rate | | HP-hMG oocytes | 10.0 (mean) | | rFSH oocytes | 11.8 (mean) | | Top-quality embryos | HP-hMG: 11.3% vs rFSH: 9.0% | | IVF subgroup pregnancy | HP-hMG: 31% vs rFSH: 20% (p=0.037) | Key conclusions: Despite fewer oocytes retrieved with HP-hMG, embryo quality and pregnancy rates in IVF (not ICSI) were superior, suggesting qualitative advantages of combined FSH/LH stimulation. --- Devroey et al. 2012 PMID: 22244781 — GnRH Antagonist Protocol | Parameter | Value | |-----------|-------| | Design | Multicenter, randomized, assessor-blind RCT | | N | 749 women | | Protocol | GnRH antagonist with single blastocyst transfer | | Comparison | HP-hMG vs rFSH | | Primary endpoint | Ongoing pregnancy rate | | HP-hMG ongoing pregnancy | 30% | | rFSH ongoing pregnancy | 27% | | Conclusion | Non-inferiority demonstrated | --- van Wely et al. 2011 Cochrane Review PMID: 21328277 | Parameter | Value | |-----------|-------| | Design | Cochrane systematic review and meta-analysis | | Trials included | 42 RCTs | | N | 9,606 women | | Comparisons | rFSH vs HMG, HP-hMG, purified FSH, HP-FSH | | Primary outcome | Live birth per woman randomized | | Key finding | Significantly fewer live births with rFSH vs HMG (OR 0.84, 95% CI 0.72-0.99) | | Clinical interpretation | For 25% live birth rate with HMG, rFSH would yield 19-25% | --- Male Hypogonadotropic Hypogonadism Trials Burris et al. 1988 PMID: 3928676 — Spermatogenesis Induction | Parameter | Value | |-----------|-------| | Design | Prospective cohort | | N | 13 men with hypogonadotropic hypogonadism | | Treatment | hCG alone, then hCG + hMG | | Duration | Variable (months to years) | | Spermatogenesis achieved | 12 of 13 (92%) | | Key finding | Prior androgen treatment did not adversely affect outcomes | --- Liu et al. 2009 PMID: 19211802 — Combined hCG/hMG Therapy | Parameter | Value | |-----------|-------| | Design | Meta-analysis and systematic review | | N | Multiple studies | | Population | Men with idiopathic hypogonadotropic hypogonadism | | Treatment | hCG 500-2500 IU + hMG 75-225 IU, 2-3x/week | | Success rate | 75-85% achieve spermatogenesis | | Pregnancy rate | 50% partner pregnancy in those achieving spermatogenesis | --- Preclinical Evidence Animal Studies | Study Type | Model | Key Findings | Clinical Relevance | |------------|-------|--------------|-------------------| | Receptor characterization | Rodent, primate | FSH and LH receptor structures and distributions | Foundation for dual-hormone approach | | Two-cell model | In vitro, rodent | LH-stimulated androgen required for estrogen synthesis | Explains HMG vs FSH-only differences | | Follicular development | Primate | Multi-follicular development dose-dependent | Guides human dosing | | Spermatogenesis | Rodent, primate | Combined FSH/LH required for complete spermatogenesis | Supports male HH treatment | | Ovarian reserve | Multiple species | Gonadotropins deplete primordial follicle pool | Long-term safety consideration | In Vitro Studies Receptor binding: - FSH binds FSHR with high affinity (Kd 1-10 nM) - LH/hCG binds LHCGR with high affinity - Both receptors couple to Galphas/cAMP pathway Steroidogenesis models: - Theca-granulosa co-culture confirms two-cell model - FSH alone produces less estradiol than FSH + LH - HP-hMG stimulates physiologic steroid ratios Limitations of Preclinical Data - Rodent ovarian physiology differs significantly from humans - Primate studies limited but most relevant - Urinary-derived product complexity difficult to model - Species differences in receptor expression patterns --- Safety Profile Common Adverse Events | Adverse Event | Incidence | Severity | Notes | |---------------|-----------|----------|-------| | Injection site reaction | 5-15% | Mild | Redness, bruising, swelling | | Headache | 5-10% | Mild | Usually transient | | Abdominal pain/bloating | 10-20% | Mild-moderate | Related to ovarian enlargement | | Pelvic discomfort | 10-15% | Mild-moderate | Expected with follicular growth | | Nausea | 3-5% | Mild | May be related to estrogen rise | | Fatigue | 5-10% | Mild | Hormonal fluctuation effects | Serious Adverse Events and Warnings | Risk | Evidence Type | Frequency | Severity | Current Understanding | |------|---------------|-----------|----------|----------------------| | Ovarian Hyperstimulation Syndrome (OHSS) | Clinical trials, registries | Moderate: 3-5%; Severe: 0.5-2% | Moderate-severe | Primary safety concern; preventable with monitoring | | Multiple pregnancy | Clinical trials | 20-30% in ovulation induction | Moderate | Intrinsic to multi-follicular development | | Ectopic pregnancy | Clinical trials | Slightly increased | Serious | General ART risk | | Ovarian torsion | Case reports | Rare (<1%) | Serious | Related to ovarian enlargement | | Thromboembolic events | Case reports | Rare (with severe OHSS) | Serious | Secondary to OHSS | | Allergic reactions | Post-marketing | Very rare | Variable | Urinary proteins may trigger sensitivity | Ovarian Hyperstimulation Syndrome: Detailed Assessment What is OHSS: OHSS is the most significant complication of gonadotropin therapy, characterized by: - Ovarian enlargement with multiple cysts - Capillary leak leading to third-space fluid accumulation - Ascites, pleural effusion, hemoconcentration - In severe cases: renal failure, ARDS, thromboembolism OHSS Classification: | Grade | Symptoms | Management | |-------|----------|------------| | Mild | Bloating, mild discomfort, ovaries <8 cm | Outpatient monitoring | | Moderate | Nausea, vomiting, ultrasound evidence of ascites, ovaries 8-12 cm | Close monitoring, hydration | | Severe | Tense ascites, oliguria, hemoconcentration, ovaries >12 cm | Hospitalization | | Critical | Renal failure, ARDS, thromboembolism | ICU care | Risk factors for OHSS with HMG: 1. Young age (<35 years) 2. Low body weight/BMI 3. Polycystic ovary syndrome (PCOS) 4. High antral follicle count (>24) 5. Elevated AMH levels 6. High estradiol during stimulation 7. Large number of developing follicles 8. Use of hCG trigger (vs GnRH agonist) 9. Previous OHSS history OHSS incidence data: - SHOview study PMID: 28461237 : 2.4% moderate-severe OHSS with HP-hMG - General IVF population: 1-5% moderate; <1% severe - PCOS population: Up to 12.5% OHSS in some studies Prevention strategies: - Individualized starting doses based on AFC/AMH - Careful monitoring with estradiol and ultrasound - GnRH antagonist protocols (allow agonist trigger) - GnRH agonist trigger in high-risk patients - Coasting (withhold gonadotropins if E2 excessive) - Freeze-all strategy (no fresh transfer) - Lower total gonadotropin exposure Multiple Pregnancy Risk Multiple pregnancy is an expected consequence of multi-follicular development: | Setting | Twin Rate | Higher-Order Multiple Rate | |---------|-----------|---------------------------| | Ovulation induction (non-IVF) | 15-25% | 3-5% | | IVF with single embryo transfer | <5% | <1% | | IVF with double embryo transfer | 20-30% | 1-2% | Mitigation: Single embryo transfer policies in IVF have dramatically reduced multiple pregnancy rates while maintaining acceptable pregnancy rates. Contraindications 1. Primary ovarian failure (elevated FSH/low AMH) 2. Uncontrolled thyroid or adrenal dysfunction 3. Sex hormone-dependent tumors (breast, ovarian, uterine, testicular) 4. Abnormal uterine bleeding of undetermined origin 5. Ovarian cyst or enlargement not due to PCOS 6. Pregnancy 7. Known allergy to menotropins or components Special Populations | Population | Recommendation | Notes | |------------|----------------|-------| | Pregnancy | Contraindicated | Teratogenicity not expected but no indication | | Breastfeeding | Avoid | May interfere with lactation | | Renal impairment | Caution | Monitor for accumulation | | Hepatic impairment | Caution | Limited data | | PCOS patients | Careful monitoring | Highest OHSS risk population | | Advanced maternal age | May require higher doses | Diminished ovarian reserve | --- Pharmacokinetics Key Pharmacokinetic Parameters FSH Component PMID: 27638053 : | Parameter | Subcutaneous | Intramuscular | Clinical Relevance | |-----------|--------------|---------------|-------------------| | Bioavailability | 74% | 74% | Routes equivalent | | Tmax | 22-27 hours | Similar | Delayed peak | | Elimination half-life | 39-53 hours | 39-45 hours | Once-daily dosing appropriate | | Steady state | 4-5 days | 4-5 days | Reached with daily dosing | LH Component: - Terminal half-life: 0.5-4 hours (much shorter than FSH) - LH activity maintained through hCG supplementation in HP-hMG - hCG half-life: 24-36 hours (provides sustained LH-like activity) Clinical Dosing Ovulation induction (non-IVF): - Starting dose: 75-150 IU/day - Route: Subcutaneous or intramuscular - Adjustment: Increase by 37.5-75 IU increments every 7 days if no response - Maximum: 225-450 IU/day - Duration: Until follicle(s) mature (typically 7-12 days) - Trigger: hCG 5,000-10,000 IU when lead follicle ≥18mm IVF/ART protocols: - Starting dose: 150-225 IU/day (individualized based on AFC, AMH, age) - GnRH agonist protocol: Start after pituitary down-regulation - GnRH antagonist protocol: Start on cycle day 2-3 - Monitoring: Estradiol + ultrasound every 2-3 days - Duration: 8-14 days typically - Trigger: hCG or GnRH agonist when ≥2-3 follicles ≥17mm Male hypogonadotropic hypogonadism: - hCG: 500-2500 IU 2-3 times weekly (establish testosterone first) - HMG: 75-150 IU 2-3 times weekly (add after 3-6 months hCG) - Duration: 12-24 months for spermatogenesis induction - Monitoring: Testosterone levels, semen analysis Administration - Reconstitution: HP-hMG supplied as lyophilized powder; reconstitute with supplied diluent - Injection volume: 0.5-1 mL per injection - Subcutaneous sites: Abdomen, thigh (rotate sites) - Intramuscular sites: Upper outer quadrant of gluteus or lateral thigh - Storage: Reconstituted solution stable 28 days refrigerated (product-specific) --- Regulatory Status Global Approval Status | Region | Product | Indications | Approval Year | |--------|---------|-------------|---------------| | USA (FDA) | Pergonal | Ovulation induction, ART, male HH | 1969 (discontinued 2014) | | USA (FDA) | Repronex | Ovulation induction, ART | 1999 (discontinued) | | USA (FDA) | Menopur | Ovulation induction, ART | 2004 | | EU (EMA) | Menopur | ART, anovulatory infertility | Multiple approvals | | Japan (PMDA) | HMG preparations | Fertility indications | Various | | China (NMPA) | Multiple brands | Fertility indications | Various | | Israel | Menopur, others | Fertility indications | Various | | Australia (TGA) | Menopur | Fertility indications | Approved | | Russia | Menopur, Menogon | Fertility indications | Approved | FDA-Approved Indications (Menopur) Women: 1. Development of multiple follicles in ovulatory patients as part of an Assisted Reproductive Technology (ART) cycle 2. Induction of ovulation and pregnancy in anovulatory infertile patients in whom the cause of infertility is functional and not due to primary ovarian failure Men: 1. Induction of spermatogenesis in men with primary or secondary hypogonadotropic hypogonadism in whom the cause of infertility is not due to primary testicular failure Off-Label Uses The following are not FDA-approved but may be encountered: - Male fertility preservation during testosterone therapy (usually hCG + HMG) - Controlled ovarian hyperstimulation in surrogacy - Egg donation protocols WADA Status HMG/menotropins are not specifically listed by WADA as prohibited substances. However: - FSH and LH are endogenous hormones - Use in males could theoretically affect testosterone production - Athletes should verify with sports medicine physicians --- Related Peptides & Comparisons HMG vs Recombinant FSH (rFSH) | Factor | HMG (HP-hMG) | Recombinant FSH | |--------|--------------|-----------------| | Source | Postmenopausal urine | CHO cell recombinant | | Components | FSH + LH activity | FSH only | | Consistency | Batch variation possible | Highly consistent | | Live birth rate | Slightly higher in some studies | Reference standard | | Oocyte yield | Slightly fewer | More oocytes typically | | Embryo quality | May be better (IVF) | Standard | | OHSS risk | Similar or slightly lower | Standard | | Cost | Generally lower | Generally higher | | Administration | SC or IM | SC | Cochrane conclusion PMID: 21328277 : Small but significant advantage for HMG in live birth rates (OR 0.84 favoring HMG vs rFSH). HMG vs hCG | Factor | HMG | hCG | |--------|-----|-----| | Primary use | Follicular development | Ovulation trigger / luteal support | | Mechanism | FSH + LH receptor activation | LH receptor activation only | | Timing | Throughout stimulation | Single injection at end | | Male use | With hCG for spermatogenesis | Primary therapy for testosterone | HMG vs Clomiphene Citrate | Factor | HMG | Clomiphene Citrate | |--------|-----|--------------------| | Mechanism | Direct gonadotropin replacement | Indirect (blocks estrogen feedback) | | Potency | High | Moderate | | Multiple follicles | Common | Less common | | OHSS risk | High

name: IGF-1 LR3
summary: A modified form of IGF-1 with an extended half-life due to reduced binding protein affinity. Used primarily in research for muscle growth and metabolic effects. Not approved for human use; significant safety concerns exist.
content: Overview Overview Quick Summary - Modified IGF-1 analog with extended biological half-life - 83 amino acids (native IGF-1 is 70 amino acids) - Key modification: Arginine substitution at position 3 + 13-amino acid N-terminal extension - Extended activity: Approximately 20-30 hours vs 12-15 minutes for native IGF-1 - Not FDA approved for any indication - Primarily used in research and cell culture applications Evidence Strength: Moderate — Substantial preclinical data supporting mechanism and effects. Limited human clinical data; most human evidence comes from case reports and observational studies rather than controlled trials. Identity & Origins | Property | Value | |----------|-------| | Generic name | IGF-1 LR3 (Long R3 Insulin-like Growth Factor-1) | | Chemical name | Long Arg3 Insulin-like Growth Factor-I | | Class | IGF-1 receptor agonist | | Molecular weight | 9,111 Da | | Amino acids | 83 (vs 70 in native IGF-1) | | Key modification | Glu3 to Arg3 substitution + 13-aa N-terminal extension | | Development origin | Originally developed for research applications | Structural Modifications IGF-1 LR3 differs from native IGF-1 in two key ways: 1. N-terminal extension: 13 additional amino acids (MFPAMPLSSL from methionine initiation) 2. Position 3 substitution: Glutamic acid (Glu) replaced with Arginine (Arg) These modifications dramatically reduce binding to IGF binding proteins (IGFBPs), resulting in: - Higher free (bioactive) IGF-1 concentration - Extended biological half-life - Greater potency at equivalent doses Development Context - 1980s-1990s: Development as research tool for studying IGF-1 biology - 1990s: Emergence in bodybuilding/performance enhancement communities - 2000s-present: Continued use in research; increasing concern over misuse - Current status: Sold as "research chemical"; not approved for human use IGF-1 Receptor Signaling IGF-1 LR3 (free, not bound to IGFBPs) | IGF-1R binding (Type 1 IGF Receptor) | Receptor autophosphorylation | IRS-1/2 recruitment and phosphorylation | +--> PI3K/Akt pathway | | | +--> mTORC1 activation --> Protein synthesis upward | | | +--> GSK3-beta inhibition --> Glycogen synthesis upward | | | +--> FOXO inhibition --> Protein degradation downward | +--> Ras/MAPK pathway | +--> Cell proliferation | +--> Differentiation signals IGFBP Binding Affinity | Form | IGFBP Binding | Half-life | Potency | |------|---------------|-----------|---------| | Native IGF-1 | High (greater than 99% bound) | 12-15 min | Baseline | | IGF-1 LR3 | Very low (less than 1% affinity) | 20-30 hours | Approximately 2-3x | | Des(1-3) IGF-1 | Reduced | Intermediate | Approximately 1.5x | Anabolic Mechanisms Protein Synthesis Stimulation: - Activates mTORC1 through PI3K/Akt pathway - Increases ribosomal biogenesis - Enhances translation initiation - Promotes amino acid uptake Anti-Catabolic Effects: - Inhibits FOXO transcription factors - Reduces MuRF1 and atrogin-1 expression - Suppresses autophagy in muscle - Decreases proteasome activity Satellite Cell Effects: - Promotes satellite cell proliferation - Enhances myoblast differentiation - Supports muscle fiber hypertrophy - May increase muscle fiber number (hyperplasia - debated) Glucose and Metabolic Effects | Effect | Mechanism | Magnitude | |--------|-----------|-----------| | Glucose uptake | GLUT4 translocation | Significant | | Hypoglycemia risk | Insulin-like activity | High concern | | Lipid metabolism | Fat oxidation | Variable | | Insulin sensitivity | IGF-1R/IR crosstalk | Complex | --- Research Summary Evidence Overview | Category | Count | Notes | |----------|-------|-------| | Human studies | 8 | Case reports, observational, limited RCT data | | Animal studies | 18 | Rodent, livestock, primate models | | In vitro | 10 | Cell culture, myoblast differentiation | | Reviews/Commentary | 7 | Safety concerns, doping detection | | Analytical/Detection | 5 | Anti-doping methods | | Total sources | 48 | 32 open access | Geographic Research Distribution | Region | Focus | Key Institutions | |--------|-------|------------------| | USA | Basic mechanism, cancer biology | NIH, academic centers | | Australia | Livestock applications, muscle biology | CSIRO, University of Melbourne | | UK | Endocrinology, diabetes research | Oxford, Cambridge | | Germany | Anti-doping detection | German Sports University Cologne | | Japan | IGF-1 signaling pathways | University of Tokyo, RIKEN | | China | Agricultural applications | Chinese Academy of Sciences | | Sweden | Muscle physiology | Karolinska Institute | Key Evidence Summary 1. Mechanism confirmed: Robust preclinical evidence for enhanced potency via reduced IGFBP binding 2. Muscle effects demonstrated: Animal studies show significant muscle growth enhancement 3. Human data limited: Few controlled trials; most human data from case reports 4. Safety concerns significant: Hypoglycemia, cancer risk, cardiac effects 5. Agricultural research: Substantial data in livestock for growth promotion 6. Detection methods: Well-characterized for anti-doping purposes --- Key Human Trials Clinical Development Context Unlike many peptides, IGF-1 LR3 was primarily developed as a research reagent rather than a therapeutic candidate. Consequently, formal clinical trials are limited. Most human evidence comes from: 1. Studies using native IGF-1 (providing mechanistic context) 2. Case reports of adverse events 3. Anti-doping research 4. Small observational studies Native IGF-1 Clinical Data (Relevant Context) Mecasermin (rhIGF-1) Trials: | Trial | N | Population | Duration | Key Finding | PMID | |-------|---|------------|----------|-------------|------| | Chernausek et al. | 76 | Severe IGF-1 deficiency | 8 years | Height improvement, frequent hypoglycemia | 17684053 | | Backeljauw & Underwood | 136 | Primary IGFD | Long-term | Growth response, safety profile | 11141537 | | Guevara-Aguirre et al. | 22 | Laron syndrome | 2 years | Catch-up growth, metabolic effects | 8366407 | IGF-1 LR3 Specific Human Data Case Reports and Series: | Source | Year | Type | Key Finding | |--------|------|------|-------------| | Adverse event reports | 2010-2024 | Case series | Hypoglycemia, injection site reactions | | Doping cases | Various | Analytical | Detection in urine/blood samples | | Observational surveys | 2015-2023 | Self-report | Subjective muscle gain, side effects | Documented Adverse Events in Humans | Event | Frequency | Severity | Notes | |-------|-----------|----------|-------| | Hypoglycemia | Common | Moderate-Severe | Most frequently reported | | Injection site reactions | Common | Mild | Pain, swelling, nodules | | Headache | Occasional | Mild | IGF-1 class effect | | Jaw/hand growth | Rare (chronic use) | Moderate | Acromegaly-like features | | Cardiac effects | Unknown | Potentially severe | Theoretical concern | Limitation of Human Evidence Critical note: There are no published randomized controlled trials of IGF-1 LR3 in healthy adults for muscle building or performance enhancement. Claims of efficacy largely derive from: - Extrapolation from native IGF-1 data - Animal studies - Anecdotal reports - Mechanistic plausibility --- Preclinical Evidence In Vitro Studies Myoblast and Muscle Cell Research: | Study Type | Model | Key Findings | PMID | |------------|-------|--------------|------| | Myoblast proliferation | C2C12 cells | Enhanced proliferation vs native IGF-1 | 8621789 | | Differentiation | Primary myoblasts | Accelerated myotube formation | 9851974 | | Protein synthesis | L6 muscle cells | 2-3x greater stimulation | 10684262 | | Satellite cell activation | Human primary | Enhanced activation | 11356983 | Animal Studies Rodent Models: | Study | Model | Intervention | Key Findings | PMID | |-------|-------|--------------|--------------|------| | Tomas et al. 1998 | Rat | IGF-1 LR3 infusion | Increased muscle mass, reduced adiposity | 9597107 | | Bark et al. 1998 | Mouse | Systemic IGF-1 LR3 | Whole-body anabolic effects | 9651638 | | Alzghoul et al. 2004 | Mouse | AAV-mediated IGF-1 | Muscle hypertrophy, regeneration | 15475016 | | Schertzer et al. 2007 | mdx mouse | IGF-1 LR3 | Improved dystrophic muscle function | 17110584 | Livestock Research (Agricultural Context): | Species | Application | Results | Reference | |---------|-------------|---------|-----------| | Pigs | Growth promotion | Increased lean mass, reduced fat | Various CSIRO | | Cattle | Muscle development | Enhanced growth efficiency | Australian studies | | Sheep | Research model | Validated muscle effects | University of Melbourne | Key Preclinical Findings Muscle Effects: - 10-20% increase in muscle mass in rodent models - Enhanced muscle regeneration after injury - Increased satellite cell proliferation - Fiber hypertrophy (increased size) demonstrated - Potential hyperplasia (increased number) - debated Metabolic Effects: - Improved glucose disposal - Reduced adiposity in some models - Enhanced insulin sensitivity (acute) - Potential for hypoglycemia (chronic) Potential Negative Effects: - Organ growth (heart, kidney, liver) - Tumor promotion in cancer models - Altered cardiac function at high doses - Long-term safety unknown --- Safety Profile Known Risks | Risk | Evidence Level | Mechanism | Mitigation | |------|----------------|-----------|------------| | Hypoglycemia | High | Insulin-like activity | Monitor glucose, timing with meals | | Organ growth | Moderate | Uncontrolled mitogenic signaling | Unknown; may be irreversible | | Cancer promotion | Moderate (theory) | Mitogenic/anti-apoptotic signaling | Avoid with any cancer history | | Cardiac hypertrophy | Moderate | Direct cardiac IGF-1R activation | Unknown | | Acromegaly-like effects | Low-Moderate | Chronic IGF-1 excess | Time-limited use (theoretical) | Hypoglycemia Risk The most immediate and well-documented risk: Why it occurs: - IGF-1 has approximately 1% insulin receptor affinity - At supraphysiological doses, this becomes significant - IGF-1 LR3's extended half-life prolongs hypoglycemia risk - Risk is highest 1-3 hours post-injection Symptoms to recognize: - Sweating, shakiness - Confusion, irritability - Rapid heartbeat - In severe cases: seizures, loss of consciousness Cancer Considerations Mechanistic concern: - IGF-1 signaling promotes cell proliferation - IGF-1 inhibits apoptosis (programmed cell death) - Epidemiological data links high IGF-1 to cancer risk - IGF-1R is overexpressed in many cancers Evidence context: - No direct evidence that IGF-1 LR3 causes cancer - Theoretical risk based on mechanism - Higher concern with prolonged use - Absolutely contraindicated with active malignancy Cardiovascular Concerns Potential effects: - Cardiac muscle hypertrophy - Left ventricular wall thickening - Altered cardiac function - Unknown long-term impact Warnings and Contraindications Absolute contraindications: - Active or suspected malignancy - History of cancer (relative) - Diabetic retinopathy - Pregnancy/lactation Strong cautions: - Diabetes mellitus - Cardiac disease - Family history of cancer - Adolescents (growth plate effects) Lack of Long-Term Safety Data Critical limitation: There are no long-term safety studies of IGF-1 LR3 in humans. Given its mechanism of action (prolonged, unregulated IGF-1 signaling), there is legitimate concern about: - Cumulative organ effects - Cancer risk over time - Metabolic consequences - Irreversible changes --- Pharmacokinetics Key Parameters | Parameter | IGF-1 LR3 | Native IGF-1 | Notes | |-----------|-----------|--------------|-------| | Half-life | 20-30 hours | 12-15 minutes | Major difference | | IGFBP binding | Less than 1% | Greater than 99% | Key modification effect | | Bioavailability (SC) | High | Variable | Due to low IGFBP binding | | Route | SC, IM, IV (research) | SC | Subcutaneous most common | | Time to peak | 1-2 hours | Minutes | After SC injection | Comparison of Half-Lives | Compound | Half-life | Binding Status | |----------|-----------|----------------| | Native IGF-1 (free) | 12-15 minutes | Rapid IGFBP capture | | Native IGF-1 (bound) | 12-15 hours | In ternary complex | | IGF-1 LR3 | 20-30 hours | Minimal binding | | Des(1-3) IGF-1 | 3-5 hours | Reduced binding | Dosing Patterns (Research Context) Note: The following information is provided for educational/research context only. IGF-1 LR3 is not approved for human use. | Context | Typical Research Dose | Frequency | Duration | |---------|----------------------|-----------|----------| | Cell culture | 10-100 ng/mL | Continuous | Variable | | Animal research | 0.1-1 mg/kg | Daily | Weeks | | Reported human use | 20-100 mcg | Daily | 4-6 weeks | Distribution - Widely distributed to tissues - Crosses blood-brain barrier (limited) - Concentrates in muscle, liver, kidney - No significant IGFBP sequestration (unlike native IGF-1) --- Regulatory Status Global Regulatory Position | Region | Status | Classification | Notes | |--------|--------|----------------|-------| | USA (FDA) | Not approved | Not a drug | Sold as "research chemical" | | EU (EMA) | Not approved | Not authorized | Similar status | | UK (MHRA) | Not approved | Unlicensed | Import restrictions | | Australia (TGA) | Not approved | Schedule 4 | Prescription only (not available) | | Canada (Health Canada) | Not approved | Not authorized | Not sold legally | | Japan (PMDA) | Not approved | Research only | Restricted | FDA Position - Never submitted for drug approval - Not approved for any indication - Marketed as "research chemical" or "not for human consumption" - No quality standards for commercial products - FDA has issued warnings about IGF-1 products WADA Status | Status | Year Listed | Category | |--------|-------------|----------| | Prohibited | 2005+ | S2: Peptide Hormones, Growth Factors | IGF-1 LR3 is prohibited: - In-competition - Out-of-competition - All sports Legal Considerations Selling/Purchasing: - Often sold as "research chemical" - "Not for human consumption" labels - Quality and purity unverified - Legal status varies by jurisdiction Important: Purchasing IGF-1 LR3 for personal use occupies a legal gray area in most jurisdictions. | USADA | US anti-doping | Public | | CAS Awards | Doping case law | Public | --- Frequently Asked Questions Is IGF-1 LR3 legal? IGF-1 LR3 occupies a legal gray area. It is not approved as a drug by any regulatory agency. It is commonly sold as a "research chemical" with disclaimers like "not for human consumption." Purchasing for personal use may or may not be legal depending on your jurisdiction. It is explicitly banned in sports by WADA. How is IGF-1 LR3 different from HGH? HGH (growth hormone) works indirectly by stimulating your liver to produce IGF-1. IGF-1 LR3 directly activates the IGF-1 receptor. IGF-1 LR3 is more potent milligram-for-milligram, acts faster, and has a longer half-life than native IGF-1. However, it bypasses normal physiological regulation, which raises safety concerns. name: Ipamorelin
summary: A growth hormone secretagogue that stimulates GH release without significant effects on cortisol or prolactin. Developed by Novo Nordisk (Denmark); limited clinical development despite promising early data.
content: Overview Overview Quick Summary - Selective growth hormone secretagogue that binds to ghrelin/GHSR receptors - Not FDA approved for any indication; development discontinued - Minimal cortisol/prolactin effects compared to other GH secretagogues - Limited human clinical data despite early-phase trials - Popular in wellness/anti-aging communities despite lack of approval Evidence Strength: Low — Some Phase 1/2 human data exists showing GH release, but clinical development was not completed. Identity & Origins | Property | Value | |----------|-------| | Generic name | Ipamorelin | | Research code | NNC 26-0161, NNC-26-0161 | | Class | Growth hormone secretagogue (GHS) | | Receptor | Ghrelin/GHSR receptor agonist | | Developer | Novo Nordisk (Bagsvaerd, Denmark) - discontinued | | Structure | Pentapeptide (Aib-His-D-2-Nal-D-Phe-Lys-NH2) | | Molecular Weight | 711.85 Da | Development History - 1984: Foundational GHRP discovery by Bowers et al. at Tulane University (USA) - 1995-1996: GHS-R receptor identified by Merck Research (USA) - 1998: Ipamorelin characterized by Novo Nordisk (Denmark) as first selective GH secretagogue - 1999: Human PK/PD studies; Danish-American collaboration with SUNY Buffalo - 1999: Ghrelin discovered in Japan (Kojima et al.); explains ipamorelin's mechanism - 2000-2001: Bone effects studied in Scandinavian collaboration (Denmark/Sweden) - 2014: Phase 2 trial for postoperative ileus completed (USA/Canada) - Current: Development discontinued; no active pharmaceutical development GHS-R1a Receptor Binding Ipamorelin is the first selective GH secretagogue with selectivity for GH release similar to GHRH. It binds to the growth hormone secretagogue receptor type 1a (GHS-R1a), also known as the ghrelin receptor. Receptor Classification (IUPHAR): - Official name: Ghrelin receptor (GHSR) - Gene: GHSR (human) - Class: Class A G protein-coupled receptor (7 transmembrane domains) - Endogenous ligand: Ghrelin (pKd 8.6-9.4) - Constitutive activity: Yes - receptor is active even without ligand binding Primary Signaling Cascade: Ipamorelin binds GHS-R1a (GPCR on somatotrophs) ↓ Gq/11 protein activation ↓ Phospholipase C (PLC) activation ↓ IP3 + DAG generation ↓ Intracellular Ca²⁺ mobilization ↓ GH vesicle exocytosis Secondary Signaling Pathways: - Gi/Go and G12/G13 coupling - G protein-independent beta-arrestin signaling - MAPK/ERK phosphorylation - RhoA activation Unique Selectivity Profile The Key Difference from GHRP-6/GHRP-2: Ipamorelin does not release ACTH or cortisol even at doses >200-fold higher than the ED50 for GH release. | Effect | GHRP-6 | GHRP-2 | Ipamorelin | |--------|--------|--------|------------| | GH release | Strong | Strong | Strong | | ACTH/Cortisol | ↑ Moderate | ↑ Higher | None | | Prolactin | ↑ Moderate | ↑ Higher | None | | Appetite | ↑ Strong | ↑ Moderate | Minimal | Structural Features Ipamorelin is a pentapeptide: Aib-His-D-2-Nal-D-Phe-Lys-NH2 Design elements contributing to selectivity: - Alpha-aminoisobutyric acid (Aib) at N-terminus - D-amino acids at positions 3 and 4 (D-naphthylalanine, D-phenylalanine) - These modifications: - Enhance peptidase resistance - Optimize receptor binding affinity - Extend half-life vs endogenous GHRH GHS-R1a Receptor Distribution | Tissue | Expression | Effect of Activation | |--------|------------|---------------------| | Anterior pituitary | High | GH release from somatotrophs | | Hypothalamus (arcuate) | High | Food intake, energy balance | | Hippocampus | Moderate | Cognitive effects | | Myocardium | Low | Potential cardioprotection | GH Release Pattern Studies show ipamorelin produces: - Dose-dependent GH release with physiologic pulses - Peak GH at 30-60 minutes post-injection - Effects attenuated with repeated dosing (some tachyphylaxis) - Synergistic with endogenous GHRH --- Research Summary Evidence Overview | Category | Count | Notes | |----------|-------|-------| | Human RCTs | 2 | Phase 1/2 | | Human PK/PD | 2 | Pharmacology studies | | Preclinical | 9 | Mechanism, selectivity, bone effects | | Reviews | 5 | Comprehensive GHS reviews | | Total sources | 18 | 11 open access | Geographic Distribution of Research | Region | Studies | Key Institutions | |--------|---------|-----------------| | Scandinavia | 5 | Novo Nordisk (Denmark), Sahlgrenska University (Sweden), University of Aarhus | | Europe | 7 | University of Essen (Germany), Charite Berlin, University of Milan (Italy) | | North America | 7 | Merck Research, Tulane University, Baylor College of Medicine, SUNY Buffalo | | Asia | 3 | Kurume University (Japan), Nippon Medical School, University of Tokyo | Key Evidence Highlights 1. GH release: Confirmed dose-dependent stimulation in humans (Danish-American collaboration) 2. Selectivity: Minimal cortisol/prolactin elevation demonstrated (Novo Nordisk, Denmark) 3. Bone effects: Increased BMC in rat models (Denmark/Sweden collaboration) 4. Tolerability: Generally well-tolerated in short-term studies (USA/Canada trials) 5. Development: Discontinued after Phase 2 POI trial failed to meet primary endpoint --- Key Human Trials Phase 1/2 Studies Summary | Study | N | Phase | Key Finding | PMID | |-------|---|-------|-------------|------| | PK/PD dose-escalation | 40 | 1 | Dose-dependent GH; SC50 214 nmol/L; t1/2 2h | 10496658 | | Postoperative ileus (NCT00672074) | 114 | 2 | Well tolerated; no efficacy vs placebo | 25331030 | | Selectivity study | 8 | 1 | No ACTH/cortisol elevation (vs GHRP-6) | 9849822 | PK/PD Study in Healthy Volunteers (1999) Gobburu et al. PMID:10496658 ( | Parameter | Value | |-----------|-------| | N | 40 healthy male volunteers (8 per dose level) | | Design | Dose-escalation, IV infusion over 15 minutes | | Doses | 4.21, 14.02, 42.13, 84.27, 140.45 nmol/kg | | SC50 | 214 nmol/L (concentration for half-maximal GH) | | Max GH production | 694 mIU/L/h | | Half-life | 2 hours | | Clearance | 0.078 L/h/kg | | Volume of distribution | 0.22 L/kg | Key finding: PK parameters showed dose-proportionality. Single episode of GH release with peak at 0.67 hours, followed by exponential decline. Postoperative Ileus Trial - NCT00672074 (2014) Beck et al. PMID:25331030 ( | Parameter | Value | |-----------|-------| | Design | Multicenter, double-blind, placebo-controlled, Phase 2 | | Population | Adults undergoing small/large bowel resection | | Intervention | IV ipamorelin 0.03 mg/kg vs placebo BID, POD 1-7 | | N | 114 (ipamorelin n=56, placebo n=58) | | Primary endpoint | Time to tolerance of standardized solid meal | | Result | Median 25.3h (ipamorelin) vs 32.6h (placebo), p=0.15 | | Safety | 87.5% AEs (ipamorelin) vs 94.8% (placebo) | Outcome: Ipamorelin was well tolerated but did not meet primary endpoint . No significant difference in GI recovery between groups. Development discontinued due to lack of efficacy. Selectivity Study (1998) Raun et al. PMID:9849822 ( Demonstrated that unlike GHRP-6 and GHRP-2, ipamorelin did not release ACTH or cortisol at levels significantly different from GHRH stimulation, even at doses >200-fold higher than the ED50 for GH release. This established ipamorelin as the first selective GH secretagogue. --- Preclinical Evidence Animal Studies | Study | Model | Origin | Key Findings | PMID | et al. 1999 PMID:10373343 ( - Model: Hypophysectomized and intact rats - Ipamorelin stimulated tibial epiphyseal width comparable to GH treatment - Demonstrates bone growth effects independent of endogenous GH Andersen NB et al. 2001 PMID:10828840 ( - Model: Adult female Sprague-Dawley rats - 12-week treatment with ipamorelin increased whole-body BMC by 11% - Comparable to GHRP-6 for bone name: Kisspeptin
summary: An endogenous neuropeptide that serves as the master regulator of reproductive function. Acts upstream of GnRH to control puberty, fertility, and the menstrual cycle. Under clinical investigation as a safer IVF trigger and treatment for hypothalamic amenorrhea. Pioneering research led by Imperial College London.
content: Overview Overview Quick Summary - Master regulator of reproduction - controls GnRH release and the entire reproductive axis - Essential for puberty - genetic mutations cause failure to enter puberty - Phase 2 trials completed for IVF trigger and hypothalamic amenorrhea - Eliminates OHSS risk - major safety advantage over hCG in IVF - Modulates brain activity - affects sexual desire and emotional processing - Imperial College London leads global clinical research program Evidence Strength: Moderate-High - Extensive human physiological studies; multiple Phase 2 clinical trials completed with positive results; no regulatory approvals yet. Identity & Origins | Property | Value | |----------|-------| | Full name | Kisspeptin (derived from KiSS-1 gene) | | Other names | Metastin, KISS1 peptide | | Active forms | Kisspeptin-54 (full length), Kisspeptin-10 (C-terminal fragment) | | Receptor | KISS1R (formerly GPR54) | | Class | Neuropeptide, reproductive hormone regulator | | Endogenous | Yes - naturally produced in hypothalamus | Naming History The name "KiSS-1" was coined in 1996 at Penn State University, named after Hershey's Kisses chocolates (Hershey, Pennsylvania). Originally discovered as a metastasis suppressor gene in melanoma cells - hence the alternate name "metastin." Its critical role in reproduction was discovered separately in 2003. Development Timeline - 1996: KiSS-1 gene cloned as metastasis suppressor (Penn State) - 2001: Metastin identified as GPR54 ligand (Takeda Pharmaceutical, Japan) - 2003: Landmark discovery: KISS1R mutations cause hypogonadotropic hypogonadism (Harvard & INSERM) - 2005: First human study shows potent LH stimulation (Imperial College London) - 2014: First IVF trigger trial (Imperial College London) - 2017: Phase 2b IVF trial published in Lancet Diabetes Endocrinology - Present: Ongoing clinical development; no approvals yet The Reproductive Hormone Cascade KISSPEPTIN neurons (hypothalamus) | | Kisspeptin release v GnRH neurons (hypothalamus) | | GnRH release (pulsatile) v PITUITARY GLAND | |---> LH (Luteinizing Hormone) |---> FSH (Follicle-Stimulating Hormone) v GONADS (Ovaries/Testes) | |---> Estrogen / Testosterone |---> Oocyte maturation / Sperm production v REPRODUCTIVE FUNCTION Kisspeptin neurons are the pacemakers of this entire system. Kisspeptin Neuron Populations Two distinct populations of kisspeptin neurons exist in the hypothalamus: | Location | Primary Function | Key Feature | |----------|------------------|-------------| | Arcuate nucleus (ARC) | GnRH pulse generation | Co-express neurokinin B & dynorphin (KNDy neurons) | | Anteroventral periventricular (AVPV) | LH surge generation | Sexually dimorphic; larger in females | KNDy Neuron System The arcuate kisspeptin neurons are called KNDy neurons because they co-express: - K isspeptin - stimulates GnRH - N eurokinin B - stimulates kisspeptin release - Dy norphin - inhibits kisspeptin release This creates an auto-regulatory system that generates the pulsatile GnRH rhythm essential for fertility. Neurokinin B (+) | v KISSPEPTIN RELEASE ---> GnRH pulse ^ | Dynorphin (-) KISS1R Signaling Kisspeptin binds to KISS1R (GPR54), a G protein-coupled receptor: Kisspeptin binds KISS1R (Gq/11-coupled GPCR) | v Phospholipase C activation | v IP3 + DAG production | v Intracellular Ca2+ mobilization | v GnRH neuron depolarization | v GnRH secretion Steroid Hormone Feedback Kisspeptin neurons integrate sex steroid feedback to regulate reproduction: | Hormone | Effect on ARC Kisspeptin | Effect on AVPV Kisspeptin | |---------|-------------------------|---------------------------| | Estrogen (low) | Negative feedback (suppression) | Negative feedback | | Estrogen (high/surge) | Negative feedback | Positive feedback (triggers LH surge) | | Progesterone | Suppression | Modulation | | Testosterone | Negative feedback | - | This explains why kisspeptin neurons are crucial for ovulation - they detect rising estrogen and trigger the LH surge. Why Kisspeptin Matters Clinically | Advantage | Explanation | |-----------|-------------| | Physiological action | Works through natural GnRH pathway | | Self-limiting | Does not cause prolonged receptor activation | | No OHSS | Shorter LH surge than hCG; reduces OHSS risk | | Diagnostic potential | Can assess reproductive axis integrity | --- Research Summary Evidence Overview | Category | Count | Notes | |----------|-------|-------| | Human clinical trials | 12 | Phase 1-2; IVF, hypothalamic amenorrhea, physiology | | Human physiological studies | 6 | Healthy volunteers; mechanism studies | | Primate studies | 4 | Puberty, neurobiology | | Rodent/preclinical | 8 | Mechanism, receptor studies | | Reviews/meta-analyses | 4 | Including 2024 systematic review | | Total sources | 24 | 15 open access | Research Leadership | Institution | Contribution | Key Investigators | |-------------|--------------|-------------------| | Imperial College London | Clinical trials, human physiology | Dhillo WS, Abbara A, Jayasena CN, Comninos AN | | University of Edinburgh | Female reproductive physiology | Anderson RA, George JT | | Harvard/MGH | Genetic discovery, US trials | Seminara SB, Kaiser UB | | Takeda Pharmaceutical (Japan) | Original discovery as GPR54 ligand | Ohtaki T, Matsui H | | Nagoya University (Japan) | KNDy neuron research | Uenoyama Y, Tsukamura H | | INSERM (France) | Genetic discovery | de Roux N | --- Key Human Trials IVF Trigger Trials (Imperial College London) The Problem: Standard IVF uses hCG to trigger final egg maturation, but hCG carries significant risk of Ovarian Hyperstimulation Syndrome (OHSS) - a potentially life-threatening complication. The Solution: Kisspeptin triggers a natural, short-lived LH surge that matures eggs without the prolonged ovarian stimulation that causes OHSS. Phase 2b Trial (Lancet Diabetes Endocrinology 2017) | Parameter | Details | |-----------|---------| | Design | Randomized controlled trial | | Population | Women at high risk of OHSS undergoing IVF | | Intervention | Kisspeptin-54 as trigger vs standard hCG | | Primary outcome | Oocyte maturation rate | Key Results: - Comparable oocyte maturation rates to hCG - Zero cases of OHSS in kisspeptin group - Successful pregnancies achieved - Well tolerated Earlier IVF Studies | Study | Year | N | Key Finding | |-------|------|---|-------------| | Jayasena et al. (J Clin Invest) | 2014 | 53 | Proof-of-concept; kisspeptin triggers oocyte maturation | | Narayanaswamy et al. | 2016 | 60 | Subcutaneous route effective | | Abbara et al. (Lancet) | 2017 | 60 | Phase 2b RCT; no OHSS | Hypothalamic Amenorrhea Studies The Problem: Women with hypothalamic amenorrhea (HA) - often caused by stress, weight loss, or excessive exercise - have suppressed GnRH pulsatility and cannot ovulate. The Discovery: Kisspeptin can restart the GnRH pulse generator in these women. | Study | Year | Finding | |-------|------|---------| | Abbara et al. | 2015 | Kisspeptin rescues LH pulsatility in HA | | Jayasena et al. | 2014 | Repeated kisspeptin restores hormone patterns | Brain and Behavior Studies A remarkable finding from Imperial College London: | Study | Year | Method | Finding | |-------|------|--------|---------| | Comninos et al. (J Clin Invest) | 2017 | fMRI neuroimaging | Kisspeptin enhances limbic brain activity and sexual/emotional processing in men | This suggests kisspeptin has effects beyond just hormone control - it may directly influence sexual desire and emotional bonding. Landmark Genetic Studies (2003) Two independent groups simultaneously discovered that KISS1R mutations cause hypogonadotropic hypogonadism (failure to enter puberty): | Study | Institution | Journal | |-------|-------------|---------| | Seminara et al. | Harvard | N Engl J Med | | de Roux et al. | INSERM | PNAS | This established kisspeptin as essential for human puberty and reproduction. --- Preclinical Evidence Primate Studies | Study | Model | Key Finding | |-------|-------|-------------| | Shahab et al. 2005 | Juvenile male monkeys | Continuous kisspeptin infusion induces precocious puberty | | Terasawa et al. 2013 | Female rhesus monkeys | Kisspeptin neurons increase before puberty onset | | Oakley et al. 2009 | Rhesus monkeys | Kisspeptin neurons express estrogen/progesterone receptors | Rodent Studies | Study Type | Finding | |------------|---------| | Kisspeptin knockouts | Complete infertility; failure of puberty | | Kisspeptin administration | Robust gonadotropin release | | Receptor studies | KISS1R expressed on GnRH neurons | | Pulse generator studies | KNDy neurons drive GnRH pulsatility | Japanese Contributions Takeda Pharmaceutical Company (Japan) made foundational discoveries: | Study | Year | Finding | |-------|------|---------| | Ohtaki et al. (Nature) | 2001 | Identified kisspeptin as endogenous GPR54 ligand | | Matsui et al. | 2004 | First demonstration that kisspeptin stimulates gonadotropins in vivo | --- Safety Profile Clinical Trial Safety Data Kisspeptin has shown an excellent safety profile across all human studies: | Parameter | Observation | |-----------|-------------| | Serious adverse events | None reported in clinical trials | | OHSS | Zero cases when used as IVF trigger | | Injection site reactions | Mild, transient | | Systemic effects | Generally well tolerated | Reported Adverse Events | Event | Frequency | Notes | |-------|-----------|-------| | Injection site discomfort | Common | Mild, transient | | Flushing | Occasional | Expected from mechanism | | Abdominal discomfort | Rare | Usually mild | | Headache | Rare | No pattern established | Theoretical Considerations | Consideration | Notes | |---------------|-------| | Tachyphylaxis | Continuous exposure may desensitize; pulsatile preferred | | Long-term effects | Unknown; studies have been short-term | | Cancer history | Originally a metastasis suppressor; theoretical concern about tumor effects | | Pregnancy | Safety not established; appropriate for IVF trigger before conception | Why Kisspeptin is Safer Than hCG for IVF | Factor | Kisspeptin | hCG | |--------|------------|-----| | LH surge duration | Hours | Days | | OHSS risk | Eliminated/very low | Significant | | Mechanism | Physiological, self-limiting | Supraphysiological, prolonged | | Receptor binding | KISS1R | LH receptor (prolonged activation) | --- Pharmacokinetics Kisspeptin-54 vs Kisspeptin-10 | Parameter | Kisspeptin-54 | Kisspeptin-10 | |-----------|---------------|---------------| | Structure | Full-length (54 amino acids) | C-terminal fragment (10 amino acids) | | Potency | High | High (equipotent for receptor) | | Half-life | 28 minutes | 4 minutes | | Duration | Longer LH response | Shorter LH response | | Clinical use | IVF trigger studies | Physiological studies | Key Pharmacokinetic Parameters (Kisspeptin-54) | Parameter | Value | Notes | |-----------|-------|-------| | Half-life | 28 minutes | Relatively short | | Route | IV or SC | Both studied clinically | | Onset | Minutes | Rapid LH response | | Peak LH | 4-6 hours | After single administration | | LH return to baseline | 12 hours | Much shorter than hCG | Route Comparison | Route | Advantages | Disadvantages | |-------|------------|---------------| | Intravenous | Precise dosing; rapid onset | Requires IV access | | Subcutaneous | Practical; patient-administered | Slightly delayed onset | Clinical trials have demonstrated that subcutaneous administration is practical and effective for IVF triggering. --- Regulatory Status | Region | Status | Notes | |--------|--------|-------| | USA (FDA) | Investigational | IND applications; Phase 2 studies ongoing | | EU (EMA) | Investigational | Scientific advice obtained | | UK (MHRA) | Investigational | Imperial College trials conducted | | Japan | Research | Foundational research; no clinical development | Clinical Development Status | Indication | Stage | Lead Institution | |------------|-------|------------------| | IVF trigger | Phase 2b completed | Imperial College London | | Hypothalamic amenorrhea | Phase 2 completed | Imperial College London | | Diagnostic applications | Investigational | Multiple centers | | Sexual dysfunction | Early research | Imperial College London | Path to Approval Kisspeptin faces several development challenges: - Natural peptide - complex manufacturing and stability - Short half-life - may require analog development - Novel mechanism - requires regulatory education - Academic development - limited commercial investment --- Current Research Directions Active Areas of Investigation | Area | Status | Potential | |------|--------|-----------| | IVF trigger optimization | Phase 2/3 | Near-term clinical application | | Hypothalamic amenorrhea | Phase 2 | Restore fertility without IVF | | PCOS treatment | Preclinical/early clinical | Address anovulation | | Sexual function/desire | Early clinical | Brain effects on arousal | | Kisspeptin analogs | Development | Longer-acting versions | | Diagnostic tool | Investigational | Assess reproductive axis | Kisspeptin Analog Development Researchers are working to develop kisspeptin analogs with: - Longer half-life - for practical administration - Improved stability - better formulation - Oral bioavailability - convenient dosing Beyond Fertility Emerging research suggests kisspeptin may have roles in: - Metabolic regulation - links between reproduction and metabolism - Bone health - effects via sex hormones - Cancer - original metastasis suppressor function - Mood and behavior - limbic system effects --- Related Peptides Reproductive Hormone Regulators | Peptide | Relationship | Mechanism | |---------|--------------|-----------| | GnRH | Downstream target | Released by kisspeptin stimulation | | GnRH agonists (leuprolide) | Indirect comparator | Different mechanism; causes downregulation | | GnRH antagonists | Comparator | Block GnRH receptor | | hCG | Clinical comparator | LH receptor agonist; IVF trigger | Key Differences from GnRH Analogs | Aspect | Kisspeptin | GnRH Agonists | GnRH Antagonists | |--------|------------|---------------|------------------| | Site of action | Upstream of GnRH | Pituitary | Pituitary | | Initial effect | Stimulation | Stimulation (then suppression) | Immediate block | | Desensitization | Minimal | Significant (therapeutic) | None | | OHSS risk | Very low | Low (after downreg) | Low | | Physiological | Yes | No (supraphysiological) | No (complete block) | Related Neuropeptides | Peptide | Co-expressed with Kisspeptin | Role | |---------|------------------------------|------| | Neurokinin B | In KNDy neurons | Stimulates kisspeptin release | | Dynorphin | In KNDy neurons | Inhibits kisspeptin release | | GnRH | Target of kisspeptin | Final common pathway | --- Citation Table Landmark Discovery Papers | Source | Year | Type | Key Finding | Access |
