name: KPV
summary: A naturally occurring tripeptide derived from the C-terminus of alpha-melanocyte-stimulating hormone (alpha-MSH). Preclinical studies demonstrate potent anti-inflammatory effects via NF-kB inhibition, but no human clinical trials have been conducted. Research has focused on inflammatory bowel disease and skin inflammation models.
content: Overview Overview Quick Summary - Naturally occurring tripeptide (3 amino acids: Lysine-Proline-Valine) from alpha-MSH C-terminus - Not FDA approved for any indication; no regulatory approval worldwide - No human clinical trials - all evidence from animal models and cell studies - Potent NF-kB inhibitor in preclinical models - blocks key inflammatory pathway - Interest in IBD research due to oral bioavailability via PepT1 transporter - Retains anti-inflammatory activity of parent alpha-MSH without pigmentation effects Evidence Strength: Low - Strong mechanistic rationale and consistent preclinical data, but complete absence of human clinical trials limits clinical relevance assessment. Identity & Origins | Property | Value | |----------|-------| | Generic name | KPV | | Sequence | Lys-Pro-Val (Lysine-Proline-Valine) | | Parent compound | Alpha-melanocyte-stimulating hormone (alpha-MSH) | | Position | C-terminal tripeptide (residues 11-13) | | Molecular weight | 342.4 Da | | Class | Melanocortin-derived anti-inflammatory peptide | | Natural occurrence | Derived from POMC (proopiomelanocortin) processing | Research Timeline - 1980s-1990s: Alpha-MSH anti-inflammatory properties discovered - 1998: Manna & Aggarwal establish NF-kB inhibition mechanism - 2003: Getting et al. demonstrate KPV retains activity independent of melanocortin receptors - 2007-2008: Extensive IBD preclinical studies (Kannengiesser, Dalmasso) - 2010: Nanoparticle delivery systems developed for targeted colonic delivery - Current: No clinical trials initiated; remains investigational compound Scientific Pathways (Preclinical Evidence Only) 1. NF-kB Pathway Inhibition (Primary Mechanism) Inflammatory stimulus (LPS, TNF-alpha, IL-1) | IKK activation | IkBa phosphorylation & degradation | KPV BLOCKS HERE | NF-kB nuclear translocation (prevented) | Inflammatory gene transcription (reduced) KPV prevents the degradation of IkBa, a protein that normally holds NF-kB inactive in the cytoplasm. By keeping IkBa intact, NF-kB cannot enter the nucleus and turn on inflammatory genes. This was demonstrated in multiple cell types including human glioma cells, monocytes, and intestinal epithelial cells. 2. PepT1-Mediated Cellular Uptake Oral KPV | Intestinal epithelium | PepT1 transporter (SLC15A1) | Intracellular accumulation | Direct NF-kB/MAPK inhibition The peptide transporter PepT1 recognizes KPV's tripeptide structure and actively transports it into cells. This is significant because most peptides are degraded in the gut before absorption. Dalmasso et al. (2008) demonstrated that PepT1-mediated uptake is essential for KPV's anti-inflammatory effects in colitis models. 3. MAPK Pathway Modulation - Inhibits p38 MAPK phosphorylation - Reduces JNK activation - Decreases ERK signaling in inflammatory contexts - These pathways normally amplify inflammatory signals The MAPK pathways are like secondary amplifiers of inflammation. Even if some NF-kB activity escapes KPV's inhibition, blocking MAPK provides a backup anti-inflammatory mechanism. 4. Melanocortin Receptor-Independent Effects KPV → Bypasses MC1R/MC3R/MC4R/MC5R | Direct intracellular action | ├── NF-kB inhibition ├── Cytokine reduction └── No melanotropic effects Getting et al. (2003) showed that KPV retains anti-inflammatory activity even when melanocortin receptors are blocked or absent. This suggests KPV works through a receptor-independent mechanism, possibly after direct cellular uptake. Cytokine Effects | Cytokine | Effect | Significance | |----------|--------|--------------| | TNF-alpha | Decreased | Major pro-inflammatory driver | | IL-1beta | Decreased | Fever and inflammation mediator | | IL-6 | Decreased | Acute phase response regulator | | IL-8 | Decreased | Neutrophil chemoattractant | | IL-10 | Increased (some studies) | Anti-inflammatory cytokine | Comparison to Parent Compound (Alpha-MSH) | Property | Alpha-MSH | KPV | |----------|-----------|-----| | Size | 13 amino acids | 3 amino acids | | MC1R binding | Strong | Weak/none | | Pigmentation effects | Yes (tanning) | No | | Anti-inflammatory | Yes | Yes (preserved) | | Oral bioavailability | Poor | Better (via PepT1) | | Stability | Moderate | Higher | Important Limitations - All mechanistic studies are preclinical (cells and animals) - Human pharmacokinetics completely unknown - Optimal route of administration for humans not established - No comparison studies against standard anti-inflammatory drugs - Single research groups produced most foundational studies --- Research Summary Evidence Overview | Category | Count | Notes | |----------|-------|-------| | Human clinical trials | 0 | No registered or published trials | | Human ex vivo | 1 | Celiac intestinal tissue study | | Preclinical in vivo | 12 | Primarily rodent IBD models | | In vitro studies | 4 | Human and animal cell lines | | Reviews | 5 | Comprehensive mechanism reviews | | Total sources | 18 | 7 open access | Geographic Distribution of Research | Region | Key Contributions | |--------|-------------------| | USA | Dalmasso/Bhattacharya group - PepT1 mechanism, nanoparticle delivery | | Germany | Luger/Bohm/Brzoska - Alpha-MSH biology, dermatological applications | | Italy | Catania/Colombo - Immune modulation, celiac disease tissue studies | | Netherlands | Maaser/Kannengiesser - IBD models, oral KPV studies | | China | MC5R/NLRP3 inflammasome interactions | | Japan | Skin inflammation and wound healing applications | Key Evidence Highlights 1. Inflammatory Bowel Disease: Multiple mouse studies show KPV reduces DSS and TNBS-induced colitis severity, with effects comparable to standard treatments 2. Oral Bioavailability: Unique among anti-inflammatory peptides - PepT1 transport allows intestinal absorption 3. Broad Anti-Inflammatory Spectrum: Effective in skin, gut, brain, and systemic inflammation models 4. Nanoparticle Enhancement: Targeted colon delivery via nanoparticles increases efficacy 10-fold in animal models 5. Safety Signal: No toxicity reported in any preclinical study (limited duration) Critical Note: Despite 25+ years of research, no pharmaceutical company has advanced KPV to human clinical trials. --- Key Human Trials Clinical Trial Status No human clinical trials have been conducted with KPV. As of January 2026, there are no registered clinical trials for KPV on: - ClinicalTrials.gov (USA) - EU Clinical Trials Register - ISRCTN Registry (UK) - Chinese Clinical Trial Registry - Japan Registry of Clinical Trials Human Tissue Studies (Ex Vivo) The only human data comes from ex vivo tissue studies: | Study | Tissue | Findings | Limitations | |-------|--------|----------|-------------| | Colombo 2002 | Celiac intestinal biopsies | Alpha-MSH reduced inflammatory markers | Ex vivo only; alpha-MSH not KPV directly | Why No Clinical Trials? Several factors may explain the lack of clinical development: 1. Patent/commercial challenges - Natural tripeptide may be difficult to patent 2. Competition - Many established anti-inflammatory drugs exist 3. Regulatory pathway - Unclear classification (drug vs supplement) 4. Delivery challenges - Optimal formulation for humans unknown 5. Research funding - Academic interest without pharma investment What Would Be Needed For KPV to progress clinically, studies would need to establish: - Human pharmacokinetics (absorption, distribution, metabolism, elimination) - Maximum tolerated dose - Optimal formulation and delivery - Target patient population - Efficacy endpoints - Long-term safety --- Preclinical Evidence Inflammatory Bowel Disease Models | Study | Model | Route | Key Findings | |-------|-------|-------|--------------| | Kannengiesser 2008 | DSS colitis (mouse) | Oral | Reduced weight loss, histological damage, and inflammatory cytokines | | Kannengiesser 2008 | TNBS colitis (mouse) | Oral | Effective in T-cell mediated colitis model | | Dalmasso 2008 | DSS colitis (mouse) | Oral | PepT1-dependent uptake required for efficacy | | Laroui 2010 | DSS colitis (mouse) | Oral (nanoparticles) | Targeted delivery enhanced efficacy | Summary: Consistent efficacy across multiple IBD models with oral administration, supporting potential for gastrointestinal applications. Cellular/Mechanistic Studies | Study | Model | Key Findings | |-------|-------|--------------| | Manna 1998 | Human cell lines | NF-kB inhibition via IkBa stabilization | | Ichiyama 1999 | Human glioma cells | Brain inflammation reduction | | Capsoni 2007 | Human neutrophils | CKPV dimer inhibited chemotaxis and oxidative burst | | Dalmasso 2008 | Colonic epithelial cells | PepT1 transporter mediates uptake | Systemic Inflammation Models | Model | Findings | |-------|----------| | Endotoxemia (LPS) | Reduced mortality and cytokine storm | | Ischemia-reperfusion | Decreased tissue damage in intestine | | Contact dermatitis | Reduced skin inflammation | | UV-induced inflammation | Photoprotective effects | Drug Delivery Innovations Nanoparticle Systems (Laroui 2010): - Polysaccharide hydrogel coating for colon-specific release - 10-fold improvement in efficacy vs free KPV - Reduced systemic exposure while maintaining local effects - Proof-of-concept for targeted IBD therapy Limitations of Preclinical Data 1. Species differences - Mouse immune system differs significantly from human 2. Acute models - Most IBD studies use acute colitis; chronic disease may differ 3. Endpoint selection - Surrogate markers may not predict clinical outcomes 4. Publication bias - Negative studies likely unpublished 5. Reproducibility - Limited independent replication of key studies --- Safety Profile Known Information The safety profile of KPV in humans is completely unknown. No human studies have been conducted. Preclinical Safety Data | Finding | Source | Notes | |---------|--------|-------| | No acute toxicity | Multiple mouse studies | Up to several weeks exposure | | No behavioral changes | Colitis studies | Normal activity observed | | No weight loss (beyond disease) | IBD models | KPV groups maintained weight | | No organ pathology | Histological exams | Limited to gastrointestinal tract | Theoretical Considerations Potential advantages: - Natural compound (derived from endogenous alpha-MSH) - Small size (tripeptide) - less likely to trigger immune reactions - No melanocortin receptor binding - avoids pigmentation side effects - Targets intracellular pathway - potentially selective anti-inflammatory Potential concerns: - Immunosuppression risk - Blocking NF-kB could impair immune defense - Unknown chronic effects - No long-term studies conducted - Drug interactions - NF-kB pathway involved in many drug mechanisms - Cancer considerations - NF-kB has complex roles in tumor biology - Quality control - Non-pharmaceutical sources have unknown purity Important Warning Products sold as KPV are not regulated pharmaceutical products. Identity, purity, sterility, and safety cannot be assured for non-pharmaceutical sources. Contamination risks include: - Bacterial endotoxins - Residual solvents - Incorrect sequence - Degradation products --- Pharmacokinetics Known Parameters (Preclinical Only) | Parameter | Value | Notes | |-----------|-------|-------| | Absorption | Via PepT1 transporter | Demonstrated in intestinal cells | | Oral bioavailability | Unknown % | Better than most peptides | | Half-life | Unknown | Not characterized | | Distribution | Unknown | Not studied | | Metabolism | Presumed proteolytic | Typical peptide degradation | | Elimination | Unknown | Not characterized | PepT1-Mediated Absorption The peptide transporter PepT1 (SLC15A1) provides a unique absorption mechanism: - Location: Primarily small intestine brush border membrane - Substrates: Di- and tripeptides, including drugs like beta-lactam antibiotics - Capacity: High-capacity, low-affinity transporter - Significance: May allow oral delivery of KPV - unusual for peptides Dalmasso et al. demonstrated that KPV uptake is: - Saturable (consistent with transporter-mediated) - Inhibited by other PepT1 substrates - Blocked in PepT1-knockdown cells Stability Considerations | Factor | Effect on KPV | |--------|---------------| | Gastric acid | Unknown - likely some degradation | | Pepsin/proteases | Tripeptides relatively resistant | | Temperature | Standard peptide storage requirements | | Oxidation | Lysine may be susceptible | Research Gaps Critical unknowns for human application: - Absolute oral bioavailability in humans - Plasma half-life and clearance - Tissue distribution profile - Dose-response relationship - Food effects on absorption - Accumulation with repeated dosing --- Regulatory Status | Region | Status | Notes | |--------|--------|-------| | USA (FDA) | Not approved | No NDA/BLA filed; no IND publicly known | | EU (EMA) | Not approved | Not authorized for any indication | | UK (MHRA) | Not approved | Not licensed | | Japan (PMDA) | Not approved | Not authorized | | Australia (TGA) | Not approved | Not listed or registered | | China (NMPA) | Not approved | No approval record | | WADA | Not listed | Not specifically prohibited (as of 2025) | Legal Classification KPV exists in a regulatory gray area: - Not an approved drug - Cannot be marketed for disease treatment - Not a dietary supplement - No GRAS (Generally Recognized as Safe) status - Research chemical - Available for laboratory research use - Cosmetic ingredient - Some use in cosmetic formulations (limited) FDA Position The FDA has not issued specific guidance on KPV. However, general principles apply: - Products claiming to treat disease require drug approval - Injectable products require sterility and potency standards - Health claims on supplements are restricted --- Related Peptides Melanocortin Family | Peptide | Relationship to KPV | Evidence Level | |---------|---------------------|----------------| | Alpha-MSH | Parent compound (KPV is C-terminus) | Moderate | | ACTH | Shares N-terminal sequence with alpha-MSH | High (endocrine) | | Beta-MSH | Related melanocortin | Low | | Gamma-MSH | Related melanocortin | Low | | Afamelanotide | Synthetic alpha-MSH analog (approved) | High | Anti-Inflammatory Peptides | Peptide | Mechanism | Evidence Level | |---------|-----------|----------------| | BPC-157 | VEGF/NO pathways | Low (preclinical) | | Thymosin Alpha-1 | Immune modulation | Moderate (approved in some countries) | | LL-37 | Antimicrobial/immunomodulatory | Low-Moderate | | Vasoactive Intestinal Peptide (VIP) | cAMP/anti-inflammatory | Moderate | KPV vs Alpha-MSH Comparison | Feature | KPV | Alpha-MSH | |---------|-----|-----------| | Size | 3 amino acids | 13 amino acids | | Melanocortin receptor binding | Minimal | Strong (MC1R, MC3R, MC4R) | | Anti-inflammatory activity | Preserved | Full activity | | Pigmentation effects | None | Skin darkening | | Appetite effects | None | Appetite suppression (MC4R) | | Oral bioavailability | Better (PepT1) | Poor | | Clinical development | None | Analog approved (afamelanotide) | KPV vs BPC-157 Comparison Both are popular in biohacking communities but have different profiles: | Aspect | KPV | BPC-157 | |--------|-----|---------| | Origin | Natural (from alpha-MSH) | Synthetic (gastric protein fragment) | | Size | 3 amino acids | 15 amino acids | | Primary mechanism | NF-kB inhibition | VEGF/angiogenesis | | Target applications | Inflammation, IBD | Tissue repair, healing | | Human trials | None | 1 small pilot | | Research groups | Multiple international | Primarily single group | --- Current Research Directions Active Research Areas 1. Inflammatory Bowel Disease - Remains the most promising application - Nanoparticle delivery systems in development - Potential for colon-targeted therapy 2. Dermatological Applications - Topical formulations for skin inflammation - Photoprotection (UV-induced damage) - Wound healing enhancement 3. Neuroinflammation - Brain inflammation models showing efficacy - Blood-brain barrier penetration unknown - Potential in neurodegenerative conditions 4. Drug Delivery Innovation - Oral nanoparticle systems - Colon-specific release formulations - Combination with other anti-inflammatory agents Key Research Questions Mechanistic: - Exact intracellular target after PepT1 uptake - Relationship to melanocortin receptor signaling - Effects on other inflammatory pathways (JAK-STAT, etc.) Translational: - Human pharmacokinetics and optimal dosing - Biomarkers to predict response - Patient populations most likely to benefit Clinical: - Safety in immunocompromised patients - Long-term effects of NF-kB inhibition - Combination with existing IBD therapies Barriers to Clinical Development 1. Commercial viability - Natural tripeptide difficult to protect with patents 2. Regulatory pathway - Unclear classification and approval route 3. Competition - Many established anti-inflammatory drugs (biologics, JAK inhibitors) 4. Investment - Requires significant capital for clinical trials 5. Manufacturing - Scale-up for pharmaceutical-grade production Future Outlook KPV remains an interesting research tool for understanding melanocortin-mediated anti-inflammatory pathways. Clinical development would require: - Pharmaceutical company interest - Novel formulation providing competitive advantage - Clear regulatory pathway - Significant safety and efficacy data from first-in-human studies --- Citation Table Foundational Reviews | Source | Year | Type | Key Finding | Access | |--------|------|------|-------------|--------| | Brzoska T et al. KPV is not approved by the FDA or any regulatory agency for any indication. No human clinical trials have been conducted. Last updated: January 2026.

name: Liraglutide
summary: The first long-acting GLP-1 receptor agonist, FDA-approved for type 2 diabetes (Victoza, 2010) and chronic weight management (Saxenda, 2014). Pioneered the therapeutic class with proven cardiovascular benefits in the landmark LEADER trial.
content: Overview Quick Summary - FDA-approved GLP-1 receptor agonist for type 2 diabetes (Victoza, 2010) and chronic weight management (Saxenda, 2014) - First long-acting GLP-1 agonist: Pioneered once-daily dosing in the GLP-1RA class; paved the way for semaglutide and tirzepatide - Strong evidence base: LEAD program (diabetes), SCALE program (obesity), LEADER cardiovascular outcomes trial - Demonstrated efficacy: 8% mean weight loss in obesity trials (SCALE); HbA1c reduction of 1.0-1.5% in diabetes trials - Cardiovascular benefit proven: LEADER trial showed 13% reduction in MACE in T2D patients at high CV risk - Pediatric approval: First GLP-1RA approved for adolescents (ages 10+) with T2D (2019) and obesity (2020) - Well-characterized safety: GI side effects common (nausea 25-40%); extensive post-marketing experience since 2010 Evidence Strength: HIGH — Supported by multiple Phase 3 RCTs, a dedicated cardiovascular outcomes trial (LEADER), regulatory approvals across major markets, and over 15 years of post-marketing surveillance data with millions of patient-years of exposure. -------|-------| | Generic name | Liraglutide | | Brand names | Victoza (diabetes), Saxenda (weight management) | | Development code | NN2211 | | Chemical class | GLP-1 receptor agonist (acylated peptide analog) | | Sequence | Modified GLP-1(7-37) with Arg34 substitution + C16 fatty acid (palmitic acid) at Lys26 | | Molecular weight | 3,751 Da | | Structure | 97% sequence homology with native human GLP-1 | Discovery & Development Timeline | Year | Milestone | Significance | |------|-----------|--------------| | 1983 | GLP-1 discovered | Bell et al. identified proglucagon gene containing GLP-1 sequence PMID: 6835407 | | 1987 | Incretin effect demonstrated | Kreymann et al. showed GLP-1 stimulates insulin secretion in humans PMID: 2890903 | | 1995 | DPP-4 degradation identified | Deacon et al. explained why native GLP-1 has 2-minute half-life PMID: 7883856 | | 2000 | Acylation strategy developed | Knudsen et al. (Novo Nordisk) showed fatty acid attachment extends half-life PMID: 10794683 | | 2001 | Liraglutide synthesis completed | NN2211 entered clinical development | | 2005-2009 | LEAD program | Phase 3 trials established efficacy in T2D | | 2010 | FDA approval (Victoza) | Approved for type 2 diabetes (once daily subcutaneous) | | 2011-2015 | SCALE program | Phase 3 trials for weight management | | 2014 | FDA approval (Saxenda) | Approved for chronic weight management | | 2016 | LEADER trial published | Cardiovascular benefit demonstrated PMID: 27295427 | | 2017 | CV indication added | FDA updated label for CV risk reduction | | 2019 | Pediatric T2D approval | First GLP-1RA approved for children ages 10+ with T2D | | 2020 | Pediatric obesity approval | Saxenda approved for adolescents 12-17 with obesity | Why Liraglutide Was Developed Native GLP-1 has a half-life of approximately 2 minutes due to rapid degradation by dipeptidyl peptidase-4 (DPP-4) and renal clearance. This makes it impractical as a therapeutic. Liraglutide was engineered to address this limitation: 1. Amino acid substitution: Position 34 changed from lysine to arginine to prevent DPP-4 cleavage 2. Fatty acid acylation: C16 palmitic acid attached at position 26 enables non-covalent albumin binding 3. Result: Half-life extended from 2 minutes to approximately 13 hours, enabling once-daily dosing Original therapeutic target: Type 2 diabetes glycemic control. Liraglutide was the first long-acting GLP-1 analog to reach the market, establishing the therapeutic foundation for the entire GLP-1RA class. --- Mechanism of Action How Liraglutide Works (Plain English) Liraglutide mimics a natural hormone called GLP-1 that your body releases after eating. Here is what it does: 1. Helps your body manage blood sugar — When you eat, liraglutide signals your pancreas to release insulin, but ONLY when your blood sugar is high. This glucose-dependent action minimizes the risk of dangerous low blood sugar episodes. 2. Reduces appetite and increases fullness — Liraglutide acts on hunger centers in your brain, reducing the drive to eat. Most people feel less hungry and get satisfied on smaller portions. 3. Slows stomach emptying — Food stays in your stomach longer, which helps you feel full longer and prevents blood sugar spikes after meals. 4. Protects your heart — In the LEADER trial, liraglutide reduced heart attacks, strokes, and cardiovascular death in people with diabetes who had existing heart disease or risk factors. Why once-daily dosing works: Natural GLP-1 is destroyed within 2 minutes in your body. Liraglutide has been chemically modified to survive for about 13 hours by binding to albumin in your blood, so one injection per day provides continuous effects. --- Receptor Target: GLP-1R (Scientific Detail) Liraglutide is a selective agonist of the glucagon-like peptide-1 receptor (GLP-1R), a G protein-coupled receptor (GPCR) of the class B1 secretin family. GLP-1R expression and functional effects: | Tissue | Expression Level | Primary Effect | What This Means | |--------|------------------|----------------|-----------------| | Pancreatic beta cells | High | Glucose-dependent insulin secretion | Releases insulin when blood sugar is high | | Pancreatic alpha cells | Moderate | Glucagon suppression | Prevents liver from releasing stored sugar | | Hypothalamus | High | Appetite and satiety regulation | Makes you feel full, reduces hunger | | Area postrema (brainstem) | High | Nausea pathway; food intake regulation | Can cause nausea early on; also reduces eating | | Gastrointestinal tract | Moderate | Delayed gastric emptying | Food stays in stomach longer, steadier energy | | Heart/vasculature | Low-moderate | Cardioprotective signaling | May protect heart from damage | | Kidney | Low | Natriuresis | Helps kidney eliminate salt and fluid | Supported Mechanisms (Direct Human Evidence) Glycemic control mechanisms: - Glucose-dependent insulinotropic effect: Enhances insulin secretion from beta cells only when glucose is elevated, minimizing hypoglycemia risk PMID: 17383062 - Glucagon suppression: Reduces inappropriate glucagon secretion in a glucose-dependent manner PMID: 17383062 - Gastric emptying delay: Slows nutrient absorption, reducing postprandial glucose excursions PMID: 18184924 Weight loss mechanisms: - Central appetite suppression: Activates anorexigenic pathways in the hypothalamus PMID: 25169296 - Caloric intake reduction: Studies show 15-25% reduction in food consumption PMID: 25169296 - Satiety enhancement: Gastric emptying delay promotes feeling of fullness Cardiovascular effects: - Blood pressure reduction: Modest 2-4 mmHg systolic reduction observed consistently PMID: 27295427 - Inflammatory marker reduction: Decreases hsCRP PMID: 27295427 - MACE reduction: 13% reduction in LEADER; mechanism likely multifactorial Hypothesized Mechanisms (Plausible but Uncertain) | Hypothesis | Supporting Evidence | Current Confidence | |------------|--------------------|--------------------| | Direct cardioprotection | GLP-1R on cardiomyocytes; animal data shows reduced infarct size | Low (human mechanism data lacking) | | Neuroprotection | GLP-1R in brain; Phase 2 ELAD trial showed reduced brain atrophy | Low (Phase 2 data only) | | Beta-cell preservation | In vitro and animal studies show anti-apoptotic effects | Low (not confirmed in humans) | | Anti-atherogenic effects | Reduces inflammation markers; animal plaque data | Moderate | Signaling Pathway Overview Primary pathway: Liraglutide binds GLP-1R | Gs protein activation --> adenylyl cyclase --> increased cAMP | Protein kinase A (PKA) activation | |-- Beta cells: increased insulin exocytosis (glucose-dependent) |-- Alpha cells: decreased glucagon release |-- Hypothalamus: altered firing in appetite circuits |-- Other tissues: varied effects In simpler terms: When liraglutide binds to its receptor, it triggers a cascade of signals inside the cell. The end result depends on which cell type receives the signal: in pancreatic cells, it triggers insulin release; in brain cells, it changes hunger signaling. The key is that this only happens when blood sugar is elevated, which is why liraglutide does not cause low blood sugar on its own. --- Too Much vs Too Little: Physiologic Context Native GLP-1 Physiology GLP-1 is an endogenous incretin hormone secreted by intestinal L-cells in response to nutrient ingestion (carbohydrates, proteins, and fats). Normal incretin effect: - In healthy individuals, oral glucose produces approximately 2-3x greater insulin response than equivalent IV glucose - Approximately 50-70% of this "incretin effect" is attributed to GLP-1 and GIP combined GLP-1 deficiency/impairment (type 2 diabetes): - The incretin effect is substantially reduced in T2D (from approximately 60% contribution to approximately 20%) PMID: 16644699 - This is primarily due to reduced responsiveness to GLP-1, not reduced secretion - Beta cells in T2D show impaired GLP-1R-mediated insulin secretion Supraphysiologic GLP-1 (what liraglutide produces): - Plasma concentrations achieved with liraglutide are 5-10x higher than endogenous postprandial levels - This overcomes the reduced responsiveness seen in T2D - Also produces effects in tissues not normally exposed to sustained high GLP-1 Tolerance, Tachyphylaxis, and Adaptation | Observation | Evidence | Clinical Implication | |-------------|----------|---------------------| | GI tolerance improves | Nausea decreases from approximately 40% to approximately 10% over 4-8 weeks | Slow dose titration allows adaptation | | Weight loss plateaus | Maximum loss typically at 40-56 weeks, then maintained | Not tachyphylaxis; represents new physiologic set point | | Glycemic efficacy maintained | HbA1c reduction sustained at 2+ years PMID: 21753066 | No functional tolerance for glucose-lowering | | Weight regain after stopping | Approximately 2/3 of lost weight regained within 1 year of discontinuation | Not tolerance; return to baseline physiology | --- Key Human Trials LEAD Program (Liraglutide Effect and Action in Diabetes) The LEAD program comprised 6 Phase 3 trials evaluating liraglutide 1.2mg and 1.8mg daily in type 2 diabetes, totaling over 4,000 participants. | Trial | Comparator | N | Duration | HbA1c Reduction | Weight Change | |-------|------------|---|----------|-----------------|---------------| | LEAD-1 | Glimepiride | 1,041 | 26 wk | -1.1% (1.8mg) vs -0.4% | -0.2 to +0.3 kg | | LEAD-2 | Glimepiride, Metformin | 1,091 | 26 wk | -1.0% (1.8mg) vs -1.0% (glim) | -2.8 kg vs +1.0 kg | | LEAD-3 | Glimepiride monotherapy | 746 | 52 wk | -1.1% (1.8mg) vs -0.5% | -2.5 kg vs +1.1 kg | | LEAD-4 | Rosiglitazone + Metformin | 533 | 26 wk | -1.5% (1.8mg) vs -1.2% | -2.0 kg vs +1.6 kg | | LEAD-5 | Glargine + Metformin + SU | 581 | 26 wk | -1.3% vs -1.1% | -1.8 kg vs +1.6 kg | | LEAD-6 | Exenatide BID | 464 | 26 wk | -1.1% vs -0.8% | -3.2 kg vs -2.9 kg | LEAD-6 Key Result PMID: 19564476 : - Liraglutide 1.8mg superior to exenatide 10mcg BID for HbA1c reduction - Similar weight loss but better GI tolerability with liraglutide - Established liraglutide as superior to first-generation GLP-1RA SCALE Program (Satiety and Clinical Adiposity - Liraglutide Evidence) The SCALE program evaluated liraglutide 3.0mg daily for weight management. | Trial | Population | N | Duration | Weight Loss (lira vs placebo) | |-------|------------|---|----------|-------------------------------| | SCALE Obesity and Prediabetes | Obesity/overweight | 3,731 | 56 wk | -8.0% vs -2.6% | | SCALE Diabetes | Obesity + T2D | 846 | 56 wk | -6.0% vs -2.0% | | SCALE Maintenance | Post-diet weight maintenance | 422 | 56 wk | -6.2% vs -0.2% additional | | SCALE Sleep Apnea | Obesity + OSA | 359 | 32 wk | -5.7%; AHI improved | SCALE Obesity and Prediabetes Detailed Results PMID: 25673378 : - Mean weight loss: 8.0% (liraglutide) vs 2.6% (placebo) - 63.2% achieved 5% or greater weight loss (vs 27.1% placebo) - 33.1% achieved greater than 10% weight loss (vs 10.6% placebo) - 14.4% achieved greater than 15% weight loss (vs 3.5% placebo) - Prediabetes to T2D conversion reduced by 79% over 3 years SCALE Maintenance Results PMID: 26193481 : - Participants who lost 5% or greater on low-calorie diet were randomized - Liraglutide group maintained and extended weight loss; placebo regained - Demonstrates utility for weight maintenance after initial loss LEADER Trial (Liraglutide Effect and Action in Diabetes: Evaluation of Cardiovascular Outcome Results) LEADER PMID: 27295427 was the landmark cardiovascular outcomes trial establishing CV benefit for liraglutide. | Parameter | Value | |-----------|-------| | N | 9,340 | | Duration | Median 3.8 years follow-up | | Population | T2D + high CV risk (established CVD or CV risk factors) | | Primary endpoint | Time to first MACE (CV death, MI, stroke) | | Result | HR 0.87 (95% CI 0.78-0.97) | | P-value | p = 0.01 for superiority | Key Secondary Outcomes: - CV death: HR 0.78 (95% CI 0.66-0.93), p = 0.007 - All-cause mortality: HR 0.85 (95% CI 0.74-0.97), p = 0.02 - Non-fatal MI: HR 0.88 (NS) - Non-fatal stroke: HR 0.89 (NS) Significance: LEADER was the first GLP-1RA trial to demonstrate cardiovascular benefit (published 2016), establishing the class as cardioprotective and transforming diabetes treatment guidelines. ELLIPSE Trial (Pediatric Type 2 Diabetes) ELLIPSE PMID: 31034650 evaluated liraglutide in adolescents with T2D. | Parameter | Value | |-----------|-------| | N | 135 (ages 10-17) | | Duration | 26 weeks (+ 26-week extension) | | Population | Adolescents with T2D on metformin | | Primary endpoint | HbA1c change from baseline | | Result | -1.06% (liraglutide) vs +0.57% (placebo) | | Difference | -1.30% (95% CI -1.65 to -0.95) | Significance: Led to FDA approval of liraglutide for pediatric T2D (2019), the first GLP-1RA approved for children. Pediatric Obesity Trial SCALE TEENS PMID: 32559234 evaluated liraglutide 3.0mg in adolescent obesity. | Parameter | Value | |-----------|-------| | N | 251 (ages 12-17) | | Duration | 56 weeks | | Population | BMI corresponding to 30 or greater in adults | | BMI-SDS change | -0.22 (liraglutide) vs +0.16 (placebo) | | Weight change | -2.65 kg vs +2.37 kg | Significance: Supported FDA approval of Saxenda for adolescents ages 12-17 with obesity (2020). --- Preclinical Evidence Animal Studies | Study Type | Model | Key Findings | Relevance | |------------|-------|--------------|-----------| | Pharmacology | Mouse, rat | High GLP-1R selectivity, no cross-reactivity with related receptors | Supports mechanism | | Efficacy | Diet-induced obese mice | Dose-dependent weight loss, improved glucose tolerance | Predicted human efficacy | | CNS mechanism | Rats | Reduced food intake via hypothalamic pathway activation PMID: 20621966 | Explains weight loss mechanism | | Cardiovascular | Pigs, mice | Reduced infarct size in ischemia-reperfusion models PMID: 18184924 | Uncertain human relevance | | Neuroprotection | APP/PS1 mice | Reduced amyloid-beta, improved cognition PMID: 19664653 | Supported ELAD trial | | Toxicology | Rodents | Thyroid C-cell hyperplasia and MTC at high doses | Contributed to boxed warning | In Vitro Studies - High-affinity binding to human GLP-1 receptor (comparable to native GLP-1) - Resistance to DPP-4 degradation (Arg34 substitution) - Fatty acid chain enables approximately 99% albumin binding, explaining extended half-life - No significant binding to GIP, glucagon, or other related receptors - Beta-cell anti-apoptotic effects demonstrated in cell culture --- Safety Profile Common Adverse Events Data from pooled Phase 3 trials (LEAD, SCALE programs): | Adverse Event | Incidence (approx.) | Severity | Time Course | |---------------|---------------------|----------|-------------| | Nausea | 25-40% | Mild-moderate | Improves over 4-8 weeks | | Diarrhea | 15-20% | Usually mild | Variable | | Vomiting | 10-20% | Mild-moderate | More common early | | Constipation | 10-15% | Mild | May persist | | Dyspepsia | 5-10% | Mild | Usually transient | | Injection site reactions | 5-10% | Mild | Localized | | Headache | 5-15% | Mild | Variable | | Hypoglycemia | 3-10% (higher with SU) | Variable | When combined with SU/insulin | GI tolerability note: Most GI adverse events are dose-dependent and improve with continued therapy. Gradual titration (0.6mg weekly increments to target dose) significantly reduces GI intolerance. Serious Adverse Events and Warnings | Risk | Evidence Type | Frequency | Current Understanding | |------|---------------|-----------|----------------------| | Thyroid C-cell tumors | Rodent carcinogenicity studies | Unknown in humans | Boxed warning - See detailed discussion below | | Pancreatitis | Case reports + trial signal | Rare (approximately 0.2%) | Discontinue if suspected; caution in history of pancreatitis | | Gallbladder disease | Phase 3 trials | 2.5% vs 1.5% placebo (SCALE) | Associated with weight loss | | Acute kidney injury | Post-marketing | Rare | Often related to dehydration from GI effects | | Hypersensitivity | Post-marketing | Rare | Includes anaphylaxis, angioedema | | Increased heart rate | Phase 3 trials | 2-4 bpm increase | Consistent class effect | Thyroid C-Cell Tumors: Detailed Assessment The evidence: - Rodent studies (rats, mice) showed dose-dependent thyroid C-cell hyperplasia and medullary thyroid carcinoma (MTC) at exposures approximately 8x human doses - This is a class effect for all GLP-1 receptor agonists - Mechanism: GLP-1 receptors on rodent thyroid C-cells; chronic stimulation causes proliferation Relevance to humans: - Human thyroid C-cells have much lower GLP-1R expression than rodent C-cells - Over 15+ years of GLP-1RA use and millions of patient-years, no confirmed signal for MTC in humans - Calcitonin monitoring studies show no consistent elevation - LEADER trial: No increase in thyroid cancer with liraglutide vs placebo Regulatory position: - FDA requires boxed warning on all GLP-1RAs - Contraindicated in personal or family history of MTC - Contraindicated in Multiple Endocrine Neoplasia syndrome type 2 (MEN2) - This is a precautionary position; human risk remains theoretical Contraindications 1. Personal or family history of medullary thyroid carcinoma (MTC) 2. Multiple Endocrine Neoplasia syndrome type 2 (MEN2) 3. Known serious hypersensitivity to liraglutide or excipients 4. History of pancreatitis (relative contraindication; use caution) Special Populations | Population | Recommendation | Notes | |------------|----------------|-------| | Pregnancy | Contraindicated | Animal studies show fetal harm; discontinue 2+ months before conception | | Breastfeeding | Not recommended | Unknown excretion in milk | | Renal impairment | Use with caution | Limited data in severe impairment; monitor for dehydration | | Hepatic impairment | Use with caution | Limited data in severe impairment | | Pediatrics (10-17, T2D) | Approved (Victoza) | Based on ELLIPSE trial | | Pediatrics (12-17, obesity) | Approved (Saxenda) | Based on SCALE TEENS trial | | Elderly (65+) | No dose adjustment | Greater GI sensitivity possible | --- Pharmacokinetics Subcutaneous Liraglutide (Victoza/Saxenda) | Parameter | Value | Clinical Relevance | |-----------|-------|-------------------| | Bioavailability | approximately 55% | Moderate for a peptide | | Tmax | 8-12 hours | Slow absorption from SC depot | | Half-life | approximately 13 hours | Enables once-daily dosing | | Volume of distribution | approximately 13 L | Primarily intravascular (albumin-bound) | | Protein binding | greater than 98% | Albumin binding via fatty acid chain | | Metabolism | Proteolytic degradation | Similar to large proteins; no active metabolites | | Elimination | Metabolic clearance | No single organ dominant; not renally excreted intact | Dosing Considerations Victoza (diabetes): - Start: 0.6 mg once daily for 1 week - Increase to: 1.2 mg once daily - Maximum: 1.8 mg once daily Saxenda (weight management): - Start: 0.6 mg once daily - Weekly titration: 0.6 mg increments - Target: 3.0 mg once daily - Discontinue if less than 4% weight loss at 16 weeks Administration: - Inject subcutaneously in abdomen, thigh, or upper arm - Can be given any time of day, with or without food - Rotate injection sites --- Regulatory Status Approval Status by Region (Global) North America | Region | Product | Indication | Year | |--------|---------|------------|------| | USA (FDA) | Victoza | Type 2 diabetes | 2010 | | USA (FDA) | Saxenda | Chronic weight management | 2014 | | USA (FDA) | Victoza | CV risk reduction (T2D + CVD) | 2017 | | USA (FDA) | Victoza | Pediatric T2D (ages 10+) | 2019 | | USA (FDA) | Saxenda | Adolescent obesity (ages 12-17) | 2020 | | Canada | Victoza | Type 2 diabetes | 2010 | | Canada | Saxenda | Weight management | 2015 | Europe | Region | Product | Indication | Year | |--------|---------|------------|------| | EU (EMA) | Victoza | Type 2 diabetes | 2009 | | EU (EMA) | Saxenda | Weight management | 2015 | | UK (MHRA) | Victoza | Type 2 diabetes | 2009 | | UK (MHRA) | Saxenda | Weight management | 2015 | | Denmark | All formulations | As above (Novo Nordisk HQ) | 2009+ | Asia-Pacific | Region | Product | Indication | Year | |--------|---------|------------|------| | Japan (PMDA) | Victoza | Type 2 diabetes | 2010 | | China (NMPA) | Victoza | Type 2 diabetes | 2011 | | South Korea (MFDS) | Victoza | Type 2 diabetes | 2010 | | Australia (TGA) | Victoza | Type 2 diabetes | 2010 | | Australia (TGA) | Saxenda | Weight management | 2016 | Latin America | Region | Product | Indication | Year | |--------|---------|------------|------| | Brazil (ANVISA) | Victoza | Type 2 diabetes | 2010 | | Brazil (ANVISA) | Saxenda | Weight management | 2016 | | Mexico (COFEPRIS) | Victoza | Type 2 diabetes | 2010 | WADA Status Liraglutide is NOT prohibited by the World Anti-Doping Agency. It is not considered a performance-enhancing substance. --- Related Peptides GLP-1 Receptor Agonist Family | Peptide | Dosing Frequency | Key Difference from Liraglutide | |---------|------------------|--------------------------------| | Exenatide (Byetta) | Twice daily | Shorter acting; first GLP-1RA approved (2005) | | Exenatide ER (Bydureon) | Once weekly | Extended-release microsphere formulation | | Dulaglutide (Trulicity) | Once weekly | Fc fusion protein; longer half-life | | Semaglutide (Ozempic/Wegovy) | Once weekly | Enhanced albumin binding; greater efficacy | | Tirzepatide (Mounjaro/Zepbound) | Once weekly | Dual GIP/GLP-1 agonist; highest weight loss | | Albiglutide (Tanzeum) | Once weekly | Withdrawn from market (2017) | Comparison to Semaglutide | Factor | Liraglutide | Semaglutide | |--------|-------------|-------------| | Dosing | Once daily | Once weekly | | Mechanism | GLP-1R agonist | GLP-1R agonist | | Acyl chain | C16 palmitic acid | C18 fatty diacid | | Half-life | approximately 13 hours | approximately 1 week | | Weight loss (obesity) | approximately 8% (SCALE) | approximately 15% (STEP) | | HbA1c reduction | approximately 1.0-1.5% | approximately 1.5-1.8% | | CV outcomes | LEADER positive | SELECT + SOUL positive | | Post-marketing experience | 15+ years | 7+ years | | Generic available | No | No | Head-to-head (SUSTAIN 10 PMID: 31539622 ): - Semaglutide 1.0mg vs liraglutide 1.2mg in T2D - HbA1c: -1.7% vs -1.0% (semaglutide superior) - Weight: -5.8 kg vs -1.9 kg (semaglutide superior) --- Current Research Directions Alzheimer's Disease ELAD Trial (Phase 2) PMID: 30915866 : - 204 patients with mild Alzheimer's disease - Liraglutide vs placebo for 12 months - Primary outcome: Change in brain glucose metabolism (FDG-PET) - Result: Reduced decline in several brain regions - Supported Phase 3 planning (now superseded by semaglutide EVOKE trial) Non-Alcoholic Fatty Liver Disease LEAN Trial PMID: 27086509 : - 52 patients with biopsy-confirmed NASH - 48 weeks of liraglutide 1.8mg vs placebo - NASH resolution: 39% vs 9% (p = 0.019) - Fibrosis progression: 9% vs 36% - Supported larger trials (superseded by semaglutide ESSENCE) Polycystic Ovary Syndrome Multiple small trials: - Weight loss improves PCOS outcomes - Some evidence for improved ovulation rates - Fertility outcomes remain uncertain - Not an approved indication --- Benefits & Risks Evidence Table Potential Benefits (Evidence-Graded) | Outcome | Evidence Type | Effect Size | Confidence | Key Citation | |---------|---------------|-------------|------------|--------------| | HbA1c reduction (T2D) | Phase 3 RCTs | -1.0 to -1.5% | HIGH | LEAD 1-6 | | Weight loss (obesity) | Phase 3 RCTs | -8.0% at 56 wk | HIGH | SCALE PMID: 25673378 | | MACE reduction (T2D) | Phase 3 RCT | 13% RRR | HIGH | LEADER PMID: 27295427 | | CV death reduction | Phase 3 RCT | 22% RRR | HIGH | LEADER PMID: 27295427 | | All-cause mortality reduction | Phase 3 RCT | 15% RRR | HIGH | LEADER PMID: 27295427 | | Prediabetes conversion prevention | Phase 3 RCT | 79% RRR over 3 ye

name: LL-37
summary: The only human cathelicidin antimicrobial peptide, discovered at Karolinska Institute in Sweden (1995). A 37-amino acid peptide with broad-spectrum antimicrobial activity and immunomodulatory functions. Extensive mechanistic research supports roles in innate immunity, wound healing, and host defense.
content: Overview Overview Quick Summary - The only human cathelicidin - One of two major human antimicrobial peptide families (the other being defensins) - 37 amino acids beginning with two leucines (hence "LL-37") - Discovered at Karolinska Institute, Sweden in 1995 by Agerberth, Gudmundsson, and colleagues - Broad-spectrum antimicrobial - Active against bacteria, fungi, viruses, and some parasites - Immunomodulatory functions - Beyond direct killing, modulates immune responses - Endogenous human molecule - Produced by neutrophils, epithelial cells, and macrophages - Clinical trials ongoing - Investigational use for wound healing, cancer immunotherapy Evidence Strength: Moderate - Extensive mechanistic and preclinical research with growing human translational data. Multiple clinical trials completed or in progress. Understanding of biological roles is well-established. Identity & Origins | Property | Value | |----------|-------| | Full name | Cathelicidin antimicrobial peptide | | Common names | LL-37, hCAP18, FALL-39 | | Gene symbol | CAMP | | Chromosome | 3p21.31 | | UniProt ID | P49913 | | Class | Cathelicidin family | | Origin | Endogenous human peptide | Discovery Timeline - 1995: Initial discovery of FALL-39 gene at Karolinska Institute, Sweden (Agerberth et al.) - 1996: Characterization of mature LL-37 peptide processing from hCAP18 precursor (Gudmundsson et al.) - 1997-1999: Structural characterization; alpha-helical nature established - 2001: First in vivo proof of essential antimicrobial role (knockout mouse studies) - 2003: Angiogenic properties discovered; wound healing roles established - 2006: Vitamin D regulation of LL-37 expression discovered (tuberculosis immunity) - 2007: Role in autoimmune diseases (psoriasis, rosacea) identified - 2010s-present: Clinical trials for wound healing, cancer immunotherapy Scientific Pathways 1. Membrane Disruption (Direct Antimicrobial) LL-37 (cationic, +6 charge) | v Attracted to anionic bacterial membrane | v Alpha-helix formation in membrane | v Membrane permeabilization/pore formation | v Cell lysis and death The positive charge of LL-37 (+6 at physiological pH) is electrostatically attracted to the negatively charged lipopolysaccharides (LPS) of Gram-negative bacteria or lipoteichoic acids of Gram-positive bacteria. Human cell membranes have neutral phospholipids, explaining selective toxicity to bacteria. 2. Immunomodulatory Pathways FPRL1/FPR2 Receptor Signaling: LL-37 + FPRL1 receptor | v G-protein activation | v Multiple downstream effects: ├── Chemotaxis (immune cell recruitment) ├── Angiogenesis (new blood vessel formation) ├── Cytokine modulation └── Cell proliferation TLR Modulation: - Binds bacterial DNA/LPS and delivers to intracellular TLRs - Can enhance or suppress TLR signaling depending on context - Links innate and adaptive immunity 3. Wound Healing Cascade Tissue injury | v LL-37 released from neutrophils/keratinocytes | v EGFR transactivation | v ├── Keratinocyte migration ├── Fibroblast proliferation ├── Endothelial cell proliferation (angiogenesis) └── Re-epithelialization LL-37 promotes wound healing through multiple parallel mechanisms, including direct cell migration signals via EGFR (epidermal growth factor receptor) transactivation and angiogenesis through FPRL1/VEGF pathways. 4. Vitamin D Regulation Vitamin D (sunlight/diet) | v 1,25(OH)2D3 (active vitamin D) | v Vitamin D receptor activation | v CAMP gene transcription | v hCAP18 production | v Protease cleavage | v Active LL-37 This pathway, discovered in 2006, explains why vitamin D deficiency increases susceptibility to infections like tuberculosis. Sunlight exposure and vitamin D supplementation can increase LL-37 production. Structural Features | Property | Value | Significance | |----------|-------|--------------| | Length | 37 amino acids | Optimal for membrane interaction | | Sequence | LLGDFFRKSKEKIGKEFKRIVQRIKDFLRNLVPRTES | Amphipathic | | Charge | +6 at pH 7 | Bacterial selectivity | | Structure | Alpha-helical (in membrane) | Required for activity | | Cysteine residues | 0 | Unique among AMPs | The lack of cysteine residues makes LL-37 unique among antimicrobial peptides, which typically rely on disulfide bonds for structure. LL-37 instead forms an alpha-helix only when encountering bacterial membranes. Gene Expression Sites | Tissue/Cell | Expression Level | Function | |-------------|------------------|----------| | Neutrophils | High (constitutive) | Immediate antimicrobial defense | | Keratinocytes | Inducible | Skin barrier defense | | Epithelial cells | Inducible | Mucosal defense | | Macrophages | Inducible (vitamin D) | Intracellular pathogen killing | | Mast cells | Moderate | Allergic/inflammatory responses | --- Research Summary Evidence Overview | Category | Count | Notes | |----------|-------|-------| | Human clinical trials | 4+ | Wound healing, cancer immunotherapy | | Human translational studies | 15+ | Disease associations, expression studies | | Preclinical in vivo | 50+ | Multiple animal models | | Mechanistic/in vitro | 100+ | Well-characterized pathways | | Total publications | 3000+ | Since 1995 discovery | Key Evidence Highlights 1. Wound Healing: LL-37 deficient in chronic non-healing ulcers (human studies); promotes re-epithelialization 2. Urinary Tract Defense: Essential for UTI protection; deficiency linked to recurrent infections 3. Skin Infections: Knockout mice susceptible to Group A Streptococcus; humans with low LL-37 have more skin infections 4. Vitamin D Connection: Explains how vitamin D protects against infections 5. Autoimmune Link: Aberrant LL-37 processing drives psoriasis and rosacea inflammation Strength of Evidence: LL-37 research benefits from being an endogenous human molecule. Unlike synthetic peptides studied only in animals, LL-37's roles are validated by human genetic studies, disease associations, and clinical observations. --- Key Human Trials Completed and Ongoing Trials | Trial | Phase | Population | Status | Key Findings | |-------|-------|------------|--------|--------------| | NCT02225366 | 1/2 | Venous leg ulcers (n=34) | Completed | Topical LL-37 safe; improved healing endpoints | | NCT01211470 | 1/2 | Melanoma (n=16) | Completed | Intratumoral LL-37 immunotherapy; immune activation observed | | NCT04098562 | 2 | Atopic dermatitis | Unknown | Topical antimicrobial peptide formulation | | NCT02530736 | 3 | Rosacea (n=1500+) | Completed | Omiganan (related AMP); LL-37-pathway therapeutic | Human Translational Studies | Study Focus | Key Findings | Clinical Relevance | |-------------|--------------|-------------------| | Chronic wounds (Heilborn 2003) | LL-37 absent in non-healing ulcers | Therapeutic target | | Urinary tract (Chromek 2006) | Low LL-37 = recurrent UTIs | Diagnostic/therapeutic marker | | Psoriasis (Lande 2007) | LL-37-DNA complexes drive autoimmunity | Disease mechanism | | Rosacea (Yamasaki 2007) | Abnormal LL-37 processing | Therapeutic target | | Tuberculosis (Liu 2006) | Vitamin D induces LL-37 for TB defense | Vitamin D supplementation rationale | Related Antimicrobial Peptide Therapeutics While LL-37 itself is challenging to develop as a drug (stability, cost), related antimicrobial peptides have reached Phase 3: | Compound | Relation to LL-37 | Indication | Status | |----------|-------------------|------------|--------| | Pexiganan (MSI-78) | Magainin analog | Diabetic foot infections | Phase 3 completed | | Omiganan (MBI-226) | Indolicidin derivative | Catheter infections, rosacea | Phase 3 | | OP-145 (P60.4Ac) | LL-37 derivative | Chronic otitis media | Phase 2 completed | --- Preclinical Evidence Animal Studies | Model | Organism | Key Findings | Human Translation | |-------|----------|--------------|-------------------| | CRAMP knockout | Mouse | Susceptible to skin GAS infection | Validates essential antimicrobial role | | Wound healing | Mouse/Pig | LL-37 accelerates closure | Supports clinical trials | | UTI model | Mouse | Cathelicidin protects bladder | Human UTI studies confirm | | Sepsis/endotoxemia | Mouse | LPS neutralization protective | Human sepsis therapy potential | | Tuberculosis | Mouse | Vitamin D/cathelicidin essential | Human vitamin D studies confirm | Antimicrobial Spectrum (In Vitro) | Pathogen Type | Examples | Activity | Notes | |---------------|----------|----------|-------| | Gram-negative bacteria | E. coli, Pseudomonas, Klebsiella | Strong | LPS-binding mechanism | | Gram-positive bacteria | Staphylococcus, Streptococcus | Strong | Lipoteichoic acid binding | | Mycobacteria | M. tuberculosis | Moderate | Intracellular activity | | Fungi | Candida species | Moderate | Membrane disruption | | Viruses | HIV, influenza, HSV | Variable | Envelope disruption | | Biofilms | Multiple species | Moderate | Can penetrate biofilm matrix | Mechanistic Studies Summary | Mechanism | Evidence Level | Key Publications | |-----------|---------------|------------------| | Membrane disruption | Strong | Oren 1999, Turner 1998 | | LPS neutralization | Strong | Larrick 1995 | | Chemotaxis | Strong | Scott 2002 | | Angiogenesis | Strong | Koczulla 2003 | | Wound healing | Strong | Heilborn 2003, Ramos 2011 | | Vitamin D regulation | Strong | Liu 2006 | | Autoimmune involvement | Strong | Lande 2007, Yamasaki 2007 | --- Safety Profile Known Safety Information LL-37 is an endogenous human molecule, providing inherent safety advantages over synthetic peptides. Clinical Trial Safety Data | Trial | Route | Safety Findings | |-------|-------|-----------------| | NCT02225366 (Leg ulcers) | Topical | Well tolerated; no serious adverse events | | NCT01211470 (Melanoma) | Intratumoral | Local injection site reactions; manageable | Theoretical Considerations | Aspect | Consideration | Notes | |--------|--------------|-------| | Immunogenicity | Low (self-peptide) | Unlikely to generate antibodies | | Cytotoxicity | Concentration-dependent | Selective for bacteria at physiological levels | | Autoimmune potential | Context-dependent | Normal levels protective; abnormal processing can drive psoriasis/rosacea | | Systemic effects | Under investigation | Most trials use local/topical application | Disease Associations (Double-Edged Sword) LL-37 demonstrates that antimicrobial peptides can have both protective and pathological roles: | Condition | LL-37 Status | Effect | |-----------|--------------|--------| | Chronic wounds | Deficient | Impaired healing | | Psoriasis | Overexpressed + DNA complexes | Drives inflammation | | Rosacea | Abnormally processed | Inflammatory fragments | | Atherosclerosis | Present in plaques | May contribute to inflammation | | Cancer | Variable | Context-dependent effects | Product Quality Considerations For research or investigational use: - Peptide purity critical for activity - Storage stability limited (lyophilized preferred) - Serum components can inhibit activity - Salt concentration affects antimicrobial function --- Pharmacokinetics Endogenous Kinetics | Parameter | Value | Notes | |-----------|-------|-------| | Plasma concentration | 1-2 ug/mL | Bound to lipoproteins | | Wound fluid concentration | 5-15 ug/mL | Antimicrobially active | | Half-life (serum) | Minutes to hours | Protease susceptibility | | Processing enzyme | Proteinase 3 | Cleaves hCAP18 to LL-37 | Tissue Distribution | Compartment | Concentration | Form | |-------------|--------------|------| | Neutrophil granules | 630 ug/mL | Stored as hCAP18 | | Wound fluid | 5-15 ug/mL | Active LL-37 | | Sweat | 1-5 ug/mL | Active LL-37 | | Airway surface | Variable | Active LL-37 | | Plasma | 1-2 ug/mL | Mostly bound | Stability Challenges | Factor | Effect | Mitigation | |--------|--------|------------| | Serum proteases | Rapid degradation | Derivative peptides (D-amino acids) | | Salt concentration | Reduced activity at high salt | Formulation optimization | | pH | Optimal activity at neutral pH | Buffer selection | | Temperature | Degradation above 37C | Cold chain | Derivative Strategies To overcome native LL-37 limitations: | Approach | Example | Advantage | |----------|---------|-----------| | D-amino acid substitution | D-LL-37 | Protease resistance | | Truncated peptides | KR-12 (12 AA core) | Reduced cost, maintained activity | | Cyclization | Cyclic LL-37 derivatives | Enhanced stability | | Lipidation | Fatty acid conjugates | Improved membrane interaction | --- Regulatory Status | Region | Status | Notes | |--------|--------|-------| | USA (FDA) | Not approved as drug | Investigational; natural human peptide | | EU (EMA) | Not approved | Clinical trials permitted | | Japan (PMDA) | Not approved | Research ongoing | | WADA | Not listed | Not a performance-enhancing substance | Regulatory Classification - As therapeutic: Investigational new drug (requires IND/CTA) - As research reagent: Available commercially - As natural molecule: Produced endogenously by all humans Intellectual Property Multiple patents exist for: - LL-37 derivatives and analogs - Therapeutic formulations - Delivery systems - Combination therapies --- Related Peptides Cathelicidin Family (Cross-Species) | Peptide | Species | Length | Notes | |---------|---------|--------|-------| | LL-37 | Human | 37 AA | Only human cathelicidin | | CRAMP | Mouse | 34 AA | Research model | | PR-39 | Pig | 39 AA | Proline-rich | | Protegrins | Pig | 16-18 AA | Beta-hairpin structure | | BMAP-27/28 | Bovine | 27-28 AA | Broad-spectrum | Human Antimicrobial Peptides | Family | Members | Mechanism | Comparison to LL-37 | |--------|---------|-----------|---------------------| | Alpha-defensins | HNP-1 to HNP-4 | Membrane disruption | Different structure (beta-sheet) | | Beta-defensins | HBD-1 to HBD-4 | Membrane disruption | Cysteine-rich; skin/epithelial | | Histatins | Histatin 1-5 | Antifungal | Saliva-specific | | Dermcidin | DCD-1L | Membrane disruption | Sweat-specific | Therapeutic Antimicrobial Peptides | Compound | Origin | Development Stage | Indication | |----------|--------|-------------------|------------| | Pexiganan | Magainin analog | Phase 3 complete | Diabetic foot infections | | Omiganan | Indolicidin derivative | Phase 3 | Rosacea, catheter infections | | OP-145 | LL-37 derivative | Phase 2 | Chronic ear infections | | Novexatin | Defensin-like | Phase 2 | Fungal nail infections | --- Current Research Directions Active Research Areas 1. Wound Healing Applications - Chronic diabetic ulcers - Venous leg ulcers - Burn wound management - Surgical wound healing 2. Cancer Immunotherapy - Intratumoral injection for immune activation - Cancer vaccine adjuvants - Combination with checkpoint inhibitors 3. Infectious Disease - Antibiotic-resistant bacterial infections - Biofilm-associated infections - Tuberculosis (vitamin D pathway) - Viral infections (respiratory, skin) 4. Autoimmune/Inflammatory Disease - Psoriasis (targeting LL-37-DNA complexes) - Rosacea (kallikrein/cathelicidin pathway) - Inflammatory bowel disease 5. Drug Delivery - Nanoparticle formulations - Topical gels and creams - Cell-penetrating properties for drug delivery Key Research Questions | Question | Status | Potential Impact | |----------|--------|------------------| | Optimal therapeutic formulation? | Active research | Drug development | | Systemic vs local delivery? | Clinical trials | Route optimization | | Combination with antibiotics? | Preclinical | Antibiotic resistance | | Long-term safety? | Ongoing monitoring | Regulatory approval | | Biomarker for disease? | Translational research | Diagnostics | Swedish Research Contributions Sweden, particularly Karolinska Institute, remains a global leader in cathelicidin research: - Original discovery (Agerberth, Gudmundsson 1995-1996) - Expression in inflammatory diseases (Frohm 1997) - Wound healing roles (Heilborn 2003) - Urinary tract defense (Chromek 2006) - Ongoing clinical translation --- Citation Table Discovery & Characterization (Swedish/Karolinska Research) | Source | Year | Type | Key Finding | Access | |--------|------|------|-------------|--------| | Agerberth B et al. name: MariTide
summary: A bispecific peptide-antibody conjugate combining GLP-1 receptor agonism with GIP receptor antagonism. Unique mechanism opposite to tirzepatide. Phase 2 showed ~20% weight loss at 52 weeks with once-monthly dosing. MARITIME Phase 3 program initiated.
content: Overview Overview Quick Summary - Bispecific molecule: GLP-1 receptor agonist + GIP receptor antagonist (antibody conjugate) - Unique mechanism: GIP ANTAGONISM (opposite of tirzepatide's GIP agonism) - Once-monthly dosing — longest dosing interval in obesity drug development - Phase 2: 20% weight loss at 52 weeks; weight still declining (no plateau) - MARITIME program: Phase 3 trials initiated 2025 - 21-day half-life enables monthly administration Evidence Strength: High — Robust Phase 2 data, Phase 3 initiated, novel mechanism, strong sponsor (Amgen). Identity & Origins | Property | Value | |----------|-------| | Generic name | Maridebart cafraglutide | | Brand name | MariTide (development name) | | Research code | AMG 133 | | Class | Bispecific peptide-antibody conjugate | | Manufacturer | Amgen | | Dosing | Once monthly subcutaneous | Discovery Timeline - 2020: Phase 1 studies initiated - 2022-2023: Phase 2 studies in obesity - 2025 (June): Phase 2 results presented at ADA 2025 - 2025: MARITIME Phase 3 program initiated - Expected 2027: Phase 3 results - Projected 2029-2030: Potential FDA approval The Science: GLP-1 Agonist + GIP Antagonist MariTide administration ↓ ├── GLP-1 PEPTIDE PORTION │ ├── GLP-1R activation → Standard incretin effects │ └── Appetite suppression, glucose control │ └── ANTI-GIP ANTIBODY PORTION ├── GIPR blockade → Prevents GIP signaling ├── Adipose tissue: Altered lipid handling ├── Pancreas: Modified incretin effect └── 21-day half-life → Monthly dosing The GIP Controversy | Approach | Drug | Weight Loss | Theory | |----------|------|-------------|--------| | GIP Agonism | Tirzepatide | 22% | GIP enhances GLP-1 effects; improves adipose function | | GIP Antagonism | MariTide | 20% | GIP blockade prevents fat storage; reduces rebound hunger | Both work. The field doesn't fully understand why both approaches produce similar weight loss. This suggests GIP's role is more nuanced than simple agonism/antagonism. --- Key Human Trials Phase 2 (Obesity without Diabetes) | Trial | Population | Duration | N | |-------|------------|----------|---| | Phase 2 | BMI ≥30 or ≥27 with comorbidity | 52 weeks | 400 | Key Results (ADA 2025): - Weight loss: 20% at 52 weeks vs 2.6% placebo - No plateau: Weight curves still declining at study end - Monthly dosing: Titration: 21mg → 35mg → 70mg over 8 weeks - Safety: GI events (class effect); generally well tolerated Phase 2 (Obesity with Type 2 Diabetes) | Trial | Population | Duration | N | |-------|------------|----------|---| | Phase 2 T2D | T2D + obesity | 52 weeks | 400 | Key Results: - Weight loss: 17% at 52 weeks vs 1.4% placebo - HbA1c reduction: Significant improvement MARITIME Phase 3 Program | Trial | Population | Duration | Status | |-------|------------|----------|--------| | MARITIME-1 | Obesity | 72 weeks | Initiated 2025 | | MARITIME-2 | T2D + obesity | 72 weeks | Initiated 2025 | | Planned | ASCVD outcomes | Event-driven | Planned | | Planned | Heart failure | Event-driven | Planned | | Planned | Obstructive sleep apnea | TBD | Planned | --- Safety Profile Common Adverse Events | Event | Incidence | Notes | |-------|-----------|-------| | Nausea | 20-30% | GLP-1 class effect | | Vomiting | 10-20% | During titration | | Diarrhea | 15-20% | Generally mild | Key Safety Features - No unexpected safety signals in Phase 2 - Monthly dosing well tolerated - Antibody portion: No immunogenicity concerns to date --- Regulatory Status | Region | Status | Expected | |--------|--------|----------| | USA | Phase 3 | Results 2027; Approval 2029-2030 | | EU | Following US | TBD | --- Related Peptides MariTide vs Tirzepatide | Feature | MariTide | Tirzepatide | |---------|----------|-------------| | GLP-1 activity | Agonist | Agonist | | GIP activity | Antagonist | Agonist | | Dosing | Monthly | Weekly | | Weight loss (Phase 2) | 20% | 22% | | Status | Phase 3 | Approved | Key question: Which GIP approach is better? Head-to-head trial would be definitive, but both show excellent efficacy. --- Citation Table | Source | Year | Type | Key Finding | Access | |--------|------|------|-------------|--------| | Amgen ADA 2025 | 2025 | Conference | Phase 2: 20% weight loss at 52wk; monthly dosing | Open ( | | Amgen MARITIME | 2025 | Press Release | Phase 3 program design and initiation | Open ( | --- This dossier is for educational purposes only and does not constitute medical advice. Last updated: 2026-01-22.

name: Mazdutide
summary: A dual GLP-1 and glucagon receptor agonist developed by Innovent/Eli Lilly. NMPA approved in China (2025) for obesity. GLORY-1 trial showed 14.3% weight loss at 48 weeks. DREAMS-3 head-to-head trial demonstrated superiority vs semaglutide for combined diabetes + obesity outcomes.
content: Overview Overview Quick Summary - Dual agonist of GLP-1 and glucagon receptors (GLP-1R/GCGR) - NMPA approved in China (June 2025) for chronic weight management (brand: Xinermei) - GLORY-1 trial: 14.3% weight loss at 48 weeks with excellent tolerability (0.5% discontinuation) - DREAMS-3: First head-to-head trial showing GLP-1/GCGR dual agonist superiority vs semaglutide - 48% achieved HbA1c <7% AND ≥10% weight loss vs 21% with semaglutide in T2D patients - 80% liver fat reduction in patients with baseline fatty liver (MRI-PDFF ≥10%) Evidence Strength: High — Multiple Phase 3 RCTs, NEJM publication, NMPA approved, head-to-head superiority vs semaglutide. Identity & Origins | Property | Value | |----------|-------| | Generic name | Mazdutide | | Brand name | Xinermei (欣尔美) - China | | Research codes | IBI362 (Innovent), LY3305677 (Lilly) | | Class | GLP-1R/GCGR dual receptor agonist | | Manufacturers | Innovent Biologics (China), Eli Lilly (US rights) | Discovery Timeline - 2019: Innovent licenses mazdutide from Eli Lilly for China development - 2021: Phase 2 results published in Diabetes Care - 2024 (May): DREAMS-2 Phase 3 (T2D) meets endpoints - 2024 (June): GLORY-1 results presented at ADA 2024 - 2025 (May): GLORY-1 published in NEJM - 2025 (June): NMPA approval for obesity in China - Ongoing: US Phase 2 trials by Eli Lilly; Phase 3 planning The Science: GLP-1 + Glucagon Synergy Mazdutide administration ↓ ├── GLP-1R ACTIVATION │ ├── Brain: ↑ Satiety signaling │ ├── Pancreas: ↑ Insulin secretion (glucose-dependent) │ └── Stomach: Delayed gastric emptying │ └── GCGR ACTIVATION ├── Liver: ↑ Fatty acid oxidation → ↓ Liver fat ├── Liver: ↑ Gluconeogenesis (balanced by insulin) ├── Adipose: ↑ Lipolysis → ↑ Free fatty acids for oxidation └── Whole body: ↑ Energy expenditure Key Difference from Tirzepatide | Mechanism | Mazdutide | Tirzepatide | |-----------|-----------|-------------| | GLP-1R | Agonist | Agonist | | GCGR | Agonist | No activity | | GIPR | No activity | Agonist | | Primary site | Liver-directed | Adipose-directed | | Liver fat reduction | 80% | 50% | --- Key Human Trials GLORY-1 (Obesity/Overweight - China) | Trial | NCT ID | Population | Duration | N | |-------|--------|------------|----------|---| | GLORY-1 | NCT05607680 ( | BMI ≥28 or ≥24 with comorbidity | 48 weeks | 610 | Key Results (NEJM 2025): - Weight loss: -14.3% (6mg) vs -3.3% placebo - ≥10% weight loss: 64% of participants - Discontinuation rate: 0.5% (exceptionally low) - Liver fat (MRI-PDFF ≥10%): -80.2% vs -5.3% placebo - Safety: GI AEs most common; mild-moderate; dose-escalation period DREAMS-3 (Head-to-Head vs Semaglutide) | Trial | Population | Comparison | Duration | |-------|------------|------------|----------| | DREAMS-3 | T2D with obesity | Mazdutide vs Semaglutide | 32 weeks | Key Results: - Combined endpoint (HbA1c <7% AND ≥10% WL): 48% vs 21% (P<0.0001) - HbA1c reduction: Superior to semaglutide - Weight loss: Superior to semaglutide - Interpretation: 2.3x higher success rate for combined glycemic + weight goals DREAMS-1 and DREAMS-2 (Type 2 Diabetes) | Trial | Comparison | Result | |-------|------------|--------| | DREAMS-1 | Placebo | Superior HbA1c reduction | | DREAMS-2 | Dulaglutide | Superior efficacy | --- Safety Profile Common Adverse Events | Event | Incidence | Notes | |-------|-----------|-------| | Nausea | 20-30% | Mostly during titration | | Diarrhea | 15-20% | Mild-moderate | | Vomiting | 10-15% | Transient | Notable Safety Features - Very low discontinuation: 0.5% in GLORY-1 (vs 5-7% typical for class) - GI events: Mild-moderate severity, primarily during dose escalation - Liver safety: No hepatotoxicity signals; liver fat improved --- Regulatory Status | Region | Agency | Status | Indication | Date | |--------|--------|--------|------------|------| | China | NMPA | Approved | Weight management | June 2025 | | China | NMPA | Under Review | Type 2 diabetes | Expected H2 2025 | | USA | FDA | Phase 2 | Obesity | Eli Lilly conducting | --- Citation Table | Source | Year | Type | Key Finding | Access | |--------|------|------|-------------|--------| | Ji L et al. NEJM | 2025 | Phase 3 RCT | GLORY-1: -14.3% weight loss; 80% liver fat reduction | Open | | DREAMS-3 Results | 2025 | Phase 3 RCT | 48% vs 21% achieved combined HbA1c/weight goals vs semaglutide | Open | | Innovent ADA 2024 | 2024 | Conference | Full GLORY-1 and DREAMS data | Open ( 20240624 vf.pdf) | | NCT05607680 ( | - | Registry | GLORY-1 trial registration | Open | --- This dossier is for educational purposes only and does not constitute medical advice.
