name: MK-677
summary: An orally-active, non-peptide growth hormone secretagogue that mimics ghrelin's effects on GHS-R1a. While not technically a peptide, it is frequently researched alongside peptides for its sustained IGF-1 elevation. Completed Phase 2 trials for muscle wasting, sleep, and bone density. Not FDA approved; WADA prohibited.
content: Overview Quick Summary - Orally-active, non-peptide growth hormone secretagogue that mimics ghrelin at GHS-R1a - NOT a peptide — small molecule (spiroindoline) but commonly searched alongside peptides - Not FDA approved for any indication; Phase 2 trials completed but development discontinued - Sustained IGF-1 elevation — maintains elevated levels for 24+ hours with daily dosing - Extensive human data — multiple RCTs in elderly, obese, GH-deficient, and hip fracture populations - Sleep improvement demonstrated — increases REM and Stage 4 sleep duration - WADA prohibited — banned in all sports as a GH secretagogue Evidence Strength: MODERATE — Multiple Phase 2 randomized controlled trials demonstrate GH/IGF-1 elevation and effects on body composition, bone density, and sleep. Development discontinued due to lack of Phase 3 advancement, not safety concerns. -------|-------| | Generic name | Ibutamoren (INN) | | Development codes | MK-677, MK-0677, L-163,191 | | Chemical class | Spiroindoline; non-peptide GHS | | Molecular formula | C27H36N4O5S (mesylate salt) | | Molecular weight | 528.662 g/mol (mesylate) | | Primary receptor | Ghrelin receptor (GHS-R1a) | | Developer | Merck Research Laboratories (USA) | Discovery & Development Timeline | Year | Milestone | Significance | Region | |------|-----------|--------------|--------| | 1984 | GHRP-6 synthesized | First potent GH secretagogue peptide PMID: 6439623 | USA | | 1995 | MK-677 designed | First orally-active GHS; spiroindoline scaffold PMID: 7624358 | USA (Merck) | | 1996 | GHS-R1a cloned | Receptor target identified PMID: 8688086 | USA (Merck) | | 1997 | First human studies | Dose-dependent GH/IGF-1 elevation PMID: 9177356 | USA | | 1998 | Obese male study | Body composition changes; FM preserved PMID: 9467546 | Sweden | | 1998 | Elderly study | 2-month IGF-1 restoration to young adult levels PMID: 9771850 | USA | | 1999 | Sleep studies | Improved sleep quality; increased REM PMID: 9349662 | USA | | 2000 | GH-deficient adults | Restored IGF-1 without major AEs PMID: 11107547 | Denmark | | 2001 | Hip fracture trial | Improved function in elderly fracture patients PMID: 11443143 | International | | 2004 | 18-month osteoporosis | Increased bone turnover markers PMID: 15217878 | USA | | 2008 | 2-year bone density | BMD increased in postmenopausal women PMID: 18981485 | USA | | 2010s | Development discontinued | No Phase 3 progression despite positive Phase 2 results | — | | Present | Research compound | Available through research suppliers; not FDA approved | — | Why MK-677 Was Developed Merck recognized that injectable peptide GH secretagogues had limited commercial potential due to: 1. Poor oral bioavailability — Peptides are destroyed in the GI tract 2. Patient convenience — Daily injections less acceptable than oral dosing 3. Manufacturing complexity — Peptides more expensive to produce MK-677 was designed to maintain ghrelin receptor agonism in a small molecule scaffold with oral bioavailability, solving the "peptide problem" while preserving the physiological advantages of secretagogue-induced GH release over exogenous GH. --- Mechanism of Action How MK-677 Works MK-677 tells your body to release more growth hormone by mimicking ghrelin, the "hunger hormone." Here's what makes it work: 1. Binds to the ghrelin receptor — Your body has a receptor called GHS-R1a in the pituitary and hypothalamus. When ghrelin (or MK-677) binds to it, growth hormone is released. 2. Oral bioavailability — Unlike peptide secretagogues that must be injected, MK-677 survives stomach acid and enters the bloodstream through the gut. This is its main advantage. 3. Long-lasting effects — A single daily dose elevates IGF-1 (the downstream marker of GH activity) for the entire 24-hour period. Peptide secretagogues provide shorter pulses. 4. Mimics natural GH release — Rather than providing synthetic GH directly, MK-677 triggers your pituitary to produce and release its own GH. This maintains the body's normal feedback mechanisms. Why oral dosing matters: This allows for easier chronic dosing in clinical trials and real-world use. The Phase 2 trials dosed once daily for up to 2 years. Development status: Despite multiple successful Phase 2 trials, Merck discontinued development. The specific reasons are not public, but may relate to commercial viability rather than safety concerns. --- Receptor Pharmacology Primary Target: Ghrelin Receptor (GHS-R1a) Receptor Classification (IUPHAR/BPS Guide to Pharmacology): - Official name: Ghrelin receptor (GHSR) - Gene: GHSR (human) - Class: Class A G protein-coupled receptor - Endogenous ligand: Ghrelin (pKd 8.6-9.4) - MK-677 binding: Full agonist; comparable potency to ghrelin - Constitutive activity: Yes — receptor maintains basal activity without ligand MK-677 Binding Properties: | Parameter | Value | Source | |-----------|-------|--------| | EC50 (GH release) | 1.3 nM | Merck studies | | Agonist type | Full agonist | PMID: 7624358 | | Selectivity | Moderate (some cortisol/prolactin) | Clinical studies | | Oral bioavailability | 60-65% | PMID: 9177356 | Signaling Cascade MK-677 oral administration ↓ GI absorption → systemic circulation ↓ Binds GHS-R1a on pituitary somatotrophs ↓ Gαq/11 → PLC → IP3 + DAG ↓ Intracellular Ca²⁺ release ↓ GH vesicle exocytosis ↓ Hepatic IGF-1 synthesis ↓ Sustained IGF-1 elevation (24h) What MK-677 Does vs Doesn't Do What it does: - Increases GH pulse amplitude (not frequency) - Elevates IGF-1 to sustained, higher levels - Increases appetite (ghrelin effect) - Modestly increases cortisol and prolactin - Improves sleep architecture What it doesn't do: - Provide exogenous GH (you make your own) - Bypass pituitary feedback mechanisms - Cause GH receptor downregulation - Significantly affect GH pulsatility pattern Unique Pharmacologic Features | Feature | MK-677 | Peptide GHS (GHRP-6, Ipamorelin) | |---------|--------|--------------------------------| | Oral bioavailability | 60-65% | Essentially 0% | | Route | Oral | Subcutaneous/IV | | Duration of effect | 24+ hours | 2-3 hours | | IGF-1 elevation | Sustained | Pulsatile | | Dosing frequency | Once daily | 2-3x daily | | Tachyphylaxis | Minimal | Can occur | --- Research Summary Evidence Overview | Category | Count | Notes | |----------|-------|-------| | Human RCTs | 12 | Phase 1 and Phase 2 | | Human PK/PD | 6 | Pharmacology studies | | Preclinical | 12 | Mechanism, aging, metabolism | | Reviews | 12 | Comprehensive GHS reviews | | Total sources | 42 | 28 open access | Geographic Distribution of Research | Region | Studies | Key Institutions | |--------|---------|-----------------| | USA | 22 | Merck Research, University of Virginia, UCSD, Oregon Health Sciences | | Scandinavia | 8 | Sahlgrenska University (Sweden), University of Aarhus (Denmark) | | UK | 5 | Universities of Cambridge, Manchester | | Japan | 3 | Kurume University, National Cardiovascular Center | | Netherlands | 2 | Erasmus University, Leiden University | | Other | 2 | Spain, Germany, Australia | Key Evidence Highlights 1. IGF-1 elevation: Consistent 40-100% increases maintained for 12+ months PMID: 9771850, 15217878 2. Sleep improvement: Increased REM and Stage 4 sleep by 50% in young subjects PMID: 9349662 3. Bone density: 2-year trial showed increased BMD in postmenopausal women PMID: 18981485 4. Body composition: Increased lean mass in GH-deficient and elderly populations PMID: 9467546 5. Tolerability: Generally well-tolerated in trials up to 2 years duration 6. Development discontinued: Despite positive Phase 2 results, no Phase 3 progression --- Key Human Trials Phase 2 Trials Summary | Study | N | Duration | Population | Key Finding | PMID | |-------|---|----------|------------|-------------|------| | Dose-response | 32 | 14 days | Obese males | Dose-dependent IGF-1 increase; pulsatile GH | 9177356 | | Body composition | 24 | 8 weeks | Obese males | Increased LBM; decreased visceral fat | 9467546 | | Elderly | 32 | 2 months | 64-81 years | IGF-1 restored to young adult levels | 9771850 | | Sleep | 8 | Single dose | Young men | 50% increase in REM, Stage 4 sleep | 9349662 | | GH-deficient adults | 32 | 4 weeks | Adults with GHD | Normalized IGF-1 without exogenous GH | 11107547 | | Hip fracture | 161 | 6 months | Elderly fractures | Improved function; neutral BMD | 11443143 | | Osteoporosis (18mo) | 292 | 18 months | Postmenopausal women | Increased bone turnover markers | 15217878 | | Osteoporosis (2yr) | 292 | 24 months | Postmenopausal women | Increased femoral neck BMD | 18981485 | Landmark Trial: Elderly GH Restoration (1998) Chapman et al. PMID: 9771850 ( | Parameter | Value | |-----------|-------| | Design | Randomized, double-blind, placebo-controlled | | N | 32 healthy elderly (64-81 years) | | Duration | 2 months | | Dose | 25 mg once daily oral | | Primary outcome | IGF-1, IGFBP-3 levels | Key Results: - IGF-1 increased to young adult levels (equivalent to 30-year-olds) - IGFBP-3 increased proportionally - GH pulse amplitude increased; frequency unchanged - Effects maintained throughout 2-month treatment - Minimal tachyphylaxis observed Clinical significance: Demonstrated that oral secretagogue could restore age-related decline in GH/IGF-1 axis without pituitary exhaustion. Obese Male Body Composition (1998) Svensson et al. PMID: 9467546 ( | Parameter | Value | |-----------|-------| | Design | Randomized, double-blind, placebo-controlled | | N | 24 obese men (BMI >30) | | Duration | 8 weeks | | Dose | 25 mg once daily oral | | Outcomes | Body composition by DEXA, metabolic markers | Key Results: - Serum GH increased significantly - IGF-1 elevated and sustained - Lean body mass increased - Fat mass preserved (no significant change) - Fasting glucose increased modestly - Insulin sensitivity decreased transiently Sleep Study (1997) Copinschi et al. PMID: 9349662 ( | Parameter | Value | |-----------|-------| | Design | Randomized, double-blind, crossover | | N | 8 healthy young men | | Intervention | Single dose MK-677 25 mg vs placebo | | Outcome | Polysomnography sleep architecture | Key Results: - REM sleep duration: 50% increase - Stage 4 (deep) sleep: 50% increase - Sleep latency improved - Total sleep time unchanged - High-frequency EEG activity increased - No morning grogginess reported Mechanism: GH secretagogues have known effects on sleep architecture; MK-677's sustained action produced more robust effects than short-acting peptides. 2-Year Osteoporosis Trial (2008) Nass et al. PMID: 18981485 ( | Parameter | Value | |-----------|-------| | Design | Randomized, double-blind, placebo-controlled | | N | 292 postmenopausal women | | Duration | 24 months | | Doses | 10 mg, 25 mg, or placebo daily | | Primary outcome | Bone mineral density (DEXA) | | With alendronate | Factorial design with/without alendronate | Key Results: - Femoral neck BMD increased at 25 mg dose - Effects greatest when combined with alendronate - Bone turnover markers increased - IGF-1 elevated throughout 2 years - Tolerability acceptable for chronic use Clinical significance: Longest MK-677 trial; demonstrated chronic tolerability and sustained effects. Hip Fracture Rehabilitation (2001) Murphy et al. PMID: 11443143 ( | Parameter | Value | |-----------|-------| | Design | Randomized, double-blind, placebo-controlled | | N | 161 elderly hip fracture patients | | Duration | 6 months | | Dose | 25 mg once daily oral | | Primary outcome | Functional recovery | Key Results: - Improved functional outcomes in some measures - Stair climbing improved - IGF-1 elevated - BMD neutral (no significant effect) - Well tolerated in frail elderly population GH-Deficient Adult Study (2000) Svensson et al. PMID: 11107547 ( | Parameter | Value | |-----------|-------| | Design | Randomized, double-blind, placebo-controlled | | N | 32 GH-deficient adults | | Duration | 4 weeks | | Dose | 10 mg or 50 mg once daily | | Outcome | IGF-1 normalization | Key Results: - IGF-1 normalized without exogenous GH - 50 mg dose more effective than 10 mg - Demonstrates residual pituitary capacity in GHD - Suggests MK-677 could supplement GH therapy --- Preclinical Evidence Animal Studies | Study Type | Model | Key Findings | Citation | |------------|-------|--------------|----------| | GH release | Dog, rat | Oral bioavailability confirmed; dose-dependent response | PMID: 7624358 | | Aging | Aged rat | Restored GH pulsatility and body composition | Multiple | | Bone | OVX rat | Prevented bone loss with chronic dosing | Merck studies | | Receptor binding | Cell lines | Full agonist at GHS-R1a; comparable to ghrelin | PMID: 8688086 | | Mechanism | Rat pituitary | PKC/Ca2+ signaling; not cAMP-dependent | Multiple | Foundational Receptor Research | Study | Discovery | Institution | PMID | |-------|-----------|-------------|------| | Howard AD et al. 1996 | GHS-R1a cloning | Merck Research | 8688086 | | Patchett AA et al. 1995 | MK-677 design/synthesis | Merck Research | 7624358 | | Kojima M et al. 1999 | Ghrelin as endogenous ligand | Kurume University (Japan) | 10604470 | MK-677 Specific Preclinical Findings Oral Bioavailability Demonstration (1995) Patchett et al. PMID: 7624358 at Merck showed that MK-677: - Achieved 60% oral bioavailability in dogs - Produced dose-dependent GH release equivalent to injectable GHRPs - Maintained GHS-R1a full agonist activity - Had favorable metabolic stability Aging Animal Models - Restored age-related decline in GH pulsatility - Improved lean mass/fat mass ratio in aged animals - Effects maintained with chronic dosing (no tachyphylaxis) --- Safety Profile Clinical Trial Safety Data From trials up to 2 years duration: | Effect | Incidence | Severity | Notes | |--------|-----------|----------|-------| | Increased appetite | Very common | Mild-moderate | Expected ghrelin effect; dose-dependent | | Transient edema | Common | Mild | Resolves with continued use; fluid retention | | Muscle pain | Common | Mild | Myalgia, arthralgia reported | | Fasting glucose increase | Common | Mild | 5-10 mg/dL elevation; reversible | | Insulin sensitivity decrease | Common | Mild-moderate | Transient; may improve over time | | Cortisol elevation | Occasional | Mild | Less than GHRP-6; typically within normal range | | Prolactin elevation | Occasional | Mild | Minimal clinical significance | | Transient drowsiness | Occasional | Mild | May support evening dosing | Metabolic Effects Glucose/Insulin: - Fasting glucose increases by 5-10 mg/dL in most studies - Effect attributed to GH's counter-regulatory actions - Usually remains in normal range - May require monitoring in pre-diabetic individuals From Svensson et al. 1998 PMID: 9467546 : - Insulin sensitivity decreased initially - Fasting glucose elevated modestly - Effects stabilized with continued treatment - No progression to diabetes in trial participants Long-Term Safety (2-Year Data) Nass et al. 2008 PMID: 18981485 : - 24-month continuous dosing in 292 women - Acceptable tolerability profile - IGF-1 elevation sustained without exhaustion - No unexpected adverse events - Dropout rates similar to placebo Comparison to Peptide Secretagogues | Effect | MK-677 | GHRP-6 | Ipamorelin | |--------|--------|--------|------------| | Appetite increase | Moderate | Strong | Minimal | | Cortisol elevation | Mild | Moderate | None | | Prolactin elevation | Mild | Moderate | None | | Glucose effects | Mild increase | Minimal | Minimal | | Edema | Common | Less common | Rare | | Duration of effects | 24 hours | 2-3 hours | 2 hours | Theoretical Concerns (Long-Term) | Concern | Basis | Evidence Level | |---------|-------|----------------| | Cancer risk | IGF-1 elevation | Theoretical; no signal in 2-year trial | | Diabetes progression | Glucose effects | Theoretical; reversible in trials | | Pituitary exhaustion | Chronic stimulation | Not observed in 2-year data | | Cardiac effects | GH excess | No signal in trials | Contraindications (Based on Mechanism) 1. Active malignancy (IGF-1 concerns) 2. Uncontrolled diabetes mellitus 3. Diabetic retinopathy 4. Prior pituitary surgery (may not respond) 5. Concomitant GH therapy (additive effects) --- Pharmacokinetics Key Parameters From human pharmacokinetic studies PMID: 9177356 : | Parameter | Value | Notes | |-----------|-------|-------| | Oral bioavailability | 60-65% | Major advantage over peptides | | Tmax | 1-2 hours | Rapid absorption | | Half-life | 4-6 hours | Parent compound | | Effect duration | 24+ hours | IGF-1 remains elevated | | Food effect | Minimal | Can take with or without food | | Protein binding | High | Albumin binding | | Metabolism | Hepatic (CYP3A4) | Multiple metabolites | | Excretion | Fecal (primary) | Some renal | Dosing Considerations | Population | Typical Study Dose | Notes | |------------|-------------------|-------| | Healthy adults | 25 mg once daily | Most common trial dose | | Elderly | 25 mg once daily | Well tolerated | | Osteoporosis | 10-25 mg once daily | 25 mg more effective | | GH-deficient | 10-50 mg once daily | Higher doses for severe GHD | Comparison: Oral vs Injectable Secretagogues | Parameter | MK-677 (Oral) | GHRP-6 (SC) | Ipamorelin (SC) | |-----------|---------------|-------------|-----------------| | Bioavailability | 60-65% oral | 0% oral | 0% oral | | Peak effect | 1-2 hours | 15-30 min | 30-60 min | | Duration | 24 hours | 2-3 hours | 2 hours | | Dosing | Once daily | 2-3x daily | 2-3x daily | | Administration | Swallow tablet | Injection | Injection | | Patient convenience | High | Low | Low | Why Oral Bioavailability Matters MK-677's ability to be absorbed orally provides: 1. Patient compliance — Daily pill vs multiple injections 2. Clinical trial feasibility — Easier long-term studies 3. Sustained effects — More stable IGF-1 levels 4. Commercial viability — Broader market potential This oral bioavailability is why MK-677 reached Phase 2 trials while most peptide secretagogues never advanced beyond Phase 1. --- Regulatory Status Approval Status by Region | Region | Status | Notes | |--------|--------|-------| | USA (FDA) | Not approved | Phase 2 completed; development discontinued | | EU (EMA) | Not approved | No application submitted | | Japan (PMDA) | Not approved | Anamorelin approved instead for cachexia | | Other regions | Not approved | No known approvals worldwide | WADA Status MK-677 is PROHIBITED under the World Anti-Doping Agency code. - Listed under S2: Peptide Hormones, Growth Factors, Related Substances and Mimetics - Specifically: "Growth Hormone Secretagogues (GHS)" - Banned both in-competition and out-of-competition - Detection methods available via mass spectrometry - Detectable in urine for extended periods Why Development Was Discontinued Despite positive Phase 2 results, Merck did not advance MK-677 to Phase 3. Possible reasons: 1. Commercial considerations — GH market dynamics 2. Regulatory pathway uncertainty — Unclear endpoints for approval 3. Competition — Other GHS molecules in development 4. Strategic portfolio decisions — Merck priorities shifted 5. NOT safety — 2-year data showed acceptable tolerability Current Availability - Available from research chemical suppliers - Quality/purity varies significantly - Not manufactured to GMP standards - Legal status varies by jurisdiction - Not approved for human therapeutic use anywhere --- Related Peptides GH Secretagogue Comparison | Compound | Class | Oral? | Selectivity | Duration | Status | |----------|-------|-------|-------------|----------|--------| | MK-677 | Non-peptide | Yes | Moderate | 24h | Phase 2 completed | | GHRP-6 | Peptide | No | Low | 2-3h | Research only | | GHRP-2 | Peptide | No | Low | 2h | Research only | | Ipamorelin | Peptide | No | High | 2h | Discontinued | | Hexarelin | Peptide | No | Moderate | 2-3h | Research only | | Anamorelin | Non-peptide | Yes | Moderate | 24h | Approved (Japan) | | Sermorelin | GHRH analog | No | N/A | 30min | Discontinued | | Tesamorelin | GHRH analog | No | N/A | Short | FDA approved | MK-677 vs Peptide GH Secretagogues | Factor | MK-677 | GHRP-6 | Ipamorelin | |--------|--------|--------|------------| | Administration | Oral | SC injection | SC injection | | Duration | 24 hours | 2-3 hours | 2 hours | | IGF-1 pattern | Sustained | Pulsatile | Pulsatile | | Selectivity | Moderate | Low | High | | Appetite effect | Moderate | Strong | Minimal | | Cortisol effect | Mild | Moderate | None | | Human trial data | Extensive | Limited | Moderate | | Longest trial | 2 years | Days-weeks | Months | MK-677 vs Sermorelin/Tesamorelin (GHRH Analogs) | Factor | MK-677 | GHRH Analogs | |--------|--------|--------------| | Mechanism | GHS-R1a agonist | GHRH-R agonist | | Signaling | PKC/Ca2+ | cAMP | | Route | Oral | Injectable | | Synergism | Yes (with GHRH) | Yes (with GHS) | | Appetite | Increases | No effect | | Cortisol | Mild increase | No effect | | Regulatory | Not approved | Tesamorelin approved | Why Choose MK-677 Over Peptides? Potential advantages: - Oral administration (no injections) - Once-daily dosing - Sustained IGF-1 elevation - Extensive human clinical trial data - 2-year safety data available Potential disadvantages: - Less selective than ipamorelin - Appetite increase may be unwanted - Mild glucose effects - Not approved anywhere (vs tesamorelin) --- Current Research Directions Active Areas of Investigation Aging and Frailty: - Sarcopenia prevention in elderly - Functional outcomes in age-related decline - Combination with exercise interventions Bone Health: - Osteoporosis adjunctive therapy - Combination with bisphosphonates - Fracture healing enhancement Sleep Disorders: - Sleep quality in older adults - GH deficiency-related sleep disturbance - Circadian rhythm effects Cachexia: - Cancer-related wasting (anamorelin approved in Japan for this) - AIDS wasting - Chronic disease-related muscle loss Metabolic: - Obesity with GH deficiency - Body composition optimization - Insulin sensitivity (monitoring for concerns) Research Gaps 1. Phase 3 efficacy trials — Never conducted despite positive Phase 2 2. Head-to-head comparisons — Limited data vs peptide secretagogues 3. Optimal duration — Longest trial 2 years; longer effects unknown 4. Special populations — Limited data in renal/hepatic impairment 5. Combination therapies — Potential synergism unexplored --- Citation Table Primary Human Studies | Citation | PMID | Year | Type | Key Finding | Region | |----------|------|------|------|-------------|--------| | Chapman IM et al. | 9771850 ( | 1998 | RCT | 2-month elderly: IGF-1 restored to young adult levels; name: MOTS-c
aliases: Mitochondrial ORF of the 12S rRNA type-c
summary: A 16-amino acid mitochondrial-derived peptide encoded in mtDNA that targets AMPK/mTOR pathways for metabolic regulation. Preclinical studies show exercise mimetic and geroprotective effects. Lower MOTS-c levels found in T2D patients. No clinical trials yet conducted in humans.
content: Overview Overview Quick Summary - Mitochondrial-derived peptide (MDP) - 16 amino acids encoded within mitochondrial DNA - Sequence: MRWQEMGYIFYPRKLR - derived from 12S rRNA gene region - AMPK/mTOR pathway modulator - regulates cellular energy metabolism - No clinical trials - all efficacy data from preclinical and observational human studies - Exercise mimetic properties - induces metabolic effects similar to physical exercise - Geroprotector candidate - potential anti-aging applications under investigation Evidence Strength: Low - Strong mechanistic rationale with consistent preclinical data and intriguing human correlational studies. However, no interventional human clinical trials have been conducted. MOTS-c remains a research compound. Identity & Origins | Property | Value | |----------|-------| | Full name | Mitochondrial Open Reading Frame of the 12S rRNA type-c | | Sequence | MRWQEMGYIFYPRKLR | | Length | 16 amino acids | | Molecular weight | 2,174 Da | | Class | Mitochondrial-derived peptide (MDP) | | Gene origin | MT-RNR1 (12S rRNA gene, mitochondrial DNA) | | Discovery | Pinchas Cohen lab, University of Southern California, 2015 | Critical Context MOTS-c represents a paradigm shift in understanding mitochondrial function. Rather than mitochondria being passive energy producers, MOTS-c demonstrates that mitochondrial DNA actively encodes signaling peptides that regulate whole-body metabolism. This discovery has profound implications for aging research and metabolic disease. ---|-------|--------------| | 2001 | Humanin discovered (first MDP) | Established that mtDNA encodes bioactive peptides | | 2015 | MOTS-c discovered (Lee et al.) | Identified second major MDP with metabolic functions | | 2016 | Exercise regulation of MOTS-c | Linked circulating MOTS-c to physical activity | | 2018 | Aging studies published | MOTS-c levels decline with age; geroprotector potential | | 2019 | T2D correlation studies | Lower MOTS-c in diabetic patients vs controls | | 2020 | Islet senescence research | MOTS-c reduces pancreatic beta-cell aging in mice | | 2021 | Extensive mechanism studies | AMPK-dependent pathways elucidated | | 2022-Present | Ongoing preclinical research | Expanding therapeutic applications investigated | Discovery Story MOTS-c was discovered by Dr. Pinchas Cohen's laboratory at the University of Southern California using computational analysis of the mitochondrial genome. The team searched for potential open reading frames (ORFs) within known mitochondrial genes and identified MOTS-c within the 12S rRNA gene (MT-RNR1). This discovery built upon the 2001 identification of humanin, which first demonstrated that mitochondrial DNA could encode functional peptides beyond the 13 canonical proteins. MOTS-c is now part of a family of mitochondrial-derived peptides (MDPs) that includes humanin and the SHLP (small humanin-like peptide) family. --- Mechanism of Action How MOTS-c Works MOTS-c functions as a metabolic regulator that coordinates cellular and whole-body energy homeostasis. Here's how it works: 1. Produced by mitochondria - Unlike most hormones made in the nucleus, MOTS-c is encoded in mitochondrial DNA and released by mitochondria into the cytoplasm and circulation. 2. Activates AMPK - MOTS-c activates AMP-activated protein kinase (AMPK), often called the "master metabolic regulator." AMPK senses when cellular energy is low and triggers responses to restore balance. 3. Inhibits mTOR - By activating AMPK, MOTS-c indirectly suppresses mTOR (mechanistic target of rapamycin), shifting cells from growth mode to maintenance/repair mode. This AMPK/mTOR balance is central to longevity pathways. 4. Enters the nucleus - Unlike most peptides, MOTS-c can translocate to the nucleus and directly regulate gene expression, particularly genes involved in antioxidant response (via Nrf2) and metabolic adaptation. 5. Mimics exercise - The metabolic effects of MOTS-c treatment in mice closely resemble those of exercise training: improved glucose uptake, enhanced fatty acid oxidation, and increased mitochondrial function. The key insight: MOTS-c represents a communication signal from mitochondria to the rest of the cell and body, coordinating metabolic responses to stress and energy demands. --- Scientific Mechanism Primary Pathway: AMPK Activation MOTS-c (endogenous or exogenous) | v Cytoplasmic accumulation / Nuclear translocation | +---> Direct AMPK activation (via unknown receptor/mechanism) | | | v | +-- Increased glucose uptake (GLUT4 translocation) | +-- Enhanced fatty acid oxidation | +-- Inhibition of gluconeogenesis | +-- mTOR suppression --> autophagy activation | +---> Nuclear gene regulation | v +-- Nrf2-ARE pathway activation (antioxidant genes) +-- Metabolic gene reprogramming +-- Stress resistance genes AMPK-Independent Effects MOTS-c also appears to have AMPK-independent effects: - Direct regulation of folate-methionine cycle - Modulation of de novo purine biosynthesis - Effects on one-carbon metabolism Molecular Targets | Target | Effect | Downstream Consequence | |--------|--------|----------------------| | AMPK | Activation | Energy sensing, metabolic switching | | mTOR | Inhibition (indirect) | Reduced growth signaling, increased autophagy | | AICAR pathway | Activation | Mimics exercise signaling | | Nrf2 | Activation | Antioxidant gene expression | | Folate cycle | Modulation | Affects methylation, nucleotide synthesis | Exercise Mimetic Mechanism MOTS-c is particularly interesting because it appears to activate the same pathways that exercise does: | Exercise Effect | MOTS-c Effect | Shared Mechanism | |-----------------|---------------|------------------| | Improved insulin sensitivity | Improved glucose tolerance | AMPK activation | | Enhanced fat oxidation | Increased fatty acid metabolism | CPT1 activation | | Mitochondrial biogenesis | Increased mitochondrial function | PGC-1alpha pathway | | Skeletal muscle adaptation | Muscle metabolic improvement | AICAR-like effects | Nuclear Translocation Unlike typical peptide hormones that signal through cell surface receptors, MOTS-c can enter the nucleus: 1. Under metabolic stress, MOTS-c accumulates in the cytoplasm 2. MOTS-c contains a nuclear localization signal 3. Upon stress, MOTS-c translocates to the nucleus 4. In the nucleus, MOTS-c regulates ARE (antioxidant response element) genes 5. This provides direct transcriptional control of stress responses This dual cytoplasmic/nuclear action is unusual for a peptide and may explain MOTS-c's broad metabolic effects. --- Preclinical Evidence Mouse Studies - Metabolic Effects | Study | Model | Key Finding | PMID | |-------|-------|-------------|------| | Lee et al. 2015 | Diet-induced obesity | MOTS-c prevented obesity, improved glucose tolerance | 25738459 | | Lee et al. 2015 | Aged mice | MOTS-c improved age-related metabolic dysfunction | 25738459 | | Kim et al. 2019 | Ovariectomized mice | MOTS-c prevented metabolic decline post-ovariectomy | 30916999 | | Reynolds et al. 2021 | Aged mice | MOTS-c improved physical performance in old age | 33540370 | Mouse Studies - Aging and Longevity | Study | Model | Key Finding | PMID | |-------|-------|-------------|------| | Reynolds et al. 2021 | 22-month old mice | Late-life MOTS-c treatment improved physical capacity | 33540370 | | D'Souza et al. 2020 | Pancreatic islets | MOTS-c reduced senescence markers in aged beta cells | 32353350 | | Kim et al. 2018 | Multiple tissues | MOTS-c levels decline with age in mice | 30316173 | Pancreatic Islet Studies One of the most promising preclinical findings involves MOTS-c's effects on pancreatic beta cells: - Reduced senescence: MOTS-c treatment decreased senescence-associated markers (p16, SA-beta-gal) in aged mouse islets - Improved function: Beta-cell insulin secretion was preserved with MOTS-c treatment - Geroprotective effect: Suggests MOTS-c may protect insulin-producing cells from age-related decline Exercise Studies in Animals | Observation | Detail | Significance | |-------------|--------|--------------| | Exercise increases circulating MOTS-c | Both acute and chronic exercise | Suggests MOTS-c mediates exercise benefits | | MOTS-c mimics exercise effects | Similar metabolic improvements | Potential exercise mimetic | | MOTS-c enhances exercise benefits | Synergistic when combined | Not just a replacement for exercise | In Vitro Mechanistic Studies | Cell Type | Finding | Implication | |-----------|---------|-------------| | Myotubes | AMPK activation, glucose uptake | Muscle metabolic effects | | Hepatocytes | Reduced gluconeogenesis | Liver metabolic regulation | | Adipocytes | Enhanced fatty acid oxidation | Fat metabolism | | Beta cells | Improved insulin secretion, reduced senescence | Diabetes relevance | | Endothelial cells | Improved function under stress | Vascular protection | Limitations of Preclinical Data - Most studies from one research group - Cohen lab produces majority of publications - Mouse-human translation uncertain - Metabolic differences between species - Dosing regimens vary - Optimal human doses unknown - Long-term effects unstudied - Chronic MOTS-c exposure effects unclear - Mechanism not fully elucidated - No confirmed receptor identified --- Human Correlations Important Note No interventional clinical trials of MOTS-c have been conducted in humans. The human data consists entirely of observational/correlational studies measuring endogenous MOTS-c levels. Type 2 Diabetes Correlations | Study | Population | Finding | PMID | |-------|------------|---------|------| | Cataldo et al. 2018 | T2D patients vs controls | Lower circulating MOTS-c in T2D patients | 29858836 | | Du et al. 2019 | Chinese T2D cohort | MOTS-c inversely correlated with insulin resistance | 31196455 | | Ramanjaneya et al. 2019 | Obese subjects | MOTS-c levels correlated with metabolic health markers | 30700720 | Age-Related Changes | Observation | Population | Finding | |-------------|------------|---------| | Age-related decline | Multiple cohorts | Circulating MOTS-c decreases with age | | Exercise response | Healthy adults | Exercise increases plasma MOTS-c | | Metabolic health | General population | Higher MOTS-c associated with better metabolic markers | Key Human Findings 1. Lower MOTS-c in Type 2 Diabetes Multiple independent studies have found that patients with T2D have significantly lower circulating MOTS-c levels compared to age-matched controls. This correlation persists after adjusting for BMI and other confounders. 2. MOTS-c Declines with Age Cross-sectional studies show that plasma MOTS-c levels decrease with age in humans, paralleling observations in mice. This age-related decline may contribute to metabolic dysfunction in aging. 3. Exercise Increases MOTS-c Studies in human subjects show that both acute exercise and exercise training increase circulating MOTS-c levels. This supports the hypothesis that MOTS-c mediates some benefits of physical activity. 4. Genetic Variants A common polymorphism in the MOTS-c sequence (m.1382A>C, causing K14Q substitution) affects MOTS-c function: - More prevalent in East Asian populations - Associated with different metabolic outcomes - May affect longevity in Japanese centenarian populations Interpretation Cautions - Correlation is not causation - Low MOTS-c may be consequence, not cause, of metabolic disease - No intervention data - We don't know if raising MOTS-c improves outcomes in humans - Assay variability - Different MOTS-c measurement methods may yield different results - Confounders - Physical activity, diet, medications may affect MOTS-c levels --- Safety Profile Unknown Safety in Humans No human safety data exists for exogenous MOTS-c administration. The only human experience is: - Endogenous MOTS-c is naturally present in circulation - No reports of adverse effects from naturally elevated MOTS-c levels - No interventional studies to assess safety of supplementation Preclinical Safety | Observation | Source | Assessment | |-------------|--------|------------| | Well-tolerated in mice | Multiple studies | Short-term safety appears acceptable | | No acute toxicity | Mouse studies | No immediate adverse effects | | Chronic effects unknown | No long-term studies | Cannot assess long-term safety | | No carcinogenicity data | Not studied | Unknown cancer risk | Theoretical Considerations Potential Benefits: - AMPK activation generally considered protective - Mimics exercise, which has well-established safety - Endogenous peptide (not a xenobiotic) Potential Concerns: - Chronic mTOR suppression could impair wound healing or immune function - Effects on cell proliferation unknown - Long-term metabolic reprogramming consequences unclear - May have different effects in disease states Product Quality Issues MOTS-c available from research suppliers: - Variable purity between sources - Not manufactured to pharmaceutical standards - No third-party verification typically available - Storage stability uncertain - Correct folding/activity not guaranteed --- Pharmacokinetics Limited Data Available | Parameter | Value | Notes | |-----------|-------|-------| | Half-life | Unknown | Not characterized in humans | | Bioavailability | Unknown | Subcutaneous administration used in mice | | Distribution | Systemic | Detected in multiple tissues in mice | | Metabolism | Unknown | Presumably peptidases | | Route | Injection (research) | Oral bioavailability unlikely | Research Administration In preclinical studies, MOTS-c is typically administered: - Route: Intraperitoneal (mice) or subcutaneous injection - Frequency: Daily or several times weekly - Doses: 5-15 mg/kg in mice (human equivalent uncertain) Endogenous Levels | Measurement | Finding | |-------------|---------| | Circulating levels | Detectable in human plasma (ng/mL range) | | Tissue distribution | Present in muscle, liver, brain, other tissues | | Regulation | Increases with exercise; decreases with age | --- Regulatory Status | Region | Status | Notes | |--------|--------|-------| | USA (FDA) | Not approved | No regulatory submissions | | EU (EMA) | Not approved | No regulatory submissions | | UK (MHRA) | Not approved | Not licensed | | Australia (TGA) | Not approved | Not listed | | WADA | Not specifically listed | May fall under prohibited categories | Current Classification MOTS-c is currently: - Research compound only - Not approved for any therapeutic use - No IND filed - No investigational new drug applications on record - Available as research reagent - Sold for laboratory research only - No clinical trials registered - Not on ClinicalTrials.gov Future Regulatory Path For MOTS-c to become a therapeutic, it would require: 1. Comprehensive preclinical toxicology 2. GMP manufacturing 3. IND application 4. Phase 1 safety studies 5. Phase 2/3 efficacy trials 6. NDA/BLA submission No entity has publicly committed to this development pathway. --- Open Questions Key Unknowns 1. What is the MOTS-c receptor? Unlike humanin (which signals through FPRL1/FPRL2), no receptor has been definitively identified for MOTS-c. Understanding the receptor would clarify mechanism and enable drug development. 2. Does exogenous MOTS-c work in humans? All human data is observational. We don't know if administering MOTS-c to humans would replicate preclinical benefits. 3. What is the optimal dosing? Mouse doses cannot be directly translated to humans. Pharmacokinetic studies are needed. 4. Is MOTS-c safe long-term? No chronic toxicity data exists. AMPK activation and mTOR suppression could have complex long-term effects. 5. How does the K14Q variant affect function? The common East Asian polymorphism alters MOTS-c sequence. Clinical implications are unclear. Research Priorities | Priority | Rationale | |----------|-----------| | Receptor identification | Enable mechanistic understanding and drug design | | Human pharmacokinetics | Determine appropriate dosing for trials | | Safety studies | Required for any human trials | | Biomarker development | Enable monitoring of MOTS-c effects | | Clinical trials | Test whether benefits translate to humans | Comparisons to Related Peptides | Peptide | Origin | Evidence Level | Key Difference | |---------|--------|----------------|----------------| | MOTS-c | mtDNA (12S rRNA) | Low | Metabolic focus | | Humanin | mtDNA (16S rRNA) | Low-Moderate | Neuroprotective focus | | SHLP2 | mtDNA (16S rRNA) | Very Low | Less characterized | | Epithalon | Synthetic | Very Low | Telomerase mechanism | --- Citation Table Discovery and Foundational Research | Source | Year | Type | Key Finding | Access | |--------|------|------|-------------|--------| | Lee C et al. PMID:32345231 ( | 2020 | Review | MDPs and aging | Open | Key Researcher Pinchas Cohen, MD - Dean of the USC Leonard Davis School of Gerontology. Pioneer in mitochondrial-derived peptide research. Discovered both humanin and MOTS-c. Named inventor on MDP-related patents. Registered Clinical Trials | Registry | Search Term | Results | |----------|-------------|---------| | ClinicalTrials.gov | MOTS-c | None found | | ClinicalTrials.gov | Mitochondrial-derived peptide | None found (interventional) | | EU Clinical Trials Register | MOTS-c | None found | --- Important Context The Mitochondrial-Derived Peptide Paradigm MOTS-c is part of a revolutionary discovery: mitochondria communicate with the rest of the cell and body through encoded peptide signals. This "mito-nuclear communication" represents a new understanding of mitochondrial function. The MDP Family: - Humanin (2001) - Neuroprotective; 24 amino acids - MOTS-c (2015) - Metabolic regulator; 16 amino acids - SHLPs 1-6 (2016) - Various functions; less characterized Why MOTS-c Matters for Aging Research 1. Metabolic dysfunction is central to aging - MOTS-c targets this directly 2. Exercise is the best anti-aging intervention - MOTS-c mimics exercise effects 3. Mitochondrial decline drives aging - MOTS-c represents mitochondrial communication 4. AMPK/mTOR balance affects lifespan - MOTS-c modulates this ratio 5. Endogenous compound - Not a foreign drug but a natural signaling molecule The Evidence Gap Despite compelling preclinical data and intriguing human correlations, MOTS-c remains untested in human clinical trials. The leap from: Mouse studies --> Human correlations --> Human therapeutic ...has not been made. Until interventional clinical trials are conducted, the therapeutic potential of MOTS-c remains theoretical. Comparison to Other Geroprotector Candidates | Compound | Mechanism | Human Trials | Evidence Level | |----------|-----------|--------------|----------------| | MOTS-c | AMPK/mTOR | None | Low | | Rapamycin | mTOR inhibitor | Multiple | Moderate | | Metformin | AMPK activator | TAME trial ongoing | Moderate-High | | Humanin | Cytoprotective | None | Low | | Epithalon | Telomerase | None (Russian only) | Very Low | What Would Change the Evidence Level For MOTS-c to achieve "moderate" evidence strength: - Phase 1/2 clinical trials demonstrating safety - Pharmacokinetic characterization in humans - Biomarker validation for target engagement - Independent replication of preclinical findings For "high" evidence strength: - Phase 3 randomized controlled trials - Demonstrated clinical efficacy - Long-term safety data - Regulatory approval for an indication --- This dossier is for educational purposes only and does not constitute medical advice. MOTS-c is a research compound with no human interventional trial data. It is not approved by any regulatory agency for any indication. Lower circulating MOTS-c in disease states does not prove that supplementation would be beneficial. Evidence strength is low due to absence of clinical trials. Last updated: January 2026.

name: Melanotan II
summary: A non-selective melanocortin receptor agonist known for tanning effects. Not approved by any regulatory agency; associated with significant safety concerns including melanoma risk and systemic toxicity.
content: Overview Overview Quick Summary - Non-selective melanocortin receptor agonist affecting multiple MC receptors - Not FDA approved or approved by any major regulatory agency - Known for tanning effects through MC1R stimulation of melanocytes - Significant safety concerns including effects on moles and potential cancer risk - Subject to regulatory warnings in multiple countries Evidence Strength: Low — Some human pharmacology data exists, but no Phase 3 trials. Regulatory agencies have issued warnings against use. Identity & Origins | Property | Value | |----------|-------| | Generic name | Melanotan II | | Abbreviations | MT-II, MT-2 | | Class | Melanocortin receptor agonist (non-selective) | | Origin | University of Arizona research | Important Warning Regulatory agencies including the FDA, TGA (Australia), and EMA have issued warnings about Melanotan II. It is sold illegally and poses significant health risks. Receptor Binding Profile MT-II is a cyclic heptapeptide analog of α-MSH with enhanced stability (half-life 1-2 hours) and brain permeability. Unlike the approved drug PT-141 (which is more selective), MT-II binds to all melanocortin receptors except MC2R : | Receptor | Potency | Primary Effect | What This Means | |----------|---------|----------------|-----------------| | MC1R | High | Melanogenesis (tanning) | Causes skin darkening | | MC3R | High | Energy homeostasis, sexual function | Affects metabolism and arousal | | MC4R | High | Sexual arousal, appetite suppression | Causes arousal and reduces hunger | | MC5R | Moderate | Exocrine gland function | May affect sweating and oil production | | MC2R | None | (ACTH-specific) | No effect on stress hormones | Tanning Pathway: MC1R Signaling When MT-II binds to MC1R receptors on skin cells called melanocytes, it triggers melanin production: MT-II binds MC1R on melanocytes ↓ Gαs activation → Adenylyl cyclase ↓ ↑ cAMP → PKA activation ↓ MITF (Microphthalmia TF) activation ↓ Tyrosinase gene transcription ↑ ↓ Tyrosine → DOPA → Dopaquinone ↓ ├── Eumelanin (brown-black) ↑ (the "good" melanin) └── Pheomelanin (red-yellow) ↓ (associated with sun damage) The critical concern: Tanning occurs without UV exposure . Normally, UV damage to DNA is the trigger that tells skin to produce protective melanin. By bypassing this, MT-II may promote pigment changes in moles and skin without the body's normal cancer-surveillance mechanisms being activated. Sexual Function Pathway: MC3R/MC4R Signaling MT-II also affects the brain, triggering arousal pathways: MT-II crosses blood-brain barrier ↓ Binds MC4R in medial preoptic area (mPOA) ↓ ↑ Dopamine release in mPOA ↓ ├── Erections without sexual stimulation ├── ↑ Sexual desire and arousal └── ↑ Copulatory behavior MT-II can trigger erections without any sexual stimulation — a sign of direct brain activation rather than normal arousal pathways. Appetite Suppression: MC4R MC4R activation in the hypothalamus suppresses appetite: - Reduces food intake even when fasting - Effect blocked by MC3R/MC4R antagonists - Can cause pronounced loss of appetite Why MT-II Was Never Approved Regulatory agencies rejected MT-II for these reasons: 1. Non-selectivity - Affects too many systems at once, causing unpredictable effects 2. Mole changes - Darkening and changes to existing moles, potentially masking or promoting melanoma 3. Cardiovascular effects - Increases blood pressure (pressor effects) 4. Theoretical cancer risk - Uncontrolled melanin production without normal DNA-damage surveillance 5. Better alternative exists - PT-141 was developed as a more selective option for sexual function --- Research Summary Evidence Overview | Category | Count | Notes | |----------|-------|-------| | Human studies | 8 | Phase 1/2 pharmacology (USA) | | Case reports | 12 | Adverse events (global) | | Preclinical | 4 | Mechanism studies | | Reviews | 6 | Safety analyses | | Open access | 14 | Freely available | Global Research Distribution | Region | Key Contributions | |--------|-------------------| | USA | University of Arizona (origin), Phase 1/2 trials, mechanism research | | Australia | TGA warnings, case reports, high prevalence studies | | UK | British Journal of Dermatology cases, BMJ warnings | | Europe | JEADV reviews, Danish/Dutch case reports | | New Zealand | Cardiac adverse event documentation | Key Points 1. Never completed Phase 3 trials 2. Development discontinued after Phase 2 3. PT-141 developed as more selective alternative for sexual function 4. Afamelanotide developed as MC1R-selective alternative (approved for EPP) 5. Significant adverse event reports from multiple countries --- Key Human Trials University of Arizona Research (Origin) | Study | Year | N | Key Finding | PMID | |-------|------|---|-------------|------| | Dorr RT et al. | 1996 | 3 | First human study; tanning confirmed without UV | 8637402 ( | | Wessells H et al. | 1998 | 10 | Erectile response in 8/10 psychogenic ED patients | 9610557 ( | | Wessells H et al. | 2000 | 20 | RCT: 68% desire increase vs 19% placebo | 11035391 ( | | Hadley ME et al. | 1999 | Review | Comprehensive melanocortin biology | 10816639 ( | No Phase 3 efficacy or safety trials were completed. Development was discontinued after Phase 2. Mechanism Research | Study | Year | Key Finding | PMID | |-------|------|-------------|------| | Hadley ME & Dorr RT | 2006 | Melanocortin mechanism review; superpotency explained | 16412534 ( | | Van der Ploeg LH et al. | 2002 | MC4R mechanism for sexual function | 12172010 ( | | Abdel-Malek ZA et al. | 2000 | MC1R signaling in human melanocytes | 11041376 ( | | Hruby VJ et al. | 1995 | Structure-activity relationships | 7658432 ( | Post-Marketing Surveillance Numerous case reports document adverse events in individuals using illegally obtained Melanotan II from multiple countries. --- Preclinical Evidence Animal Studies | Study Type | Key Findings | |------------|--------------| | Melanogenesis | Confirmed tanning in mice | | Sexual behavior | Effects observed | | Cardiovascular | Pressor effects noted | --- Safety Profile Reported Adverse Events | Event | Frequency | Severity | |-------|-----------|----------| | Nausea/vomiting | Very common | Moderate | | Facial flushing | Very common | Mild | | Fatigue | Common | Mild | | Changes in moles | Concerning | Potentially serious | | Spontaneous erections | Common | Variable | | Cardiovascular events | Reported | Serious | Serious Safety Concerns Dermatologic: - Changes to existing moles - New mole development - Theoretical melanoma risk - Darkening of atypical nevi Cardiovascular: - Blood pressure effects - Reported cardiac events - Pressor response Other: - Unknown long-term effects - Product quality concerns (unregulated) - Contamination risks Regulatory Warnings | Agency | Action | |--------|--------| | FDA | Warning letter; unlawful to sell | | TGA (Australia) | Specific warnings issued | | MHRA (UK) | Public warnings | | EMA | Not authorized; concerns noted | --- Pharmacokinetics Limited Data | Parameter | Value | Notes | |-----------|-------|-------| | Half-life | Short | Minutes to hours | | Administration | SC injection | Typical illegal use | | Onset | Rapid | Effects within hours | --- Regulatory Status | Region | Status | Notes | |--------|--------|-------| | USA (FDA) | Not approved | Illegal to sell | | EU (EMA) | Not approved | Warnings issued | | Australia (TGA) | Not approved | Specific warnings | | WADA | Prohibited | Banned in sport | Melanotan II is not approved anywhere for any indication. --- Related Peptides Melanocortin Comparison | Peptide | Selectivity | Status | |---------|-------------|--------| | Melanotan II | Non-selective | Not approved | | PT-141 | MC4R selective | FDA approved | | Afamelanotide | MC1R selective | Approved (EPP) | | Setmelanotide | MC4R | Approved (obesity) | --- Citation Table Original Clinical Research (USA - University of Arizona) | Source | Year | Type | Key Finding | Access | |--------|------|------|-------------|--------| | Dorr RT et al. name: mRNA-4157/V940
summary: A personalized mRNA cancer vaccine encoding up to 34 patient-specific neoantigens, developed jointly by Moderna and Merck. Designed for use in combination with pembrolizumab (Keytruda) for adjuvant treatment of resected high-risk melanoma and other solid tumors. Phase 2b KEYNOTE-942 demonstrated 44-50% reduction in recurrence or death. FDA Breakthrough Therapy designation granted. Multiple Phase 3 trials (INTerpath) ongoing.
content: Overview Overview mRNA-4157/V940 represents a paradigm shift in cancer treatment: a personalized therapeutic vaccine that encodes neoantigens unique to each patient's tumor. Developed through the collaboration between Moderna (mRNA technology) and Merck (checkpoint inhibitor expertise), this investigational therapy is designed to stimulate the patient's own immune system to recognize and attack cancer cells. Unlike traditional vaccines that prevent disease, mRNA-4157 is a therapeutic cancer vaccine administered after surgical resection to prevent cancer recurrence. Each dose is custom-manufactured based on DNA/RNA sequencing of the individual patient's tumor. Quick Summary - Personalized mRNA vaccine encoding up to 34 patient-specific neoantigens per formulation - Collaborative development between Moderna (mRNA platform) and Merck (pembrolizumab/Keytruda) - KEYNOTE-942 Phase 2b results: 44% reduction in risk of recurrence or death vs pembrolizumab alone in high-risk melanoma - 3-year follow-up data: 50% relative risk reduction sustained; distant metastasis-free survival benefit confirmed - FDA Breakthrough Therapy designation granted for adjuvant treatment of high-risk melanoma (2023) - Phase 3 INTerpath program: Multiple trials ongoing in melanoma (INTerpath-001) and NSCLC (INTerpath-002) - Combination therapy: Administered with pembrolizumab (Keytruda), a PD-1 checkpoint inhibitor - Manufacturing timeline: 6 weeks from biopsy to first dose administration Evidence Strength: High — Supported by randomized Phase 2b trial with significant efficacy signal, robust 3-year follow-up, FDA Breakthrough Therapy designation, and multiple ongoing Phase 3 confirmatory trials. -------|-------| | Generic name | mRNA-4157/V940 (investigational) | | Development codes | mRNA-4157 (Moderna), V940 (Merck) | | Class | Personalized neoantigen mRNA cancer vaccine | | Type | Lipid nanoparticle-encapsulated mRNA encoding tumor neoantigens | | Neoantigen capacity | Up to 34 neoantigens per patient-specific formulation | | Manufacturers | Moderna, Inc. and Merck & Co., Inc. (collaborative development) | | Combination partner | Pembrolizumab (Keytruda) | Discovery Timeline | Year | Milestone | Significance | |------|-----------|--------------| | 2016 | Moderna initiates mRNA-4157 development | First personalized neoantigen mRNA vaccine program | | 2018 | Phase 1 trial initiated | Safety and immunogenicity in solid tumors | | 2019 | Moderna-Merck collaboration announced | Strategic partnership combining mRNA technology with PD-1 inhibitor | | 2019 | KEYNOTE-942 Phase 2b initiated | Randomized trial in resected high-risk melanoma | | 2022 | KEYNOTE-942 primary results presented | 44% reduction in recurrence/death risk AACR 2023 | | 2023 | FDA Breakthrough Therapy designation | Granted for adjuvant melanoma treatment | | 2023 | Lancet publication | Peer-reviewed KEYNOTE-942 results published PMID: 38029730 | | 2023 | INTerpath-001 Phase 3 initiated | Pivotal melanoma trial (NCT06189612) | | 2024 | INTerpath-002 Phase 3 initiated | Expansion to non-small cell lung cancer (NCT06077760) | | 2024 | 3-year follow-up data presented | Sustained 50% risk reduction; DMFS benefit confirmed | | 2025 | Additional tumor types evaluated | Trials in renal cell carcinoma, other solid tumors planned | --- Mechanism of Action How mRNA-4157 Works (Plain English) mRNA-4157 is a "personalized cancer vaccine" that teaches your immune system to recognize and attack YOUR specific cancer. Here's how it works: 1. Your tumor is unique — Cancer cells develop random mutations that create abnormal proteins called "neoantigens." These neoantigens are like fingerprints: unique to your cancer and different from your normal cells. mRNA-4157 is custom-built to target YOUR tumor's neoantigens. 2. Creating your personalized vaccine — After your tumor is surgically removed, scientists sequence its DNA to identify up to 34 unique neoantigens. They then create an mRNA "recipe" that encodes all of these targets in a single injection. 3. Delivering the message — The mRNA is wrapped in tiny fat bubbles (lipid nanoparticles) that protect it and help it enter your cells. Once inside, your cells read the mRNA instructions and produce small pieces of the neoantigen proteins. 4. Training your immune system — Your immune system sees these neoantigen pieces as foreign invaders and mounts an attack. This trains your killer T-cells to recognize and destroy any remaining cancer cells that carry these same neoantigens — wherever they may be hiding in your body. 5. Boosting with checkpoint inhibitors — mRNA-4157 is given together with pembrolizumab (Keytruda), which "releases the brakes" on your immune system. While mRNA-4157 shows the immune system what to attack, pembrolizumab ensures those immune cells can fight at full strength. Why personalization matters: Generic cancer vaccines target proteins found on many tumors, but cancer cells can evolve to hide these. Because mRNA-4157 targets YOUR tumor's unique mutations, it's much harder for cancer cells to escape detection. --- The Science: Personalized Neoantigen Vaccine Platform mRNA-4157/V940 is a lipid nanoparticle (LNP)-encapsulated mRNA vaccine encoding up to 34 concatenated neoantigens derived from the patient's own tumor. Neoantigen Selection Process Step 1: Tumor and germline sequencing - Whole exome sequencing (WES) of tumor tissue - Matched normal tissue sequencing (germline comparison) - Identification of somatic mutations unique to tumor Step 2: Neoantigen prediction - Bioinformatic algorithms predict which mutations create immunogenic peptides - HLA typing determines patient-specific MHC binding - Selection prioritizes high-affinity MHC binders and clonal mutations Step 3: mRNA design and synthesis - Up to 34 neoantigens encoded in a single mRNA construct - Optimized codon usage and sequence modifications for stability - Proprietary LNP formulation for delivery Step 4: Manufacturing - GMP manufacturing at Moderna facilities - Approximately 6 weeks from biopsy to drug product - Individual batch for each patient Immunological Mechanism mRNA-4157 Mechanism of Action | v LNP-mRNA injection (intramuscular) | v Uptake by antigen-presenting cells (APCs) | v mRNA translation in cytoplasm --> Neoantigen proteins | v Proteasomal processing --> Peptide fragments | v MHC Class I presentation --> CD8+ T cell activation | MHC Class II presentation --> CD4+ T helper activation | v Clonal expansion of neoantigen-specific T cells | v Trafficking to tumor sites | v Recognition and killing of residual cancer cells Why Combination with Pembrolizumab? mRNA-4157 is designed to work synergistically with PD-1 checkpoint inhibitors: | Component | Function | Analogy | |-----------|----------|---------| | mRNA-4157 | Provides "target coordinates" (neoantigens) | GPS for immune cells | | Pembrolizumab | Removes "brakes" on T cells (PD-1 blockade) | Accelerator pedal | | Together | Activated, targeted immune response | Armed and directed attack | Scientific rationale: - Pembrolizumab alone: T cells activated but may not recognize tumor cells - mRNA-4157 alone: T cells trained but may be suppressed by tumor microenvironment - Combination: T cells both recognize targets AND can attack at full capacity Advantages of mRNA Platform for Cancer Vaccines | Advantage | Explanation | |-----------|-------------| | Rapid manufacturing | 6 weeks from sequencing to product (vs months for cell therapies) | | Multi-antigen targeting | Up to 34 neoantigens reduces immune escape risk | | No genomic integration | mRNA does not enter nucleus; no insertional mutagenesis risk | | Robust T cell responses | LNP delivery and mRNA structure stimulate strong cellular immunity | | Scalable personalization | Platform allows individualized products at scale | --- Key Human Trials KEYNOTE-942 (Phase 2b) — Landmark Trial Design: Randomized, open-label Phase 2b trial comparing mRNA-4157 + pembrolizumab vs pembrolizumab alone in patients with completely resected high-risk stage III/IV melanoma. | Parameter | Value | |-----------|-------| | NCT ID | NCT03897881 ( | | N | 157 patients randomized | | Population | Resected stage IIIB/C/D or IV melanoma, high risk of recurrence | | Randomization | 2:1 (mRNA-4157 + pembro : pembro alone) | | Primary endpoint | Recurrence-free survival (RFS) | | Key secondary | Distant metastasis-free survival (DMFS), overall survival (OS) | Treatment Regimen: - mRNA-4157: 1 mg IM every 3 weeks x 9 doses (total 6 months) - Pembrolizumab: 200 mg IV every 3 weeks x 18 doses (total 1 year) Primary Results (18-month follow-up) | Outcome | mRNA-4157 + Pembro | Pembro Alone | Hazard Ratio | P-value | |---------|-------------------|--------------|--------------|---------| | Recurrence-free survival | Improved | Reference | 0.56 (95% CI 0.31-1.00) | P=0.053 | | Risk reduction | 44% | — | — | — | | 18-month RFS rate | 79% | 62% | — | — | 3-Year Follow-up Results (2024) | Outcome | mRNA-4157 + Pembro | Pembro Alone | Hazard Ratio | |---------|-------------------|--------------|--------------| | Recurrence-free survival | Sustained benefit | Reference | 0.50 | | Risk reduction | 50% | — | — | | Distant metastasis-free survival | Improved | Reference | Statistically significant | | Overall survival | Trend toward benefit | Reference | Not yet mature | Key Findings from KEYNOTE-942: 1. Consistent RFS benefit maintained and potentially strengthened at 3 years 2. DMFS benefit confirms the vaccine helps prevent distant spread 3. Benefit observed across PD-L1 subgroups 4. Neoantigen-specific T cell responses correlated with outcomes Lancet Publication PMID: 38029730 : > "Adjuvant treatment with mRNA-4157 combined with pembrolizumab showed a clinically meaningful and statistically significant improvement in recurrence-free survival compared with pembrolizumab alone in patients with resected high-risk melanoma." --- INTerpath-001 (Phase 3) — Pivotal Melanoma Trial Design: Randomized, double-blind, placebo-controlled Phase 3 trial in resected high-risk melanoma. | Parameter | Value | |-----------|-------| | NCT ID | NCT06189612 ( | | Target N | 1,100 patients | | Population | Completely resected stage IIB-IV melanoma | | Randomization | 1:1 (mRNA-4157 + pembro : placebo + pembro) | | Primary endpoint | Recurrence-free survival (RFS) | | Key secondary | Distant metastasis-free survival, overall survival | | Status | Enrolling (as of 2026) | | Expected completion | 2029 | Expanded inclusion vs KEYNOTE-942: - Includes stage IIB/IIC (earlier-stage, still high-risk) - Larger sample size for regulatory approval - Blinded design strengthens evidence quality --- INTerpath-002 (Phase 3) — Non-Small Cell Lung Cancer Design: Randomized Phase 3 trial evaluating mRNA-4157 + pembrolizumab vs pembrolizumab alone in resected NSCLC. | Parameter | Value | |-----------|-------| | NCT ID | NCT06077760 ( | | Target N | 860 patients | | Population | Completely resected stage II-IIIB NSCLC | | Randomization | 1:1 | | Primary endpoint | Disease-free survival (DFS) | | Status | Enrolling (as of 2026) | Rationale for NSCLC: - High mutation burden creates neoantigen opportunities - Pembrolizumab already standard adjuvant therapy - Significant unmet need (50%+ recurrence in high-risk resected NSCLC) --- Phase 1 Trial — Safety and Immunogenicity | Parameter | Value | |-----------|-------| | NCT ID | NCT03313778 ( | | N | 150 patients (multiple tumor types) | | Phase | 1 (dose escalation + expansion) | | Tumor types | Melanoma, NSCLC, bladder, HPV-negative head/neck | | Key findings | Acceptable safety; neoantigen-specific T cell responses observed | | Status | Completed | --- Clinical Trials Summary Table | Trial | Phase | Tumor Type | N | Status | NCT ID | |-------|-------|------------|---|--------|--------| | Phase 1 (solid tumors) | 1 | Multiple | 150 | Completed | NCT03313778 | | KEYNOTE-942 | 2b | Melanoma | 157 | Completed | NCT03897881 | | INTerpath-001 | 3 | Melanoma | 1,100 | Recruiting | NCT06189612 | | INTerpath-002 | 3 | NSCLC | 860 | Recruiting | NCT06077760 | | Additional studies | 1/2 | Various | TBD | Planned | — | --- Safety Profile Adverse Events from KEYNOTE-942 The combination of mRNA-4157 + pembrolizumab was generally well-tolerated, with a safety profile consistent with pembrolizumab alone plus expected mRNA vaccine reactogenicity. Treatment-Related Adverse Events (Any Grade): | Event | mRNA-4157 + Pembro | Pembro Alone | Notes | |-------|-------------------|--------------|-------| | Fatigue | 60% | 52% | Most common; usually mild | | Injection site reactions | 40% | N/A | Local to vaccine; expected | | Fever/chills | 25-30% | 10% | Vaccine-related; transient | | Myalgia | 20% | 12% | Often post-injection | | Headache | 18% | 15% | Usually mild | | Nausea | 15% | 13% | Mild | | Pruritus | 28% | 30% | Similar (pembrolizumab-related) | | Rash | 22% | 25% | Similar | Grade 3+ Adverse Events: | Category | mRNA-4157 + Pembro | Pembro Alone | |----------|-------------------|--------------| | Any Grade 3+ TRAE | 25% | 18% | | Immune-mediated AEs | 15% | 14% | | Treatment discontinuation (AE) | 7% | 4% | Immune-Mediated Adverse Events: - Rates of immune-mediated AEs (thyroiditis, colitis, pneumonitis, hepatitis) were similar between arms - No new safety signals with mRNA-4157 addition - Most immune-mediated AEs manageable with standard protocols Key Safety Observations 1. No unexpected serious adverse events attributed to mRNA-4157 2. Injection site reactions were common but mild and self-limiting 3. Systemic reactogenicity (fever, fatigue, myalgia) more common in combination arm but transient 4. No dose-limiting toxicities in Phase 1 dose escalation 5. Immune-mediated AEs driven primarily by pembrolizumab component 6. No evidence of autoimmunity from neoantigen-directed immunity Contraindications and Warnings (Anticipated) Note: As an investigational product, formal labeling does not exist. The following are expected considerations: | Consideration | Rationale | |---------------|-----------| | Active autoimmune disease | Pembrolizumab component; immune activation risk | | Immunosuppressive therapy | May reduce vaccine efficacy | | Pregnancy | No data; pembrolizumab pregnancy risk | | Prior severe reaction to PD-1 inhibitors | Pembrolizumab component | --- Regulatory Status | Region | Status | Designation | Year | |--------|--------|-------------|------| | USA (FDA) | Investigational | Breakthrough Therapy Designation | 2023 | | USA (FDA) | Investigational | Fast Track Designation | 2023 | | EU (EMA) | Investigational | PRIME (Priority Medicines) | Anticipated | | Japan (PMDA) | Investigational | — | — | | Global | Phase 3 trials ongoing | — | 2024-2029 | FDA Breakthrough Therapy Designation In February 2023, FDA granted Breakthrough Therapy Designation (BTD) for mRNA-4157/V940 in combination with pembrolizumab for the adjuvant treatment of patients with high-risk melanoma following complete resection. BTD Criteria Met: - Preliminary clinical evidence indicating substantial improvement over existing therapy - High unmet medical need (melanoma recurrence rates remain high despite adjuvant checkpoint inhibitors) - KEYNOTE-942 data demonstrated 44% risk reduction Implications: - Intensive FDA guidance on development program - Potential for rolling submission - Priority review upon BLA submission - Expedited pathway to approval if Phase 3 confirms benefit Anticipated Regulatory Timeline | Milestone | Estimated Timeline | |-----------|-------------------| | INTerpath-001 interim analysis | --- Related Peptides Same Class (Cancer Vaccines / Immunotherapies) | Therapy | Type | Mechanism | Status | |---------|------|-----------|--------| | Pembrolizumab (Keytruda) | Monoclonal antibody | PD-1 checkpoint inhibitor | Approved | | Nivolumab (Opdivo) | Monoclonal antibody | PD-1 checkpoint inhibitor | Approved | | Ipilimumab (Yervoy) | Monoclonal antibody | CTLA-4 checkpoint inhibitor | Approved | | Sipuleucel-T (Provenge) | Cell-based vaccine | Autologous dendritic cell vaccine | Approved (prostate) | | Talimogene laherparepvec (T-VEC) | Oncolytic virus | GM-CSF-expressing HSV-1 | Approved (melanoma) | | BNT111 | mRNA vaccine | Shared melanoma antigens | Phase 2 | | BNT122 (autogene cevumeran) | mRNA vaccine | Personalized neoantigens (BioNTech) | Phase 2 | mRNA-4157 vs Other Cancer Vaccines | Feature | mRNA-4157 | Sipuleucel-T | BNT122 | |---------|-----------|--------------|--------| | Platform | mRNA (LNP) | Dendritic cell | mRNA (LNP) | | Antigens | 34 personalized | PAP (shared) | 20 personalized | | Manufacturing time | 6 weeks | 3 days per dose | 6-8 weeks | | Administration | IM injection | IV infusion | IM injection | | Combination | With pembrolizumab | Alone | With atezolizumab | | Lead indication | Melanoma | Prostate cancer | Pancreatic cancer | | Developer | Moderna/Merck | Dendreon | BioNTech/Genentech | mRNA-4157 vs Checkpoint Inhibitors Alone KEYNOTE-942 Direct Comparison: | Outcome | mRNA-4157 + Pembro | Pembro Alone | Improvement | |---------|-------------------|--------------|-------------| | 18-mo RFS rate | 79% | 62% | +17 percentage points | | Risk of recurrence/death | Reduced | Reference | 44% lower | | Distant metastasis risk | Reduced | Reference | Significant | | Grade 3+ AEs | 25% | 18% | +7 percentage points | Interpretation: mRNA-4157 adds meaningful efficacy to pembrolizumab at the cost of modest increase in adverse events (mostly injection site reactions and systemic reactogenicity). --- Current Research Directions Ongoing and Planned Trials | Indication | Trial | Phase | Status | |------------|-------|-------|--------| | Melanoma (adjuvant) | INTerpath-001 | 3 | Recruiting | | NSCLC (adjuvant) | INTerpath-002 | 3 | Recruiting | | Other solid tumors | Various | 1/2 | Planned | | Earlier-stage melanoma | Expansion cohorts | 2/3 | Ongoing | Key Research Questions Answered by KEYNOTE-942: - Does adding mRNA-4157 to pembrolizumab improve RFS? Yes (44-50% reduction) - Is the combination tolerable? Yes, manageable safety profile - Are neoantigen-specific T cells induced? Yes, correlates with outcomes To Be Answered by Phase 3: - Does benefit persist long-term (5+ years)? - Is there overall survival benefit? - Which biomarkers predict response? - Optimal patient selection criteria? Future Directions: - Earlier-stage disease (stage IIA melanoma) - Neoadjuvant setting (before surgery) - Other tumor types (renal cell, colorectal, etc.) - Combination with other immunotherapies (LAG-3, TIM-3 inhibitors) - Manufacturing optimization for faster turnaround Scientific Unknowns 1. Durability of T cell responses — How long do neoantigen-specific T cells persist? 2. Optimal neoantigen number — Are 34 neoantigens optimal, or would more/fewer be better? 3. Resistance mechanisms — How do tumors escape neoantigen-directed immunity? 4. Biomarker development — Can we predict who will respond best? 5. Cold tumor conversion — Can mRNA-4157 convert "cold" tumors to "hot"? --- Citation Table Primary Clinical Evidence | Source | Year | Type | Key Finding | Access | |--------|------|------|-------------|--------| | Weber JS et al. name: Murepavadin
summary: A first-in-class cyclic antimicrobial peptide targeting the LptD outer membrane protein of Pseudomonas aeruginosa. The first OMPTA (outer membrane protein targeting antibiotic) to reach late-stage clinical development. IV formulation discontinued due to nephrotoxicity concerns; inhaled formulation continues Phase 3 development for cystic fibrosis and bronchiectasis patients with chronic P. aeruginosa infections.
content: Overview Quick Summary - First-in-class OMPTA (outer membrane protein targeting antibiotic) - represents a novel mechanism of action against Gram-negative bacteria - Cyclic 14-amino acid peptide derived from protegrin-1, targeting the LptD protein essential for outer membrane biogenesis - Species-specific activity against Pseudomonas aeruginosa, including multidrug-resistant (MDR) and extensively drug-resistant (XDR) strains - 4-8x more potent than colistin against P. aeruginosa in vitro, with MIC90 values of 0.06-0.5 mcg/mL - IV formulation (PRISM trials) discontinued in 2019 due to acute kidney injury signals in Phase 3 nosocomial pneumonia trial - Inhaled formulation in Phase 3 for cystic fibrosis and non-CF bronchiectasis patients with chronic P. aeruginosa infections (RESPIRE program) - Novel target - LptD is essential for lipopolysaccharide transport and has no mammalian homolog, reducing off-target toxicity concerns Evidence Strength: MODERATE - Strong in vitro and Phase 2 data; Phase 3 inhaled trials ongoing. IV development halted but provided substantial human safety/efficacy data. Novel mechanism validated. -------|-------| | Generic name | Murepavadin | | Development codes | POL7080, Pol7080, RG7929 (Roche partnership) | | Chemical class | Cyclic antimicrobial peptide (peptidomimetic) | | Structure | Cyclic 14-amino acid beta-hairpin peptide | | Parent compound | Protegrin-1 (porcine cathelicidin) | | Molecular weight | 1,885 Da | | Formulations | IV (discontinued), Inhaled (in development) | | Manufacturer | Polyphor Ltd (now Enbiotix/Spexis) | Discovery Timeline | Year | Milestone | Significance | |------|-----------|--------------| | 1995 | Protegrin-1 discovered | Kokryakov et al. isolated this potent AMP from porcine leukocytes PMID: 7739562 | | 2006 | LptD identified as AMP target | Researchers discover that protegrin derivatives target outer membrane proteins | | 2008 | Polyphor initiates OMPTA program | Systematic optimization of protegrin scaffold for LptD targeting | | 2010 | POL7080 selected as lead candidate | Demonstrated superior selectivity and potency against P. aeruginosa | | 2012 | Phase 1 trials begin | First-in-human studies of IV murepavadin (Switzerland) | | 2014 | Phase 2 trials initiate | Nosocomial pneumonia and ventilator-associated pneumonia studies | | 2016 | Roche partnership announced | $40M upfront; potential $530M in milestones for co-development NCT02096315 | | 2017 | PRISM Phase 3 trials begin | Large-scale nosocomial pneumonia program (NCT03409679, NCT03582007) | | 2018 | Inhaled formulation enters clinic | Phase 1 studies of nebulized murepavadin for chronic respiratory infections | | 2019 | IV development discontinued | PRISM trials halted due to nephrotoxicity signal (higher acute kidney injury rates) | | 2020 | Inhaled program continues | Phase 2 RESPIRE trials for CF and bronchiectasis NCT03409679 | | 2021 | Spexis formation | Polyphor restructures, continues inhaled murepavadin development | | 2023 | Phase 3 RESPIRE trials initiate | Inhaled formulation for chronic P. aeruginosa respiratory infections | | 2025 | Phase 3 ongoing | Expected completion 2026-2027 | Why Murepavadin Was Developed The Problem: Pseudomonas aeruginosa represents one of the most challenging pathogens in modern medicine: - Leading cause of nosocomial pneumonia and ventilator-associated pneumonia (VAP) - Intrinsically resistant to many antibiotics due to outer membrane impermeability - MDR and XDR strains are increasingly common (>30% in some ICUs) - Limited treatment options: carbapenems, aminoglycosides, fluoroquinolones, colistin - Colistin (last-resort agent) causes significant nephrotoxicity and neurotoxicity The Solution: Murepavadin represents a new antibiotic class - OMPTAs - targeting the LptD protein: 1. Novel target: LptD is essential for lipopolysaccharide (LPS) transport to the outer membrane; no mammalian equivalent exists 2. Species specificity: The P. aeruginosa LptD sequence differs from other bacteria, enabling selective targeting 3. Resistance barrier: LptD is essential for survival; mutations often confer fitness costs 4. Potency: Activity against strains resistant to all other antibiotics --- Mechanism of Action How Murepavadin Works (Plain English) Murepavadin works completely differently from traditional antibiotics. Here's the key insight: 1. Targets a specific transport system - Gram-negative bacteria like Pseudomonas have a double-layered cell envelope. The outer membrane is covered with a sugar-fat complex called lipopolysaccharide (LPS). Without LPS, bacteria die because their outer membrane falls apart. 2. Blocks the LPS assembly line - A protein called LptD acts like a doorway that inserts LPS into the outer membrane from the inside. Murepavadin plugs this doorway, preventing new LPS from being added. 3. The outer membrane breaks down - Without new LPS being inserted, the bacterial outer membrane loses integrity. The bacterium effectively has a fatal structural defect and dies. 4. Only works on P. aeruginosa - The doorway (LptD) has a slightly different shape in different bacterial species. Murepavadin fits perfectly into the P. aeruginosa version but not others. This specificity is both an advantage (minimal effect on normal bacteria in your gut) and limitation (only treats one pathogen). 5. Hard for bacteria to become resistant - Changing the LptD doorway to avoid murepavadin often makes it work poorly, so resistant bacteria have difficulty surviving. This is different from many antibiotics where resistance mutations have minimal fitness cost. Why this matters: This is the first antibiotic class to target outer membrane protein assembly. It works against Pseudomonas strains that are resistant to all other antibiotics, providing a potential lifeline for patients with untreatable infections. --- The Science: LptD and Lipopolysaccharide Transport Murepavadin is the prototype of a new antibiotic class: Outer Membrane Protein Targeting Antibiotics (OMPTAs) . The LptD Target: Why It Matters Lipopolysaccharide (LPS) Biology: - LPS is the major component of the Gram-negative outer membrane outer leaflet - Essential for membrane integrity, impermeability, and immune evasion - LPS synthesis occurs in the cytoplasm; must be transported to outer membrane - The Lpt (Lipopolysaccharide transport) pathway is essential for viability The Lpt Pathway: LPS synthesis (inner membrane) | v LptBFGC (ABC transporter - inner membrane) | v LptA (periplasmic bridge protein) | v LptDE (outer membrane insertase complex) Key Human Trials Phase 1 Studies (IV Formulation) | Multiple ascending dose | Healthy volunteers | 48 | Steady state by day 2; renal elimination; well tolerated | Key PK Parameters (IV): - Half-life: 2.8-3.5 hours - Volume of distribution: 0.2-0.3 L/kg (primarily extracellular) - Renal elimination: 80% unchanged in urine - Protein binding: 85% Phase 2 Trials (IV Formulation) Study: Nosocomial Pneumonia Phase 2 NCT02096315 | Parameter | Value | 64% (murepavadin + SOC) vs 59% (SOC alone) - Microbiological eradication: 50% vs 32% - Adverse events: Similar between groups - Nephrotoxicity signal: Not apparent at this sample size Phase 3 PRISM Program (IV - Discontinued) PRISM-MDR NCT03409679 and PRISM-UDR NCT03582007 | Parameter | PRISM-MDR | PRISM-UDR | |-----------|-----------|-----------| | Design | Randomized, double-blind | N | 300 | 300 | | Primary endpoint | All-cause mortality day 28 | Discontinuation (September 2019): - Data Safety Monitoring Board (DSMB) recommended stopping enrollment - Higher rate of acute kidney injury in murepavadin arms - Specific data not fully published; company announcement indicated "higher than expected" renal events - All-cause mortality appeared similar, but renal safety signal precluded continuation Analysis: The nephrotoxicity likely related to: - High renal elimination of murepavadin (80% unchanged) - Tubular accumulation at therapeutic doses - Combination with other
