name: Orforglipron
summary: The first oral small-molecule (non-peptide) GLP-1 receptor agonist. NDA submitted to FDA. ATTAIN trials showed 12.4% weight loss at 72 weeks. No food/water restrictions unlike oral semaglutide. Phase 3 data in 9,000+ patients.
content: Overview Overview Quick Summary - First oral small-molecule GLP-1 agonist (NOT a peptide — included for GLP-1 class completeness) - NDA submitted to FDA for obesity indication (Priority Review) - ATTAIN-1: 12.4% weight loss at 72 weeks (36mg dose) - No food restrictions — can be taken anytime, unlike oral semaglutide (Rybelsus) - 9,000+ patients across ATTAIN (obesity) and ACHIEVE (T2D) Phase 3 programs - Convenient daily oral dosing — alternative for injection-averse patients Evidence Strength: High — Extensive Phase 3 data, NEJM publication, NDA submitted. Identity & Origins | Property | Value | |----------|-------| | Generic name | Orforglipron | | Brand name | TBD | | Research code | LY3502970 | | Class | Small-molecule GLP-1 receptor agonist (non-peptide) | | Manufacturer | Eli Lilly (licensed from Chugai 2018) | | Formulation | Oral tablet (once daily) | Discovery Timeline - 2018: Eli Lilly licenses orforglipron from Chugai Pharmaceutical - 2023: Phase 2 results published in NEJM (11.6% weight loss at 36 weeks) - 2024: Phase 3 ATTAIN program initiated - 2025 (H1): ATTAIN-1, ATTAIN-2 results; ACHIEVE trials ongoing - 2025: NDA submitted to FDA for obesity; Priority Review granted - Expected 2026: FDA decision; ACHIEVE T2D results The Science: Small Molecule vs Peptide | Feature | Orforglipron | Oral Semaglutide (Rybelsus) | |---------|--------------|----------------------------| | Molecule type | Small molecule | Peptide + absorption enhancer | | Food restrictions | None | 30 min fast, minimal water | | Dosing | Once daily | Once daily | | Bioavailability | Higher | 1% | | Metabolism | Hepatic | Proteolytic | --- Key Human Trials ATTAIN Program (Obesity) ATTAIN-1: | Trial | Population | Duration | N | |-------|------------|----------|---| | ATTAIN-1 | BMI ≥30 or ≥27 with comorbidity | 72 weeks | 1,000 | Key Results (NEJM): - Weight loss (36mg): -12.4% vs -2.2% placebo - ≥10% weight loss: 59.6% of participants - ≥5% weight loss: 77% of participants - Safety: GI AEs consistent with injectable GLP-1 class ATTAIN-2 (Obesity + T2D): - name: Oveporexton
summary: A first-in-class oral orexin receptor 2 (OX2R) selective agonist developed by Takeda for narcolepsy type 1. Though not a peptide itself, oveporexton restores orexin peptide signaling lost due to autoimmune destruction of orexin-producing neurons. Phase 3 trials demonstrate significant reductions in excessive daytime sleepiness and cataplexy, with FDA Breakthrough Therapy designation.
content: Overview Quick Summary - First-in-class oral orexin receptor 2 (OX2R) selective agonist developed by Takeda for narcolepsy type 1 - NOT a peptide but pharmacologically restores orexin neuropeptide signaling lost due to autoimmune destruction of orexin-producing neurons - Phase 3 completed: Demonstrated significant reductions in excessive daytime sleepiness (EDS) and cataplexy attacks in narcolepsy type 1 patients - FDA Breakthrough Therapy designation granted, recognizing potential for substantial improvement over existing therapies - NEJM-published results: Landmark Phase 3 trial published in the New England Journal of Medicine - Unique mechanism: Directly activates OX2R to replace missing orexin signaling, addressing the root pathophysiology of narcolepsy type 1 rather than treating symptoms Evidence Strength: HIGH — Supported by Phase 2 and Phase 3 RCTs with robust efficacy data, Breakthrough Therapy designation, and NEJM publication. This represents a novel therapeutic approach for narcolepsy. Important Note: Oveporexton is included in this peptide library not because it is a peptide, but because it directly modulates the orexin neuropeptide system. Understanding its mechanism requires knowledge of orexin peptide biology. -------|-------| | Generic name | Oveporexton | | Development code | TAK-861 | | Chemical class | Small molecule, non-peptide OX2R agonist | | Manufacturer | Takeda Pharmaceutical Company | | Mechanism | Selective orexin receptor 2 (OX2R) agonist | | Route | Oral | | Target | Orexin-2 receptor (HCRTR2/OX2R) | Discovery & Development Timeline | Year | Milestone | Significance | |------|-----------|--------------| | 1998 | Orexins discovered | De Lecea and Sakurai groups independently identify orexin-A and orexin-B neuropeptides PMID: 9491897, 9452413 | | 1999 | Orexin deficiency linked to narcolepsy | Chemelli et al. and Lin et al. establish connection PMID: 10458611, 10483067 | | 2000 | Human narcolepsy pathophysiology defined | Peyron, Thannickal et al. demonstrate orexin neuron loss in NT1 PMID: 10996548, 10839362 | | 2015 | TAK-861 discovery | Takeda initiates OX2R agonist program | | 2018 | Phase 1 initiation | First-in-human studies | | 2019 | Phase 2 initiation | Dose-finding studies in narcolepsy | | 2020 | Phase 2 results | Demonstrated efficacy on multiple sleep endpoints | | 2021 | Breakthrough Therapy designation | FDA recognizes potential for substantial improvement | | 2022 | Phase 3 initiation | ORCHESTRA program begins | | 2024 | Phase 3 completion | ORCHESTRA-NT1 trial completes | | 2024 | NEJM publication | Landmark results published | | 2025 | NDA/MAA expected | Regulatory submissions anticipated | Why Oveporexton Was Developed The Orexin Deficiency Problem: Narcolepsy type 1 (NT1) is caused by the selective autoimmune destruction of approximately 70,000 hypothalamic neurons that produce the neuropeptides orexin-A and orexin-B (also called hypocretin-1 and hypocretin-2). This results in: 1. Near-complete orexin deficiency — CSF orexin-A levels are undetectable or very low (<110 pg/mL) in >95% of NT1 patients 2. Loss of wake-promoting signals — Orexin neurons project to and stabilize arousal centers 3. Destabilized sleep-wake boundaries — Manifests as excessive daytime sleepiness, cataplexy, sleep paralysis, and hypnagogic hallucinations Limitations of Current Therapies: Existing treatments for narcolepsy do NOT address the orexin deficit: | Treatment | Mechanism | Limitation | |-----------|-----------|------------| | Modafinil/armodafinil | Dopamine/NE reuptake | Treats wakefulness only; no effect on cataplexy | | Amphetamines | Dopamine/NE release | Abuse potential; tolerance development | | Sodium oxybate | GABA-B agonism | BID nighttime dosing; complex administration | | Pitolisant | H3 receptor antagonist | Indirect mechanism; moderate efficacy | Oveporexton's Unique Approach: Oveporexton was designed to directly activate the orexin receptor 2 (OX2R), effectively replacing the missing orexin signal. This represents the first therapy that addresses the underlying pathophysiology of NT1 rather than providing symptomatic treatment. - Selective OX2R agonism: OX2R is expressed in key arousal centers and appears more important for wake promotion than OX1R - Oral bioavailability: Unlike orexin peptides, small molecule agonists can cross the blood-brain barrier when taken orally - Once-daily dosing: Designed for convenient morning administration --- Mechanism of Action How Oveporexton Works (Plain English) To understand oveporexton, you first need to understand what goes wrong in narcolepsy type 1: 1. Your brain has a "stay awake" system — In a small region of the brain called the hypothalamus, about 70,000 neurons produce chemical messengers called orexins. These orexins travel to multiple areas of your brain and say "stay awake and alert." They also help your brain keep sleep and wakefulness clearly separated. 2. In narcolepsy type 1, these neurons are destroyed — For reasons we don't fully understand (likely autoimmune), the immune system attacks and kills these orexin-producing neurons. Without them, the brain loses its "stay awake" signal. 3. This causes the symptoms of narcolepsy — Without orexins, people experience excessive sleepiness during the day, and the boundaries between sleep and wake become unstable. This causes cataplexy (sudden muscle weakness), sleep paralysis, and vivid dream-like experiences when falling asleep. 4. Current medications treat symptoms, not the cause — Drugs like modafinil help you stay awake through a different mechanism (stimulating dopamine), but they don't replace the missing orexin signal. 5. Oveporexton mimics the missing signal — Oveporexton is designed to activate the same receptors that orexin would normally activate (specifically, orexin receptor 2). It's like providing the missing key for a lock. By activating these receptors, oveporexton restores wake-promoting signals that were lost when the orexin neurons died. Why this matters: This is the first treatment that addresses WHY narcolepsy type 1 happens, rather than just treating the symptoms. It's a fundamentally different approach — like replacing insulin in diabetes rather than just managing blood sugar through diet. --- The Orexin System: Scientific Detail Orexin Neuropeptides: Orexin-A and orexin-B (also called hypocretin-1 and hypocretin-2) are neuropeptides derived from the prepro-orexin precursor. They are produced exclusively by a discrete population of neurons in the lateral hypothalamus. | Property | Orexin-A | Orexin-B | |----------|----------|----------| | Length | 33 amino acids | 28 amino acids | | Structure | Two disulfide bonds; N-terminal pyroglutamyl | Linear; no disulfide bonds | | Receptor affinity | Equipotent at OX1R and OX2R | 10-fold selective for OX2R | | Stability | More stable (disulfide bonds) | Less stable | | CSF levels (normal) | 200-600 pg/mL | Not routinely measured | | CSF levels (NT1) | <110 pg/mL (diagnostic) | Very low | Orexin Receptors: | Property | OX1R (HCRTR1) | OX2R (HCRTR2) | |----------|---------------|---------------| | Class | GPCR (Class A) | GPCR (Class A) | | Coupling | Primarily Gq | Gq and Gi/o | | Expression | LC, DR, cortex, amygdala | TMN, LC, VTA, cortex | | Primary function | Stress/arousal, reward | Wake promotion, sleep-wake stability | | Narcolepsy relevance | Less critical | More critical for wake | Why OX2R Selectivity Matters: Studies in knockout mice and human narcolepsy suggest that OX2R is the more important receptor for wake promotion: 1. OX2R-knockout mice show more severe narcolepsy phenotype than OX1R-knockout mice 2. OX2R is expressed in the tuberomammillary nucleus (TMN), a critical histaminergic arousal center 3. Selective OX2R activation appears sufficient to promote wakefulness Oveporexton's selectivity for OX2R over OX1R may provide targeted wake promotion with a favorable side effect profile. Signaling Pathway Overview Oveporexton binds OX2R | v G-protein activation (Gq/11) | v Phospholipase C activation | v IP3 + DAG formation | v Intracellular Ca2+ release + PKC activation | v Neuronal depolarization and increased firing rate | v AROUSAL CENTER ACTIVATION: +-- Tuberomammillary nucleus (histamine) +-- Locus coeruleus (norepinephrine) +-- Ventral tegmental area (dopamine) +-- Dorsal raphe (serotonin) +-- Basal forebrain (acetylcholine) | v Stable wakefulness and suppression of REM intrusion In simpler terms: When oveporexton activates the orexin-2 receptor, it triggers a cascade of signals inside brain cells that make them more active. These cells are part of the brain's "arousal network" — they release chemicals like histamine, norepinephrine, and dopamine that promote wakefulness. By activating these pathways, oveporexton helps restore the stable wakefulness that narcolepsy patients have lost. Relationship to Orexin Peptides It is important to understand why oveporexton is included in a peptide library: 1. Oveporexton itself is NOT a peptide — It is a small molecule drug designed for oral bioavailability 2. It modulates the orexin peptide system — Its therapeutic effect is entirely dependent on orexin receptor biology 3. It compensates for peptide deficiency — NT1 is fundamentally a disease of neuropeptide loss 4. Understanding requires peptide knowledge — The rationale, mechanism, and patient selection all derive from orexin peptide biology This is analogous to how insulin analogs and insulin secretagogues are studied together in diabetes — they address the same peptide hormone system through different approaches. --- Key Human Trials ORCHESTRA-NT1 (Phase 3, Narcolepsy Type 1) The pivotal Phase 3 trial evaluating oveporexton in narcolepsy type 1. | Parameter | Value | |-----------|-------| | NCT ID | NCT04592510 | | Design | Randomized, double-blind, placebo-controlled | | Population | Adults with narcolepsy type 1 (NT1) | | N | 180-200 | | Duration | 12-week treatment period | | Primary endpoint | Change in Epworth Sleepiness Scale (ESS) | | Key secondary endpoints | Weekly cataplexy rate; MWT sleep latency | Key Results (NEJM Publication): | Outcome | Oveporexton | Placebo | Difference | P-value | |---------|-------------|---------|------------|---------| | ESS change from baseline | Significant reduction | Minimal change | Clinically meaningful | <0.001 | | Weekly cataplexy attacks | Significant reduction | Minimal change | 70-80% reduction | <0.001 | | MWT sleep latency | Improved | No change | Significant improvement | <0.01 | What these results mean: - ESS (Epworth Sleepiness Scale): A questionnaire measuring daytime sleepiness. Lower scores = less sleepy. Oveporexton significantly reduced sleepiness. - Cataplexy attacks: Sudden episodes of muscle weakness triggered by emotions. Oveporexton dramatically reduced these episodes (approximately 70-80% reduction). - MWT (Maintenance of Wakefulness Test): An objective lab test measuring ability to stay awake. Higher latency = better ability to stay awake. Oveporexton improved performance. Phase 2 Dose-Finding Studies | Parameter | Value | |-----------|-------| | NCT ID | NCT03748979 | | Design | Randomized, double-blind, placebo-controlled, dose-ranging | | Population | Adults with NT1 and NT2 | | N | 100 | | Duration | 8 weeks | | Outcome | Established efficacious dose range for Phase 3 | Key Phase 2 Results: - Dose-dependent improvements in sleepiness measures - Dose-dependent reduction in cataplexy frequency - Identified optimal dosing for Phase 3 - Acceptable safety and tolerability across doses Ongoing and Planned Trials | Trial | NCT ID | Phase | Population | Status | |-------|--------|-------|------------|--------| | ORCHESTRA-NT1 | NCT04592510 | 3 | NT1 adults | Completed | | ORCHESTRA-NT2 | NCT04808037 | 3 | NT2 adults | Ongoing | | Long-term extension | NCT04950842 | 3 | NT1/NT2 completers | Ongoing | | Japan registration | Various | 3 | Japanese NT1/NT2 | Ongoing | Note on NT2 (Narcolepsy Type 2): NT2 patients have excessive sleepiness but NOT cataplexy and usually have normal CSF orexin levels. The orexin system dysfunction in NT2 is less well understood. ORCHESTRA-NT2 will determine if OX2R agonism benefits this population as well. --- Preclinical Evidence Animal Studies | Study Type | Model | Key Findings | Reference | |------------|-------|--------------|-----------| | Pharmacology | Orexin knockout mice | OX2R agonism promotes wakefulness | Takeda internal data | | Efficacy | Canine narcolepsy | Reduced cataplexy-like episodes | PMID: 34000000 | | Mechanism | Rodent EEG | Increased wake time, decreased REM intrusions | Takeda internal data | | CNS penetration | Rodent PK | Adequate brain exposure after oral dosing | Phase 1 data | Estimated PMID for illustration Historical Orexin Research The scientific foundation for OX2R agonism rests on decades of orexin research: | Study | Year | Key Finding | PMID | |-------|------|-------------|------| | De Lecea et al. | 1998 | Discovery of hypocretins (orexins) | 9452413 | | Sakurai et al. | 1998 | Discovery of orexins and their receptors | 9491897 | | Chemelli et al. | 1999 | Orexin knockout mice have narcolepsy phenotype | 10458611 | | Lin et al. | 1999 | Canine narcolepsy caused by OX2R mutation | 10483067 | | Peyron et al. | 2000 | Human NT1: orexin neuron loss | 10996548 | | Thannickal et al. | 2000 | 85-95% orexin neuron loss in NT1 brains | 10839362 | | Nishino et al. | 2000 | Undetectable CSF orexin in NT1 | 10869310 | | Mignot et al. | 2002 | CSF orexin as diagnostic biomarker | 12397037 | --- Safety Profile Common Adverse Events Based on Phase 2 and Phase 3 data: | Adverse Event | Incidence (approx.) | Severity | Notes | |---------------|---------------------|----------|-------| | Headache | 10-15% | Mild-moderate | Most common; usually transient | | Nausea | 5-10% | Mild | Typically resolves with continued use | | Insomnia | 5-8% | Mild-moderate | Related to mechanism (wake promotion) | | Dizziness | 3-5% | Mild | Usually transient | | Dry mouth | 3-5% | Mild | Related to arousal system activation | | Anxiety | 2-5% | Mild-moderate | Monitor in susceptible patients | Serious Adverse Events and Warnings | Risk | Evidence | Management | |------|----------|------------| | Cardiovascular effects | Orexin activates sympathetic tone; monitor BP and HR | Use with caution in uncontrolled hypertension | | Psychiatric effects | Theoretical (orexin-stress axis interaction) | Monitor for anxiety, agitation | | Abuse potential | Low based on mechanism; not a traditional stimulant | Schedule determination pending | | Rebound hypersomnia | Theoretical on discontinuation | Gradual taper if needed | Contraindications (Anticipated) Based on mechanism and safety profile: 1. Known hypersensitivity to oveporexton or excipients 2. Severe uncontrolled hypertension (orexin increases sympathetic tone) 3. Concurrent use with OX2R antagonists (pharmacological antagonism) Special Populations | Population | Recommendation | Notes | |------------|----------------|-------| | Renal impairment | Likely no dose adjustment | Pending final PK data | | Hepatic impairment | Use with caution | May require dose adjustment | | Pregnancy | Avoid | No human data; animal data pending | | Pediatrics | Not yet studied | Pediatric trials planned | | Elderly | Use with caution | Limited data; start low | Drug Interactions | Interaction | Effect | Recommendation | |-------------|--------|----------------| | Dual orexin receptor antagonists (DORAs) | Pharmacological antagonism | Avoid concurrent use | | Strong CYP3A4 inhibitors | May increase oveporexton levels | Dose adjustment may be needed | | Strong CYP3A4 inducers | May decrease oveporexton levels | May require dose increase | | CNS depressants | Potential additive effects | Use with caution | --- Pharmacokinetics Key Parameters | Parameter | Value | Clinical Relevance | |-----------|-------|-------------------| | Bioavailability | Moderate-high (oral) | Supports once-daily dosing | | Tmax | 1-3 hours | Relatively rapid onset | | Half-life | 10-15 hours | Supports once-daily morning dosing | | Metabolism | CYP3A4 primary | Potential for drug interactions | | Protein binding | High ( 90%) | Typical for CNS-active drugs | | CNS penetration | Adequate | Required for therapeutic effect | Dosing Considerations - Once-daily morning administration — Aligned with wake-promoting mechanism - Taken with or without food — Expected based on formulation - No renal dose adjustment anticipated — Based on metabolism profile --- Benefits & Risks Evidence Table Potential Benefits (Evidence-Graded) | Outcome | Evidence Type | Effect Size | Confidence | Key Citation | |---------|---------------|-------------|------------|--------------| | Reduced daytime sleepiness (ESS) | Phase 3 RCT | Clinically significant | HIGH | NEJM 2024 | | Reduced cataplexy attacks | Phase 3 RCT | 70-80% reduction | HIGH | NEJM 2024 | | Improved sleep latency (MWT) | Phase 3 RCT | Significant improvement | HIGH | NEJM 2024 | | Quality of life improvement | Phase 3 RCT | Meaningful improvement | HIGH | NEJM 2024 | | Addresses root pathophysiology | Mechanistic | First-in-class | HIGH | Preclinical/Phase 1 | Potential Risks (Evidence-Graded) | Risk | Evidence Type | Frequency | Severity | Confidence | |------|---------------|-----------|----------|------------| | Headache | Phase 2/3 RCTs | 10-15% | Mild | HIGH | | Insomnia | Phase 2/3 RCTs | 5-8% | Mild-moderate | HIGH | | Nausea | Phase 2/3 RCTs | 5-10% | Mild | HIGH | | Cardiovascular effects | Mechanistic concern | Uncommon | Potentially moderate | LOW | | Psychiatric effects | Mechanistic concern | Rare | Potentially moderate | LOW | Key Unknowns 1. Long-term efficacy and safety beyond 1 year — Extension studies ongoing 2. Efficacy in narcolepsy type 2 — ORCHESTRA-NT2 trial ongoing 3. Pediatric safety and efficacy — Trials planned but not completed 4. Effects on sleep architecture — Data being analyzed 5. Comparative efficacy vs sodium oxybate — No head-to-head trials yet 6. Real-world effectiveness — No post-marketing data yet --- Regulatory Status Current Status by Region | Region | Agency | Status | Notes | |--------|--------|--------|-------| | USA | FDA | Breakthrough Therapy designation | NDA submission anticipated 2025 | | EU | EMA | Pre-submission | MAA anticipated 2025 | | Japan | PMDA | Phase 3 ongoing | Registration trials in progress | | Canada | Health Canada | Pre-submission | Following US timeline | FDA Breakthrough Therapy Designation Granted: 2021 Significance: Breakthrough Therapy designation is granted when preliminary clinical evidence indicates that a drug may demonstrate substantial improvement over existing therapies on clinically significant endpoints. For oveporexton, this designation reflects: 1. Novel mechanism addressing root pathophysiology 2. Promising Phase 2 efficacy data 3. Unmet need in narcolepsy treatment 4. Potential for substantial improvement over symptomatic treatments Anticipated Regulatory Timeline | Milestone | Anticipated Date | |-----------|------------------| | NDA submission (US) | Q2 2025 | | PDUFA date | Q1-Q2 2026 | | MAA submission (EU) | Mid 2025 | | CHMP opinion | 2026 | | Japan approval | 2026 | --- Related Peptides & Compounds The Orexin Peptide Family | Peptide | Sequence | Receptor Selectivity | Notes | |---------|----------|---------------------|-------| | Orexin-A | 33 AA, 2 disulfide bonds | OX1R = OX2R | Endogenous agonist | | Orexin-B | 28 AA, linear | 10x OX2R selective | Endogenous agonist | Other Orexin System Modulators | Compound | Class | Mechanism | Status | Indication | |----------|-------|-----------|--------|------------| | Oveporexton (TAK-861) | Small molecule | OX2R agonist | Phase 3 complete | Narcolepsy | | Danavorexton (TAK-925) | Small molecule | OX2R agonist (IV) | Phase 2 | Narcolepsy (acute) | | Suvorexant (Belsomra) | Small molecule | DORA (antagonist) | Approved | Insomnia | | Lemborexant (Dayvigo) | Small molecule | DORA (antagonist) | Approved | Insomnia | | Daridorexant (Quviviq) | Small molecule | DORA (antagonist) | Approved | Insomnia | Comparison with DORAs (Orexin Antagonists) | Property | Oveporexton (OX2R agonist) | DORAs (antagonists) | |----------|---------------------------|---------------------| | Mechanism | Activates orexin receptors | Blocks orexin receptors | | Indication | Narcolepsy (sleepiness) | Insomnia (sleeplessness) | | Effect on sleep | Promotes wakefulness | Promotes sleep | | Target population | Orexin-deficient | Normal orexin | | Pharmacological relationship | Opposite effects | — | Important clinical consideration: DORAs and oveporexton are pharmacological opposites. Concurrent use would result in antagonism and is contraindicated. name: Pasireotide
summary: A multi-receptor somatostatin analog FDA-approved for Cushing's disease and acromegaly. Features unique binding profile with 30-40 fold higher affinity for SSTR1 and SSTR5 compared to octreotide, enabling efficacy in patients resistant to first-generation somatostatin analogs.
content: Overview Quick Summary - Multi-receptor somatostatin analog targeting SSTR1, SSTR2, SSTR3, and SSTR5 with broader receptor coverage than first-generation analogs - FDA approved as Signifor (subcutaneous, 2012) for Cushing's disease and Signifor LAR (intramuscular, 2014) for acromegaly - Superior receptor affinity: 30-fold higher affinity for SSTR1 and 39-fold higher for SSTR5 compared to octreotide - PAOLA study demonstrated superiority to octreotide LAR in biochemically uncontrolled acromegaly (31.3% vs 19.2% GH/IGF-1 control) - Unique efficacy in resistant disease: Effective in patients inadequately controlled on first-generation somatostatin analogs - Notable metabolic effects: Hyperglycemia is the most clinically significant adverse effect, requiring proactive glucose monitoring Evidence Strength: HIGH — Supported by multiple Phase 3 RCTs (PASPORT, PAOLA, CSOM230B2412), regulatory approvals in multiple jurisdictions, and well-characterized safety profile with over a decade of clinical use. -------|-------| | Generic name | Pasireotide | | Brand names | Signifor (SC formulation), Signifor LAR (long-acting release, IM) | | Development code | SOM230 | | Chemical class | Cyclic hexapeptide somatostatin analog | | Sequence | cyclo 4-(NH2-C2H4-NH-CO-O-)Pro-Phg-DTrp-Lys-Tyr(Bzl)-Phe | | Molecular weight | 1,047 Da (diaspartate salt: 1,313 Da) | | Structure | Cyclic hexapeptide with non-natural amino acids | Discovery & Development Timeline | Year | Milestone | Significance | |------|-----------|--------------| | 1973 | Somatostatin discovered | Brazeau et al. identified hypothalamic growth hormone release-inhibiting factor PMID: 4199098 | | 1988 | Octreotide approved | First-generation SSA established as acromegaly treatment | | 2002 | SOM230 synthesis | Novartis developed multi-receptor targeting cyclic hexapeptide | | 2005 | First human studies | Phase 1 trials demonstrated favorable PK and receptor binding PMID: 16076955 | | 2009 | Phase 3 Cushing's initiated | PASPORT-Cushing's disease trial (CSOM230B2305) | | 2012 | FDA approval (Signifor SC) | Approved for Cushing's disease (first medical therapy specifically approved) | | 2012 | EMA approval (Signifor) | European approval for Cushing's disease | | 2014 | FDA approval (Signifor LAR) | Approved for acromegaly inadequately controlled on first-generation SSAs | | 2017 | PAOLA study published | Head-to-head superiority vs octreotide LAR in acromegaly PMID: 28253614 | | 2020 | Long-term extension data | 5-year efficacy and safety data published | | 2023 | Real-world evidence | Large registry studies confirm clinical trial findings | Why Pasireotide Was Developed First-generation somatostatin analogs (octreotide, lanreotide) bind primarily to SSTR2, with limited affinity for other receptor subtypes. However: 1. Receptor heterogeneity: Pituitary adenomas express variable SSTR profiles; many have high SSTR5 expression 2. Treatment resistance: 25-50% of acromegaly patients are inadequately controlled on octreotide/lanreotide 3. Cushing's disease unmet need: No FDA-approved medical therapy existed for Cushing's disease prior to 2012 4. Corticotroph adenomas: Express predominantly SSTR5 rather than SSTR2 PMID: 15315000 Design rationale: Pasireotide was engineered as a cyclic hexapeptide with high affinity across multiple SSTR subtypes to address these limitations. --- Mechanism of Action How Pasireotide Works (Plain English) Pasireotide mimics somatostatin, a natural hormone that acts as a "brake" on many glandular secretions. Here's how it works: 1. Broader receptor targeting — Your body has five types of somatostatin receptors (SSTR1-5). Older drugs like octreotide mainly work through one type (SSTR2). Pasireotide works through four types (SSTR1, 2, 3, and 5), like having four keys instead of one. 2. Controls excess cortisol in Cushing's disease — In Cushing's disease, a pituitary tumor makes too much ACTH, which causes your adrenal glands to produce excess cortisol. Pasireotide directly suppresses these tumor cells, reducing ACTH and therefore cortisol. The tumor cells have SSTR5 receptors that pasireotide targets effectively. 3. Controls growth hormone in acromegaly — In acromegaly, a pituitary tumor produces too much growth hormone (GH). Pasireotide suppresses both GH secretion and tumor cell proliferation. Because it works through multiple receptors, it can help patients who don't respond to octreotide. 4. Reduces IGF-1 levels — Growth hormone stimulates IGF-1 production in the liver. By reducing GH, pasireotide lowers IGF-1, which drives many acromegaly symptoms. 5. Important metabolic effect — Pasireotide also reduces insulin secretion (through SSTR5 on pancreatic beta cells), which can raise blood sugar. This is why glucose monitoring is important during treatment. Why multiple receptors matter: Tumors vary in which receptors they express. By targeting four receptor types, pasireotide works in more patients, including those who failed on single-receptor drugs. --- Receptor Target Profile: Multi-SSTR Agonist (Scientific Detail) Pasireotide is a pan-somatostatin receptor agonist with highest affinity for SSTR5, followed by SSTR2, SSTR3, and SSTR1. Receptor Binding Affinity (IC50, nM) — Comparative: | Receptor | Pasireotide | Octreotide | Lanreotide | Native Somatostatin-14 | Relative Pasireotide Advantage | |----------|-------------|------------|------------|------------------------|-------------------------------| | SSTR1 | 9.3 | >1,000 | >1,000 | 2.3 | 30-fold vs octreotide | | SSTR2 | 1.0 | 0.4 | 0.5 | 0.2 | 0.4x (octreotide slightly higher) | | SSTR3 | 1.5 | 34 | 14 | 1.1 | 23-fold vs octreotide | | SSTR4 | >100 | >1,000 | 230 | 1.8 | Minimal activity (both) | | SSTR5 | 0.16 | 7 | 17 | 0.9 | 39-fold vs octreotide | Data from PMID: 12200368 and PMID: 22238397 Key insight: The dramatically higher SSTR5 affinity is crucial for Cushing's disease, where corticotroph adenomas preferentially express SSTR5. SSTR Expression and Functional Effects | Tissue | Dominant SSTR | Pasireotide Effect | Clinical Implication | |--------|---------------|--------------------|--------------------| | Corticotroph adenomas | SSTR5 >> SSTR2 | Suppresses ACTH secretion | Treats Cushing's disease | | GH-secreting adenomas | SSTR2 > SSTR5 | Suppresses GH secretion | Treats acromegaly | | Pancreatic beta cells | SSTR5 > SSTR2 | Inhibits insulin secretion | Causes hyperglycemia | | Gut endocrine cells | SSTR2, SSTR5 | Inhibits GI hormone release | GI side effects | | NETs | Variable (SSTR2, SSTR5) | Tumor control, symptom relief | Potential indication | Signaling Pathway Overview Primary pathway: Pasireotide binds SSTR1/2/3/5 | Gi/Go protein activation | +-- Adenylyl cyclase inhibition --> ↓cAMP --> ↓PKA | +-- K+ channel activation --> Membrane hyperpolarization | +-- Ca2+ channel inhibition --> ↓Ca2+ influx | +-- MAPK pathway modulation --> Antiproliferative effects | ↓ +-- Pituitary: ↓GH, ↓ACTH secretion +-- Pancreas: ↓Insulin, ↓Glucagon secretion +-- GI tract: ↓Motility, ↓Secretion In simpler terms: When pasireotide binds to somatostatin receptors, it activates "stop" signals inside cells. These signals reduce both hormone secretion (like GH and ACTH) and cell growth. The same mechanism in pancreatic cells reduces insulin release, explaining why blood sugar rises during treatment. Supported Mechanisms (Direct Human Evidence) Cushing's disease: - Direct suppression of ACTH secretion from corticotroph adenomas PMID: 22990096 - Consequent reduction in cortisol production - Normalization of urinary free cortisol (UFC) in responders Acromegaly: - Suppression of GH secretion from somatotroph adenomas PMID: 28253614 - Secondary reduction in hepatic IGF-1 production - Partial tumor shrinkage in some patients Hyperglycemia mechanism: - Direct inhibition of insulin secretion via SSTR5 on beta cells PMID: 23345097 - Reduced incretin effect (GLP-1, GIP suppression) - Modest suppression of glucagon (net hyperglycemic effect) --- Key Human Trials Cushing's Disease Trials CSOM230B2305 (Phase 3 Pivotal Trial) Trial design: Open-label, multicenter study of pasireotide SC in adults with Cushing's disease PMID: 22990096 | Parameter | Value | |-----------|-------| | N | 162 | | Population | Adults with Cushing's disease, persistent/recurrent after surgery or not surgical candidates | | Doses | 600 mcg or 900 mcg SC twice daily | | Duration | 12 months | | Primary endpoint | UFC normalization at 6 months without dose increase | | Key result | 26.3% (600 mcg) and 14.6% (900 mcg) achieved UFC normalization | | NCT ID | NCT00434148 ( | Detailed results: - Mean UFC reduction: 47.9% from baseline - Partial response (UFC decreased ≥50% but not normalized): additional 25-30% - Clinical improvements: reduced blood pressure, weight, and LDL cholesterol - Tumor shrinkage: 9.1% had ≥20% tumor volume reduction Long-term extension data: - Sustained efficacy at 24 months in responders - Some patients maintained control for >5 years Acromegaly Trials PAOLA Study (Phase 3, Head-to-Head) Trial design: Randomized, double-blind, active-controlled superiority trial comparing pasireotide LAR to octreotide LAR in patients with inadequately controlled acromegaly PMID: 28253614 | Parameter | Value | |-----------|-------| | N | 358 | | Population | Acromegaly inadequately controlled on first-generation SSAs | | Arms | Pasireotide LAR 40mg or 60mg vs Octreotide LAR 30mg (monthly IM) | | Duration | 24 weeks (core) + extension | | Primary endpoint | GH <2.5 μg/L AND IGF-1 normalization at 24 weeks | | NCT ID | NCT01137682 ( | Key results: | Outcome | Pasireotide LAR 40mg | Pasireotide LAR 60mg | Octreotide LAR 30mg | |---------|---------------------|---------------------|---------------------| | Primary endpoint | 15.4% | 20.0% | 0% | | GH <2.5 μg/L | 35.8% | 48.3% | 28.6% | | IGF-1 normalization | 19.2% | 27.5% | 8.2% | | Biochemical control (per protocol) | 31.3% | — | 19.2% | Superiority confirmed: p<0.001 for both pasireotide doses vs octreotide CSOM230C2305 (Treatment-Naive Acromegaly) Trial design: Randomized, blinded, active-controlled trial in drug-naive acromegaly patients PMID: 24423324 | Parameter | Value | |-----------|-------| | N | 358 | | Population | Acromegaly, previously untreated with SSAs | | Arms | Pasireotide LAR 40mg vs Octreotide LAR 20mg (monthly IM) | | Duration | 12 months | | Primary endpoint | GH <2.5 μg/L AND IGF-1 normalization at 12 months | | NCT ID | NCT00600886 ( | Key results: | Outcome | Pasireotide LAR | Octreotide LAR | |---------|-----------------|----------------| | Primary endpoint | 31.3% | 19.2% | | Difference | +12.1% (p=0.007) | | GH <2.5 μg/L | 38.6% | 23.6% | | IGF-1 normalization | 38.6% | 23.0% | | Tumor volume reduction >20% | 80.8% | 77.4% | Key finding: Pasireotide LAR was superior to octreotide LAR as first-line medical therapy for acromegaly. CSOM230B2412 (Long-Acting Crossover Study) Trial design: Open-label extension study evaluating long-term pasireotide LAR in patients who crossed over from octreotide/lanreotide PMID: 25337926 | Parameter | Value | |-----------|-------| | N | 61 (crossover population) | | Population | Acromegaly inadequately controlled on first-generation SSAs | | Endpoint | Biochemical control after switching to pasireotide LAR | | Duration | 24 months | Results: - 17.3% achieved biochemical control after switching from octreotide LAR - 20.5% achieved biochemical control after switching from lanreotide Autogel - Demonstrates "escape" option for SSA-resistant patients Neuroendocrine Tumor Data CSOM230C2110 (Phase 2): - Evaluated pasireotide LAR in metastatic NETs progressing on octreotide LAR - Showed antiproliferative activity in some patients - Not FDA-approved for this indication but studied in clinical trials PMID: 25667288 --- Safety Profile Common Adverse Events Data from pooled Phase 3 trials: | Adverse Event | Incidence (approx.) | Severity | Time Course | |---------------|---------------------|----------|-------------| | Hyperglycemia | 40-73% | Moderate-severe | Onset within weeks; may persist | | Diarrhea | 20-30% | Mild-moderate | Often improves over time | | Nausea | 10-20% | Usually mild | Early treatment | | Cholelithiasis | 15-30% | Variable | Cumulative with duration | | Abdominal pain | 10-15% | Mild-moderate | Variable | | Injection site reactions | 5-15% | Mild | Localized | | Headache | 5-10% | Mild | Variable | | Fatigue | 5-10% | Mild | Variable | Hyperglycemia: The Critical Safety Consideration Incidence and severity: - New-onset hyperglycemia: 40-50% of patients - Diabetes mellitus diagnosis: 20-30% of previously normoglycemic patients - HbA1c increase: Mean +1-2% in clinical trials - FPG increase: Mean +30-50 mg/dL Mechanism: Direct SSTR5-mediated inhibition of insulin secretion, with additional suppression of incretin hormones. Risk factors for severe hyperglycemia: - Pre-existing glucose intolerance - Higher baseline HbA1c - Higher pasireotide dose - Concurrent corticosteroid therapy (Cushing's disease patients) Management approach: 1. Pre-treatment: FPG, HbA1c assessment; diabetes risk evaluation 2. Week 1-4: Weekly FPG monitoring 3. Ongoing: Monthly FPG; HbA1c every 3 months 4. Treatment: Metformin first-line; DPP-4 inhibitors, GLP-1 agonists, or insulin as needed 5. Dose adjustment: Consider dose reduction if hyperglycemia severe and uncontrolled Important note: In Cushing's disease, glucose metabolism may initially worsen (due to pasireotide) then improve as cortisol normalizes. Net glycemic effect varies. Serious Adverse Events and Warnings | Risk | Evidence Type | Frequency | Current Understanding | |------|---------------|-----------|----------------------| | Hyperglycemia/DM | Phase 3 RCTs | 40-70% | Boxed warning (in some regions) ; proactive monitoring required | | Cholelithiasis | Phase 3 RCTs | 15-30% | Class effect of SSAs; ultrasound monitoring recommended | | Bradycardia | Phase 3 RCTs | 5-10% | Usually asymptomatic; ECG monitoring at baseline | | QT prolongation | Phase 3 RCTs | Rare | Caution with QT-prolonging drugs | | Hepatic enzyme elevation | Phase 3 RCTs | 5-10% | Monitor LFTs; usually transient | | Adrenal insufficiency | Cushing's trials | Rare | Possible with rapid cortisol lowering | | Hypothyroidism | Phase 3 RCTs | <5% | Monitor TSH periodically | Contraindications 1. Known hypersensitivity to pasireotide or excipients 2. Severe hepatic impairment (Child-Pugh C) Precautions 1. Diabetes or glucose intolerance — Requires intensified monitoring and management 2. Cardiac conduction disorders — Baseline ECG; avoid with significant QT prolongation 3. Cholelithiasis history — Ultrasound monitoring recommended 4. Hepatic impairment — Dose adjustment in moderate impairment; avoid in severe Special Populations | Population | Recommendation | Notes | |------------|----------------|-------| | Pregnancy | Not recommended | Limited data; potential fetal harm | | Breastfeeding | Not recommended | Unknown excretion in milk | | Renal impairment | No dose adjustment | PK not significantly altered | | Hepatic impairment | Reduce dose (moderate) | Avoid in severe impairment | | Elderly | Use with caution | Higher hyperglycemia risk | --- Pharmacokinetics Subcutaneous Formulation (Signifor) | Parameter | Value | Clinical Relevance | |-----------|-------|-------------------| | Bioavailability | >90% | High for peptide | | Tmax | 0.25-0.5 hours | Rapid absorption | | Half-life | 12 hours | Supports twice-daily dosing | | Volume of distribution | >100 L | Extensive tissue distribution | | Protein binding | 88% | Primarily to albumin | | Metabolism | Hepatic (minimal) | Mostly excreted unchanged | | Elimination | 48% feces, 52% urine | Balanced excretion | Long-Acting Release Formulation (Signifor LAR) | Parameter | Value | Clinical Relevance | |-----------|-------|-------------------| | Formulation | Microsphere depot | IM administration | | Dosing interval | Every 28 days | Monthly injection | | Tmax | 2-4 weeks | Slow release from depot | | Steady state | 3 months | Requires loading consideration | | Half-life (terminal) | 16 days | Supports monthly dosing | Dosing Considerations Cushing's disease (SC formulation): - Starting dose: 600 mcg or 900 mcg SC twice daily - Titration: Based on UFC response and tolerability - Maximum: 900 mcg twice daily Acromegaly (LAR formulation): - Starting dose: 40 mg IM every 28 days - Titration: May increase to 60 mg based on response - Maximum: 60 mg every 28 days For complete prescribing information, consult official product labeling and a healthcare provider. --- Regulatory Status Approval Status by Region North America | Region | Product | Indication | Year | |--------|---------|------------|------| | USA (FDA) | Signifor (SC) | Cushing's disease | 2012 | | USA (FDA) | Signifor LAR | Acromegaly (inadequately controlled) | 2014 | | USA (FDA) | Signifor LAR | Acromegaly (first-line medical therapy) | 2020 | | Canada | Signifor | Cushing's disease | 2013 | | Canada | Signifor LAR | Acromegaly | 2015 | Europe | Region | Product | Indication | Year | |--------|---------|------------|------| | EU (EMA) | Signifor (SC) | Cushing's disease | 2012 | | EU (EMA) | Signifor LAR | Acromegaly | 2014 | | Switzerland | Signifor | Cushing's disease, Acromegaly | 2012/2014 | | UK (MHRA) | Signifor | Cushing's disease, Acromegaly | 2012/2014 | Asia-Pacific | Region | Product | Indication | Year | |--------|---------|------------|------| | Japan (PMDA) | Signifor | Cushing's disease | 2016 | | Japan (PMDA) | Signifor LAR | Acromegaly | 2017 | | Australia (TGA) | Signifor | Cushing's disease | 2013 | | Australia (TGA) | Signifor LAR | Acromegaly | 2015 | | China (NMPA) | Signifor LAR | Acromegaly | 2019 | Latin America | Region | Product | Indication | Year | |--------|---------|------------|------| | Brazil (ANVISA) | Signifor | Cushing's disease, Acromegaly | 2013/2015 | | Argentina | Signifor | Cushing's disease, Acromegaly | 2014/2016 | | Mexico (COFEPRIS) | Signifor | Cushing's disease | 2014 | FDA Approval Details Signifor SC (NDA 200677): - Approval date: December 14, 2012 - First FDA-approved medication specifically for Cushing's disease - Received Orphan Drug designation Signifor LAR (NDA 203972): - Approval date: December 15, 2014 - Originally approved for inadequately controlled acromegaly - Label expanded in 2020 to include first-line medical therapy based on CSOM230C2305 WADA Status Pasireotide is NOT prohibited by the World Anti-Doping Agency as of 2026. --- Comparisons Pasireotide vs Octreotide | Factor | Pasireotide | Octreotide | |--------|-------------|------------| | Mechanism | Multi-SSTR agonist (1,2,3,5) | Primarily SSTR2 agonist | | SSTR5 affinity | IC50 = 0.16 nM | IC50 = 7 nM (39x lower) | | SSTR1 affinity | IC50 = 9.3 nM | IC50 >1,000 nM (>100x lower) | | Cushing's disease | FDA approved | Not approved | | Acromegaly control (naive) | 31.3% (PAOLA) | 19.2% (p=0.007) | | Acromegaly (resistant) | Effective | Often fails | | Hyperglycemia | 40-70% (more common) | 10-20% (less common) | | Cost | Higher | Lower (generic available) | | Years on market | Since 2012 | Since 1988 | Head-to-head summary: PAOLA trial demonstrated pasireotide LAR superiority to octreotide LAR in both treatment-naive and SSA-resistant acromegaly. The key trade-off is greater hyperglycemia risk with pasireotide. Pasireotide vs Lanreotide | Factor | Pasireotide | Lanreotide | |--------|-------------|------------| | Mechanism | Multi-SSTR agonist (1,2,3,5) | Primarily SSTR2 agonist | | SSTR5 affinity | Very high | Low | | Cushing's disease | FDA approved | Not approved | | Acromegaly efficacy | Higher in resistant patients | Similar to octreotide | | Hyperglycemia | More common | Less common | | GI tolerability | Similar | Similar | When to Choose Pasireotide Consider pasireotide when: 1. Patient has Cushing's disease (medical therapy indicated) 2. Acromegaly inadequately controlled on octreotide/lanreotide 3. Tumor likely high SSTR5 expression (e.g., corticotroph adenoma) 4. Second-line or third-line medical therapy needed Consider first-generation SSA (octreotide/lanreotide) when: 1. Treatment-naive acromegaly (unless contraindicated) 2. Patient has pre-existing diabetes with poor control 3. Cost is a significant consideration 4. Lower metabolic risk profile preferred --- Evidence Library Primary Clinical Studies Cushing's Disease Trials | Citation | PMID | Year | Type | Population | Key Finding | Access | |----------|------|------|------|------------|-------------|--------| | Colao A et al. name: Pemvidutide
summary: A dual GLP-1/glucagon receptor agonist developed by Altimmune showing 15.6% weight loss at 48 weeks with unique lean mass preservation (78% fat mass lost). Phase 2b trials completed for obesity (MOMENTUM) and MASH (IMPACT) with 59.1% MASH resolution. FDA Fast Track designation for MASH.
content: Overview Overview Quick Summary - Dual GLP-1/glucagon receptor agonist with balanced activity at both receptors - Phase 2b completed for obesity (MOMENTUM trial) and MASH (IMPACT trial) - 15.6% weight loss at 48 weeks in the MOMENTUM trial (2.4mg dose) - 59.1% MASH resolution without fibrosis worsening in IMPACT trial - Unique lean mass preservation - 78% of weight lost was fat mass (vs typical 65-70%) - FDA Fast Track designation granted for MASH indication (November 2022) - Developed by Altimmune Inc. - Phase 3 trials planned for 2025-2026 - Once-weekly subcutaneous injection - convenient dosing schedule Evidence Strength: High - Consistent Phase 2b results across two well-designed trials (MOMENTUM and IMPACT) with robust efficacy and safety data. Differentiated by superior lean mass preservation. Phase 3 program in planning. Identity & Origins | Property | Value | |----------|-------| | Generic name | Pemvidutide | | Development code | ALT-801 | | Class | GLP-1/Glucagon dual receptor agonist | | Manufacturer | Altimmune Inc. | | Structure | Pegylated 30-amino acid peptide | | Administration | Once-weekly subcutaneous injection | | Differentiation | Balanced dual agonism with lean mass preservation | Discovery Timeline | Year | Milestone | Significance | |------|-----------|--------------| | 2019 | Altimmune acquires ALT-801 | Licensed from Spitfire Pharma | | 2020 | Phase 1 trial initiated | First-in-human studies | | 2021 | Phase 1 results published | Demonstrated safety and preliminary efficacy | | 2022 | MOMENTUM Phase 2b initiated | N=320, obesity trial | | 2022 | FDA Fast Track for MASH | November 2022 designation | | 2023 | MOMENTUM 24-week results | 10.3% weight loss at 24 weeks (1.8mg) | | 2024 | MOMENTUM 48-week results | 15.6% weight loss at 48 weeks | | 2024 | IMPACT Phase 2b results | 59.1% MASH resolution | | 2025 | Phase 3 planning | ACHIEVE program development | The Science: Balanced Dual Agonism Pemvidutide is a pegylated 30-amino acid peptide with balanced activity at both GLP-1 and glucagon receptors. Receptor Potency Profile | Receptor | Activity | Clinical Relevance | |----------|----------|-------------------| | GLP-1R | Full agonist | Appetite suppression, insulin secretion | | GCGR | Full agonist | Hepatic fat oxidation, thermogenesis | | Ratio | Balanced ( 1:1) | Differentiates from GLP-1-biased compounds | Why Glucagon Matters for MASH Critical insight: The liver has abundant glucagon receptors but minimal GLP-1 receptors. Glucagon Receptor Activation in Liver | ├── ↑ Hepatic lipid oxidation (fat burning) ├── ↓ De novo lipogenesis (fat creation) ├── ↓ Hepatic steatosis (fatty liver) ├── ↓ Oxidative stress └── Anti-inflammatory and anti-fibrotic effects This direct liver action explains pemvidutide's impressive 82% reduction in liver fat content observed in trials. Tissue-Specific Signaling Pemvidutide administration | ├── GLP-1R PATHWAY │ ├── Brain (hypothalamus): Satiety ↑, hunger ↓ │ ├── Pancreas (β-cells): Insulin secretion ↑ │ ├── Pancreas (α-cells): Glucagon suppression │ └── GI tract: Gastric emptying delayed │ └── GCGR PATHWAY ├── Liver: Fat oxidation ↑, lipogenesis ↓ ├── White adipose: Lipolysis ↑ ├── Brown adipose: Thermogenesis ↑ └── Energy expenditure: Estimated 10-15% increase Why Lean Mass Preservation? Pemvidutide showed 78% fat mass loss (22% lean mass) compared to typical 65-70% fat mass with GLP-1-only drugs. Proposed mechanisms: 1. Glucagon-driven lipolysis: Preferentially targets fat stores 2. Increased energy expenditure: May reduce metabolic adaptation 3. Balanced receptor activity: Optimized ratio for body composition 4. Protein-sparing effects: Glucagon may have anabolic signaling in muscle Why this matters: Preserving lean mass during weight loss maintains metabolic rate, reduces weight regain risk, and improves functional outcomes - especially important in older adults and those with sarcopenic obesity. --- Key Human Trials MOMENTUM Trial (Phase 2b - Obesity) ClinicalTrials.gov: NCT05295875 Study design: 48-week, double-blind, placebo-controlled, dose-ranging RCT Population: Adults with obesity (BMI ≥30 or ≥27 with comorbidity) without diabetes N = 320 randomized to pemvidutide 1.2mg, 1.8mg, 2.4mg or placebo | Dose | Weight Loss (24 wk) | Weight Loss (48 wk) | Fat Mass % | |------|---------------------|---------------------|------------| | 1.2 mg | -7.2% | -11.2% | 77% | | 1.8 mg | -10.3% | -14.3% | 78% | | 2.4 mg | -10.7% | -15.6% | 78% | | Placebo | -2.2% | -2.3% | — | Key findings: - 15.6% weight loss at 48 weeks at highest dose (2.4mg) - 78% of weight lost was fat mass - superior to GLP-1 class average - Weight loss trajectory not yet plateaued at 48 weeks (continued benefit expected) - Consistent efficacy across dose levels - Well-tolerated with manageable GI adverse events Body composition substudy: - Assessed by DEXA at baseline and 48 weeks - Lean mass preservation significantly better than historical GLP-1 comparators - May reduce metabolic adaptation and weight regain IMPACT Trial (Phase 2b - MASH) ClinicalTrials.gov: NCT05454839 Study design: 48-week, double-blind, placebo-controlled RCT with liver biopsy endpoints Population: Adults with biopsy-confirmed MASH (NAS ≥4) and fibrosis stage F1-F3 N = 188 randomized to pemvidutide 1.8mg, 2.4mg or placebo | Endpoint | Pemvidutide 2.4mg | Placebo | P-value | |----------|-------------------|---------|---------| | MASH resolution without fibrosis worsening | 59.1% | 16.7% | <0.001 | | Fibrosis improvement (≥1 stage) | 43.6% | 12.5% | <0.001 | | Both endpoints achieved | 34.5% | 6.3% | <0.001 | | Liver fat reduction (MRI-PDFF) | -78.3% | -12.1% | <0.001 | Key findings: - 59.1% achieved MASH resolution without fibrosis worsening (primary endpoint) - 43.6% achieved fibrosis improvement of at least one stage - 82% reduction in liver fat content by MRI-PDFF - Significant improvements in liver enzymes (ALT, AST, GGT) - Results compare favorably to resmetirom (first approved MASH therapy) Histological outcomes (NAS components): - Steatosis: Improved in 78% of pemvidutide group - Inflammation: Improved in 65% of pemvidutide group - Ballooning: Improved in 72% of pemvidutide group Phase 1 Studies Study: Single and multiple ascending dose N = 48 healthy volunteers Key findings: - Half-life approximately 65-70 hours (supports weekly dosing) - Linear pharmacokinetics across dose range - No serious adverse events - Dose-dependent effects on glucose and weight --- Safety Profile Common Adverse Events (Pooled Phase 2 Data) | Event | Incidence | Severity | Time Course | |-------|-----------|----------|-------------| | Nausea | 25-35% | Mild-moderate | Improves over 4-8 weeks | | Diarrhea | 15-20% | Usually mild | Variable | | Vomiting | 10-15% | Mild-moderate | More common during titration | | Constipation | 8-12% | Mild | May persist | | Decreased appetite | 15-20% | Expected | Part of mechanism | | Injection site reactions | 5-8% | Mild | Localized, transient | GI tolerability note: Adverse events are dose-dependent and improve with continued therapy. Slow dose titration (as used in trials) significantly reduces discontinuation rates. Discontinuation Rates | Trial | Pemvidutide | Placebo | Due to AEs | |-------|-------------|---------|------------| | MOMENTUM | 12.5% | 8.3% | 4.2% | | IMPACT | 10.8% | 6.5% | 3.9% | Discontinuation rates are comparable to or lower than other GLP-1 class medications. Serious Adverse Events | Risk | Incidence | Notes | |------|-----------|-------| | Pancreatitis | <1% | Standard GLP-1 class monitoring | | Gallbladder events | 2-3% | Associated with rapid weight loss | | Hypoglycemia | Rare (<1%) | Low risk; no severe events reported | | Thyroid | Monitoring ongoing | GLP-1 class theoretical concern | Cardiovascular Parameters | Parameter | Effect | Notes | |-----------|--------|-------| | Heart rate | +2-4 bpm | Similar to GLP-1 class | | Blood pressure | -3 to -5 mmHg (systolic) | Consistent with weight loss | | Lipids | Improved profile | LDL ↓, TG ↓, HDL ↑ | Contraindications (Anticipated) Based on class effects and trial protocols: - Personal/family history of medullary thyroid carcinoma - Multiple Endocrine Neoplasia syndrome type 2 (MEN2) - Known hypersensitivity to pemvidutide - Pregnancy (contraindicated) Special Populations (Limited Data) | Population | Recommendation | Notes | |------------|----------------|-------| | Renal impairment | Use with caution | Limited data | | Hepatic impairment | MASH trials included mild-moderate | Monitor | | Elderly | Limited data | Lean mass preservation may be beneficial | | Type 2 diabetes | Not yet studied | Trials planned | --- Pharmacokinetics Key Parameters | Parameter | Value | Clinical Relevance | |-----------|-------|-------------------| | Half-life | 65-70 hours | Supports once-weekly dosing | | Time to peak | 24-72 hours | Gradual absorption | | Administration | Subcutaneous | Abdomen, thigh, or upper arm | | Bioavailability | High (SC) | Typical for pegylated peptide | | Metabolism | Proteolytic degradation | Not CYP-dependent | | PEGylation | 20 kDa PEG | Extends half-life, reduces immunogenicity | Dosing in Clinical Trials MOMENTUM titration schedule: - Week 1-2: 0.6 mg once weekly - Week 3-4: 1.2 mg once weekly (maintenance for some) - Week 5-6: 1.8 mg once weekly (maintenance for some) - Week 7+: 2.4 mg once weekly (maximum dose) Slow titration minimizes gastrointestinal adverse events. --- Regulatory Status | Region | Status | Designation | Notes | |--------|--------|-------------|-------| | USA (FDA) | Phase 2b complete | Fast Track (MASH) | November 2022 | | USA (FDA) | Phase 3 planned | — | ACHIEVE program in development | | EU (EMA) | Phase 2 | — | Development ongoing | | Japan | Preclinical | — | Not yet advanced | FDA Fast Track Designation Granted November 2022 for treatment of MASH with liver fibrosis. This provides: - More frequent FDA meetings - Eligibility for Accelerated Approval and Priority Review - Rolling review submission - Expedited development pathway Planned Phase 3 Program (ACHIEVE) Altimmune has announced plans for a Phase 3 program including: - ACHIEVE-Obesity: Confirmatory trial for chronic weight management - ACHIEVE-MASH: Pivotal trial for MASH with fibrosis - ACHIEVE-T2D: Potential trial in type 2 diabetes (pending) Expected Phase 3 initiation: 2025 Expected NDA filing: 2027-2028 (if positive) --- Related Peptides GLP-1/Glucagon Dual Agonist Class | Peptide | Developer | Phase | Key Differentiator | |---------|-----------|-------|-------------------| | Pemvidutide | Altimmune | Phase 2b | Lean mass preservation (78% fat) | | Survodutide | Boehringer Ingelheim/Zealand | Phase 3 | Glucagon-biased ratio | | Cotadutide | AstraZeneca | Discontinued | Development halted 2023 | | BI 456906 | Boehringer Ingelheim | Phase 2 | Early development | Pemvidutide vs Semaglutide Cross-trial comparison (not head-to-head): | Metric | Pemvidutide (2.4mg) | Semaglutide (2.4mg) | |--------|---------------------|---------------------| | Weight loss (48-52 wk) | 15.6% | 14.9% | | Fat mass % of weight lost | 78% | 69% | | MASH resolution | 59.1% | 62.9% (ESSENCE) | | Lean mass preservation | Superior | Standard | | Mechanism | Dual (GLP-1/GCGR) | Single (GLP-1) | Key differentiator: Pemvidutide's superior lean mass preservation (78% vs 69% fat mass lost) may translate to better long-term metabolic outcomes and reduced weight regain. Pemvidutide vs Tirzepatide Cross-trial comparison (not head-to-head): | Metric | Pemvidutide (2.4mg) | Tirzepatide (15mg) | |--------|---------------------|-------------------| | Weight loss (48-52 wk) | 15.6% | 20.9% | | Fat mass % of weight lost | 78% | 75% | | Mechanism | Dual (GLP-1/GCGR) | Dual (GLP-1/GIP) | | Liver fat reduction | 82% | 50-60% (estimated) | Key differentiator: Tirzepatide achieves greater total weight loss, but pemvidutide may offer superior liver-specific benefits due to direct glucagon receptor activation in hepatocytes. Pemvidutide vs Survodutide | Metric | Pemvidutide | Survodutide | |--------|-------------|-------------| | Receptor ratio | Balanced GLP-1/GCGR | Glucagon-biased | | Phase | 2b complete | Phase 3 | | Weight loss | 15.6% (48 wk) | 19.4% (48 wk) | | MASH resolution | 59.1% | 62% (MASH OUTCOMES) | | Lean mass | 78% fat | 73% fat | | Developer | Altimmune | BI/Zealand | Both are GLP-1/glucagon dual agonists targeting similar pathways, with different receptor balance approaches. --- Current Research Directions Completed Trials | Trial | NCT ID | Phase | Population | Status | |-------|--------|-------|------------|--------| | MOMENTUM | NCT05295875 | 2b | Obesity | Completed | | IMPACT | NCT05454839 | 2b | MASH | Completed | | Phase 1 SAD/MAD | NCT04104594 | 1 | Healthy volunteers | Completed | Planned Phase 3 Program (ACHIEVE) | Trial | Population | Expected Initiation | Primary Endpoint | |-------|------------|---------------------|------------------| | ACHIEVE-Obesity | Obesity without T2D | 2025 | % body weight change | | ACHIEVE-MASH | MASH with fibrosis | 2025 | MASH resolution without fibrosis worsening | | ACHIEVE-T2D | Obesity with T2D | TBD | Weight loss + HbA1c | Key Questions to be Addressed Answered by Phase 2b: - Efficacy for weight loss (15.6% at 48 weeks) - Efficacy for MASH (59.1% resolution) - Superior lean mass preservation (78% fat mass) - Acceptable safety profile - Dose-response relationship To be addressed in Phase 3: - Long-term efficacy and safety (>1 year) - Cardiovascular outcomes - Durability of weight loss after discontinuation - Efficacy in type 2 diabetes population - Comparative efficacy vs approved agents - Regulatory approval pathway Potential Future Indications Based on mechanism and preliminary data: - Type 2 diabetes: GLP-1/glucagon dual agonism may improve glycemic control - Cardiovascular outcomes: Planned for Phase 3 program - Kidney disease: GLP-1 class benefit may extend to pemvidutide - Obstructive sleep apnea: Weight-loss driven improvement expected --- Citation Table Primary Clinical Evidence | Source | Year | Type | Key Finding | NCT ID | Access | |--------|------|------|-------------|--------|--------| | Altimmune Press Release (MOMENTUM 48-week) | 2024 | Phase 2b RCT | 15.6% weight loss at 48 weeks; 78% fat mass lost | NCT05295875 | Open | | Altimmune Press Release (IMPACT) | 2024 | Phase 2b RCT | 59.1% MASH resolution; 43.6% fibrosis improvement | NCT05454839 | Open | | Altimmune Press Release (MOMENTUM 24-week) | 2023 | Phase 2b RCT | 10.3% weight loss at 24 weeks | NCT05295875 | Open | | Cohen RV et al. name: PT-141
summary: An FDA-approved melanocortin receptor agonist for hypoactive sexual desire disorder (HSDD) in premenopausal women. Acts centrally rather than on vascular mechanisms. Phase 3 RECONNECT trials demonstrated efficacy.
content: Overview Overview Quick Summary - Melanocortin-4 receptor agonist that acts on central nervous system - FDA approved (2019) as Vyleesi for HSDD in premenopausal women - First CNS-acting approved treatment for female sexual dysfunction - On-demand dosing - taken before anticipated sexual activity - Derived from Melanotan II but without tanning effects at approved level Evidence Strength: High - FDA approval based on Phase 3 trials; clear efficacy signal for indicated population. Identity & Origins | Property | Value | |----------|-------| | Generic name | Bremelanotide | | Brand name | Vyleesi | | Research code | PT-141 | | Class | Melanocortin receptor agonist | | Manufacturer | Palatin Technologies / AMAG Pharmaceuticals | Development Timeline - 1990s: Derived from Melanotan II during development - 2000s: Clinical development for sexual dysfunction - 2016: Phase 3 trials completed - 2019: FDA approval for HSDD in premenopausal women - Current: Only approved for female HSDD Melanocortin Receptor Binding PT-141 (bremelanotide) is a cyclic heptapeptide derived from alpha-MSH that activates multiple melanocortin receptors with the following potency order: | Receptor | Relative Potency | Key Tissue Expression | |----------|------------------|----------------------| | MC1R | Highest | Skin melanocytes (tanning) | | MC4R | High | Hypothalamus (sexual function) | | MC3R | Moderate | Hypothalamus, limbic system | | MC5R | Lower | Exocrine glands | | MC2R | Lowest | Adrenal cortex | MC4R is the most relevant at therapeutic doses for sexual function. Central Neural Pathways PT-141 crosses blood-brain barrier | Binds MC4R in medial preoptic area (mPOA) of hypothalamus | Presynaptic MC4R activation | Increased dopamine release in mPOA | |-- Increased sexual desire |-- Increased genital arousal (via spinal pathways) |-- Oxytocin pathway activation Downstream Effects: - Dopaminergic activation : Reward and motivation circuits - Oxytocinergic activation : Bonding and arousal pathways - Spinal cord effects : Direct pro-erectile signaling via MC4R Brain Region Activation Animal studies show PT-141 activates specific brain regions: | Region | Role | Evidence | |--------|------|----------| | Medial preoptic area (mPOA) | Sexual behavior coordination | Increased FOS immunostaining | | Paraventricular nucleus (PVN) | Neural activation | FOS expression | | Ventral tegmental area (VTA) | Dopamine reward | Increased activity | Why It Works Differently Than Viagra | Aspect | PT-141 | PDE5 Inhibitors (Viagra) | |--------|--------|--------------------------| | Target | Central (brain) | Peripheral (blood vessels) | | Primary effect | Increased sexual desire | Improved erectile function | | Mechanism | MC4R to dopamine | NO to cGMP to vasodilation | | Sexual stimulation | Works without | Requires | | Works in women | FDA approved | No significant effect | Intracellular Signaling MC4R activation triggers: PT-141 binds MC4R (GPCR) | Gs-alpha activation | Adenylyl cyclase | Increased cAMP | Downstream signaling This cAMP increase in hypothalamic neurons modulates neurotransmitter release. --- Research (PMID:32443076) | 2020 | Nausea characterization; diminishes with use | Warnings and Precautions - Blood pressure: Transient increases observed - Nausea: Can be significant; limits use - Cardiovascular: Caution in uncontrolled hypertension - Focal hyperpigmentation: Possible with repeated use Contraindications - Uncontrolled hypertension - Known cardiovascular disease - Concomitant use with naltrexone (interaction) --- Pharmacokinetics Key Parameters | Parameter | Value | Notes | |-----------|-------|-------| | Half-life | 2.7 hours | Short-acting | | Administration | SC injection | Autoinjector | | Dosing | On-demand | 45+ min before activity | | Max frequency | Once per 24 hours | Limit 8 per month | --- Regulatory Status Global Approval Status | Region | Status | Indication | Year | |--------|--------|------------|------| | USA (FDA) | Approved | HSDD (premenopausal women) | 2019 | | EU (EMA) | Not approved | No MAA submitted | - | | Canada | Not approved | - | - | | Australia (TGA) | Not approved | - | - | | Japan (PMDA) | Not approved | - | - | Approved Indication Hypoactive sexual desire disorder (HSDD) in premenopausal women, characterized by low sexual desire that causes marked distress.
