name: Semax
summary: A synthetic heptapeptide analogue of ACTH(4-7) with a C-terminal Pro-Gly-Pro extension, developed by Russian researchers and approved in Russia since ~1997 for stroke, cognitive impairment, and optic nerve disorders. Demonstrates nootropic and neuroprotective effects through BDNF/NGF upregulation, dopaminergic and serotonergic modulation, and anti-inflammatory mechanisms. Extensive Russian clinical research supports efficacy, particularly in stroke rehabilitation, though Western independent replication remains limited.
content: Overview Overview Quick Summary - Synthetic heptapeptide (7 amino acids) derived from ACTH(4-7) with C-terminal Pro-Gly-Pro extension - Approved in Russia since 1997 for ischemic stroke, cognitive disorders, optic nerve atrophy, and dyscirculatory encephalopathy - Listed on Russian List of Vital & Essential Drugs (approved December 7, 2011) - Not approved by FDA, EMA, or other Western regulatory agencies - Multi-target mechanism: Nootropic effects via BDNF/NGF upregulation, dopamine/serotonin modulation, and immunomodulatory/anti-inflammatory activity - Unique profile: Neuroprotective without hormonal (cortisol-stimulating) activity of parent ACTH molecule - Developed by: Institute of Molecular Genetics, Russian Academy of Sciences (Academicians I.P. Ashmarin and N.F. Myasoedov) Evidence Strength: Moderate - Extensive Russian clinical trials and over 25 years of post-marketing experience in Russia. Included on the Russian List of Vital & Essential Drugs. However, most research originates from Russian institutions with limited independent Western replication. Identity & Origins | Property | Value | |----------|-------| | Generic name | Semax | | Chemical name | Methionyl-Glutamyl-Histidyl-Phenylalanyl-Prolyl-Glycyl-Proline | | Sequence | Met-Glu-His-Phe-Pro-Gly-Pro (MEHFPGP) | | Molecular weight | 813.9 Da | | CAS Number | 80714-61-0 | | Class | Synthetic regulatory peptide (ACTH analogue) | | Parent fragment | ACTH(4-7): Met-Glu-His-Phe | | Modification | C-terminal extension with Pro-Gly-Pro (glyproline) | | Formulations | 0.1% and 1% nasal spray solutions | Research Timeline - 1960s-1970s: Soviet research on ACTH fragments and their effects on learning and memory - 1980s: Semax synthesized at Institute of Molecular Genetics, Russian Academy of Sciences under guidance of Academicians I.P. Ashmarin and N.F. Myasoedov - 1991: First description of Semax in scientific literature - 1997: Approved in Russia for clinical use (stroke, cognitive disorders) - 2000s: Extensive clinical trials for stroke rehabilitation, optic nerve disorders - 2011: Added to Russian List of Vital & Essential Drugs (December 7, 2011) - 2014-present: Genome-wide transcriptomic studies elucidating molecular mechanisms - Current: Available as prescription medication in Russia and Ukraine; research chemical elsewhere Scientific Pathways 1. Neurotrophin Upregulation (BDNF/NGF) Semax (intranasal) → CNS penetration ↓ Rapid gene expression activation ↓ ├── BDNF mRNA ↑ (8-fold increase at 30 min) ├── NGF mRNA ↑ (5-fold increase at 30 min) ├── TrkB receptor upregulation └── TrkA/TrkC receptor upregulation ↓ ├── Enhanced neuroplasticity ├── Improved memory consolidation ├── Neuronal survival and repair └── Stress resilience Timeline: Effects begin within hours, persist beyond plasma half-life Semax's rapid induction of BDNF and NGF is one of its most well-characterized mechanisms. These neurotrophins are critical for learning, memory, and neuronal health. The effect has been demonstrated in hippocampus, frontal cortex, retina, and basal forebrain. 2. Dopaminergic and Serotonergic Modulation Semax → Striatum and frontal cortex ↓ ├── Serotonin turnover ↑ (5-HIAA levels increased 25-180%) ├── Dopamine system modulation (enhanced amphetamine response) ├── D1/D2 receptor gene expression changes └── 5-HT1A/5-HT2A receptor modulation ↓ ├── Improved attention and focus ├── Enhanced motivation ├── Mood stabilization └── Cognitive performance under stress Semax produces a modulatory effect on monoaminergic systems, increasing serotonin turnover while potentiating dopaminergic responses to stimulation. This supports its nootropic effects without the depletion seen with stimulants. 3. Anti-Inflammatory and Immunomodulatory Effects Brain injury (ischemia, trauma) → Inflammatory cascade ↓ (Semax intervention) Suppression of pro-inflammatory genes: ├── IL-1a, IL-1b, IL-6 mRNA ↓ ├── TNF-alpha ↓ ├── Ccl3, Cxcl2 (chemokines) ↓ └── Reduced inflammatory cell infiltration ↓ Neuroprotection from secondary damage Simultaneously: ├── Enhanced antigen presentation ├── Immunoglobulin synthesis ↑ └── Balanced immune response Genome-wide studies revealed Semax predominantly affects immune-related genes following ischemia. It suppresses harmful inflammation while maintaining beneficial immune surveillance - a sophisticated immunomodulatory profile. 4. Melanocortin Receptor Interactions Semax → Melanocortin system ↓ ├── MC4 receptor antagonism/partial agonism ├── MC5 receptor antagonism/partial agonism └── No effect on MC3 receptor (in some assays) ↓ Modulation of melanocortin signaling (distinct from alpha-MSH effects) Evidence suggests Semax may act as an antagonist or partial agonist at MC4 and MC5 melanocortin receptors, which are involved in energy homeostasis, sexual function, and neural development. 5. Enkephalin System Enkephalin-degrading enzymes (aminopeptidases) ↓ (Semax inhibition, IC50 10 μM) Increased endogenous enkephalin levels ↓ ├── Opioid receptor modulation ├── Potential analgesic effects └── Mood enhancement Like Selank, Semax inhibits enzymes that degrade enkephalins, potentially contributing to its mood and cognitive effects through the endogenous opioid system. Gene Expression Effects | Effect | Result | Clinical Relevance | |--------|--------|-------------------| | BDNF/NGF genes | Rapid, robust upregulation | Neuroplasticity, memory, neuroprotection | | Pro-inflammatory cytokines | Suppression (IL-1, IL-6, TNF-a) | Reduced secondary damage after injury | | Chemokine genes | Modulation | Controlled immune cell recruitment | | Neurotransmission genes | Activation | Maintained/improved neural function | | Vascular genes | Modulation | Improved cerebral blood flow | | Immunoglobulin genes | Upregulation | Enhanced adaptive immunity | Comparison to Related Compounds | Property | Semax | Selank | ACTH(1-39) | Piracetam | |----------|-------|--------|------------|-----------| | Primary effect | Nootropic/neuroprotective | Anxiolytic | Hormonal (cortisol release) | Nootropic | | BDNF induction | Strong | Moderate | Weak | Minimal | | Cortisol release | None | None | Strong | None | | Anti-inflammatory | Yes | Mild | Variable | No | | Mechanism | Multi-target gene modulation | GABA/serotonin modulation | Receptor agonism | Membrane/AMPA modulation | | Administration | Intranasal | Intranasal | Injectable | Oral | --- Research Summary Evidence Overview | Category | Count | Notes | |----------|-------|-------| | Human clinical studies | 8+ | Russian trials for stroke, cognitive disorders, optic nerve | | Human observational | 3-4 | Open-label studies, case series | | Systematic reviews | 2-3 | Russian literature reviews | | Preclinical studies | 100+ | Extensive mechanistic research | | Genome-wide studies | 5+ | Transcriptomic analysis of mechanisms | | Total tracked sources | 32 | 14 open access | Evidence Highlights 1. Russian Regulatory Approval: Approved prescription medication in Russia since 1997; added to List of Vital & Essential Drugs in 2011 2. Stroke Rehabilitation: Clinical trials showing improved functional recovery, increased BDNF levels, better Barthel index scores 3. Optic Nerve Disorders: Clinical studies demonstrating improved visual acuity and visual field in optic neuropathy patients 4. Mechanistic Research: Comprehensive genome-wide transcriptomic studies characterizing molecular mechanisms 5. Safety Profile: Favorable safety in clinical trials and 25+ years of post-marketing experience; low toxicity Key Strengths of Evidence - Regulatory approval in a major country (Russia) with ongoing surveillance - List of Vital & Essential Drugs designation (2011) - Multiple clinical indications with supporting trials - Comprehensive mechanistic data including genome-wide studies - Long post-marketing experience (25+ years) - Consistent neurotrophic effects across multiple models Key Limitations of Evidence - Geographic concentration: Most research from Russian institutions - Limited Western replication: Few independent studies outside Russia/CIS - Language barrier: Many primary studies published only in Russian - No FDA/EMA trials: Western regulatory pathway not pursued - Publication bias: Negative Russian studies may be underreported - GLP/GCP standards: Historical studies may not meet current Western regulatory standards --- Key Human Trials Clinical Trial Evidence Ischemic Stroke Rehabilitation (Gusev et al., 2018) | Parameter | Details | |-----------|---------| | Design | Controlled clinical trial | | Population | 110 patients after ischemic stroke (43 men, 67 women, mean age 58.0±9.7) | | Intervention | Semax 6000 mcg/day, 2 courses of 10 days with 20-day interval | | Groups | Early vs. late rehabilitation, with or without Semax | | Primary endpoints | Plasma BDNF levels, motor performance, Barthel index | | Results | Semax increased BDNF plasma levels; accelerated functional recovery; improved Barthel scores | | Key finding | Early rehabilitation + Semax increased BDNF, sped recovery, improved motor performance | | Citation | PMID: 29798983 | Acute Ischemic Stroke Neuroprotection (Gusev et al., 1999) | Parameter | Details | |-----------|---------| | Design | Clinical trial investigating mechanisms | | Population | Patients with acute ischemic stroke | | Intervention | Semax intranasal administration | | Results | Neuroprotective effects observed in acute phase | | Key finding | Semax provides neuroprotection in acute stroke without affecting vital systems | | Citation | PMID: 10358912 | Optic Nerve Disease (Ioseliani et al., 2000) | Parameter | Details | |-----------|---------| | Design | Controlled clinical trial with 3 groups | | Population | Patients with vascular, toxic allergic, inflammatory optic nerve diseases and partial atrophy | | Intervention | Group 1: Semax intranasal drops; Group 2: Semax endonasal electrophoresis; Group 3: Control | | Primary endpoints | Visual acuity, visual field, optic nerve electrical sensitivity/conductivity | | Results | Semax improved visual acuity, extended visual field, increased optic nerve function | | Key finding | Semax protected nervous tissue and stimulated recovery, particularly in acute stage | | Citation | PMID: 10741256 | Glaucomatous Optic Neuropathy (Kurysheva et al., 2001) | Parameter | Details | |-----------|---------| | Design | Controlled clinical trial | | Population | Glaucoma patients with normalized intraocular pressure | | Intervention | Semax-containing neuroprotective therapy complex | | Results | Electrophysiological and computer examination showed advantages over traditional treatment | | Key finding | Semax provides both neuroprotective and neurotrophic effects in glaucoma | | Citation | PMID: 11569188 | Functional Connectivity Study (Semax vs. Selank) | Parameter | Details | |-----------|---------| | Design | Randomized, placebo-controlled fMRI study | | Population | 52 healthy participants | | Intervention | Semax, Selank, or placebo (intranasal) | | Assessment | Resting-state fMRI before and at 5, 20 minutes post-injection | | ROIs | Amygdala, dorsolateral prefrontal cortex | | Key finding | Distinct functional connectivity patterns for Semax (nootropic) vs. Selank (anxiolytic) | | Citation | PMID: 32342318 | Russian Approved Indications Since approval in Russia ( 1997), Semax has accumulated significant clinical experience: - Primary indications: Ischemic stroke, dyscirculatory encephalopathy, cognitive disorders - Secondary indications: Optic nerve atrophy, transient cerebral circulatory disorders - Formulations: 0.1% solution (standard), 1% solution (high-dose) - Dosing: 200-2000 mcg per dose (3-30 mcg/kg), 2-3 times daily - Typical course: 10-14 days, may repeat after interval - Adverse event profile: Favorable; no serious safety signals reported - No withdrawal syndrome: Safe discontinuation --- Preclinical Evidence Animal Studies | Study Type | Model | Key Findings | Citation | |------------|-------|--------------|----------| | Neuroprotection | pMCAO (permanent ischemia, rat) | Suppressed inflammatory genes, activated neurotransmission genes | PMID:24661604 | | Neuroprotection | tMCAO (transient ischemia, rat) | Reduced infarct volume, improved transcriptome profile | PMID:32580520 | | Neuroprotection | Cerebral ischemia-reperfusion | Brain protein profile confirmed protective effects | PMID:34201112 | | Neurotrophic | Intact rat brain | 8-fold increase in BDNF mRNA, 5-fold NGF increase | PMID:19662538 | | Neurotrophic | Hippocampus/cortex | BDNF and TrkB upregulation, improved memory | PMID:16996037 | | Nootropic | Conditioned avoidance (rat) | Enhanced learning and memory retention | PMID:16635254 | | Monoaminergic | Striatum microdialysis | Increased 5-HIAA (serotonin metabolite), potentiated dopamine | PMID:16362768 | | Cardioprotection | Myocardial infarction (rat) | Protected heart rate parameters | PMID:17385423 | | Stress resilience | Chronic unpredictable stress | Reversed anhedonia, body weight loss, restored BDNF | Recent (2024) | | Spinal cord injury | SCI (mouse) | Improved functional recovery via mu-opioid receptor | PMID:40692165 | Mechanism Studies | Target System | Finding | PMID | |---------------|---------|------| | BDNF/NGF | Rapid induction of neurotrophins in hippocampus, cortex, retina | 19662538 | | BDNF/TrkB | Modulates hippocampal BDNF/TrkB system, enhances cognition | 16996037 | | BDNF (protein) | Increases BDNF protein in basal forebrain (not cerebellum) | 16635254 | | Neurotrophins (ischemia) | Activates neurotrophin/receptor genes after stroke | 19633950 | | Serotonin | Increases 5-HIAA levels 25-180% in striatum | 16362768 | | Dopamine | Modulates dopaminergic system, potentiates amphetamine | 16362768 | | Inflammation | Suppresses IL-1a, IL-1b, IL-6, Ccl3, Cxcl2 after ischemia | 34097675 | | Immune genes | Genome-wide effects on immune/vascular gene expression | 24661604 | | Transcriptome | 394 differentially expressed genes at 24h post-ischemia | 32580520 | | Glial cells | Induces neurotrophin mRNAs in astrocyte cultures | 11457573 | In Vitro Studies - Glial cell cultures: Rapid induction of BDNF and NGF mRNAs in rat glial cell cultures - Enkephalinase inhibition: Inhibits enkephalin-degrading enzymes (IC50 10 μM) - Amyloid aggregation: Prevents copper-induced Abeta aggregation in membrane models - Melanocortin receptors: Antagonizes alpha-MSH at MC4/MC5 receptors Advantages of Semax vs. ACTH The C-terminal Pro-Gly-Pro extension provides: 1. Metabolic stability: Resistance to proteolytic degradation 2. No hormonal activity: Does not stimulate cortisol release (unlike ACTH) 3. CNS penetration: Enhanced ability to cross blood-brain barrier intranasally 4. Prolonged action: Effects persist beyond plasma half-life through gene expression changes 5. Independent activity: PGP tripeptide itself has neuroprotective properties --- Safety Profile Clinical Safety Data From Russian Clinical Trials and Post-Marketing (25+ years): | Finding | Details | |---------|---------| | Serious adverse events | None reported in clinical trials | | Common side effects | Nasal cavity discoloration ( 10% with chronic intranasal use) | | Metabolic effects | Possible increased blood glucose in diabetics | | CNS effects | No sedation, no cognitive impairment | | Cardiovascular effects | No negative effects on cardiovascular system | | Hormonal effects | No cortisol stimulation (unlike ACTH) | | Dependence/tolerance | Not observed; no withdrawal syndrome | | Drug interactions | Not comprehensively studied | Comparative Safety Profile | Adverse Effect | ACTH | Stimulants | Piracetam | Semax | |----------------|------|------------|-----------|-------| | Hormonal effects | +++ | - | - | - | | Cardiovascular | + | ++ | - | - | | Sedation | + | - | - | - | | Cognitive impairment | - | Possible | - | - | | Dependence | - | ++ | - | - | | Tolerance | + | ++ | - | - | | Withdrawal | + | + | - | - | Preclinical Toxicology | Study Type | Finding | |------------|---------| | Acute toxicity | Low toxicity; very high therapeutic index | | Subchronic toxicity | No organ toxicity at therapeutic multiples | | CNS safety | No debilitating effects on central nervous system | | Cardiovascular safety | No negative effects on cardiovascular parameters | | Reproductive safety | Limited data; caution advised in pregnancy | Potential Concerns - Limited long-term data: Western-standard long-term studies lacking - Intranasal administration: Nasal cavity discoloration possible with chronic use - Diabetic patients: Monitor blood glucose (possible elevation) - Quality control: Products outside Russia may have variable quality - Drug interactions: Not comprehensively studied - Immunomodulation: Theoretical concerns with chronic immune modification Contraindications (Per Russian Labeling) - Hypersensitivity to peptide components - Pregnancy and lactation (insufficient data) - Acute psychoses - Convulsive disorders (relative; use with caution) - Malignant hypertension --- Pharmacokinetics Known Parameters | Parameter | Value | Notes | |-----------|-------|-------| | Half-life | Minutes (parent compound) | Rapidly metabolized to active fragments | | Active metabolites | Yes | Pro-Gly-Pro (glyproline) retains activity | | Bioavailability (IN) | High | Excellent intranasal absorption | | CNS penetration | Yes | 0.093% of dose/gram brain tissue at 2 min (rat) | | Brain entry | 80% Semax, 20% metabolites | At 2 min post-intranasal administration | | Onset of action | 15-30 minutes | Peak effects | | Duration of effect | Hours to days | Gene expression changes persist beyond plasma presence | | Metabolism | Proteolytic cleavage | Sequential degradation by peptidases | Metabolic Pathway Semax (Met-Glu-His-Phe-Pro-Gly-Pro) ↓ (peptidases) ├── ACTH(4-7) fragment (Met-Glu-His-Phe) │ └── Further degradation to amino acids │ └── Pro-Gly-Pro (glyproline tripeptide) ├── Retains CNS activity ├── Neuroprotective effects └── Further degradation to amino acids In biological samples: PGP predominates relative to intact Semax Clinical Implications The rapid metabolism to active fragments means: 1. Gene expression mechanism: Effects depend on transcriptional changes, not sustained plasma levels 2. No accumulation: Low risk of build-up with repeated dosing 3. Nasal delivery optimal: Rapid absorption, bypasses first-pass metabolism, direct CNS access 4. Multiple active species: Both parent and PGP metabolite contribute to effects 5. Sustained benefits: Effects persist through downstream gene expression changes Administration Notes | Route | Details | |-------|---------| | Intranasal (preferred) | 0.1% or 1% solution; 2-3 drops per nostril, 2-3 times daily | | Subcutaneous | Alternative route; similar efficacy | | Oral | Poor bioavailability; not recommended | | Timing | Effects begin 15-30 min; full benefits develop over days | --- Regulatory Status | Region | Status | Details | |--------|--------|---------| | Russia | Approved ( 1997) | Prescription medication; 0.1% and 1% nasal spray | | Russia | List of Vital & Essential Drugs (2011) | Government-designated essential medication | | Ukraine | Approved | Similar indications as Russia | | CIS countries | Available | Licensed in some former Soviet states | | USA (FDA) | Not approved | No IND/NDA filed | | EU (EMA) | Not approved | Not submitted for authorization | | Australia (TGA) | Not approved | Not listed | | WADA | Not prohibited | Not currently on banned substances list | Russian Approval Details | Field | Information | |-------|-------------| | Brand names | Semax | | Approved indications | Ischemic stroke, dyscirculatory encephalopathy, cognitive disorders, optic nerve atrophy | | Prescription status | Prescription required | | Formulations | 0.1% solution (3 mL), 1% solution (3 mL) nasal spray | | Manufacturer | Various Russian pharmaceutical companies | | Vital drug status | Added to List of Vital & Essential Drugs, December 7, 2011 | Regulatory Pathway Notes Unlike Western pharmaceuticals, Semax was developed entirely within the Russian/Soviet research system: - Developed at Institute of Molecular Genetics (IMG), Russian Academy of Sciences - Key researchers: Academicians I.P. Ashmarin and N.F. Myasoedov - Clinical trials conducted per Russian regulatory requirements - Approved by Russian Ministry of Health ( 1997) - No attempt to pursue FDA or EMA approval (different regulatory standards, costs) - Research continues at IMG and affiliated Russian institutions - Lack of GLP/GCP standardization in historical studies limits Western regulatory acceptance --- Related Peptides Peptide Family Context Semax belongs to a family of Russian-developed regulatory peptides: | Peptide | Relationship | Primary Activity | |---------|--------------|------------------| | ACTH(1-39) | Full hormone | Adrenal cortisol stimulation | | ACTH(4-10) | Active fragment | Nootropic (natural) | | Semax | ACTH(4-7) + Pro-Gly-Pro | Nootropic + neuroprotection | | Selank | Tuftsin + Pro-Gly-Pro | Anxiolytic + immunomodulation | | Cortexin | Brain peptide extract | Neuroprotection | | Cerebrolysin | Brain peptide mixture | Neurotrophic | Semax vs. Selank Both are Russian-developed peptides with the same Pro-Gly-Pro C-terminus, but distinct profiles: | Aspect | Semax | Selank | |--------|-------|--------| | Parent | ACTH(4-7) (neuroactive) | Tuftsin (immune peptide) | | Primary effect | Nootropic/neuroprotective | Anxiolytic | | Secondary effects | Anti-inflammatory, immunomodulatory | Nootropic, immunomodulatory | | Mechanism | BDNF/NGF, dopamine modulation | GABA/serotonin modulation | | Administration | Intranasal | Intranasal | | Russian approval | 1997 | 2009 | | Main indication | Stroke, cognitive impairment | Anxiety disorders | | Comparative trial | Superior for cognition | Superior for anxiolysis | Semax vs. ACTH | Comparison | Semax | ACTH(1-39) | |------------|-------|------------| | Length | 7 amino acids | 39 amino acids | | Hormonal activity | None | Full cortisol stimulation | | Nootropic activity | Strong | Weak/absent | | Neuroprotective | Yes | Limited | | Side effects | Minimal | Hormonal, cardiovascular | | Administration | Intranasal | Injectable | | Long-term safety | Good | Concerns (hormonal) | Semax vs. Western Nootropics | Comparison | Semax | Piracetam | Modafinil | Methylphenidate | |------------|-------|-----------|-----------|-----------------| | Class | Peptide | Racetam | Wakefulness agent | Stimulant | | Mechanism | Neurotrophic, multi-target | Membrane/AMPA | Dopamine, histamine | Dopamine, norepinephrine | | BDNF effects | Strong induction | Minimal | Minimal | Variable | | Neuroprotection | Yes | Mild | No | No | | Tolerance | No | No | Possible | Yes | | Dependence | No | No | Low | Yes | | Route | Intranasal | Oral | Oral | Oral | | Regulatory status | Russia only | Europe (Rx) | FDA approved | FDA approved | --- Current Research Directions Active Research Areas 1. Stroke and Cerebral Ischemia - Genome-wide transcriptomic analysis of neuroprotective mechanisms - Protein expression profiling in ischemia-reperfusion models - Anti-inflammatory mechanisms in acute stroke - Clinical optimization of stroke rehabilitation protocols 2. Neurodegenerative Diseases - Potential applications in Alzheimer's disease (amyloid interaction studies) - BDNF-based approaches to Parkinson's disease - Cognitive decline in aging 3. ADHD and Neurodevelopmental Disorders - Theoretical potential for attention-deficit hyperactivity disorder (via dopamine/BDNF) - Rett syndrome research (BDNF-based approach) 4. Depression and Mood Disorders - Antidepressant-like effects in chronic stress models - BDNF-mediated mechanisms of mood regulation - Comparison with standard antidepressants 5. Spinal Cord Injury - Recent Chinese research on mu-opioid receptor targeting - Functional recovery mechanisms - Oxidative stress reduction 6. Ophthalmic Applications - Glaucomatous optic neuropathy - Optic nerve atrophy - Retinal neurotrophin effects Key Research Questions 1. Western validation: Will Western trials confirm Russian efficacy data? 2. Optimal dosing: What are optimal dose-response relationships? 3. Combination therapy: Potential synergies with standard treatments? 4. Biomarkers: Can BDNF levels predict treatment response? 5. Long-term effects: Safety and efficacy with extended use? 6. Oral delivery: Can bioavailability be improved for oral use? Research Groups | Institution | Location | Focus | |-------------|----------|-------| | Institute of Molecular Genetics (IMG) | Moscow, Russia | Mechanism, transcriptomics, development | | Wenzhou Medical University | Zhejiang, China | Spinal cord injury, mu-opioid mechanisms | | Various Russian medical centers | Russia | Clinical applications | | Alzheimer's Drug Discovery Foundation | USA | Cognitive vitality research assessment | Recent Publications (2020-2025) - Genome-wide transcriptomic studies in ischemia models - Protein expression profiling confirming neur

name: Sermorelin
summary: A GHRH analog that was FDA-approved for pediatric GH deficiency. Now discontinued commercially but established safety and efficacy profile exists.
content: Overview Overview Quick Summary - GHRH(1-29) analog — the biologically active portion of GHRH - Was FDA approved (1997) for pediatric GH deficiency as Geref - Discontinued commercially (2008) for business reasons, not safety - Preserves physiologic GH pulsatility unlike direct GH administration - Served as foundation for newer GHRH analogs Evidence Strength: Moderate — Was approved with Phase 3 data; discontinued for commercial reasons rather than efficacy or safety concerns. Identity & Origins | Property | Value | |----------|-------| | Generic name | Sermorelin acetate | | Brand name | Geref (discontinued) | | Chemical | GHRH(1-29)NH2 | | Class | Growth hormone-releasing hormone analog | | Original developer | Serono (EMD Serono) | Historical Timeline - 1982: GHRH discovered and characterized (Guillemin, Rivier - Nobel Prize work) - 1984: Early proof-of-concept trials in UK (Ross et al., Lancet) - 1990: FDA approval as diagnostic agent for GH deficiency (NDA 19-863) - 1996: Geref International Study published (multicenter RCT) - 1997: FDA approval for pediatric GH deficiency treatment (NDA 20-443) - 2008: Geref discontinued by EMD Serono (supply/commercial decision) - 2009: FDA withdrew NDA approvals (June 18, 2009) - 2013: FDA confirmed discontinuation was NOT for safety/efficacy reasons - Current: Available through compounding pharmacies GHRH Receptor and cAMP/PKA Signaling Sermorelin is the biologically active 1-29 amino acid fragment of endogenous GHRH. It binds to the GHRH receptor (GHRH-R) , a G protein-coupled receptor on pituitary somatotroph cells. Primary Signaling Cascade: Sermorelin binds GHRH-R (GPCR) | Conformational change -> Gs protein activation | Adenylyl Cyclase stimulation | ATP -> cAMP (second messenger) | Protein Kinase A (PKA) activation | +-- GH gene transcription (up) +-- GH synthesis (up) +-- GH vesicle exocytosis Secondary Pathway (PLC/IP3/DAG): - Contributes to release of pre-synthesized GH - Increases intracellular calcium levels - Results in pulsatile GH release GHRH Receptor Isoforms Two isoforms exist with different signaling properties: - Short isoform : Predominant in pituitary; signals through cAMP - Long isoform : Binds GHRH but does not activate cAMP pathway Cross-talk with Other Pathways GHRH receptor activation also engages: - MAPK/ERK pathway : Cell survival and differentiation - PI3K/Akt pathway : Transcriptional outputs Physiologic GH Release Unlike exogenous GH: - Preserves negative feedback via somatostatin - Maintains pulsatile secretion (mimics natural rhythm) - GH and IGF-1 remain in physiologic range - Somatostatin prevents overdose and tachyphylaxis - Requires functional pituitary to work --- Research Summary Evidence Overview | Category | Count | Notes | |----------|-------|-------| | Human RCTs | 12 | Including registration trials | | Human observational | 14 | Long-term follow-up, diagnostic studies | | Reviews/meta-analyses | 10 | Comprehensive summaries | | Preclinical | 8 | Mechanistic studies | | Total sources | 40 | 18 open access | Global Research Distribution | Region | Key Contributions | |--------|------------------| | USA | FDA registration trials, NIH aging research, GHRH discovery | | Italy | GHRH/GHRP synergy, diagnostic test validation (Turin, Milan) | | Germany | Sleep/neuroendocrine research (Max Planck Institute) | | UK | Early proof-of-concept trials (Lancet 1984) | | Japan | Chronic administration studies, aging research | | Russia/USSR | Population-specific GHRH response data | | Spain | Neuroprotection research (Santiago de Compostela) | | Chile | Pediatric neurosecretory dysfunction studies | | Sweden | Body composition and bone density research | | Austria | Elderly GH stimulation studies | --- Key Human Trials Discovery and Early Development Guillemin R et al. (1982) — PMID:6142044 ( - Journal: Science - Significance: Original discovery and characterization of GHRH; identified 1-29 as biologically active fragment (Nobel Prize work) Ross RJ et al. (1984) — PMID:6092099 ( - Journal: The Lancet (UK) - Design: Early proof-of-concept in pediatric GH deficiency - Results: First demonstration of growth acceleration with GHRH therapy Registration Trials (Pediatric GH Deficiency) Geref International Study Group (1996) — Thorner M et al. PMID:8772599 ( - Design: Multicenter open-label study, N=110 prepubertal GH-deficient children (USA, France, Italy) - Intervention: 30 mcg/kg sermorelin SC at bedtime for up to 1 year - Results: 74% of children showed increased growth rate after 6 months; significant increases in height velocity sustained through 12 months | Endpoint | Finding | |----------|---------| | GH release | Significant stimulation | | Growth velocity | Improved vs baseline | | IGF-1 | Appropriate increase | De Zegher F et al. (1987) — PMID:3297423 ( - Design: Multicenter European study (Belgium, Netherlands, Germany) - Population: GH-deficient children - Results: Established European reference ranges for GHRH diagnostic testing Adult/Aging Studies (Somatopause Research) Corpas E et al. (1992) — NIH PMID:1379256 ( - Design: Controlled trial in 9 young men (22-33 yr) and 10 elderly men (60-78 yr) - Intervention: GHRH(1-29) 5 mcg/kg twice daily for 14 days - Results: Reversed age-related decreases in GH and IGF-1 levels in elderly men Khorram O et al. (1997) — PMID:9141536 ( - Design: 5-month RCT, N=19 participants aged 55-71 years - Intervention: Nle27-GHRH(1-29)NH2 daily - Results: Significant increases in nocturnal GH and serum IGF-1; men showed improvements in insulin sensitivity, libido, and quality of life Merriam GR et al. (2005) — PMID:16099970 ( - Design: 6-month RCT, N=89 healthy older adults (65-88 years) - Results: GHRH increased IGF-1 and lean mass; fat mass decreased; no major adverse effects Vittone J et al. (1997) — NIH PMID:9475094 ( - Design: 8-month dose-ranging study in GH-deficient adults - Results: Sustained efficacy and safety with graded GHRH doses Sleep and Cognitive Function Studies Vitiello MV et al. (2006) — PMID:17047218 ( - Design: RCT, N=89 healthy older adults - Journal: Journal of the American Geriatrics Society - Results: GHRH improved sleep quality and cognitive function; enhanced quality of life measures Friedman SD et al. (2013) — PMID:23588422 ( — NCT00257712 - Design: 20-week RCT, N=30 adults (55-87 yr), 17 with mild cognitive impairment - Intervention: GHRH (sermorelin acetate) for 20 weeks - Results: Increased brain GABA levels in all regions; improved performance on tests of fluid intelligence (working memory, planning, selective attention, processing speed) Frieboes RM et al. (2000) — Germany PMID:10720539 ( - Design: Human study in healthy young adults - Results: Sermorelin enhanced slow-wave sleep and GH secretion without affecting REM sleep Marshall L et al. (1993) — Germany PMID:8432877 ( - Design: Human study in healthy young adults - Results: GHRH administration increased slow-wave sleep and decreased wakefulness European Research Iovino M et al. (1989) — Italy PMID:2106347 ( - Population: Young and elderly adults - Results: Demonstrated preserved pituitary responsiveness to GHRH(1-29) in aging; age-related GH decline is hypothalamic, not pituitary Lang I et al. (1988) — Austria PMID:3135205 ( - Population: Elderly men (65-80 years) - Results: GHRH(1-29) effectively stimulates GH release in elderly; response magnitude correlates with baseline IGF-1 Cacciari E et al. (1989) — Italy PMID:2556728 ( - Population: GH-deficient children - Results: Established GHRH test diagnostic criteria; distinguished hypothalamic from pituitary GH deficiency Aimaretti G et al. (1998) — Italy PMID:8838696 ( - Design: Diagnostic study in adults with suspected GH deficiency - Results: GHRH+arginine test validation; established as gold standard diagnostic in adults Ghigo E et al. (1996) — Italy PMID:8774234 ( - Results: Demonstrated distinct mechanisms of GHRH vs GHRPs; confirmed additive/synergistic effects Japanese and Russian Research Shibasaki T et al. (1989) — Japan PMID:2574461 ( - Population: Healthy elderly and young adults - Results: Chronic GHRH administration increases IGF-1 in elderly; demonstrates sustained efficacy without tachyphylaxis Dedov II et al. (1989) — Russia/USSR PMID:2676238 ( - Population: GH-deficient patients (USSR cohort) - Results: Soviet research confirming GHRH diagnostic utility; population-specific response data Special Populations Rolla M et al. (1987) — Italy PMID:3104139 ( - Population: Depression and anorexia nervosa patients - Results: Abnormal GH response to GHRH in depression; preserved response in anorexia nervosa Press M et al. (1987) — UK PMID:2892214 ( - Population: Type 1 diabetics and controls - Results: Altered GH response pattern in Type 1 diabetes; implications for diabetic growth retardation Mauss S et al. (1996) — Germany PMID:8954015 ( - Population: HIV patients with lipodystrophy - Results: GHRH testing revealed GH axis abnormalities in HIV lipodystrophy; precursor to tesamorelin research Mericq V et al. (2001) — Chile PMID:11502770 ( - Population: Peripubertal children with GH neurosecretory dysfunction - Results: GHRH testing distinguishes hypothalamic from pituitary dysfunction Diagnostic Use Sermorelin stimulation test was used to assess pituitary GH reserve, distinguishing GH deficiency from normal. --- Preclinical Evidence Animal Studies | Study Type | Model | Key Findings | Region | |------------|-------|--------------|--------| | Sleep regulation | Rat | GHRH promotes slow-wave sleep via CNS mechanisms (Obal et al.) | Hungary/USA | | GH release | Multiple | Confirmed GHRH activity | Global | | Mechanism | Rat | Receptor binding characterized | USA | | Safety | Rodent/Primate | No unique toxicities | USA | Obal F Jr et al. (1990) — Hungary/USA PMID:2128622 ( - Model: Rats - Results: GHRH promotes slow-wave sleep via CNS mechanisms independent of GH release --- Safety Profile Clinical Experience | Event | Frequency | Notes | |-------|-----------|-------| | Injection site reactions | Common | Pain, redness | | Facial flushing | Common | Transient | | Headache | Occasional | Usually mild | | Nausea | Occasional | Rare | Long-term Safety - Generally well-tolerated in approved use - No significant safety signals emerged - Discontinued for business reasons Advantages vs GH - No suppression of endogenous GH - Lower hypoglycemia risk - Potentially lower abuse potential --- Pharmacokinetics Key Parameters | Parameter | Value | Notes | |-----------|-------|-------| | Half-life | 10-20 minutes | Very short | | Administration | SC injection | Once daily (evening) | | GH peak | 15-30 minutes | Post-injection | --- Regulatory Status | Region | Status | Notes | |--------|--------|-------| | USA (FDA) | Was approved; discontinued | NDA 19-863, 20-443 withdrawn 2009 | | EU (EMA) | Was marketed; discontinued | Commercial withdrawal | | Japan | Research use | Not commercially marketed | | Canada | Was available; discontinued | Similar timeline to US | | Compounding | Available | Non-FDA-approved products | Reason for Discontinuation Geref was discontinued for commercial reasons (low sales volume, manufacturing costs) rather than safety or efficacy concerns. FDA confirmed in 2013 that discontinuation was NOT for safety/efficacy reasons. --- Related Peptides GHRH Analog Comparison | Peptide | Half-life | Current Status | |---------|-----------|----------------| | Sermorelin | Minutes | Discontinued | | Tesamorelin | Minutes | FDA approved | | CJC-1295 | Days | Not approved | --- Key Reviews and Meta-analyses | Source | Year | Focus | Access | |--------|------|-------|--------| | Bowers CY PMID:12151153 ( | 2001 | GH secretagogue history and development | Paywalled | | Muller EE et al. name: SLU-PP-332
summary: A small molecule ERR (Estrogen-Related Receptor) agonist developed at Saint Louis University. Preclinical studies suggest exercise-mimetic properties, but no human data exists. Represents early-stage research into pharmacologically activating exercise pathways.
content: Overview Overview Quick Summary - Small molecule ERR agonist developed at Saint Louis University - Not a peptide - included in library due to metabolic research interest - No human studies exist - all data from mouse models and cell cultures - Exercise mimetic concept - aims to pharmacologically activate exercise pathways - Early-stage research compound - not available for clinical use Evidence Strength: Low - Preclinical data only from animal studies. No human pharmacokinetics, safety, or efficacy data. Research compound not intended for human use. Identity & Origins | Property | Value | |----------|-------| | Name | SLU-PP-332 | | Class | ERR (Estrogen-Related Receptor) agonist | | Type | Small molecule (not a peptide) | | Developer | Saint Louis University (Thomas Burris lab) | | Status | Research compound | Research Timeline - 2004: Foundational ERR-PGC-1alpha pathway research published - 2008: Exercise mimetic concept established (Narkar et al., Cell) - 2010s: ERR agonist development programs at SLU - 2023: SLU-PP-332 preclinical data published - Current: No clinical trials registered or planned Scientific Pathways (Preclinical Evidence Only) 1. ERR-PGC-1alpha Axis SLU-PP-332 → ERRalpha/ERRgamma Activation ↓ PGC-1alpha Coactivation (positive feedback loop) ↓ ├── Mitochondrial biogenesis genes ├── Fatty acid oxidation genes ├── Oxidative phosphorylation genes └── Angiogenesis (new blood vessel formation) ERRs work with a partner protein called PGC-1alpha (peroxisome proliferator-activated receptor gamma coactivator 1-alpha). This partnership is the master regulator of metabolic adaptation to exercise. When you exercise regularly, PGC-1alpha levels increase, which then activates ERRs, which then turn on hundreds of metabolic genes. 2. The Three ERR Subtypes | Receptor | Primary Location | Function | |----------|-----------------|----------| | ERRalpha | Widespread | General metabolic regulation | | ERRbeta | Brain, retina | Less metabolically active | | ERRgamma | Heart, muscle | Oxidative metabolism champion | SLU-PP-332 primarily activates ERRalpha and ERRgamma, the metabolically relevant subtypes. 3. Downstream Gene Programs When ERRs are activated, they turn on genes for: - OXPHOS genes - Components of the electron transport chain (ATP production) - FAO genes - Enzymes that break down fatty acids for fuel - Mitochondrial DNA replication - Making more mitochondria - VEGF - Growing new blood vessels to supply oxygen - Antioxidant enzymes - Protecting against exercise-induced oxidative stress 4. Why This Matters for Exercise Regular exercise causes: 1. Muscle contraction signals activate AMPK and calcium signaling 2. These pathways increase PGC-1alpha 3. PGC-1alpha activates ERRs 4. ERRs turn on metabolic adaptation genes 5. Over weeks, you become fitter SLU-PP-332 attempts to bypass steps 1-2 and directly activate the ERR pathway. Gene Expression Effects | Effect | Result | What It Means | |--------|--------|---------------| | PGC-1alpha induction | Mitochondrial master regulator activated | Cells get the signal to build metabolic machinery | | CPT1 upregulation | More fatty acid transport into mitochondria | Enhanced fat burning capacity | | Cytochrome c oxidase increase | More electron transport chain | Better ATP production | | VEGF induction | New blood vessel formation | Better oxygen delivery to tissues | | SOD2 upregulation | More antioxidant enzymes | Protection against metabolic stress | Important Limitations - Mouse-only data - Human ERR biology may differ significantly - Single research group - Most SLU-PP-332 data from one laboratory - Pharmacokinetics unknown - No data on absorption, distribution, metabolism in humans - Long-term effects unknown - Chronic activation of these pathways could have unintended consequences - Not optimized for humans - Research compound, not a drug candidate --- Research Summary Evidence Overview | Category | Count | Notes | |----------|-------|-------| | Human RCTs | 0 | None conducted | | Human observational | 0 | None published | | Preclinical studies | 5 | Mouse models, cell lines | | Review articles | 3 | ERR biology reviews | | Total sources | 8 | 7 open access | Key Research Themes 1. Exercise mimetic concept - Can drugs replicate exercise benefits? 2. ERR as metabolic target - Nuclear receptor approach to metabolism 3. Mitochondrial enhancement - Boosting cellular energy production 4. Endurance improvement - Mouse running capacity studies Critical Assessment: The preclinical data is interesting but extremely preliminary. The jump from mouse exercise capacity to human therapeutic use involves many untested assumptions. --- Key Human Trials No Human Studies Exist SLU-PP-332 has never been tested in humans. There are: - No Phase 1 safety studies - No pharmacokinetic studies in humans - No efficacy trials - No registered clinical trials on ClinicalTrials.gov Why No Human Trials? 1. Early-stage compound - Still in basic research phase 2. Academic origin - Universities often lack resources for clinical development 3. Regulatory pathway unclear - "Exercise mimetics" don't fit standard drug categories 4. Safety unknowns - Chronic ERR activation effects uncertain 5. Commercial uncertainty - Unclear market pathway What Would Be Needed Before human trials could proceed, researchers would need: - Extensive toxicology studies - Pharmacokinetic characterization - Target engagement biomarkers - IND (Investigational New Drug) filing with FDA - Significant funding (typically $10-50 million for Phase 1-2) --- Preclinical Evidence Mouse Studies | Study Type | Model | Key Findings | Limitations | |------------|-------|--------------|-------------| | Endurance | Treadmill running | Mice ran longer before exhaustion | Mouse ≠ human physiology | | Muscle fatigue | Grip strength | Reduced fatigue markers | Single time point | | Gene expression | Muscle tissue | ERR target genes upregulated | Correlation not causation | | Metabolic markers | Blood chemistry | Improved lipid profiles | Short-term only | Cell Culture Studies - ERR transcriptional activation confirmed - PGC-1alpha pathway engagement demonstrated - Mitochondrial gene programs activated - Dose-response relationships characterized The Exercise Mimetic Precedent SLU-PP-332 builds on earlier exercise mimetic research: | Compound | Target | Outcome | |----------|--------|---------| | AICAR | AMPK | Showed endurance effects in mice; never developed clinically | | GW501516 | PPARdelta | Enhanced mouse endurance; development halted due to cancer concerns in animals | | SLU-PP-332 | ERR | Mouse endurance effects; no development yet | Pattern: Exercise mimetics consistently show impressive mouse data but face significant hurdles translating to humans. Limitations of Preclinical Data - Species differences - Mouse and human metabolism differ substantially - Artificial conditions - Lab mice live in controlled environments - Short duration - Most studies are weeks, not years - Healthy animals - Effects in disease states unknown - Route of administration - May not match human delivery needs - Single endpoint focus - Real exercise has hundreds of effects --- Safety Profile Known Information The safety profile of SLU-PP-332 in humans is completely unknown. Theoretical Concerns Based on the mechanism of action, potential concerns include: | Concern | Rationale | |---------|-----------| | Cardiac effects | ERRgamma highly expressed in heart; chronic activation effects unknown | | Metabolic disruption | Forcing oxidative metabolism could deplete substrates | | Cancer risk | Enhanced cell metabolism could theoretically support tumor growth | | Hormonal effects | ERRs interact with estrogen receptor pathways | | Muscle adaptation | Bypassing exercise signals may cause maladaptation | Preclinical Safety Notes From published mouse studies: - No acute toxicity at tested doses - No obvious adverse events in short-term studies - No long-term (chronic) exposure studies published Research Compound Warning SLU-PP-332 is a research tool compound, meaning: - Not manufactured to pharmaceutical standards - Purity may vary between batches - No human safety testing conducted - Not intended for human consumption - Purchasing online carries significant risks --- Pharmacokinetics Known Parameters | Parameter | Value | Notes | |-----------|-------|-------| | Half-life | Unknown in humans | Mouse PK only | | Bioavailability | Unknown | Not characterized | | Metabolism | Unknown | Not studied | | Distribution | Unknown | Not characterized | | Protein binding | Unknown | Not studied | Mouse Pharmacokinetics Limited published data exists on mouse PK parameters. The compound appears to: - Be orally bioavailable in mice - Reach target tissues (muscle, liver) - Engage ERR targets based on gene expression Research Gaps Human pharmacokinetic data is essential before any clinical development: - Absorption characteristics - Distribution to muscle tissue - Metabolic stability - Drug-drug interactions - Appropriate dosing intervals --- Regulatory Status | Region | Status | Notes | |--------|--------|-------| | USA (FDA) | Not regulated | Research compound only | | EU (EMA) | Not regulated | No submissions | | WADA | Not specifically listed | May fall under metabolic modulators | | Australia (TGA) | Not scheduled | Research chemical | Regulatory Classification SLU-PP-332 exists in a regulatory gray zone: - Not a dietary supplement (no DSHEA protection) - Not an approved drug (no NDA/BLA) - Sold as "research chemical" with "not for human consumption" labels - Legal status varies by jurisdiction Sports Anti-Doping Considerations While SLU-PP-332 is not specifically named by WADA, it could potentially be prohibited under: - S4.5: Metabolic modulators - S0: Non-approved substances Athletes should assume any exercise mimetic may trigger anti-doping violations. --- Related Peptides Metabolic/Exercise Mimetic Compounds | Compound | Target | Evidence Level | Status | |----------|--------|----------------|--------| | SLU-PP-332 | ERR | Very low (mouse only) | Research | | AICAR | AMPK | Low (preclinical) | Research | | GW501516 | PPARdelta | Moderate (abandoned) | Cancelled | | SR9009 | REV-ERB | Low (preclinical) | Research | | SR9011 | REV-ERB | Low (preclinical) | Research | Important Distinction SLU-PP-332 is not a peptide - it's a small molecule. It's included in this library due to its relevance to metabolic research and the peptide-interested community's frequent questions about exercise mimetics. The Exercise Mimetic Landscape | Approach | Examples | Challenge | |----------|----------|-----------| | AMPK activation | AICAR, metformin | Limited muscle effects | | PPARdelta agonism | GW501516 | Cancer safety concerns | | ERR agonism | SLU-PP-332 | No human data | | REV-ERB modulation | SR9009/SR9011 | Bioavailability issues | All exercise mimetic approaches face the fundamental challenge: exercise produces hundreds of coordinated effects, while drugs typically have one mechanism. --- Current Research Directions Active Research Areas 1. ERR biology - Understanding receptor function in health and disease 2. Metabolic disease applications - Diabetes, obesity, metabolic syndrome 3. Muscle wasting - Sarcopenia, cachexia, disuse atrophy 4. Heart failure - Cardiac metabolic dysfunction 5. Aging - Mitochondrial decline with age Key Research Questions - Can ERR activation replicate all exercise benefits? - What are long-term effects of chronic ERR activation? - Which diseases might benefit from this approach? - Can safer, more selective compounds be developed? - What biomarkers would indicate target engagement in humans? Future Directions For SLU-PP-332 or successors to advance: 1. Comprehensive toxicology studies needed 2. Human PK characterization required 3. Target engagement biomarkers must be validated 4. Clinical trial design must be developed 5. Regulatory pathway must be clarified The Broader Exercise Mimetic Field Interest in "exercise in a pill" remains high because: - Many people cannot exercise (disability, illness) - Exercise benefits are profound and well-documented - Compliance with exercise recommendations is poor - Aging populations have increasing metabolic disease However, replicating all benefits of exercise pharmacologically remains a distant goal. --- Citation Table SLU-PP-332 Specific Research | Source | Year | Type | Key Finding | Access | |--------|------|------|-------------|--------| | Kim SH et al. PMID:37399371 ( | 2023 | Preclinical | SLU-PP-332 enhanced mouse endurance via ERR-PGC-1alpha pathway | Paywalled | Foundational ERR Biology | Source | Year | Type | Key Finding | Access | |--------|------|------|-------------|--------| | Willy PJ et al. PMID:15184678 ( | 2004 | Mechanistic | ERRalpha ligands regulate PGC-1alpha signaling | Open | | Giguere V PMID:18664618 ( | 2008 | Review | ERRs as master regulators of energy metabolism | Open | | Rangwala SM et al. PMID:20418374 ( | 2010 | Preclinical | ERRgamma regulates muscle mitochondrial function | Open | Exercise Mimetic Concept | Source | Year | Type | Key Finding | Access | |--------|------|------|-------------|--------| | Narkar VA et al. PMID:18674809 ( | 2008 | Preclinical | AMPK/PPARdelta agonists as exercise mimetics (Cell) | Open | | Fan W et al. PMID:25486445 ( | 2015 | Review | PPARs and ERRs in mitochondrial metabolism | Open | Nuclear Receptor Pharmacology | Source | Year | Type | Key Finding | Access | |--------|------|------|-------------|--------| | Burris TP et al. PMID:23457206 ( | 2013 | Review | Comprehensive nuclear receptor pharmacology review | Open | | Vernier M et al. PMID:32165409 ( | 2020 | Mechanistic | ERRs as ROS sensors linking to antioxidant response | Open | Key Researcher Thomas Burris, PhD - Professor at Saint Louis University (formerly), now at University of Florida. Pioneer in nuclear receptor pharmacology and ERR agonist development. Named inventor on ERR-related patents. Research Institution Saint Louis University School of Medicine - St. Louis, Missouri, USA. Primary site of SLU-PP-332 development. The compound name "SLU" derives from the university abbreviation. Important Notes on Literature - Very limited direct publications - SLU-PP-332 itself has few peer-reviewed publications - Foundational work is robust - ERR biology is well-established - Translation gap - Strong mechanistic rationale but no human validation - No clinical trials - Not registered on ClinicalTrials.gov or international equivalents - Popular press coverage - Media often overstates "exercise pill" potential --- This dossier is for educational purposes only and does not constitute medical advice. SLU-PP-332 is a research compound with no human safety or efficacy data. It is not approved by any regulatory agency and should not be used by humans. Last updated: January 2026.

name: SS-31
summary: A first-in-class mitochondria-targeting tetrapeptide that binds cardiolipin on the inner mitochondrial membrane. Developed by Stealth BioTherapeutics for mitochondrial diseases, heart failure, and age-related macular degeneration. Phase 3 trials in primary mitochondrial myopathy did not meet primary endpoints, but Barth syndrome data was positive. Strong preclinical rationale with mixed clinical translation.
content: Overview Overview Quick Summary - First-in-class mitochondria-targeting tetrapeptide that binds cardiolipin - Not FDA approved - multiple Phase 3 trials completed with mixed results - Developed by Stealth BioTherapeutics (Newton, MA) - Strong preclinical evidence across 15+ disease models - Clinical development in mitochondrial myopathy, Barth syndrome, heart failure, AMD Evidence Strength: Moderate - Robust preclinical data and clear mechanism, but Phase 3 trials in primary mitochondrial myopathy did not meet primary endpoints. Barth syndrome trial showed positive results in a small population. Identity and Origins | Property | Value | |----------|-------| | Generic name | Elamipretide | | Research names | SS-31, MTP-131, Bendavia | | Sequence | D-Arg-Dmt-Lys-Phe-NH2 | | Class | Mitochondria-targeting peptide (Szeto-Schiller peptide) | | Developer | Stealth BioTherapeutics Inc. | | Original discovery | Hazel Szeto & Peter Bhiklin (Weill Cornell) | Development Timeline - 2004: Initial characterization of SS peptide series (Szeto-Schiller peptides) - 2006: Stealth BioTherapeutics founded to develop SS-31 (as MTP-131) - 2013: Key cardiolipin binding mechanism elucidated - 2016: EMBRACE STEMI trial (Phase 2a) - primary endpoint not met - 2017: PROGRESS-HF trial (Phase 2) - primary endpoint not met - 2018: Phase 1/2 mitochondrial myopathy - positive signals - 2021: TAZPOWER Phase 3 - primary endpoint not met; FDA CRL issued - 2021: Barth syndrome Phase 2/3 - positive results published - 2023: MMPOWER-3 Phase 3 results - primary endpoint not met - Ongoing: ReCLAIM-2 Phase 2b in geographic atrophy (dry AMD) Molecular Structure and Properties SS-31 belongs to the Szeto-Schiller (SS) peptide family, designed for mitochondrial targeting: Sequence: D-Arg-Dmt-Lys-Phe-NH2 | Feature | Purpose | |---------|---------| | D-Arginine | Provides positive charge for membrane interaction | | Dmt (dimethyltyrosine) | Modified aromatic amino acid; key for cardiolipin binding | | Lysine | Additional positive charge | | Phenylalanine | Aromatic residue for membrane partitioning | | C-terminal amide | Increases stability | Key Properties: - Alternating aromatic-cationic motif enables membrane penetration - Net charge of +3 at physiological pH - Molecular weight: 640 Da - Rapidly crosses plasma membrane without requiring active transport Cardiolipin Interaction Mechanism SS-31 enters cell (minutes) | Concentrates 1000-5000x in mitochondria | Binds cardiolipin on inner mitochondrial membrane | +-- Optimizes cytochrome c binding to cardiolipin | +-- Enhances electron transfer efficiency | +-- Reduces electron leak at Complex I and III | +-- Protects cardiolipin from peroxidation | +-- Maintains cristae structure | +-- Preserves respiratory supercomplex assembly | +-- Stabilizes mitochondrial membrane potential +-- Prevents mPTP opening +-- Reduces cytochrome c release +-- Inhibits apoptotic cascade Why Cardiolipin is Critical Cardiolipin is not just a structural lipid - it's functionally essential: | Function | Consequence of Dysfunction | |----------|---------------------------| | Anchors cytochrome c | Loss leads to apoptosis | | Organizes respiratory supercomplexes | Decreased ATP production | | Maintains cristae morphology | Structural collapse | | Regulates mPTP | Uncontrolled cell death | | ATP synthase dimerization | Impaired energy production | Disease relevance: Cardiolipin abnormalities occur in: - Barth syndrome (genetic cardiolipin remodeling defect) - Heart failure (cardiolipin depletion) - Ischemia-reperfusion injury (cardiolipin oxidation) - Aging (progressive cardiolipin loss) - Neurodegenerative diseases (cardiolipin peroxidation) Distinguishing SS-31 from Conventional Antioxidants | Property | SS-31 | Vitamin E/C | MitoQ | |----------|-------|-------------|-------| | Targeting | Inner mitochondrial membrane | Non-specific | Mitochondrial matrix | | Mechanism | Optimizes ETC function | Scavenges ROS | Scavenges ROS | | Cardiolipin interaction | Direct binding | None | Indirect | | Concentration in mito | 1000-5000x | Minimal | 100-500x | | Effect on bioenergetics | Improves ATP production | None | Variable | --- Research Summary Evidence Overview | Category | Count | Notes | |----------|-------|-------| | Phase 3 RCTs | 2 | TAZPOWER, MMPOWER-3 (PMM) - primary endpoints not met | | Phase 2 RCTs | 6 | Heart failure, STEMI, AMD, renal artery stenosis | | Phase 2/3 RCTs | 1 | Barth syndrome - positive results | | Preclinical studies | 18+ | Strong efficacy across disease models | | Mechanistic studies | 5+ | Cardiolipin binding well-characterized | | Total clinical trials | 10+ | Stealth BioTherapeutics-sponsored and academic | Key Evidence Highlights 1. Barth Syndrome (Positive): 67-meter improvement in 6-minute walk test; significant improvements in muscle strength and fatigue (n=12) 2. Primary Mitochondrial Myopathy (Negative for primary endpoint): TAZPOWER and MMPOWER-3 Phase 3 trials did not meet primary endpoint (6MWT at 24 weeks); post-hoc subgroup analyses showed improvements 3. Heart Failure (Mixed): PROGRESS-HF showed trends but not significant improvements in LVESV; preclinical data remains compelling 4. STEMI (Negative for primary endpoint): EMBRACE STEMI did not reduce CK-MB AUC; exploratory anterior MI subgroup showed signals 5. Age-Related Macular Degeneration (Promising): ReCLAIM Phase 2 showed improved low-luminance visual acuity; ReCLAIM-2 ongoing 6. Preclinical: Consistent efficacy in 15+ animal models including heart failure, ischemia-reperfusion, ALS, Parkinson's, kidney disease, muscle atrophy --- Key Human Trials Phase 3 Trials: Primary Mitochondrial Myopathy | Trial | NCT ID | N | Duration | Primary Endpoint | Result | |-------|--------|---|----------|------------------|--------| | TAZPOWER | NCT02805790 | 218 | 24 weeks | 6MWT change | Not met | | MMPOWER-3 | NCT03323749 | 120 | 24 weeks | 6MWT change | Not met | TAZPOWER (2021): 218 patients with genetically confirmed primary mitochondrial myopathy; randomized to elamipretide 40 mg SC daily or placebo. Primary endpoint (change in 6-minute walk test at 24 weeks) was not statistically significant. Long-term extension (144 weeks) showed durable improvements in completers. MMPOWER-3 (2023): Confirmatory Phase 3 trial with subcutaneous formulation. Primary endpoint not met. Post-hoc analyses identified potential responder populations. FDA Response: Complete Response Letter issued in 2021; requested additional data for PMM indication. Phase 2/3 Trial: Barth Syndrome | Parameter | Elamipretide | Placebo | p-value | |-----------|--------------|---------|---------| | N | 12 | Crossover | - | | Duration | 12 weeks | 12 weeks | - | | 6MWT improvement | +67 meters | - | <0.05 | | Muscle strength | Improved | - | <0.05 | | Fatigue score | Improved | - | <0.05 | Reference: Reid Thompson W et al. Genet Med. 2021 (PMID: 32994548) Why Barth is different: Barth syndrome is caused by mutations in TAZ gene, which encodes tafazzin - the enzyme responsible for cardiolipin remodeling. This creates a direct mechanistic rationale for SS-31, which targets cardiolipin function. Phase 2 Trials: Cardiovascular | Trial | NCT ID | N | Indication | Primary Endpoint | Result | |-------|--------|---|------------|------------------|--------| | EMBRACE STEMI | NCT02388464 | 118 | STEMI + PCI | CK-MB AUC | Not met | | PROGRESS-HF | NCT02814097 | 71 | HFrEF | LVESV change | Not met | | EVREST | NCT02693119 | 14 | Renal artery stenosis | Cortical perfusion | Positive | EMBRACE STEMI (2016): Elamipretide given IV at time of reperfusion in STEMI patients undergoing PCI. CK-MB AUC (marker of infarct size) was not significantly reduced. Exploratory analyses showed trends in anterior MI subgroup. PROGRESS-HF (2020): 71 patients with HFrEF (LVEF ≤40%) randomized to elamipretide or placebo for 28 days. Primary endpoint (LVESV change) not met. Secondary endpoints showed trends toward improved LVEF. Phase 2 Trials: Ophthalmology | Trial | NCT ID | N | Indication | Key Finding | |-------|--------|---|------------|-------------| | ReCLAIM | NCT03098797 | 40 | Dry AMD | Improved LLVA | | ReCLAIM-2 | NCT03891875 | 180 | Geographic atrophy | Ongoing | ReCLAIM (2019): Subcutaneous elamipretide in patients with dry age-related macular degeneration. Showed statistically significant improvement in low-luminance visual acuity (LLVA). Supported larger Phase 2b trial. ReCLAIM-2 (Ongoing): Phase 2b trial evaluating effect on geographic atrophy progression. Results expected 2026. --- Preclinical Evidence Animal Model Efficacy Summary | Disease Model | Species | Key Findings | PMID | |---------------|---------|--------------|------| | Heart failure | Dog | 3-month Rx improved LVEF, normalized mito respiration | 26839394 | | Ischemia-reperfusion | Rabbit/Sheep | 18-35% reduction in infarct size at reperfusion | 23130143 | | Hypertensive cardiomyopathy | Mouse | Prevented cardiac hypertrophy and fibrosis | 21620606 | | Renal ischemia | Pig | Restored renal blood flow and GFR; reduced fibrosis | 24947415 | | MPTP (Parkinson's) | Mouse | Protected dopaminergic neurons | 19203217 | | SOD1 ALS | Mouse | Extended survival, improved motor function | 16895581 | | Alzheimer's (APP) | Mouse | Restored mitochondrial transport, synaptic viability | 22791748 | | Aging skeletal muscle | Mouse | 1 hour treatment reversed age-related dysfunction | 23692507 | | Mechanical ventilation | Rat | Prevented diaphragm weakness | 21460706 | | Immobilization atrophy | Rat | Attenuated muscle loss by 30% | 21817113 | | Chemotherapy neuropathy | Mouse | Prevented paclitaxel-induced allodynia | 29578686 | | High-fat diet insulin resistance | Mouse | Prevented metabolic dysfunction | 19188683 | Key Preclinical Insights Aging and Muscle: - Single 1-hour SS-31 treatment in aged mice reversed mitochondrial dysfunction - Improved fatigue resistance and ATP production - Effects rapid and sustained Heart Failure: - 3-month treatment in canine HF model normalized mitochondrial respiration - Improved LVEF from 25% to 35% - Reduced LV end-systolic and end-diastolic volumes - Decreased reactive oxygen species generation Ischemia-Reperfusion: - Effective when administered at time of reperfusion (clinically relevant) - Reduced infarct size across multiple species - Protected mitochondrial function without affecting hemodynamics Neurodegeneration: - Efficacy in MPTP Parkinson's model (protected dopamine neurons) - Extended survival in ALS model - Restored mitochondrial transport in Alzheimer's model Translational Gap Despite strong preclinical efficacy, clinical translation has been challenging: | Factor | Preclinical | Clinical | |--------|-------------|----------| | Population | Homogeneous models | Heterogeneous patients | | Disease stage | Early/acute | Advanced/chronic | | Endpoints | Objective (infarct size) | Functional (6MWT) | | Duration | Days to weeks | Months | | Mitochondrial defect | Defined | Variable | --- Safety Profile Clinical Trial Safety Summary SS-31/Elamipretide has demonstrated a favorable safety profile across clinical trials: | Event | Incidence | Notes | |-------|-----------|-------| | Injection site reactions | 15-25% | Most common; mild erythema, pain | | Headache | 5-10% | Mild to moderate | | Fatigue | 3-8% | Transient | | Nausea | 3-5% | Generally mild | | Dizziness | 2-5% | Transient | Serious Adverse Events - No drug-related serious adverse events attributed to elamipretide in Phase 2/3 trials - No significant laboratory abnormalities - No cardiac safety signals (QTc, arrhythmias) - No immunogenicity concerns Long-Term Safety Long-term extension studies (up to 144 weeks) showed: - Continued favorable safety profile - No cumulative toxicity observed - Injection site reactions decreased over time Preclinical Safety - No genotoxicity in standard battery - No carcinogenicity signals in chronic studies - No reproductive/developmental toxicity observed - Wide therapeutic index in animal studies Populations Not Yet Studied - Pediatric patients (limited data) - Pregnancy and lactation - Severe renal or hepatic impairment - Drug-drug interactions not fully characterized --- Pharmacokinetics Key Parameters | Parameter | Value | Notes | |-----------|-------|-------| | Half-life | 1-3 hours (plasma) | Rapid tissue distribution | | Bioavailability | High (SC) | >80% estimated | | Tmax | 30-60 minutes | After SC administration | | Volume of distribution | Large | Extensive tissue penetration | | Protein binding | Low | Facilitates tissue distribution | | Metabolism | Peptidases | No CYP involvement | | Elimination | Renal | Parent and metabolites | Mitochondrial Pharmacokinetics Unique consideration: Plasma levels do not reflect tissue/mitochondrial concentrations. - SS-31 concentrates 1,000-5,000x in mitochondria - Rapid uptake (minutes) into mitochondria - Tissue half-life likely longer than plasma half-life - Cardiolipin binding creates functional "reservoir" Route of Administration | Route | Status | Notes | |-------|--------|-------| | Intravenous | Phase 2 | Used in acute settings (STEMI, HF) | | Subcutaneous | Phase 3 | Primary development route | | Topical/Ophthalmic | Exploratory | Not in clinical development | Clinical Implications - Once-daily SC dosing achieves steady state within days - No dose adjustment needed for mild-moderate renal impairment - Food does not affect absorption - No significant drug-drug interactions identified --- Regulatory Status | Region | Status | Notes | |--------|--------|-------| | USA (FDA) | Not approved | CRL issued 2021 for PMM indication | | EU (EMA) | Not approved | No MAA filed | | Japan (PMDA) | Not approved | Early development | | Orphan designation | Yes (FDA, EMA) | PMM, Barth syndrome, LHON | | Fast Track | Yes (FDA) | PMM | | Breakthrough Therapy | No | Not granted | FDA Complete Response Letter (2021) The FDA issued a Complete Response Letter for the PMM indication, citing: - Primary endpoint (6MWT) not met in TAZPOWER Phase 3 - Requested additional clinical data - Did not question safety profile Current Development Status | Indication | Phase | Status | |------------|-------|--------| | Primary mitochondrial myopathy | 3 | Completed; primary endpoint not met | | Barth syndrome | 2/3 | Completed; positive results | | Geographic atrophy (AMD) | 2b | Ongoing (ReCLAIM-2) | | Heart failure | 2 | Completed; no further development announced | | STEMI | 2a | Completed; no further development | | LHON | 2 | Completed | Regulatory Path Forward Stealth BioTherapeutics is pursuing: 1. Geographic atrophy: Results from ReCLAIM-2 expected to inform Phase 3 decision 2. Barth syndrome: Potential NDA pathway given orphan designation and positive data 3. PMM: Company evaluating options based on long-term extension data --- Related Peptides Szeto-Schiller Peptide Family SS-31 is one of several mitochondria-targeting peptides developed by the Szeto lab: | Peptide | Sequence | Key Feature | |---------|----------|-------------| | SS-31 | D-Arg-Dmt-Lys-Phe-NH2 | Clinical development | | SS-02 | Dmt-D-Arg-Phe-Lys-NH2 | Preclinical | | SS-20 | Phe-D-Arg-Phe-Lys-NH2 | Less potent variant | Comparison with Other Mitochondrial Therapeutics | Agent | Class | Mechanism | Clinical Status | |-------|-------|-----------|-----------------| | SS-31/Elamipretide | Peptide | Cardiolipin binding | Phase 3 | | MitoQ | Quinone | Matrix antioxidant | Supplement | | Idebenone | Quinone analog | CoQ10 analog | Approved (LHON, EU) | | CoQ10 | Cofactor | ETC electron carrier | Supplement | | NAD+ precursors | Vitamins | NAD+ restoration | Supplements/trials | | Urolithin A | Metabolite | Mitophagy | Phase 2 | SS-31 vs MitoQ | Property | SS-31 | MitoQ | |----------|-------|-------| | Target | Inner membrane (cardiolipin) | Matrix | | Mechanism | ETC optimization | ROS scavenging | | Concentration | 1000-5000x | 100-500x | | Clinical data | Phase 3 | Phase 2 (PD) | | Structure | Peptide | TPP+-linked quinone | SS-31 vs Idebenone | Property | SS-31 | Idebenone | |----------|-------|-----------| | Target | Inner membrane | Complex I bypass | | Approved? | No | Yes (EU, LHON) | | Indication | Multiple | LHON, muscular dystrophy | | Route | SC/IV | Oral | --- Current Research Directions Active Clinical Development | Indication | Trial | Phase | Status | Rationale | |------------|-------|-------|--------|-----------| | Geographic atrophy | ReCLAIM-2 | 2b | Active | RPE mitochondrial dysfunction | | Barth syndrome | Extension | OLE | Active | Direct cardiolipin mechanism | Academic Research Areas Aging and Longevity: - SS-31 rapidly reverses age-related mitochondrial dysfunction - Potential for "mitochondrial rejuvenation" - Studies in aged mice show rapid onset of benefit (hours) Heart Failure with Preserved Ejection Fraction (HFpEF): - Emerging interest given mitochondrial dysfunction in HFpEF - No clinical trials yet initiated Acute Kidney Injury: - Strong preclinical data in ischemia-reperfusion models - Academic interest in transplant nephrology Chemotherapy Protection: - Paclitaxel-induced neuropathy prevention in preclinical models - Potential for cardio-oncology applications Mechanistic Research Ongoing investigations: - Precise stoichiometry of SS-31:cardiolipin binding - Effects on supercomplex assembly dynamics - Tissue-specific pharmacokinetics - Biomarkers of mitochondrial response Future Directions 1. Better patient selection: Identifying mitochondrial dysfunction biomarkers 2. Optimal endpoints: Beyond 6MWT to capture mitochondrial-specific effects 3. Combination therapy: With other mitochondrial-targeting agents 4. Earlier disease stages: When mitochondria are still rescuable --- Citation Table Mechanism and Discovery | Source | Year | Type | PMID | Key Finding | Access | |--------|------|------|------|-------------|--------| | Zhao K et al.
