name: Sulanemadlin
summary: First-in-class stapled alpha-helical peptide and dual MDM2/MDMX inhibitor that reactivates wild-type p53 tumor suppressor function. Phase 1/2 trials demonstrated 59% disease control rate with notably low hematologic toxicity compared to MDM2-only inhibitors. Novel chemoprotection application being developed for retinoblastoma with Advancium Biosciences partnership.
content: Overview Overview Quick Summary - First-in-class stapled alpha-helical peptide that disrupts MDM2/MDMX-p53 interactions - Dual inhibitor targeting both MDM2 and MDMX (unlike small molecule MDM2-only inhibitors) - Phase 1/2 completed: 59% disease control rate in TP53 wild-type solid tumors and lymphomas - Low hematologic toxicity: Does not cause severe neutropenia/thrombocytopenia seen with MDM2-only inhibitors - Novel chemoprotection application: Induces transient, reversible cell cycle arrest in normal cells to protect from chemotherapy - Retinoblastoma development: Partnered with Advancium Biosciences for pediatric eye cancer indication - Developed by: Aileron Therapeutics (Cambridge, MA) Evidence Strength: MODERATE — Phase 1/2 completed with published efficacy and safety data. Unique mechanism with favorable hematologic safety profile. Pivotal trials and regulatory approval pending. -------|-------| | Generic name | Sulanemadlin | | Development codes | ALRN-6924, MP-4897 | | Chemical class | Stapled alpha-helical peptide | | Target | Dual MDM2/MDMX inhibitor | | Molecular weight | 2,100 Da | | Structure | Hydrocarbon-stapled i, i+7 alpha-helix mimicking p53 transactivation domain | | Developer | Aileron Therapeutics, Inc. | Discovery & Development Timeline | Year | Milestone | Significance | |------|-----------|--------------| | 1979 | p53 discovered | Lane and Crawford; Linzer and Levine independently identify p53 protein | | 1989 | p53 as tumor suppressor | Vogelstein et al. establish p53 as critical tumor suppressor | | 1992 | MDM2-p53 interaction | Momand et al. identify MDM2 as negative regulator of p53 PMID: 1535557 | | 1996 | MDMX (MDM4) identified | Shvarts et al. discover second p53 regulator PMID: 8943037 | | 2000 | Stapled peptide technology | Verdine lab (Harvard) develops hydrocarbon stapling method | | 2007 | Aileron Therapeutics founded | Company established to develop stapled peptide therapeutics | | 2010 | ALRN-6924 discovery | Lead optimization of stapled p53 peptides | | 2013 | IND-enabling studies | Preclinical development completed | | 2016 | Phase 1 initiated | First-in-human study NCT02264613 begins | | 2017 | First efficacy signals | Preliminary responses observed in Phase 1 | | 2018 | Phase 2a expansion | Solid tumors and lymphoma cohorts added | | 2019 | Chemoprotection concept | Phase 1 study NCT02264613 chemoprotection arm initiated | | 2020 | Phase 1b combination | Studies with palbociclib, paclitaxel initiated | | 2021 | Clinical data published | Phase 1/2 results published in peer-reviewed journals | | 2022 | Chemoprotection data | Normal cell protection from chemotherapy demonstrated | | 2023 | Advancium partnership | License agreement for retinoblastoma development | | 2024 | Ongoing development | Multiple trials active; regulatory strategy refinement | | 2025 | Current status | Phase 2 development continues; retinoblastoma program advancing | Why Sulanemadlin Was Developed The p53 Problem: - p53 is mutated or inactivated in 50% of all human cancers - In the remaining 50% with wild-type p53, the protein is often functionally suppressed by overexpression of MDM2 and/or MDMX - MDM2 and MDMX bind to p53's transactivation domain, blocking its tumor suppressor function Why a Stapled Peptide: 1. Protein-protein interactions (PPIs) — MDM2/MDMX-p53 binding involves a large, shallow interface that is difficult for small molecules to disrupt effectively, especially MDMX 2. Dual targeting requirement — Small molecule MDM2 inhibitors (nutlins, idasanutlin) have limited MDMX activity. Cancer cells can escape through MDMX overexpression 3. Stapling technology — Hydrocarbon staples: - Stabilize the alpha-helical structure that mimics native p53 - Increase cell permeability (peptides are typically cell-impermeable) - Improve proteolytic stability and half-life - Enable oral bioavailability potential Original therapeutic target: TP53 wild-type solid tumors and hematologic malignancies. Chemoprotection application emerged as secondary but potentially transformative indication. --- Mechanism of Action How Sulanemadlin Works (Plain English) Think of p53 as your body's "guardian of the genome" - a protein that watches for DNA damage and decides whether to repair cells or eliminate them if they're too damaged. Cancer cells need to disable this guardian to survive. 1. The normal process — When p53 is working properly, it detects damaged or abnormal cells and either pauses their growth (allowing repair) or triggers their self-destruction (apoptosis). This prevents cancer. 2. How cancers cheat — Many cancers produce too much of proteins called MDM2 and MDMX. These proteins act like handcuffs, grabbing onto p53 and preventing it from doing its job. The cancer cells effectively "arrest" the guardian. 3. What sulanemadlin does — Sulanemadlin is designed to look like the part of p53 that MDM2 and MDMX grab onto. It acts as a decoy, binding to MDM2 and MDMX instead of p53. This frees p53 to resume its tumor-suppressing activity. 4. Why it's special — Previous drugs could only block MDM2. Sulanemadlin blocks BOTH MDM2 and MDMX at the same time. This is important because cancer cells can adapt by making more MDMX when MDM2 is blocked. 5. The chemoprotection twist — Normal healthy cells also have active p53. When sulanemadlin is given before chemotherapy, it briefly activates p53 in normal cells, causing them to pause and "hide" during chemotherapy. Cancer cells (with mutant or suppressed p53) can't do this, so only they get killed by chemo. --- Receptor Targets: MDM2 and MDMX (Scientific Detail) Sulanemadlin is a dual antagonist of the MDM2-p53 and MDMX-p53 protein-protein interactions. It directly binds to the p53-binding pockets of both MDM2 and MDMX. Target Binding Affinities: | Target | Kd (nM) | Selectivity | What This Means | |--------|---------|-------------|-----------------| | MDM2 | 1 nM | High | Very tight binding to MDM2's p53 pocket | | MDMX | 10 nM | High | Potent MDMX binding (unlike small molecules) | | p53 binding displaced | Both | Complete | Liberates p53 from both suppressors | Structural Basis: Stapled Peptide Design Sulanemadlin contains a hydrocarbon staple spanning positions i to i+7 on the alpha-helix. This: - Stabilizes the bioactive conformation — Locks the peptide in the shape that fits MDM2/MDMX - Increases proteolytic stability — Resists breakdown by cellular enzymes - Enhances cell penetration — Allows the peptide to cross cell membranes - Extends half-life — Improves pharmacokinetics for clinical use Signaling Pathway Overview Primary pathway: Sulanemadlin administration | v Binds MDM2 + MDMX with high affinity | v p53 released from MDM2/MDMX suppression | v p53 activated (phosphorylated, stabilized) | v +-- Transcriptional activation of target genes: | +-- p21 (CDKN1A) --> Cell cycle arrest (G1/S checkpoint) | +-- PUMA, BAX, NOXA --> Apoptosis | +-- MDM2 (negative feedback) --> Limits p53 activity | +-- GADD45 --> DNA repair In simpler terms: Sulanemadlin removes the "brakes" that cancer cells put on p53. Once freed, p53 activates genes that stop cell division and trigger cancer cell death. In normal cells, p53 activation causes temporary growth arrest (protective), while in cancer cells it causes apoptosis (therapeutic). Supported Mechanisms (Direct Human Evidence) p53 Reactivation: - Dose-dependent increase in serum MIC-1/GDF-15 (p53 target gene) in Phase 1 PMID: 31852831 - Induction of p21 and PUMA in tumor biopsies - Apoptosis markers elevated in responding patients Dual MDM2/MDMX Inhibition: - Activity maintained in MDMX-overexpressing cell lines - Overcomes resistance mechanisms seen with MDM2-only inhibitors - p53 pathway activation confirmed even in high-MDMX tumors Chemoprotection Mechanism: - Transient G1 arrest in normal cells (bone marrow, GI epithelium) - Reversible within 24-48 hours after sulanemadlin clearance - Does not protect TP53-mutant cancer cells - Demonstrated reduction in chemotherapy-induced neutropenia PMID: 35789377 Key Advantage Over Small Molecule MDM2 Inhibitors | Parameter | MDM2-Only Inhibitors | Sulanemadlin | |-----------|---------------------|--------------| | MDM2 binding | High | High | | MDMX binding | Weak/None | High | | Hematologic toxicity | Severe (dose-limiting) | Minimal | | Resistance via MDMX | Common | Overcome | | p53-mutant tumors | Ineffective | Ineffective | Why lower hematologic toxicity? Small molecule MDM2 inhibitors cause severe neutropenia and thrombocytopenia because they activate p53 strongly in bone marrow progenitors. Sulanemadlin's balanced MDM2/MDMX inhibition and pharmacokinetic profile result in less sustained p53 activation in marrow, preserving hematopoiesis. --- Key Human Trials Phase 1/2 Monotherapy (NCT02264613) The pivotal Phase 1/2 trial evaluated sulanemadlin in patients with TP53 wild-type advanced solid tumors and lymphomas. Study Design: | Parameter | Value | |-----------|-------| | NCT ID | NCT02264613 ( | | Phase | 1/2 | | Design | Dose escalation + expansion cohorts | | Population | TP53 wild-type solid tumors and lymphomas | | Route | Intravenous (IV) infusion | | Schedule | Days 1, 4, 8, 11 of 21-day cycles | | Enrollment | 120 patients | | Sites | Multiple US centers | Efficacy Results PMID: 31852831, PMID: 35789377 : | Outcome | Result | Notes | |---------|--------|-------| | Disease control rate (DCR) | 59% | CR + PR + SD | | Best response in lymphoma | Up to 50% ORR | T-cell and B-cell lymphomas | | Best response in solid tumors | Stable disease common | Durable in some patients | | Duration of response | Variable | Some responses >6 months | | p53 pathway activation | Confirmed | MIC-1/GDF-15 increased dose-dependently | Safety Results: | Adverse Event | Incidence | Grade 3-4 | |---------------|-----------|-----------| | Nausea | 45% | 2% | | Vomiting | 30% | 1% | | Fatigue | 35% | 5% | | Diarrhea | 25% | 2% | | Anemia | 20% | 5% | | Neutropenia | 12% | 3% | | Thrombocytopenia | 10% | 2% | Key Finding: Low Hematologic Toxicity The remarkably low rates of severe neutropenia (3% Grade 3-4) and thrombocytopenia (2% Grade 3-4) distinguish sulanemadlin from small molecule MDM2 inhibitors, which typically cause dose-limiting myelosuppression in 30-50% of patients. Phase 1 Chemoprotection Study (NCT02264613 Arm) A novel application explored using sulanemadlin to protect normal cells from chemotherapy toxicity. Concept: - Administer sulanemadlin 24-48 hours before chemotherapy - p53 activation induces transient G1 arrest in normal cells (bone marrow, GI) - Normal cells "hide" during peak chemotherapy exposure - Cancer cells with mutant/suppressed p53 remain in cycle and are killed - Sulanemadlin effect wears off; normal cells resume proliferation Results PMID: 35789377 : | Parameter | Without Sulanemadlin | With Sulanemadlin | |-----------|---------------------|-------------------| | Grade 4 neutropenia | 63% | 21% | | Neutropenia duration | 7-10 days | 3-5 days | | Thrombocytopenia | Common | Reduced | | Chemotherapy dose intensity | Often reduced | Maintained | Significance: Sulanemadlin may enable delivery of full-dose chemotherapy while reducing toxicity - a paradigm shift in supportive oncology. Phase 1b Combination Studies With Palbociclib (CDK4/6 inhibitor): - Rationale: CDK4/6 inhibitors also induce G1 arrest; potential synergy - Status: Early-phase evaluation completed - Results: Tolerability established; efficacy signal observed With Paclitaxel: - Rationale: Chemoprotection application - Status: Evaluated in NCT02264613 expansion - Results: Reduced hematologic toxicity with maintained antitumor activity Ongoing/Planned Trials | Indication | Phase | NCT ID | Status | Notes | |------------|-------|--------|--------|-------| | Solid tumors | 1/2 | NCT02264613 | Ongoing | Multiple expansion cohorts | | Lymphoma | 2 | NCT02264613 | Completed | TP53wt PTCL, CTCL | | Retinoblastoma | 1/2 | Planned | Advancium | Intravitreal delivery | | Chemoprotection | 2 | Planned | Aileron | Multiple tumor types | --- Preclinical Evidence Animal Studies | Study Type | Model | Key Findings | Reference | |------------|-------|--------------|-----------| | Efficacy | SJSA-1 osteosarcoma xenograft | Dose-dependent tumor regression | PMID: 23946421 | | Efficacy | MCF-7 breast cancer xenograft | Growth inhibition | Aileron data | | Mechanism | Multiple TP53wt cell lines | p53 stabilization, p21/PUMA induction | PMID: 23946421 | | PK/PD | Mouse, rat, monkey | Linear PK; p53 target engagement confirmed | FDA filing | | Safety | GLP toxicology | Acceptable safety profile in primates | IND package | | Chemoprotection | Mouse models | Bone marrow protection from 5-FU, cyclophosphamide | PMID: 35789377 | In Vitro Studies Binding Studies: - Kd 1 nM for MDM2; 10 nM for MDMX - Competitive inhibition of p53 binding to both targets - Alpha-helical structure confirmed by CD spectroscopy Cell-Based Studies: - IC50 10-100 nM in TP53wt cancer cell lines - Inactive in TP53-mutant lines (as expected) - Synergistic with chemotherapy in TP53wt cells - Antagonistic with chemotherapy in chemoprotection setting (normal cells protected) Selectivity: - No significant activity at kinases, GPCRs, or ion channels - Mechanism-based activity (p53-dependent) - TP53-mutant tumors serve as negative control --- Safety Profile Common Adverse Events Data from Phase 1/2 clinical trials: | Adverse Event | Incidence (All Grades) | Grade 3-4 | Time Course | |---------------|------------------------|-----------|-------------| | Nausea | 45% | 2% | During infusion; manageable | | Vomiting | 30% | 1% | During/after infusion | | Fatigue | 35% | 5% | Cumulative | | Diarrhea | 25% | 2% | Variable | | Decreased appetite | 20% | 1% | Common | | Infusion reactions | 10% | <1% | First infusions | Hematologic Safety (Key Differentiator) | Event | Sulanemadlin | MDM2-Only Inhibitors | |-------|--------------|---------------------| | Any neutropenia | 12% | 60-80% | | Grade 3-4 neutropenia | 3% | 30-50% | | Thrombocytopenia (any) | 10% | 40-60% | | Grade 3-4 thrombocytopenia | 2% | 20-30% | | Dose-limiting hematotoxicity | Rare | Common | Why the difference? The dual MDM2/MDMX mechanism and peptide pharmacokinetics result in p53 activation kinetics that are less toxic to bone marrow progenitors while maintaining antitumor activity. Serious Adverse Events - Tumor lysis syndrome: Rare; seen in lymphomas with rapid responses - QTc prolongation: Not observed (unlike some MDM2 inhibitors) - Hepatotoxicity: Transient transaminase elevations; not dose-limiting - Hypersensitivity: Infusion reactions manageable with premedication Contraindications & Precautions Contraindications: 1. TP53-mutant tumors — Mechanism requires functional wild-type p53 2. Known hypersensitivity to sulanemadlin or excipients Precautions: 1. Confirm TP53 status — Molecular testing required before treatment 2. Monitor for GI toxicity — Antiemetic prophylaxis recommended 3. Hepatic monitoring — LFTs at baseline and on-treatment 4. Pregnancy — Expected teratogenic based on mechanism; contraindicated Special Populations | Population | Recommendation | Notes | |------------|----------------|-------| | Renal impairment | Caution | Limited data | | Hepatic impairment | Caution | Peptide metabolism may be affected | | Elderly | No specific adjustment | Included in trials | | Pediatric | Under investigation | Retinoblastoma program | --- Pharmacokinetics Key Parameters | Parameter | Value | Notes | |-----------|-------|-------| | Route | Intravenous | Current formulation | | Half-life | 4-8 hours | Supports twice-weekly dosing | | Volume of distribution | 10-15 L | Moderate tissue distribution | | Protein binding | 60-70% | Primarily albumin | | Metabolism | Proteolytic | Non-CYP mediated | | Elimination | Renal + hepatic | Peptide fragments | | Drug interactions | Low potential | Non-CYP metabolism | Pharmacodynamic Markers | Marker | Effect | Utility | |--------|--------|---------| | MIC-1/GDF-15 (serum) | Increases with dose | Pharmacodynamic biomarker | | p21 (tumor) | Induced | p53 activation marker | | PUMA (tumor) | Induced | Apoptosis marker | | MDM2 (tumor) | Induced | Negative feedback; confirms activity | Dosing Considerations Current clinical regimen: - Days 1, 4, 8, 11 of 21-day cycle - IV infusion over 60 minutes - Dose escalated based on toxicity Chemoprotection timing: - Administered 24-48 hours before chemotherapy - p53 activation induces G1 arrest - Normal cells protected during chemo exposure - Effect reversible after sulanemadlin clearance --- Regulatory Status Current Status by Region | Region | Status | Notes | |--------|--------|-------| | USA (FDA) | Investigational | Phase 1/2 completed; no NDA filed | | EU (EMA) | Not submitted | US development focus | | Japan (PMDA) | Not submitted | Potential future market | | Orphan designation | Under consideration | Retinoblastoma indication | | Fast Track | Potential | Novel mechanism; unmet need | Regulatory Designations | Designation | Status | Indication | |-------------|--------|------------| | Orphan Drug | Potential | Retinoblastoma | | Rare Pediatric Disease | Potential | Retinoblastoma | | Fast Track | Not yet granted | Various | | Breakthrough Therapy | Not yet granted | Chemoprotection | Development Partnership Advancium Biosciences Partnership (2023): - Exclusive license for retinoblastoma - Intravitreal delivery development - Pediatric formulation - Global rights for eye cancer indication - Milestone-based structure --- Related Peptides MDM2/MDMX Inhibitor Landscape | Compound | Class | MDM2 | MDMX | Status | Developer | |----------|-------|------|------|--------|-----------| | Sulanemadlin | Stapled peptide | +++ | +++ | Phase 2 | Aileron | | Idasanutlin | Small molecule | +++ | + | Phase 3 (term.) | Roche | | Navtemadlin | Small molecule | +++ | + | Phase 2 | Kartos | | Milademetan | Small molecule | +++ | + | Phase 2 | Rain | | APG-115 | Small molecule | +++ | + | Phase 2 | Ascentage | | SAR405838 | Small molecule | +++ | + | Discontinued | Sanofi | Sulanemadlin vs Small Molecule MDM2 Inhibitors | Feature | Sulanemadlin | Small Molecules | |---------|--------------|-----------------| | MDM2 inhibition | High | High | | MDMX inhibition | High | Low/None | | Hematologic toxicity | Low | High (DLT) | | Drug class | Stapled peptide | Nutlin derivatives | | Route | IV (current) | Oral (most) | | Resistance pattern | Less MDMX escape | MDMX upregulation | | Development stage | Phase 2 | Phase 2-3 | Why MDMX Inhibition Matters Small molecule MDM2 inhibitors have repeatedly faced challenges in clinical development: 1. Dose-limiting hematotoxicity — Severe neutropenia/thrombocytopenia limits dose escalation 2. MDMX-mediated resistance — Tumors upregulate MDMX to maintain p53 suppression 3. Incomplete p53 activation — MDM2 inhibition alone may be insufficient Sulanemadlin addresses all three issues through dual MDM2/MDMX targeting. --- Current Research Directions Active Development Programs 1. Direct Antitumor Therapy: - TP53 wild-type solid tumors and lymphomas - Expansion cohorts in peripheral T-cell lymphoma (PTCL) - Potential combination with checkpoint inhibitors 2. Chemoprotection: - Paradigm-shifting application - Protecting bone marrow from cytotoxic chemotherapy - Enables higher chemotherapy dose intensity - Multiple tumor types being evaluated 3. Retinoblastoma (Advancium): - Intravitreal delivery development - Pediatric population - Orphan indication with high unmet need - TP53wt tumor type well-suited to mechanism Key Unknowns & Future Questions 1. Optimal patient selection — Biomarkers beyond TP53 status? 2. Combination strategies — Best partners (chemo, targeted, IO)? 3. Chemoprotection breadth — Which chemotherapy regimens? 4. Resistance mechanisms — Long-term durability? 5. Oral formulation — Feasibility for stapled peptides? --- Evidence Library Primary Clinical Studies | Citation | PMID | Year | Type | Key Finding | Access | |----------|------|------|------|-------------|--------| | Saleh MN et al. name: Survodutide
summary: A dual GLP-1/glucagon receptor agonist developed by Boehringer Ingelheim/Zealand Pharma, currently in Phase 3 trials for obesity and MASH. Phase 2 data show 18.7% weight loss and 83% MASH resolution, with unique liver-targeting benefits from glucagon receptor agonism.
content: Overview Quick Summary - Dual GLP-1/glucagon receptor agonist in Phase 3 development for obesity and MASH - Developer: Boehringer Ingelheim in partnership with Zealand Pharma - Not yet FDA approved - currently in Phase 3 clinical trials (SYNCHRONIZE and ACHIEVE programs) - Phase 2 obesity results: 18.7% weight loss at 46 weeks with 4.8mg dose - Phase 2 MASH results: 83% MASH resolution and 64.5% fibrosis improvement at 48 weeks - potentially best-in-class for liver disease - Differentiated mechanism: Glucagon component provides direct liver effects that GLP-1-only agents lack - GI tolerability: Similar to GLP-1 class; manageable with dose titration Evidence Strength: MODERATE - Strong Phase 2 data across obesity, T2D, and MASH. Phase 3 trials ongoing (SYNCHRONIZE for obesity, ACHIEVE for MASH). No regulatory approval yet. Mechanism well-characterized with unique hepatic benefits from glucagon receptor agonism. -------|-------| | Generic name | Survodutide | | Development code | BI 456906, ZP6590 | | Chemical class | Dual GLP-1/glucagon receptor agonist (acylated peptide) | | Molecular type | Synthetic peptide analog | | Developer | Boehringer Ingelheim (in partnership with Zealand Pharma) | | Administration | Subcutaneous injection, once weekly | Discovery & Development Timeline | Year | Milestone | Significance | |------|-----------|--------------| | 2018 | License agreement | Boehringer Ingelheim licenses ZP6590 from Zealand Pharma | | 2019 | Phase 1 initiated | First-in-human studies (NCT04043793) | | 2020 | Phase 1 completed | Established safety, PK ( 6-7 day half-life), weekly dosing PMID: 35913766 | | 2021 | Phase 2 T2D initiated | Studies vs semaglutide comparator (NCT04153929) | | 2022 | Phase 2 obesity initiated | Dose-ranging in obesity (NCT05179576) | | 2023 | Phase 2 T2D results published | Superior weight loss vs semaglutide 1mg PMID: 36702559 | | 2024 | Phase 2 obesity results | 18.7% weight loss at 46 weeks PMID: 37840093 | | 2024 | Phase 2 MASH results | 83% MASH resolution, 64.5% fibrosis improvement PMID: 38507727 | | 2023-24 | Phase 3 initiated | SYNCHRONIZE (obesity) and ACHIEVE (MASH) programs | | 2024 | Phase 3 CVOT initiated | SYNCHRONIZE-CVOT for cardiovascular outcomes | | 2027 | Expected | Potential FDA approval (estimated based on trial timelines) | Why Survodutide Was Developed The rationale for dual GLP-1/glucagon agonism addresses limitations of GLP-1-only agents: 1. Liver targeting: The liver has abundant glucagon receptors but essentially NO GLP-1 receptors. This means GLP-1-only drugs like semaglutide cannot directly affect hepatic metabolism. 2. Enhanced energy expenditure: Glucagon increases thermogenesis via brown adipose tissue and hepatic fat oxidation. This adds an "output" component to the "input" reduction from GLP-1. 3. Direct anti-steatotic effects: Glucagon promotes hepatic lipid oxidation and reduces de novo lipogenesis, potentially superior for NASH/MASH treatment. 4. Complementary mechanisms: The GLP-1 component maintains glycemic balance, preventing the hyperglycemia that would occur with glucagon alone. Original therapeutic target: Obesity and type 2 diabetes, with particular focus on NASH/MASH given the unique hepatic mechanism. --- Mechanism of Action How Survodutide Works (Plain English) Survodutide is like having two medications in one. Here's what each part does: 1. GLP-1 component (same as Ozempic/Wegovy): - Tells your brain you're full after eating less food - Helps your pancreas release insulin when blood sugar is high - Slows down digestion so you stay full longer 2. Glucagon component (what makes survodutide unique): - Tells your liver to burn fat instead of storing it - Increases your metabolic rate - you burn more calories even at rest - May help reverse fatty liver disease by directly working on liver cells Why adding glucagon matters: Think of weight loss as having two levers - eating less (input) and burning more (output). GLP-1-only drugs mainly pull the "eating less" lever. Survodutide pulls BOTH levers simultaneously. The glucagon part revs up your internal furnace, particularly in your liver. The liver connection: Your liver is packed with glucagon receptors but has almost NO GLP-1 receptors. This is why survodutide may be especially powerful for fatty liver disease - it can "talk" directly to liver cells in a way that Ozempic cannot. Why doesn't glucagon raise blood sugar? In theory, glucagon should increase blood sugar (that's what it normally does). However, the GLP-1 component counterbalances this by boosting insulin. The net result: improved blood sugar control alongside greater weight loss. --- Receptor Targets: GLP-1R + GCGR (Scientific Detail) Survodutide is a balanced dual agonist of the glucagon-like peptide-1 receptor (GLP-1R) and glucagon receptor (GCGR). Receptor Potency: | Receptor | Relative Potency | Clinical Significance | |----------|------------------|----------------------| | GLP-1R | 1x native GLP-1 | Matches established GLP-1 agonist effects | | GCGR | 0.5-1x native glucagon | Provides hepatic and thermogenic effects | Note: Unlike tirzepatide (which is imbalanced toward GIP), survodutide has relatively balanced GLP-1R/GCGR activity. This differs from retatrutide, which targets all three receptors (GIP/GLP-1/glucagon). Tissue-Specific Signaling Survodutide administration | +--> GLP-1R PATHWAY | | | +--> Hypothalamus: POMC activation, NPY/AgRP inhibition | | --> Reduced appetite, increased satiety | | | +--> Pancreatic beta cells: Glucose-dependent insulin secretion | | --> Improved glycemic control | | | +--> Pancreatic alpha cells: Glucagon suppression (when glucose high) | | --> Prevents excessive hepatic glucose output | | | +--> GI tract: Delayed gastric emptying | --> Prolonged fullness, reduced postprandial glucose spikes | +--> GCGR PATHWAY (The Differentiator) | +--> LIVER (rich in GCGR; NO GLP-1R!) | +--> Increased hepatic lipid oxidation | +--> Decreased de novo lipogenesis | +--> Reduced hepatic steatosis | +--> Anti-fibrotic effects | --> MASH resolution mechanism | +--> Brown adipose tissue | +--> Increased thermogenesis | --> Enhanced energy expenditure | +--> Hepatic gluconeogenesis +--> Increases glucose production --> Balanced by GLP-1R-mediated insulin The Glucagon Advantage: Why Liver Disease Responds Critical insight: The liver expresses abundant GCGR but essentially NO GLP-1R. | Tissue | GLP-1R | GCGR | What This Means | |--------|--------|------|-----------------| | Hypothalamus | High | Low | GLP-1 drives satiety | | Pancreatic beta cells | High | Low | GLP-1 drives insulin | | Liver | None | High | Only glucagon can directly affect liver | | Adipose (white) | Low | Moderate | Both contribute to lipid metabolism | | Adipose (brown) | Low | High | Glucagon drives thermogenesis | This is why survodutide achieves exceptional liver fat reduction (82-86% in Phase 2 MASH trial) compared to GLP-1-only agents. Supported Mechanisms (Direct Human Evidence) Glycemic control: - Glucose-dependent insulinotropic effect via GLP-1R PMID: 36702559 - Net improvement in glycemia despite glucagon component Weight loss: - Reduced caloric intake via central GLP-1R activation PMID: 37840093 - Enhanced energy expenditure via GCGR-mediated thermogenesis - Preferential fat oxidation in liver Hepatic effects: - 82-86% liver fat reduction at 48 weeks PMID: 38507727 - 83% MASH resolution vs 18% placebo - 64.5% fibrosis improvement vs 25.9% placebo - Direct GCGR-mediated hepatic lipid oxidation Hypothesized Mechanisms (Plausible but Not Yet Proven) | Hypothesis | Supporting Evidence | Confidence | |------------|--------------------|-----------| | Anti-fibrotic effects independent of weight | High MASH resolution rate | Moderate | | Superior CV outcomes vs GLP-1-only | Mechanistic rationale | Low (CVOT ongoing) | | Preservation of lean mass | Glucagon promotes fat oxidation | Low (body composition data pending) | | Enhanced insulin sensitivity | Phase 2 glucose clamp data | Moderate | --- Too Much vs Too Little: Physiologic Context Native GLP-1 and Glucagon Physiology GLP-1 physiology: - Secreted by intestinal L-cells after eating - Half-life: 2 minutes (rapidly degraded by DPP-4) - Contributes 35-40% of the incretin effect Glucagon physiology: - Secreted by pancreatic alpha cells during fasting - Half-life: 3-6 minutes - Normally OPPOSES insulin: raises blood glucose - Key role in fasting metabolism and energy mobilization The Glucagon Paradox in Obesity/MASH Traditional view: Glucagon = bad for diabetes (raises glucose) Modern understanding: Glucagon deficiency in the liver contributes to metabolic dysfunction: - Reduced hepatic fat oxidation - Accumulation of triglycerides (fatty liver) - Impaired mitochondrial function Therapeutic insight: Controlled glucagon receptor activation, balanced by GLP-1, may restore healthy hepatic metabolism while maintaining glycemic control. Supraphysiologic Dual Agonism Survodutide produces: - GLP-1 exposure comparable to semaglutide/tirzepatide - Physiologically relevant GCGR activation without excessive hyperglycemia - Net result: the metabolic benefits of both pathways --- Key Human Trials Phase 2 in Obesity (Nature Medicine 2024) NCT05179576 Publication: le Roux CW et al. Nature Medicine. 2024 PMID: 37840093 | Parameter | Value | |-----------|-------| | N | 387 | | Population | Adults with BMI >=30 (or >=27 with comorbidity), without diabetes | | Duration | 46 weeks | | Design | Randomized, double-blind, placebo-controlled, dose-ranging | Weight Loss Results: | Dose | Weight Loss | vs Placebo | |------|-------------|------------| | 0.6 mg | -6.2% | p<0.001 | | 2.4 mg | -12.5% | p<0.001 | | 3.6 mg | -14.9% | p<0.001 | | 4.8 mg | -18.7% | p<0.001 | | Placebo | -2.1% | -- | Key findings: - 81% of participants on 4.8mg achieved >=10% weight loss (vs 14% placebo) - 63% achieved >=15% weight loss - Weight loss trajectory not yet plateaued at 46 weeks - GI adverse events dose-dependent but manageable Phase 2 in MASH (NEJM 2024) - Landmark Trial NCT04771273 Publication: Sanyal AJ et al. New England Journal of Medicine. 2024 PMID: 38507727 | Parameter | Value | |-----------|-------| | N | 293 | | Population | Adults with biopsy-confirmed MASH, fibrosis stage F1-F3 | | Duration | 48 weeks | | Primary endpoints | MASH resolution without worsening fibrosis; Fibrosis improvement | Histological Outcomes: | Dose | MASH Resolution | Fibrosis Improvement | Both Endpoints | |------|-----------------|---------------------|----------------| | 2.4 mg | 52% | 43% | 31% | | 4.8 mg | 62% | 49% | 36% | | 6.0 mg | 83% | 64.5% | 52% | | Placebo | 18% | 25.9% | 8% | Significance: These MASH resolution rates are the highest reported for any drug in Phase 2. For context: - Semaglutide Phase 2 NASH: 59% resolution - Resmetirom (Rezdiffra): 30% resolution (but FDA approved) - Survodutide 6.0mg: 83% resolution Why these results matter: The liver cannot respond directly to GLP-1 because it lacks GLP-1 receptors. Survodutide's glucagon component directly targets the liver, potentially explaining the superior MASH outcomes. Phase 2 in Type 2 Diabetes (Lancet D&E 2023) NCT04153929 Publication: Nauck MA et al. Lancet Diabetes Endocrinol. 2023 PMID: 36702559 | Parameter | Value | |-----------|-------| | N | 411 | | Population | Adults with T2D on metformin | | Duration | 16 weeks | | Comparator | Semaglutide 1mg (active control) | Results: | Outcome | Survodutide 3.6mg | Semaglutide 1mg | Placebo | |---------|-------------------|-----------------|---------| | HbA1c change | -1.9% | -1.4% | -0.1% | | Weight change | -9.6% | -5.1% | -0.6% | Key finding: Survodutide showed numerically greater weight loss than semaglutide 1mg at 16 weeks, supporting the dual mechanism hypothesis. Phase 1 First-in-Human (DOM 2022) NCT04043793 Publication: Parker VE et al. Diabetes Obes Metab. 2022 PMID: 35913766 - N = 80 (healthy volunteers + T2D patients) - Established safety profile and tolerability - Half-life 6-7 days supports weekly dosing - Linear pharmacokinetics - Dose-dependent glucose lowering and weight loss Phase 3 Program (Ongoing) | Trial | NCT ID | Population | N | Status | Expected Completion | |-------|--------|------------|---|--------|---------------------| | SYNCHRONIZE-1 | NCT05642455 | Obesity without T2D | 1,500 | Active | 2026 | | SYNCHRONIZE-2 | NCT05642468 | Obesity with T2D | 1,400 | Active | 2026 | | ACHIEVE | NCT05486065 | MASH with fibrosis | 1,600 | Active | 2026-27 | | SYNCHRONIZE-CVOT | NCT05607667 | Obesity + CVD | 10,000 | Recruiting | 2029+ | --- Preclinical Evidence Animal Studies | Study Type | Model | Key Findings | Relevance | |------------|-------|--------------|-----------| | Pharmacology | Mouse/Rat | High affinity for both GLP-1R and GCGR | Confirms dual mechanism | | Efficacy | Diet-induced obese mice | Superior weight loss vs GLP-1 agonist alone | Supports dual agonist hypothesis | | Liver effects | NASH mouse models | Reduced hepatic steatosis, fibrosis markers | Mechanistic support for MASH | | Energy expenditure | Rodent | Increased oxygen consumption, thermogenesis | Glucagon-mediated caloric burn | | Toxicology | Rodent/Primate | Standard GLP-1 class findings (thyroid C-cells in rodents) | Informs safety monitoring | In Vitro Studies - High-affinity binding to both human GLP-1R and GCGR - Balanced agonist activity (differs from imbalanced tirzepatide) - Fatty acid acylation enables albumin binding and extended half-life - cAMP signaling confirmed at both receptors Limitations of Preclinical Data - Rodent glucagon receptor biology may differ from humans - Long-term safety extrapolation limited - NASH mouse models imperfectly recapitulate human disease --- Safety Profile Common Adverse Events (Phase 2 Data) | Event | Incidence (4.8mg) | Notes | |-------|-------------------|-------| | Nausea | 35-45% | Most common; decreases over time | | Vomiting | 15-25% | Higher during dose escalation | | Diarrhea | 20-30% | Usually mild-moderate | | Constipation | 10-15% | Less common than other GI effects | | Decreased appetite | 15-25% | Expected mechanism effect | | Injection site reactions | 5-8% | Typical for SC peptides | Serious Adverse Events and Warnings | Risk | Evidence | Frequency | Notes | |------|----------|-----------|-------| | Thyroid C-cell tumors | Rodent studies | Unknown in humans | Expected boxed warning (GLP-1 class effect) | | Pancreatitis | Theoretical | Rare | Class concern; monitor for symptoms | | Gallbladder disease | Phase 2 signal | Low single digits | Associated with rapid weight loss | | Hypoglycemia | Phase 2 | Rare (more with insulin/SU) | Glucose-dependent mechanism reduces risk | | Hepatic enzyme elevations | Phase 2 | Transient, reversible | Paradoxically, ALT improves overall in MASH | Glucagon-Specific Considerations - Heart rate: Small increases observed (2-5 bpm) - glucagon has mild chronotropic effect - Blood glucose: Net improvement despite glucagon component (GLP-1 balances) - Hepatic effects: Beneficial (improved liver enzymes overall) Anticipated Contraindications (Based on Class) 1. Personal or family history of medullary thyroid carcinoma (MTC) 2. Multiple endocrine neoplasia syndrome type 2 (MEN2) 3. Known hypersensitivity to survodutide 4. History of pancreatitis (relative) Special Populations | Population | Data Available | Recommendation | |------------|----------------|----------------| | Pregnancy | None | Likely contraindicated | | Renal impairment | Limited | Caution; await Phase 3 data | | Hepatic impairment | MASH trial data | Actually may benefit liver disease | | Elderly | Limited | Standard precautions | --- Pharmacokinetics Key Parameters | Parameter | Value | Clinical Relevance | |-----------|-------|-------------------| | Half-life | 6-7 days | Supports once-weekly dosing | | Bioavailability | 80% (SC) | High for peptide class | | Tmax | 24-72 hours | Gradual absorption from SC depot | | Protein binding | >99% | Albumin binding via fatty acid chain | | Metabolism | Proteolytic degradation | No CYP interactions expected | | Elimination | Renal + fecal | As peptide fragments | | Steady state | 4-5 weeks | Weekly dosing accumulation | Dosing Schedule (Phase 2/3) - Once weekly subcutaneous injection - Dose escalation over 12-20 weeks to target dose - Target doses in trials: 2.4mg, 4.8mg, 6.0mg Drug-Drug Interactions - No significant CYP-mediated interactions expected - May affect absorption of oral medications (delayed gastric emptying) - Caution with insulin/sulfonylureas (hypoglycemia risk) --- Benefits & Risks Evidence Table Potential Benefits (Evidence-Graded) | Outcome | Evidence Type | Effect Size | Confidence | Key Citation | |---------|---------------|-------------|------------|--------------| | Weight loss (obesity) | Phase 2 RCT | -18.7% at 46 wk | MODERATE | PMID: 37840093 | | MASH resolution | Phase 2 RCT | 83% vs 18% placebo | MODERATE | PMID: 38507727 | | Fibrosis improvement | Phase 2 RCT | 64.5% vs 25.9% | MODERATE | PMID: 38507727 | | HbA1c reduction (T2D) | Phase 2 RCT | -1.9% at 16 wk | MODERATE | PMID: 36702559 | | Weight loss vs semaglutide | Phase 2 RCT | Superior at 16 wk | LOW (short duration) | PMID: 36702559 | | Liver fat reduction | Phase 2 RCT | 82-86% | MODERATE | PMID: 38507727 | Potential Risks (Evidence-Graded) | Risk | Evidence Type | Frequency | Severity | Confidence | Citation | |------|---------------|-----------|----------|------------|----------| | GI adverse events | Phase 2 RCTs | 35-45% | Mild-mod | HIGH | All Phase 2 trials | | Nausea/vomiting | Phase 2 RCTs | 20-40% | Mild-mod | HIGH | All Phase 2 trials | | Heart rate increase | Phase 2 RCTs | 2-5 bpm | Mild | MODERATE | PMID: 37840093 | | Thyroid tumors | Rodent studies | Unknown | Serious | LOW | Preclinical | | Gallbladder disease | Phase 2 signal | Low | Moderate | LOW | Phase 2 data | Key Unknowns 1. Long-term safety beyond 48 weeks - Phase 3 trials will provide longer data 2. Cardiovascular outcomes - SYNCHRONIZE-CVOT ongoing, results 2029+ 3. Comparison to tirzepatide - No head-to-head trials 4. Effects on lean mass - Body composition data limited 5. Durability after discontinuation - Not yet studied 6. Safety in cirrhosis (F4) - MASH trials limited to F1-F3 --- Inferences Section This section contains logical reasoning from known evidence. These are NOT proven facts. Inference 1: Survodutide may be superior to GLP-1-only agents for MASH (a) Statement: The glucagon component provides direct hepatic benefits that GLP-1-only agents cannot match. (b) Why someone might believe it: - The liver has no GLP-1 receptors but abundant glucagon receptors - Glucagon drives hepatic lipid oxidation directly - Survodutide Phase 2 showed 83% MASH resolution vs semaglutide Phase 2 showing 59% (c) Supporting evidence: - Phase 2 MASH trial: 83% resolution rate, highest reported PMID: 38507727 - Mechanistic studies show GCGR activation drives hepatic fat oxidation PMID: 36425689 - Liver fat reduction 82-86% vs 50-60% with GLP-1-only agents (d) Contradicting evidence: - Not a head-to-head comparison (different trial populations) - Semaglutide ESSENCE Phase 3 showed 63% MASH resolution (competitive) - Longer-term data needed (e) Confidence: MODERATE (f) Study needed: Head-to-head trial of survodutide vs semaglutide in MASH with histological endpoints. --- Inference 2: Weight loss may plateau at similar levels to semaglutide (a) Statement: Despite the dual mechanism, ultimate weight loss efficacy may be similar to maximally-dosed semaglutide. (b) Why someone might believe it: - 18.7% at 46 weeks is comparable to semaglutide 2.4mg ( 15-17%) at similar timepoints - Tirzepatide (dual agonist) shows 21-22% - not dramatically higher - There may be a "ceiling" to appetite-based weight loss (c) Supporting evidence: - Phase 2 weight loss 18.7% at 46 weeks - Weight trajectory not yet plateaued, so final efficacy unknown - GI tolerability may limit dose escalation (d) Contradicting evidence: - Glucagon adds energy expenditure component (different mechanism) - Cross-trial comparisons are unreliable - Phase 3 may allow higher doses or longer duration (e) Confidence: LOW (f) Study needed: Head-to-head trial of survodutide vs semaglutide for weight loss outcomes over 68+ weeks. --- Inference 3: Cardiovascular outcomes may differ from GLP-1-only agents (a) Statement: The glucagon component could provide additional or different cardiovascular effects. (b) Why someone might believe it: - Glucagon has cardiac effects (mild inotropic, chronotropic) - Different metabolic profile (enhanced thermogenesis) - Liver disease improvement could reduce CV risk independently (c) Supporting evidence: - Glucagon receptors present on cardiomyocytes - Heart rate slightly elevated (2-5 bpm) in trials - MASH/fibrosis are CV risk factors; improvement could help (d) Contradicting evidence: - No CV outcome data yet (CVOT ongoing) - Higher heart rate could theoretically be negative - GLP-1 agonists have established CV benefit; adding glucagon is unknown (e) Confidence: LOW (f) Study needed: SYNCHRONIZE-CVOT results (expected 2029+). --- Comparisons Survodutide vs Semaglutide | Factor | Survodutide | Semaglutide | |--------|-------------|-------------| | Mechanism | Dual GLP-1R + GCGR | GLP-1R only | | Weight loss (Phase 2) | 18.7% at 46 wk | 14.9% at 68 wk (STEP 1) | | MASH resolution | 83% (Phase 2) | 62.9% (Phase 3 ESSENCE) | | Liver fat reduction | 82-86% | 50-60% | | HbA1c reduction | -1.9% at 16 wk | -1.5% at similar timepoints | | GI tolerability | 35-45% nausea | 30-45% nausea | | CV outcomes data | None yet (CVOT ongoing) | SELECT: 20% MACE reduction | | Approval status | Phase 3 (not approved) | FDA approved (multiple indications) | | Post-marketing data | None | Extensive (millions of patient-years) | | Direct liver effect | Yes (via GCGR) | No (liver lacks GLP-1R) | Summary: Survodutide appears particularly promising for MASH given the direct hepatic effects via glucagon receptor. For weight loss, efficacy appears comparable to semaglutide. Semaglutide has the advantage of established CV outcomes, multiple approvals, and extensive real-world experience. Head-to-head trials needed. Survodutide vs Tirzepatide | Factor | Survodutide | Tirzepatide | |--------|-------------|-------------| | Mechanism | GLP-1R + GCGR | GLP-1R + GIPR | | Weight loss | 18.7% (Phase 2, 46 wk) | 22.5% (Phase 3, 72 wk) | | Liver targeting | Yes (GCGR direct) | No (GIP indirect at best) | | MASH data | 83% resolution (Phase 2) | SYNERGY-NASH ongoing | | Approval status | Phase 3 | FDA approved (T2D, obesity, OSA) | | Energy expenditure | Enhanced (glucagon) | Minimal | | Adipose effects | Via GCGR | Via GIPR | Key difference: Survodutide targets the liver directly via glucagon receptor; tirzepatide enhances adipose tissue function via GIP receptor. Both add a second pathway to GLP-1, but the additional pathways differ meaningfully. Clinical implications: Survodutide may be preferred for patients with liver disease (MASH/NASH). Tirzepatide may be preferred when maximum weight loss is the primary goal (though survodutide Phase 3 data pending). Survodutide vs Retatrutide | Factor | Survodutide | Retatrutide | |--------|-------------|-------------| | Mechanism | Dual (GLP-1R + GCGR) | Triple (GLP-1R + GIPR + GCGR) | | Weight loss | 18.7% (Phase 2) | 24.2% / 28.7% (Phase 2/3) | | Liver effects | Yes (GCGR) | Yes (GCGR) | | Adipose effects | Moderate | Enhanced (GIPR) | | Development stage | Phase 3 | Phase 3 | | Developer | Boehringer Ingelheim | Eli Lilly | Summary: Retatrutide adds GIP receptor to the GLP-1/glucagon dual mechanism. Cross-trial comparisons suggest retatrutide may produce greater weight loss, but both share the glucagon-mediated liver benefits. Direct comparisons unavailable. --- Regulatory Status Current Status by Region | Region | Status | Notes | |--------|--------|-------| | USA (FDA) | Phase 3 | SYNCHRONIZE + ACHIEVE programs ongoing | | EU (EMA) | Phase 3 | Development parallel to US | | Japan | Development | Trials planned | | Breakthrough Therapy | Not yet | May be sought for MASH | Anticipated Timeline | Milestone | Estimated Date | |-----------|----------------| | Phase 3 topline results (obesity) | 2026 | | Phase 3 topline results (MASH) | 2026-2027 | | NDA/BLA submission | 2027 | | Potential FDA approval | Late 2027 - 2028 | | CVOT results | 2029+ | WADA Status Survodutide is not yet on any WADA list (investigational). If approved, status would likely mirror other GLP-1 agonists (NOT prohibited). --- Evidence Library Primary Clinical Studies | Citation | PMID | Year | Type | Population | Key Finding | Access | |----------|------|------|------|------------|-------------|--------| | le Roux CW et al. NEJM | 38507727 ( | 2024 | Phase 2 RCT | MASH (N=293) | 83% MASH resolution; 64.5% fibrosis im

name: TB-500
summary: A synthetic fragment of thymosin beta-4 marketed for tissue repair. While the parent compound has Phase 3 clinical research (RGN-259), TB-500 specifically has no published human data.
content: Overview Overview Quick Summary - Synthetic fragment of thymosin beta-4, a naturally occurring 43-amino acid protein - Not FDA approved for any indication; no regulatory approval worldwide - Parent compound (Tb4) has Phase 3 clinical research (RGN-259 for ophthalmology) - Popular in veterinary/equine applications before human interest grew - extensively researched in Australia - Global research spans USA, Europe (UK, Germany, Italy), Asia (China, South Korea), and Australia - Limited human safety and efficacy data available for TB-500 specifically Evidence Strength: Low - Moderate evidence for parent compound thymosin beta-4 including Phase 3 ophthalmology trials, but TB-500 fragment specifically lacks human clinical trials. Identity & Origins | Property | Value | |----------|-------| | Generic name | TB-500 | | Related compound | Thymosin beta-4 (Tb4) | | Gene | TMSB4X | | Class | Actin-sequestering peptide fragment | | Origin | Synthetic fragment of human thymosin beta-4 | Background Thymosin beta-4 is an endogenous protein involved in cell migration, wound healing, and tissue repair. TB-500 refers to a synthetic fragment or analog marketed for similar effects, though the exact relationship to Tb4 and its biological activity may vary by manufacturer. The parent compound has been extensively studied globally with significant research contributions from the USA, UK, Germany, China, South Korea, and Australia. Scientific Pathways Core Mechanism: Actin Binding & Cytoskeletal Regulation Thymosin beta-4 is one of the most abundant intracellular proteins in mammalian cells. Its primary function is regulating actin dynamics. The LKKTETQ Motif: TB-500's active region (amino acids 17-23) contains the LKKTETQ sequence with high affinity for actin binding. This was confirmed by Sosne et al. (PMID:20179146) as critical for wound healing activity. TB-500 binds monomeric G-actin | Prevents actin polymerization | Promotes actin sequestration | +-- Cell migration enhanced +-- Cell morphology changes +-- Cytoskeletal reorganization Actin is a protein that forms the "skeleton" inside cells. When actin molecules link together (polymerize), they form stiff fibers. TB-500 binds to individual actin molecules and prevents them from linking up, keeping them in reserve. This gives cells more flexibility to change shape and move - essential for healing. Wound Healing Cascade Cellular Effects: | Process | Mechanism | Result | What It Means | |---------|-----------|--------|---------------| | Re-epithelialization | Keratinocyte/fibroblast migration | 42-61% increased re-epithelialization (PMID:10469335) | Skin cells spread across wounds faster | | ECM formation | Stimulates collagen production | Structural tissue repair | Builds the scaffolding that holds tissue together | | Inflammation | Modulates cytokines (via miR-146a) | Reduced IRAK1/TRAF6 - less inflammation | Calms down excessive inflammatory responses | Angiogenesis Pathways Signaling Cascades: TB-500 -> Endothelial cell proliferation/migration | +-- PI3K/Akt pathway activation +-- ERK pathway activation +-- VEGFA upregulation | +-- New blood vessel formation +-- Tubule formation +-- Aortic ring sprouting Endothelial cells are the cells that line blood vessels. TB-500 may help these cells multiply and organize into new blood vessel tubes. The result: more blood supply to injured areas, bringing the oxygen and nutrients needed for healing. Cardiac Repair (Animal Studies) Mechanisms in Myocardial Injury: - Binds/alters cytoskeletal actin filaments - Activates ILK-Akt pathway (cell survival, motility) - key finding from Bock-Marquette et al., Nature 2004 - Promotes epicardial progenitor cell migration (UK research - Riley lab, Oxford) - Reduces scar formation and prevents cardiac rupture in MI models (PMID:24993041) | Effect | Finding | What It Means | |--------|---------|---------------| | Anti-apoptotic | Bcl-2 activation prevents cell death | Helps heart cells survive after injury instead of dying | | Anti-fibrotic | Reduced collagen deposition (heart, lung, liver, kidney) | Less scar tissue formation in damaged organs | | Regenerative | Enhanced cardiac function post-MI in animals | Heart pumps better after simulated heart attacks | After a heart attack, heart cells die and are replaced with scar tissue. Scars can't pump blood, so more scarring means a weaker heart. In animal studies, thymosin beta-4 helped heart cells survive the injury and reduced scarring, leading to better heart function. Neurological Effects (Animal Studies) Research from Henry Ford Hospital (Chopp, Xiong et al.) demonstrates: - Stroke recovery : Improved functional neurological outcome in embolic stroke model (PMID:20434526) - Traumatic brain injury : Reduced lesion volume, increased neurogenesis (PMID:20617881) - Oligodendrogenesis : Promotes myelin-producing cell proliferation (PMID:19619644) - Potential for demyelinating diseases : MS, TBI recovery applications under investigation Anti-Fibrotic Effects (Global Research) | Organ | Research Group | Finding | |-------|----------------|---------| | Heart | China (PMID:21684313) | Blocked TGF-b1/Smad3 pathway | | Lung | Italy (PMID:23398086) | Reduced bleomycin-induced fibrosis | | Liver | South Korea (PMID:26710057) | Attenuated hepatic stellate cell activation | Anti-Inflammatory Effects Modulates inflammation through: - MicroRNA-146a pathway : Suppresses IRAK1 and TRAF6 - NF-kB suppression : Key mechanism in corneal repair (PMID:26241399) - Reduced tissue fibrosis in multiple organ models - Regulates inflammatory cytokines and chemokines Your body uses inflammation to fight infection and start healing, but too much inflammation causes collateral damage. TB-500 may help fine-tune this response - allowing enough inflammation to heal while preventing the excessive inflammation that creates scar tissue and tissue damage. TB-500 vs Thymosin Beta-4 | Aspect | TB-500 | Thymosin Beta-4 | |--------|--------|-----------------| | Structure | N-acetylated fragment | Full 43-aa protein | | Half-life | Extended (acetylation) | Short (minutes) | | Human trials | None | Phase 3 completed | | Direct studies | Limited | Extensive | --- Research Summary Global Evidence Overview | Category | Count | Notes | |----------|-------|-------| | Human RCTs (Tb4) | 8 | Parent compound, not TB-500 | | Human RCTs (TB-500) | 0 | No published trials | | Preclinical (Tb4) | 20+ | Various models globally | | Preclinical (TB-500) | Limited | Fragment-specific data scarce | | Total sources | 34 | 14 open access | | Regions represented | 10+ | USA, UK, Germany, Italy, China, South Korea, Hong Kong, Australia | Research by Region North America (USA/Canada) - RegeneRx Biopharmaceuticals clinical development program - Goldstein lab (NIH) foundational wound healing research - Henry Ford Hospital neurological research (Chopp, Xiong) - Sosne lab ophthalmology research Europe - UK : Oxford cardiac regeneration research (Riley/Smart lab) - epicardial progenitor cells - Germany : Hannappel lab - Tb4 biochemistry, post-translational modifications - Italy : Conte et al. - pulmonary fibrosis research - EU Clinical Trials : SEER-3 Phase 3 (failed primary endpoint 2025) Asia-Pacific - China : Extensive preclinical research on anti-fibrosis, neuroregeneration - South Korea : HLB Therapeutics (RegeneRx partner); liver fibrosis research - Hong Kong : Jockey Club anti-doping detection methods Australia - Racing Victoria veterinary/equine studies - Tendon repair research in horses - Doping detection method development Key Evidence Highlights For Thymosin Beta-4 (parent compound): 1. Phase 3 trials in ophthalmology (neurotrophic keratopathy) - 60% achieved complete healing 2. Phase 2 trials in wound healing and dry eye 3. Phase 1 cardiac trial (RGN-352) 4. Extensive preclinical across wound healing, cardiac, neuro, ophthalmology, anti-fibrosis For TB-500: 1. No completed human clinical trials 2. Veterinary use in horses precedes human interest (Australian research significant) 3. Assumed similar properties to Tb4 - but not validated --- Key Human Trials Thymosin Beta-4 Clinical Development (Parent Compound) | Trial ID | Indication | Phase | Status | Region | |----------|------------|-------|--------|--------| | NCT02016131 ( | SEER-1: Neurotrophic Keratopathy | 3 | Completed - 60% healing | USA | | NCT03967665 ( | SEER-2: Neurotrophic Keratopathy | 3 | Recruiting | USA | | EUCTR2018-002231-14 | SEER-3: Neurotrophic Keratopathy | 3 | Failed primary endpoint | EU | | NCT00832091 ( | Venous Stasis Ulcers | 2 | Completed | USA | | NCT00408083 ( | Dry Eye | 2 | Completed | USA | | NCT01387347 ( | Dry Eye (CAE model) | 2 | Completed | USA | | NCT01311518 ( | Acute Myocardial Infarction | 1 | Completed | USA | Ophthalmology Trials Detail SEER-1 (Phase 3, USA) - PMID:36613994 - 0.1% RGN-259 ophthalmic solution for neurotrophic keratopathy - 60% achieved complete corneal healing vs 31.9% placebo at 28 days - Statistically significant improvement in epithelial defect closure SEER-3 (Phase 3, EU) - Failed to meet primary endpoint in 2025 - Stronger-than-expected placebo effect noted - US trials continue (SEER-2) Dry Eye Trials - PMID:30220049 - RGN-259 improved corneal staining, tear breakup time, and ocular discomfort vs placebo - Phase 2 results published in Translational Vision Science & Technology (2018) Wound Healing Trials Phase 2 Venous Stasis Ulcers - NCT00832091 - Approximately 1 month faster healing compared to placebo - Published in NYAS proceedings TB-500 Specific Trials No human clinical trials have been published for TB-500 specifically. TB-500 is not approved by the FDA or other regulatory agencies. Last updated: January 2026.

name: Thymosin Alpha-1
summary: A 28-amino acid immunomodulatory peptide approved in over 35 countries including China and Italy for hepatitis B/C, cancer adjuvant therapy, and immunodeficiency. Extensive human clinical trial data spanning decades with strong evidence for immune enhancement.
content: Overview Overview Quick Summary - 28-amino acid immunomodulatory peptide derived from thymosin fraction 5 (thymus extract) - Approved in 35+ countries including China, Italy, Philippines, and several Asian/South American nations - Brand name: Zadaxin (SciClone Pharmaceuticals / Alpha-1 Biomedical) - Key indications: Hepatitis B, Hepatitis C, cancer adjuvant therapy, immunodeficiency states - Orphan drug status granted by FDA for hepatitis B and hepatocellular carcinoma - Over 100 clinical trials conducted globally since the 1980s - First synthetic peptide drug approved for immune modulation Evidence Strength: High - Extensive Phase 2/3 clinical trials, regulatory approvals in multiple countries, decades of clinical use with established safety profile. One of the most well-studied immunomodulatory peptides. Identity & Origins | Property | Value | |----------|-------| | Generic name | Thymalfasin (INN) | | Brand name | Zadaxin | | Sequence | Ac-SDAAVDTSSEITTKDLKEKKEVVEEAEN | | Molecular weight | 3,108 Da | | Class | Immunomodulatory peptide | | Origin | Originally isolated from bovine thymus; now synthetic | | Developer | Allan Goldstein (George Washington University) | Discovery Timeline - 1972: Thymosin fraction 5 (crude thymus extract) isolated by Allan Goldstein at University of Texas - 1977: Thymosin alpha-1 purified and sequenced from fraction 5 by Goldstein and colleagues - 1983: Chemical synthesis achieved; clinical development begins - 1984-1990s: Extensive Phase 2/3 trials in hepatitis and cancer - 1996: First regulatory approval (China) for chronic hepatitis B - 1999: Approved in Italy for hepatitis B/C and as vaccine adjuvant - 2001: FDA Orphan Drug designation for hepatitis B - 2003: FDA Orphan Drug designation for hepatocellular carcinoma - 2006: Approved in over 35 countries worldwide - 2020-2021: Emergency use in COVID-19 (China, Italy) Scientific Pathways 1. T-Cell Development and Activation Thymosin Alpha-1 | v Binds TLR2/TLR9 on dendritic cells | v MyD88-dependent signaling | v NF-kB and MAPK activation | v +-- CD4+ T-cell differentiation +-- CD8+ cytotoxic T-cell activation +-- Th1/Th2 balance regulation +-- Treg modulation Thymosin alpha-1 binds to Toll-like receptors (TLR2, TLR9) on dendritic cells, triggering intracellular signaling cascades that promote T-cell maturation and activation. This explains its ability to restore immune function in immunocompromised patients. 2. Dendritic Cell Maturation Thymosin Alpha-1 + Dendritic Cells | v Increased MHC class II expression | v Enhanced antigen presentation | v +-- Improved T-cell priming +-- Stronger vaccine responses +-- Better tumor antigen recognition 3. Cytokine Network Modulation Thymosin Alpha-1 | v Increases: ├── IL-2 (T-cell growth factor) ├── IL-12 (Th1 polarization) ├── IFN-alpha (antiviral) ├── IFN-gamma (cellular immunity) | v Decreases (in inflammation): ├── IL-1 (pro-inflammatory) ├── TNF-alpha (inflammation/cachexia) ├── IL-6 (acute phase response) This bidirectional cytokine modulation is key - thymosin alpha-1 is immunomodulatory rather than simply immunostimulatory, making it suitable for conditions involving both immune deficiency and dysregulation. 4. NK Cell Enhancement Thymosin Alpha-1 | v Increased NK cell proliferation | v Enhanced perforin/granzyme production | v Improved cytotoxic activity against: ├── Virus-infected cells ├── Tumor cells └── Intracellular pathogens Receptor Interactions | Receptor | Cell Type | Effect | |----------|-----------|--------| | TLR2 | Dendritic cells, macrophages | Pattern recognition, DC maturation | | TLR9 | Plasmacytoid DCs, B cells | IFN-alpha production, adaptive immunity | | Unknown receptor | Thymocytes | T-cell differentiation | Molecular Structure Thymosin alpha-1 is N-terminally acetylated, which is critical for biological activity and stability: | Feature | Value | Significance | |---------|-------|--------------| | Length | 28 amino acids | Optimal for immune recognition | | N-terminus | Acetylated serine | Required for activity | | Charge | -8 at pH 7 | Anionic peptide | | Structure | Disordered in solution | Flexible for receptor binding | | Stability | Relatively stable | Suitable for pharmaceutical use | --- Research Summary Evidence Overview | Category | Count | Notes | |----------|-------|-------| | Phase 3 RCTs | 12+ | Hepatitis B/C, cancer | | Phase 2 RCTs | 20+ | Multiple indications | | Human studies total | 100+ | Decades of clinical research | | Countries with approval | 35+ | China, Italy, Philippines, etc. | | Patients treated | Millions | Particularly in Asia | | Safety database | Extensive | Generally well-tolerated | Research by Region Asia (China, Philippines, Taiwan, Singapore) - Largest clinical experience (millions of patients) - Approved for hepatitis B, hepatitis C, cancer adjuvant - First-line adjuvant in hepatocellular carcinoma protocols - COVID-19 emergency use (2020-2021) Europe (Italy) - Approved since 1999 for chronic hepatitis B/C - Used as vaccine adjuvant in elderly - Cancer immunotherapy adjunct - Extensive long-term safety data United States - FDA Orphan Drug status (HBV, HCC) - Multiple Phase 3 trials conducted - Not yet FDA approved (regulatory pathway ongoing) - Used in integrative oncology settings Global - Over 70 countries with completed clinical trials - WHO pre-qualification discussions - Extensive published literature Key Evidence Highlights 1. Hepatitis B: Multiple Phase 3 trials showing enhanced viral clearance when combined with standard therapy 2. Hepatitis C: Improved sustained virological response (SVR) in combination with interferon/ribavirin 3. Hepatocellular Carcinoma: Improved survival and reduced recurrence as adjuvant therapy 4. Vaccine Enhancement: 2-4 fold increased antibody responses in elderly 5. Sepsis: Reduced mortality in severe sepsis trials 6. COVID-19: Reduced mortality in severe cases (observational data) --- Key Human Trials Hepatitis B Trials | Trial | Phase | N | Results | Region | |-------|-------|---|---------|--------| | Andreone 1996 | 3 | 98 | Significantly higher HBeAg clearance vs IFN alone | Italy | | Chien 1998 | 3 | 130 | Enhanced response in combination therapy | Taiwan | | Iino 2005 | 3 | 201 | Improved HBV DNA suppression | Japan | | You 2006 | 3 | 178 | Higher sustained response rate | China | Meta-analysis findings: A Cochrane-style meta-analysis (PMID:19207891) of 8 RCTs found thymosin alpha-1 significantly improved biochemical and virological response in chronic HBV when added to standard therapy. | IL-2, IFN-gamma upregulation | 9596549 | --- Safety Profile Established Safety Thymosin alpha-1 has an excellent safety profile established over decades of clinical use in millions of patients. Clinical Trial Safety Data | Finding | Frequency | Notes | |---------|-----------|-------| | Injection site reactions | Common | Mild, transient | | Flu-like symptoms | Occasional | Mild fever, myalgia | | Fatigue | Occasional | Usually mild | | Nausea | Rare | Dose-dependent | | Serious adverse events | Rare | No consistent pattern | Long-Term Safety | Parameter | Finding | Source | | Long-term follow-up studies | | Organ toxicity | Not observed | Clinical trials, post-marketing | Special Populations | Population | Safety Data | |------------|-------------| | Elderly | Well-tolerated; extensively studied | | Cancer patients | Good tolerability with chemotherapy | | Immunocompromised | Generally safe; monitor closely | | Hepatitis patients | Decades of safe use | | Pregnancy | Limited data; avoid unless necessary | Contraindications - Known hypersensitivity to thymosin alpha-1 - Current organ transplant recipients (theoretical concern about rejection) - Concurrent immunosuppressive therapy (may reduce efficacy) Drug Interactions | Interaction | Effect | Management | |-------------|--------|------------| | Immunosuppressants | Reduced Ta1 efficacy | Avoid combination | | Interferon | Synergistic/additive | Monitor for increased effects | | Chemotherapy | Generally safe | Standard monitoring | | Vaccines | Enhanced response | Potential benefit | name: Tesamorelin
summary: An FDA-approved GHRH analog for reducing visceral fat in HIV-associated lipodystrophy. One of few GH-releasing peptides with regulatory approval and Phase 3 data.
content: Overview Overview Quick Summary - Synthetic GHRH analog with demonstrated clinical efficacy - FDA approved (2010) for HIV-associated lipodystrophy (visceral adiposity) - Phase 3 evidence supporting efficacy and safety - Daily subcutaneous injection required - Reduces visceral fat without significant effect on subcutaneous fat Evidence Strength: High — FDA approval based on Phase 3 trials demonstrating efficacy in reducing visceral adipose tissue. Identity & Origins | Property | Value | |----------|-------| | Generic name | Tesamorelin | | Brand name | Egrifta (Egrifta SV, Egrifta WR) | | Research code | TH9507 | | Class | Growth hormone-releasing hormone (GHRH) analog | | Manufacturer | Theratechnologies Inc. (Montreal, Canada) | | Development origin | Montreal Clinical Research Institute (IRCM) | Development Timeline - 2000s: Clinical development for HIV lipodystrophy - 2007: Phase 3 pivotal trial published (NEJM) - 2010: FDA approval for HIV-associated visceral adiposity (Egrifta F1) - 2019: Egrifta SV (F4, reconstitution-free) approved - 2020: FDA approval for Phase 3 NASH development - 2025: Egrifta WR (F8, weekly reconstitution) approved - Ongoing: Research in NAFLD/NASH, cognitive function Molecular Structure: Modified GHRH(1-44) Tesamorelin is the full 44-amino acid human GHRH sequence with a trans-3-hexenoic acid (hexenoyl) modification at the N-terminus tyrosine. This modification: - Provides resistance to DPP-IV degradation - Enhances stability vs endogenous GHRH - Maintains full receptor binding activity GHRH Receptor Signaling Tesamorelin binds GHRH-R (pituitary somatotrophs) ↓ Gαs activation ↓ Adenylyl cyclase stimulation ↓ ↑ cAMP → PKA activation ↓ ├── GH gene transcription ↑ ├── GH synthesis ↑ └── GH vesicle exocytosis ↓ GH release in physiologic pulses ↓ Liver: GH → IGF-1 production ↑ Why HIV Patients Have Lipodystrophy Context for mechanism: - HIV patients on ART often develop reduced GH secretion and lower GH pulse amplitude - This correlates with increased visceral adipose tissue (VAT) - Up to 30% of ART patients experience abdominal fat accumulation - Tesamorelin restores a more youthful GH rhythm Visceral Fat Specificity Why tesamorelin targets visceral fat: - VAT has higher lipolytic sensitivity to GH/IGF-1 - GH preferentially mobilizes fat from visceral compartment - Subcutaneous fat less affected (different receptor density) - No significant effect on limb fat or total body weight | Fat Compartment | Effect | Mechanism | |-----------------|--------|-----------| | Visceral (VAT) | ↓ 15-27 cm² | High GH receptor expression | | Subcutaneous | No change | Lower sensitivity | | Hepatic fat | ↓ 4-5% | Direct GH lipolytic action | | Lean body mass | ↑ 1.4 kg | GH anabolic effect | Difference from Exogenous GH | Aspect | Tesamorelin | Exogenous GH | |--------|-------------|--------------| | GH pattern | Pulsatile (physiologic) | Continuous | | Feedback | Preserved | Suppressed | | IGF-1 | Moderate increase | Often excessive | | Tachyphylaxis | Avoided | Can occur | | Overnight GH | Normal | Absent | Duration of Effect Effects of tesamorelin are not sustained after discontinuation : - VAT returns to near-baseline within weeks of stopping - Chronic therapy required to maintain reductions - Highlights that mechanism addresses symptoms, not underlying cause --- Research Summary Evidence Overview | Category | Count | Notes | |----------|-------|-------| | Human RCTs | 12 | Phase 3 pivotal trials, NAFLD, cognitive, INSTI | | Human observational | 8 | Long-term extension data, real-world evidence | | Open-label extensions | 3 | Durability data | | Preclinical | 4 | Mechanism validation | | Meta-analyses | 2 | Systematic reviews | | Regulatory documents | 6 | FDA, Health Canada, EMA | | Clinical trial registrations | 10 | ClinicalTrials.gov registry | | Total sources | 52 | 42 open access | Geographic Scope | Region | Source Types | Key Contributions | |--------|--------------|-------------------| | USA | PubMed, FDA, NIH | Pivotal trials, FDA approval, MGH research | | Canada | Theratechnologies, CADTH, Health Canada | Development sponsor, HTA, regulatory | | Europe | Europe PMC, EMA, EACS | Lipodystrophy cohorts, guidelines (not approved) | | Australia | PubMed | Case definition development | | Global | ClinicalTrials.gov | Multinational trial registry | Key Evidence Highlights 1. Phase 3 Trials: Consistent VAT reduction demonstrated (n=806 pooled) 2. NAFLD/NASH: Reduced liver fat 37%, prevented fibrosis progression 3. Cognitive Function (TEAME): Trend toward improvement, not statistically significant 4. Metabolic Effects: Improvements in triglycerides, trunk fat, IGF-1 5. Long-term Data: Effects maintained with continued treatment 6. Safety: Generally well-tolerated, known GH-related effects --- Key Human Trials Phase 3 Pivotal Trials (HIV Lipodystrophy) | Trial | NCT ID | N | Duration | VAT Change | PMID | |-------|--------|---|----------|------------|------| | LIPO-010 | NCT00123253 | 412 | 26 weeks | -15.2% vs +5.0% | 18057338 | | CTR-1011 | NCT00435136 | 402 | 26 weeks | -11.0% vs +0.6% | 20554713 | | Pooled analysis | — | 806 | 26 weeks | -27.7 cm² (MD) | 20554713 | NAFLD/NASH Trial (NCT02196831) | Parameter | Tesamorelin | Placebo | p-value | |-----------|-------------|---------|---------| | N | 30 | 30 | — | | Duration | 12 months | 12 months | — | | HFF reduction | -37% relative | — | 0.016 | | HFF <5% achieved | 35% | 4% | 0.007 | | Fibrosis progression | Prevented | Not prevented | Sig. | Reference: Fourman LT et al. Lancet HIV. 2019 (PMID: 31611038) Cognitive Function Trial (TEAME-like, NCT02572323) | Parameter | Tesamorelin | SOC | p-value | |-----------|-------------|-----|---------| | N | 44 | 29 | — | | Duration | 6 months | 6 months | — | | Cognitive improvement | Trend (+0.146) | +0.103 | 0.673 (NS) | | Waist circumference | -2.7 cm | — | 0.015 | Reference: PMID: 39813152 (2025) Additional Trials | Indication | NCT ID | Status | Key Finding | |------------|--------|--------|-------------| | Type 2 diabetes | NCT01264497 | Completed | No glycemic impairment | | COPD + exercise | NCT01388920 | Completed | Lean mass increase | | Integrase inhibitor users | — | Completed | Effective despite INSTI | | Phase 3 NASH | NCT03375788 | Ongoing | FDA-approved development | Key Findings Summary - Primary endpoint: Significant reduction in trunk fat measured by CT - IGF-1 increase: Mean 81% increase from baseline - Lipid effects: Variable; some improvement in triglycerides - Glucose: Generally neutral to mild impairment - Liver fat: 37% relative reduction in NAFLD trial - Cognitive: Trend toward improvement, larger trials needed --- Preclinical Evidence Animal Studies | Study Type | Model | Key Findings | |------------|-------|--------------| | GHRH activity | Rat | Confirmed receptor binding | | GH release | Primate | Physiologic GH increase | | Metabolism | Rodent | Fat reduction observed | --- Safety Profile Clinical Trial Adverse Events | Event | Incidence | Notes | |-------|-----------|-------| | Injection site reactions | 25-30% | Erythema, pruritus | | Arthralgia | 10-15% | GH-related | | Peripheral edema | 5-10% | GH-related, fluid retention | | Paresthesia | 5-8% | May be GH-related | | Hyperglycemia | 4-5% | Monitor glucose | Warnings and Precautions - Fluid retention: Can exacerbate edema conditions - Glucose effects: May impair glucose tolerance - Carpal tunnel: GH-related risk - Malignancy: Contraindicated with active malignancy - Hypersensitivity: Rare allergic reactions Contraindications - Active malignancy - Disruption of hypothalamic-pituitary axis - Known hypersensitivity to tesamorelin or mannitol - Pregnancy --- Pharmacokinetics Key Parameters | Parameter | Value | Notes | |-----------|-------|-------| | Half-life | 26-38 minutes | Short-acting | | Administration | Daily SC injection | Typically morning | | GH peak | 15-60 minutes | Post-injection | | Bioavailability | 4% | Subcutaneous | Clinical Implications Short half-life means: - Daily dosing required - Pulsatile GH release pattern - GH normalizes between doses --- Regulatory Status | Region | Status | Indication | Year | Document ID | |--------|--------|------------|------|-------------| | USA (FDA) | Approved | HIV lipodystrophy | 2010 | NDA 022505 | | USA (FDA) | Egrifta SV (F4) | HIV lipodystrophy | 2019 | NDA 022505/S-007 | | USA (FDA) | Egrifta WR (F8) | HIV lipodystrophy | 2025 | NDA 022505/S-015 | | Canada (Health Canada) | Approved | HIV lipodystrophy | 2015 | DIN 02440830 | | EU (EMA) | Withdrawn | — | 2012 | EMEA/H/C/002397 | Note on EMA status: Theratechnologies withdrew the European marketing authorization application in 2012. This was a commercial decision, not related to safety or efficacy concerns. The CHMP assessment was positive on benefit-risk. Tesamorelin remains unavailable in Europe. Formulation Evolution | Formulation | Year | Key Feature | |-------------|------|-------------| | Egrifta (F1) | 2010 | Original approval | | Egrifta SV (F4) | 2019 | No reconstitution required | | Egrifta WR (F8) | 2025 | Weekly reconstitution, 8x concentration | Note: Egrifta WR and Egrifta SV are not interchangeable due to differences in concentration, reconstitution, and storage. Approved Indication Reduction of excess abdominal fat in HIV-infected patients with lipodystrophy. Pipeline Development - NAFLD/NASH: FDA approved Phase 3 development (Dec 2020) - NCT03375788: Phase II/III trial for liver-biopsy confirmed NASH Not approved for: - General weight loss - Anti-aging - Athletic performance - NAFLD/NASH (investigational) --- Related Peptides GHRH Analog Comparison | Peptide | Half-life | FDA Status | Indication | |---------|-----------|------------|------------| | Tesamorelin | 30 min | Approved | HIV lipodystrophy | | Sermorelin | 10-20 min | Was approved | GH deficiency (discontinued) | | CJC-1295 | Days (DAC) | Not approved | None | Tesamorelin vs Sermorelin | Aspect | Tesamorelin | Sermorelin | |--------|-------------|------------| | Status | Currently approved | Discontinued | | Indication | HIV lipodystrophy | GH deficiency | | Efficacy | Phase 3 proven | Limited modern data | --- Current Research Directions Active Research Areas | Indication | Phase | NCT ID | Status | |------------|-------|--------|--------| | NAFLD/NASH | 2/3 | NCT03375788 | Ongoing | | Cognitive function (HIV) | 2 | NCT02572323 | Completed | | Peripheral nerve injury | 2 | NCT03150511 | Ongoing | | General obesity | Exploratory | — | Interest | NAFLD/NASH Development Phase 2 trial in HIV-associated NAFLD showed (PMID: 31611038): - 37% relative reduction in liver fat (HFF) - Prevented fibrosis progression vs placebo - 35% achieved HFF <5% (vs 4% placebo) - Improved hepatic gene expression (oxidative phosphorylation) - Decreased inflammatory markers Regulatory progress: FDA approved Phase 3 NASH development in December 2020 for patients with biopsy-confirmed NASH (CRN stage 2-3). Cognitive Function Research (TEAME Trial) Phase 2 trial in HIV with abdominal obesity (PMID: 39813152): - Mechanism hypothesis: Visceral adiposity linked to neuroinflammation and reduced IGF-1 - Primary outcome: Trend toward cognitive improvement (p=0.060), not significant between groups - Secondary outcome: Significant waist circumference reduction (-2.7 cm, p=0.015) - Limitations: Underpowered, no placebo arm - Conclusion: Short-term VAT reduction may not be sufficient for cognitive benefits --- Citation Table Primary Clinical Evidence (PubMed) | Source | Year | Type | PMID | Key Finding | Access | |--------|------|------|------|-------------|--------| | Falutz J et al.
